  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CTTA methods rely on backward-alignment that rigidly aligns test data with source-domain surrogates, suffering from unreliable supervision and inability to handle evolving distribution shifts.

Method: Constructs a compact knowledge base of generated class exemplars pre-deployment. At test time, uses a multi-level bridging mechanism to dynamically inject incoming data styles into these proxies at input, statistical, and representation levels while preserving semantics, producing high-fidelity proxies for reliable on-demand supervision.

Result: Achieves consistent and substantial improvements over recent state-of-the-art methods across standard CTTA benchmarks.

Conclusion: The forward-facilitation paradigm with Dynamic Style Bridging effectively overcomes limitations of backward-alignment methods, enabling stable adaptation under continual distribution shifts.

Abstract: Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to empower perception systems to handle dynamic distribution shifts encountered after deployment. Existing methods predominantly follow a backward-alignment paradigm, which rigidly aligns incoming data with supervisory surrogates derived from the source domain. Consequently, they struggle with unreliable supervision and evolving distribution shifts. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a novel forward-facilitation paradigm through a method termed Dynamic Style Bridging. Prior to deployment, we construct a compact knowledge base of generated class exemplars. During test time, to mitigate inherent generative bias and adapt these proxies to incoming data, we propose a multi-level bridging mechanism. This mechanism dynamically injects the proxies with incoming data styles at the input, statistical, and representation levels, while preserving the original semantics of the proxies. These high-fidelity proxies are then used to provide reliable, on-demand supervisory signals, enabling stable adaptation under continual shifts. Extensive experiments across standard CTTA benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves consistent and substantial improvements over recent state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/z1358/DAS}.

</details>


### [296] [CATA: Continual Machine Unlearning via Conflict-Averse Task Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18610)
*Shen Lin,Junhao Dong,Rongjie Chen,Xiaoyu Zhang,Li Xu,Xiaofeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes CATA, a conflict-averse task arithmetic method for continual machine unlearning in vision-language models (VLMs), addressing sequential removal requests while maintaining forgetting effectiveness, model fidelity, and forgetting persistence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs face privacy, copyright, and undesirable content concerns due to large-scale training data. Existing unlearning research focuses on single-shot removal, but real-world deployment requires handling sequential (continual) unlearning requests over time.

Method: CATA (Conflict-Averse Task Arithmetic) represents each forget request as an unlearning task vector, maintains historical task vectors, and performs sign-aware conflict-averse aggregation to suppress conflicting update components that may weaken previous forgetting effects.

Result: Under both single-shot and continual settings, CATA outperforms baselines in forgetting effectiveness, model fidelity, and forgetting persistence.

Conclusion: CATA effectively addresses three key challenges in continual VLM unlearning: effectiveness (removing target knowledge), fidelity (preserving retained utility), and persistence (preventing knowledge re-emergence under sequential updates).

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown remarkable ability in aligning visual and textual representations, enabling a wide range of multimodal applications. However, their large-scale training data inevitably raises concerns about privacy, copyright, and undesirable content, creating a strong need for machine unlearning. While existing studies mainly focus on single-shot unlearning, practical VLM deployment often involves sequential removal requests over time, giving rise to continual machine unlearning. In this work, we make the first attempt to study continual unlearning for VLMs and identify three key challenges in this setting: effectiveness in removing target knowledge, fidelity in preserving retained model utility, and persistence in preventing knowledge re-emergence under sequential updates. To address these challenges, we propose CATA, a conflict-averse task arithmetic method that represents each forget request as an unlearning task vector. By maintaining historical task vectors and performing sign-aware conflict-averse aggregation, CATA suppresses conflicting update components that may weaken previous forgetting effects. Extensive experiments under both single-shot and continual settings show that CATA outperforms baselines in terms of forgetting effectiveness, model fidelity, and forgetting persistence.

</details>


### [297] [CrossView Suite: Harnessing Cross-view Spatial Intelligence of MLLMs with Dataset, Model and Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18621)
*Wei Wang,Yuqian Yuan,Tianwei Lin,Wenqiao Zhang,Siliang Tang,Jun Xiao,Yueting Zhuang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CrossView Suite addresses three major gaps in cross-view spatial reasoning for MLLMs by introducing a large-scale dataset (CrossViewSet), a comprehensive benchmark (CrossViewBench), and a three-stage reasoning framework (CrossViewer) that explicitly aligns multi-view object representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with cross-view spatial reasoning due to: (1) scarcity of large-scale annotated training data, (2) lack of comprehensive evaluation benchmarks, and (3) absence of explicit object-level alignment mechanisms across multiple viewpoints.

Method: The authors propose CrossView Suite with three components: (1) CrossViewSet - a large-scale dataset (1.6M samples, 17 task types) curated by a multi-agent data engine; (2) CrossViewBench - a scene-disjoint benchmark for systematic evaluation; (3) CrossViewer - a progressive three-stage framework following Perception → Alignment → Reasoning paradigm, using an adaptive spatial region tokenizer for fine-grained object representations and explicit multi-view object alignment.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that large-scale training data, systematic evaluation, and explicit cross-view alignment are all critical for advancing MLLMs from single-view perception toward spatial intelligence.

Conclusion: Cross-view spatial reasoning in MLLMs requires coordinated advances in data, evaluation, and explicit alignment mechanisms, and the proposed CrossView Suite provides a comprehensive solution to bridge the gap between single-view perception and multi-view spatial intelligence.

Abstract: Spatial intelligence requires multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to move beyond single-view perception and reason consistently about objects, visibility, geometry, and interactions across multiple viewpoints. However, progress in cross-view reasoning remains limited by three major gaps: the scarcity of large-scale well-annotated training data, the lack of comprehensive benchmarks for systematic evaluation, and the absence of explicit alignment mechanisms that establish object-level consistency across views. To address these gaps, we thoroughly develop CrossView Suite across three coordinated components: CrossViewSet, CrossViewBench, and CrossViewer. Firstly, we introduce a multi-agent data engine to meticulously curate a large-scale, high-quality cross-view instruction dataset, termed CrossViewSet, covering 17 fine-grained task types with 1.6M samples. Second, we meticulously create a scene-disjoint CrossViewBench to comprehensively assess the cross-view spatial understanding capability of an MLLM, evaluating it across various aspects. Finally, we propose CrossViewer, a progressive three-stage framework for cross-view spatial reasoning in MLLMs, following a Perception -> Alignment -> Reasoning paradigm. Our method equips an adaptive spatial region tokenizer to capture fine-grained object representations, and then aligns the multi-view objects explicitly, and thus fuses aligned features for boosting the cross-view inference capacity for MLLMs. Extensive experiments and analyses show that large-scale training data, systematic evaluation, and explicit cross-view alignment are all critical for advancing MLLMs from single-view perception toward real-world spatial intelligence. The project page is available at https://github.com/Thinkirin/Crossview-Suite.

</details>


### [298] [SPIKE: An Adaptive Dual Controller Framework for Cost-Efficient Long-Horizon Game Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18636)
*Wencan Jiang,Jiangning Zhang,Jianbiao Mei,Jinzhuo Liu,Yu Yang,Xiaobin Hu,Zhucun Xue,Yong Liu,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPIKE is an adaptive dual-controller framework for long-horizon game agents that uses event-triggered strategic reasoning (instead of per-step reasoning) combined with reactive execution and hierarchical memory, achieving significant gains in success rate, token efficiency, and latency on StarDojo's Lite-100 benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long-horizon multimodal game agents struggle with a trade-off between costly per-step reasoning (high token/latency cost) and reactive execution (prone to drift, repeated failures, poor recovery). The goal is to achieve cost-efficient, goal-directed behavior without reasoning at every step.

Method: SPIKE employs a dual-controller architecture: a Strategic Controller (low-frequency global planning, failure analysis, recovery) and a Reactive Controller (fast local execution under tight token budget). An Event Trigger monitors visual changes, task progress, repeated actions, and failures to decide when to stay reactive or escalate to strategic reasoning. Hierarchical Memory separates short-term experience (State-Action Memory Bank) from structured knowledge (State Action Knowledge Graph), enabling context-appropriate retrieval for each controller.

Result: On StarDojo's Lite-100 split, SPIKE improves success rate by 5.0 percentage points (38.5% relative) over the strongest Lite-100 baseline, and Budgeted SR by 9.3 points (75.6% relative). It reduces token consumption by 54.9% and latency by 40.8% compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Event-triggered reasoning, reactive override capabilities, and heterogeneous memory each contribute to success and recovery. The results support selective reasoning over reasoning at every step as a more efficient and effective strategy for long-horizon multimodal game control.

Abstract: Long-horizon multimodal agents in open-world games must stay goal-directed across many low-level interactions under tight token and latency budgets. Existing approaches often trade off costly per-step reasoning against reactive execution that can drift, repeat failures, and recover poorly. Our key idea is to reuse strategic reasoning across locally stable segments and reinvoke it at event boundaries. We present SPIKE, an adaptive dual controller framework for cost-efficient long-horizon game control. Its Strategic Controller performs low-frequency global planning, failure analysis, and recovery, while its Reactive Controller handles fast local execution under a strict token budget. An Event Trigger monitors visual change, task progress, repeated actions, and failure signals to decide when control should stay reactive or escalate to strategic reasoning. Hierarchical Memory separates short-term experience reuse in the State-Action Memory Bank (SA-MB) from structured evidence in the State Action Knowledge Graph (SA-KG), allowing each controller to retrieve the context it needs. This design reuses strategic proposals over multiple reactive steps, supports local override when plans become stale, and reserves expensive reasoning for moments where extra deliberation is useful. On the Lite-100 split of StarDojo, SPIKE improves Lite-100 success rate (SR) by 5.0 percentage points (38.5% relative) over the strongest Lite-100 baseline and Budgeted SR by 9.3 points (75.6% relative) over the strongest budgeted baseline. It also reduces token consumption by 54.9% and latency by 40.8%. Ablations show that event triggering, reactive override, and heterogeneous memory each contribute to success and recovery, supporting selective reasoning rather than reasoning at every step.

</details>


### [299] [Leveraging Latent Visual Reasoning in Silence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18641)
*Dongyao Zhu,Zhen Wang,Xi Xiao,Han Jiang,Saeed Vahidian,Wei-Lun Chao,Tanya Berger-Wolf,Yu Su,Raju Vatsavai,Jianyang Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Latent visual reasoning tokens don't need to persist at inference to be useful; their value lies in shaping learning via an attention-based reward during RL, improving performance even when latent tokens are rarely generated after training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent visual reasoning tokens are widely used but their necessity at inference is ambiguous—replacing or removing them causes little performance drop. This raises the question of whether latent visual reasoning is still meaningful.

Method: Proposes an attention-based reward during reinforcement learning that encourages latent tokens to interact with later text tokens, promoting latent utilization when activated while preserving the flexibility of pure-text reasoning.

Result: The method improves performance across perception and visual reasoning benchmarks, even when latent tokens are rarely generated after post-training.

Conclusion: Latent visual reasoning is valuable for shaping better visual grounding and textual reasoning during training, not necessarily as an explicit inference-time format.

Abstract: Latent visual reasoning involves visual evidence more directly in multimodal reasoning by inserting continuous latent tokens before textual generation. However, the necessity of these latent tokens at inference remains ambiguous. We show that replacing latent tokens with random noise or removing them completely causes little performance degradation across spatial reasoning benchmarks. Reinforcement learning further diminishes the latent generation behavior after post-training. These observations raise a central question: Is latent visual reasoning still meaningful? We argue that its value should be measured by how effectively latent tokens guide learning, rather than whether they persist as an inference-time format. Our analysis shows that latent reasoning is unevenly favorable across question types, yet hard task-level routing for applying latent generation is brittle. Motivated by these findings, we propose an attention-based reward that encourages generated latent tokens to interact with later text tokens during RL. This reward promotes latent utilization when the latent mode is activated while preserving the flexibility to use pure-text reasoning. Experiments show that our method improves performance across perception and visual reasoning benchmarks, even when latent tokens are rarely generated after post-training. Our results highlight that, without explicit expression at inference, latent visual reasoning can shape better visual grounding and more accurate textual reasoning in silence. Our code and trained models are publicly available at \href{https://github.com/ddydyd32/silent-lvr/tree/master}{GitHub} and \href{https://huggingface.co/collections/cornuHGF/silent-lvr}{Hugging Face}.

</details>


### [300] [Articulation in Prime: Primitive-Based Articulated Object Understanding from a Single Casual Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18645)
*Arslan Artykov,Tom Ravaud,Nicolás Violante-Grezzi,Vincent Lepetit*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a category-agnostic optimization framework that recovers 3D articulated object kinematics from monocular video by fitting geometric primitives organized by joint constraints, outperforming existing methods on new benchmarks with heavy occlusions and camera motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for 3D articulated object kinematics from monocular video are brittle under occlusions, rapid camera ego-motion, or weak features, and learning-based approaches fail to generalize beyond trained categories. The authors aim to create a robust, category-agnostic solution.

Method: A category-agnostic optimization framework that treats articulated object understanding as a primitive-fitting problem using geometric primitives organized into coherent parts constrained by revolute and prismatic joints, with visibility-aware handling of occlusions and joint optimization of part segmentation and joint parameters.

Result: The method outperforms existing approaches on the proposed AiP-synth and AiP-real benchmarks, which feature significant camera motion and heavy occlusions.

Conclusion: The primitive-fitting approach robustly recovers complex 3D kinematics from casually captured monocular video without category-specific training, handling real-world challenges like occlusions and camera motion.

Abstract: Retrieving the 3D kinematics of articulated objects from monocular video is a fundamental challenge in computer vision. Existing methods rely on complex video setups or cues such as long-term point tracking or wide-baseline matching, but are frequently brittle under severe occlusions, rapid camera ego-motion, or weak local features. Learning-based methods, meanwhile, struggle to generalize beyond their training categories. We propose a category-agnostic optimization framework that treats articulated object understanding as a primitive-fitting problem. Geometric primitives serve as a proxy representation that avoids the pitfalls of unstable point tracks; a novel mechanism organizes them into coherent parts constrained by revolute and prismatic joints. Our formulation jointly optimizes part segmentation and joint parameters, recovering complex kinematics from a single casually captured video. A visibility-aware procedure handles partial observations and occlusions inherent to real-world data. We also propose the AiP-synth and AiP-real benchmarks, featuring significant camera motion and heavy occlusions, and outperform existing methods. Project page: https://aartykov.github.io/Articulation-in-Prime/

</details>


### [301] [MementoGUI: Learning Agentic Multimodal Memory Control for Long-Horizon GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18652)
*Ziyun Zeng,Hang Hua,Bocheng Zou,Mu Cai,Rogerio Feris,Jiebo Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MementoGUI is a plug-in memory framework for GUI agents that uses a learned controller (MementoCore) to selectively compress, store, and retrieve task-relevant interaction history, improving long-horizon task performance over raw replay or text-only memory baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GUI agents struggle with long-horizon tasks because they either use raw history replay (overwhelming the model with redundant screenshots) or text-only memory (discarding localized visual evidence). There is a need for intelligent memory management that balances both textual summaries and visual details.

Method: MementoGUI formulates long-horizon GUI control as an online memory-control problem with two memory types: working memory (preserves task-relevant events with text summaries + ROI-level visual evidence) and episodic memory (retrieves reusable past trajectories). MementoCore modularizes control into four operators: step processing, memory compression, episodic writing, and episodic selection. A scalable data curation pipeline converts trajectories into training data, and MementoGUI-Bench is introduced for evaluation.

Result: Experiments on GUI-Odyssey, MM-Mind2Web, and MementoGUI-Bench show MementoGUI consistently outperforms no-history, history-replay, and text-only memory baselines. Larger MementoCore backbones further improve performance.

Conclusion: MementoGUI effectively addresses the long-horizon decision-making bottleneck in GUI agents through structured, selective memory management with a learned controller, without requiring finetuning of the backbone agent.

Abstract: Recent GUI agents have made substantial progress in visual grounding and action prediction, yet they remain brittle in long-horizon tasks that require maintaining task state across many interface transitions. Existing agents typically rely on raw history replay or text-only memory, which either overwhelms the model with redundant screenshots or discards localized visual evidence needed for future decisions. To address these limitations, we introduce \textbf{MementoGUI}, a plug-in agentic memory framework that equips MLLM-based GUI agents with \textbf{MementoCore}, a learned controller for online memory selection, compression, and retrieval. Rather than treating interaction history as a fixed context, MementoGUI formulates long-horizon GUI control as an online memory-control problem: working memory selectively preserves task-relevant interface events with textual summaries and ROI-level visual evidence, while episodic memory retrieves reusable past trajectories through learned relevance selection. MementoCore modularizes memory control into specialized operators for step processing, memory compression, episodic writing, and episodic selection, enabling plug-in memory augmentation without finetuning the GUI agent backbone. We further develop a scalable data curation pipeline that converts computer-use trajectories into memory-controller training data, introduce \textbf{MementoGUI-Bench} for evaluating long-horizon decision-making in GUI agents, and design MLLM-based metrics for semantic action matching, task progress, and memory consistency. Experiments on GUI-Odyssey, MM-Mind2Web, and MementoGUI-Bench show that MementoGUI consistently improves GUI agents over no-history, history-replay, and text-only memory baselines, with larger MementoCore backbones further strengthening memory-augmented GUI control.

</details>


### [302] [Better Together: Evaluating the Complementarity of Earth Embedding Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18667)
*Thijs L van der Plas,Jacob JW Bakermans,Vishal Nedungadi,Gabrielė Tijūnaitytė,Marc Rußwurm,Ioannis N Athanasiadis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes evaluating Earth embeddings not in isolation but by their complementarity—how much fused embeddings outperform single models—finding that fused embeddings beat best single models in 4 of 6 tasks, and that complementarity varies by task and location.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations assess Earth embeddings in isolation, missing the potential benefit of fusing spatially aligned embeddings from different models to provide richer location information.

Method: Proposes an embedding complementarity index to measure performance gain of fused embeddings over the best single-model baseline. Evaluates four models (AlphaEarth, Tessera, GeoCLIP, SatCLIP) in isolation, in all pairs, and jointly across six downstream tasks.

Result: Fused embeddings outperform the best single model in four out of six tasks. Complementarity is task- and location-dependent, and partially determined by spatial scale of land cover classes in a regression task.

Conclusion: Single-embedding evaluations underestimate capabilities; the greatest future gains may come from combining multiple Earth embedding models rather than improving a single one.

Abstract: Earth embedding models transform Earth observation data into embeddings uniquely tied to locations on the Earth's surface. These models are typically evaluated in isolation, comparing the downstream task performance across different Earth embeddings. However, spatially aligned embeddings can naturally be fused, providing richer information per location, a capability that isolated evaluations fail to capture. We therefore propose assessing Earth embeddings by their complementarity: the performance gain of fused embeddings over the best single-model baseline. To operationalise this, we introduce an embedding complementarity index applicable to any embedding and task, and evaluate four Earth embedding models (AlphaEarth, Tessera, GeoCLIP, SatCLIP) in isolation, in all pairs, and jointly across six downstream tasks. Fused embeddings outperform the best single model in four out of six tasks, confirming that single-embedding evaluations often underestimate Earth embedding capabilities. Complementarity proves both task- and location-dependent. Further, for a land cover regression task, we find that complementarity is partially determined by the spatial scale of land cover classes. Complementarity reframes Earth embeddings: the greatest future gains may come not from any single Earth embedding model, but from combinations that are better together.

</details>


### [303] [Lance: Unified Multimodal Modeling by Multi-Task Synergy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18678)
*Fengyi Fu,Mengqi Huang,Shaojin Wu,Yunsheng Jiang,Yufei Huo,Hao Li,Yinghang Song,Fei Ding,Jianzhu Guo,Qian He,Zheren Fu,Zhendong Mao,Yongdong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lance is a lightweight native unified model for multimodal understanding, generation, and editing of images/videos, using a dual-stream MoE architecture with collaborative multi-task training from scratch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve unified multimodal modeling (understanding + generation + editing) without relying on massive model scaling or text-image-dominant designs, instead using a practical collaborative multi-task training paradigm.

Method: Dual-stream mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture on shared interleaved multimodal sequences; modality-aware rotary positional encoding to mitigate visual token interference; staged multi-task training with capability-oriented objectives and adaptive data scheduling.

Result: Lance substantially outperforms existing open-source unified models in image and video generation while retaining strong multimodal understanding capabilities.

Conclusion: A lightweight, unified model for image/video understanding, generation, and editing can be achieved through collaborative multi-task training with decoupled capability pathways, without requiring excessive model scaling.

Abstract: We present Lance, a lightweight native unified model supporting multimodal understanding, generation, and editing for both images and videos. Rather than relying on model capacity scaling or text-image-dominant designs, Lance explores a practical paradigm for unified multimodal modeling via collaborative multi-task training. It is grounded in two core principles: unified context modeling and decoupled capability pathways. Specifically, Lance is trained from scratch and employs a dual-stream mixture-of-experts architecture on shared interleaved multimodal sequences, enabling joint context learning while decoupling the pathways for understanding and generation. We further introduce modality-aware rotary positional encoding to mitigate interference among heterogeneous visual tokens and boost cross-task alignment. During training, Lance adopts a staged multi-task training paradigm with capability-oriented objectives and adaptive data scheduling to strengthen both semantic comprehension and visual generation performance. Experimental results demonstrate that Lance substantially outperforms existing open-source unified models in image and video generation, while retaining strong multimodal understanding capabilities. The homepage is available at https://lance-project.github.io.

</details>


### [304] [CMAG: Concept-Scaffolded Retrieval for Marketplace Avatar Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18680)
*Rajeev Goel,Jason Ding,Phani Harish Wajjala,Pavan Turaga,Tejaswi Gowda,Krishna C. Garikipati*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CMAG is a framework that generates avatars from text prompts by first creating a 3D concept scaffold to disambiguate intent, then retrieving and verifying compatible catalog assets across categories, outperforming baselines in robustness and compositional correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users want free-form text control for avatar assembly, but text-only retrieval is brittle due to ambiguous language, noisy metadata, and stylistic/geometric incompatibility among separately retrieved components.

Method: CMAG synthesizes an intermediate 3D concept scaffold for global context, uses view-aware part discovery via prompt decomposition, enforces taxonomy via a prompt-conditioned router, performs hybrid category-wise retrieval with concept-residual fallback, and applies an agentic VLM for iterative verification and assembly.

Result: CMAG achieves improved retrieval robustness and compositional correctness on diverse compositional prompts compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: 3D concept scaffolding is crucial for handling prompt ambiguity in avatar generation from marketplace assets.

Abstract: Metaverse platforms rely on creator-driven marketplaces where avatars are assembled from discrete, taxonomy-labeled 3D assets (e.g., tops, bottoms, shoes, accessories) under strict category and topology constraints. While users increasingly expect free-form text control, text-only retrieval is brittle: natural language is ambiguous with respect to platform taxonomies, metadata is often noisy or informal, and independently retrieved components can be stylistically inconsistent or geometrically incompatible. We propose \textbf{CMAG}, a concept-scaffolded retrieval and verified composition framework for marketplace avatar generation. Given a prompt, CMAG first synthesizes an intermediate 3D concept scaffold that disambiguates intent beyond text by providing global spatial and stylistic context. In parallel, a view-aware part discovery module extracts localized visual evidence via prompt decomposition and text-grounded segmentation. A prompt-conditioned taxonomy router enforces category coverage and resolves semantic-to-taxonomic mismatch, after which a hybrid category-wise retriever combines part-based fusion with a concept-residual fallback using feature suppression. Finally, an agentic vision--language model filters and re-ranks candidates across categories and drives an iterative verification loop to assemble prompt-faithful, topologically consistent avatars from catalog assets. We evaluate CMAG on diverse compositional prompts and demonstrate improved retrieval robustness and compositional correctness compared to strong baselines, highlighting the importance of 3D concept scaffolding under prompt ambiguity.

</details>


### [305] [A Large-Scale Study on the Accuracy vs Cost Trade-offs of Training and Evaluation Settings in Fine-Grained Image Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18700)
*Edwin Arkel Rios,Augusto Christian Surya,Oswin Gosal,Fernando Mikael,Mary Madeline Nicole,Kisoon Jang,Bo-Cheng Lai,Min-Chun Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A large-scale study of over 2000 experiments on fine-grained image recognition, extending Counterfactual Attention Learning with cross-image region mixing augmentation, and proposing an efficient evaluation-only variant that reduces inference costs by removing the need for discriminative crops during inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on FGIR has focused on backbone selection but neglected accuracy-vs-cost trade-offs under different training and evaluation settings.

Method: Extended Counterfactual Attention Learning (CAL) with cross-image discriminative region mixing augmentation; proposed an efficient evaluation-only variant that skips discriminative crop forward passes during inference.

Result: Data-aware augmentations during training alone enable excellent accuracy even without crops, significantly reducing inference costs.

Conclusion: Training with data-aware augmentations can eliminate the need for costly crop-based inference in FGIR, offering better accuracy-vs-cost trade-offs.

Abstract: Prior work on fine-grained image recognition (FGIR) has established the importance of the backbone selection, but has neglected the accuracy-vs-cost trade-offs under different training and evaluation settings. In this work we conduct a large-scale study with over 2000 experiments across 6 training and evaluation settings, 9 pretrained backbones, and 17 datasets. Preliminary observations on the effectiveness of data augmentation for fine-grained training motivate us to extend Counterfactual Attention Learning (CAL), a state-of-the-art method based on data-aware cropping and masking augmentations, with cross-image discriminative region mixing augmentation. We also propose an efficient evaluation-only variant that maintains competitive accuracy while reducing inference costs by forfeiting the forward pass on discriminative crops that is normally used by CAL and similar FGIR methods. Our results show that data-aware augmentations during training only can enable a model to achieve excellent accuracy even without crops, significantly reducing inference costs. To support future research we share our code and checkpoints at: \url{https://github.com/arkel23/FGIR-Backbones}

</details>


### [306] [Semantic Generative Tuning for Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18714)
*Songsong Yu,Yuxin Chen,Ying Shan,Yanwei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes Semantic Generative Tuning (SGT), a novel paradigm that uses image segmentation as a generative proxy to align visual understanding and generation in unified multimodal models, improving both comprehension and generative fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current unified multimodal models suffer from misaligned representation spaces because they independently optimize understanding (via sparse text signals) and generation (via dense pixel objectives), which prevents mutual reinforcement between the two capabilities.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic investigation into generative post-training by formulating hierarchical visual tasks as generative proxies. They find image segmentation (a high-level semantic task) is optimal. Based on this, they introduce Semantic Generative Tuning (SGT), leveraging segmentation as a proxy to align multimodal capabilities.

Result: SGT improves feature linear separability and optimizes visual-textual attention allocation patterns. Extensive evaluations show consistent improvements in both multimodal comprehension and generative fidelity across mainstream benchmarks.

Conclusion: High-level semantic tasks like segmentation are effective bridges for unifying visual understanding and generation in UMMs, and SGT successfully aligns these two capabilities for mutual reinforcement.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) strive to consolidate visual understanding and visual generation within a single architecture. However, prevailing training paradigms independently optimize understanding via sparse text signals and generation through dense pixel objectives. Such a decoupled strategy yields misaligned representation spaces, isolating visual understanding from generation and hindering their mutual reinforcement. This work presents the first systematic investigation into generative post-training, where we formulate hierarchical visual tasks as generative proxies to bridge the isolation in UMMs. Our empirical investigation reveals that high-level semantic tasks, particularly image segmentation, serve as optimal proxies. Unlike low-level tasks that distract models with texture details, segmentation provides structural semantics that significantly enhance both vision-centric perception and generative layout fidelity. Building upon these insights, we introduce Semantic Generative Tuning (SGT), a novel paradigm that leverages segmentation as a generative proxy to align and synergize multimodal capabilities. Mechanistic analyses further demonstrate that SGT fundamentally improves feature linear separability and optimizes visual-textual attention allocation pattern. Extensive evaluations show that SGT consistently improves both multimodal comprehension and generative fidelity across mainstream benchmarks. Our code is available on the https://song2yu.github.io/SGT/.

</details>


### [307] [SafeDiffusion-R1: Online Reward Steering for Safe Diffusion Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18719)
*Komal Kumar,Ankan Deria,Abhishek Basu,Fahad Shamshad,Hisham Cholakkal,Karthik Nandakumar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SafeDiffusion-R1 uses online GRPO-based RL with a steering reward mechanism (leveraging CLIP embedding properties) to make diffusion models safer without expensive supervised data or reward model fine-tuning, reducing inappropriate content to 18.07% and nudity to 15 detections while improving generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for removing unsafe content from diffusion models require expensive supervised data (unsafe-text/safe-image pairs or negative/positive image pairs) and suffer from catastrophic forgetting due to offline data generation, making them impractical to scale and degrading generation quality.

Method: The paper proposes an online reinforcement learning framework using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on both negative and positive text prompts. It introduces a steering reward mechanism that exploits CLIP embeddings' inherent property: steering text representations toward positive safety directions and away from negative ones, eliminating the need for specialized reward models.

Result: Reduced inappropriate content to 18.07% (vs. 48.9% for SD v1.4), nudity detections to 15 (vs. 646 baseline), improved compositional generation quality from 42.08% to 47.83% on GenEval, and achieved state-of-the-art safety gains across seven harm categories without supervised paired data or reward tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed online RL approach effectively addresses both data scarcity and catastrophic forgetting for safe diffusion models, achieving superior safety performance while improving generation quality, with generalization to out-of-domain unsafe prompts across multiple harm categories.

Abstract: Diffusion models have been widely studied for removing unsafe content learned during pre-training. Existing methods require expensive supervised data, either unsafe-text paired with safe-image groundtruth or negative/positive image pairs, making them impractical to scale. Furthermore, offline reinforcement learning and supervised fine-tuning approaches that generate synthetic data offline suffer from catastrophic forgetting, degrading generation quality. We propose a novel online reinforcement learning framework that addresses both data scarcity and model degradation through post-training with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on both negative and positive text prompts. To eliminate the need for fine-tuning specialized safe/unsafe reward models, we introduce a \textit{steering reward mechanism} that exploits an inherent property of CLIP embeddings: steering text representations toward positive safety directions and away from negative ones in the embedding space. Our online-policy approach enables the model to learn from diverse prompts, including explicit unsafe content, without catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method reduces inappropriate content to 18.07\% (vs. 48.9\% for SD v1.4) and nudity detections to 15 (vs. 646 baseline) while improving compositional generation quality from 42.08\% to 47.83\% on GenEval. Remarkably, these safety gains generalize to out-of-domain unsafe prompts across seven harm categories, achieving state-of-the-art performance without supervised paired data or reward tuning. Github: https://github.com/MAXNORM8650/SafeDiffusion-R1.

</details>


### [308] [Advancing Narrative Long Video Generation via Training-Free Identity-Aware Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18733)
*Jinzhuo Liu,Jiangning Zhang,Wencan Jiang,Yabiao Wang,Dingkang Liang,Zhucun Xue,Ran Yi,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IAMFlow is a training-free identity-aware memory framework for autoregressive video generation that uses LLM-extracted entity IDs and VLM-based visual verification to maintain long-term consistency across evolving prompts, achieving superior performance and 1.39× speedup on the new NarraStream-Bench benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive video generation suffers from long-term inconsistency, identity drift, character duplication, and attribute loss when handling evolving prompts with shifting entity references, as existing solutions rely on predefined compression or implicit similarity-based matching rather than explicit entity tracking.

Method: IAMFlow uses an LLM to extract entities with visual attributes from prompts and assigns unique global IDs for explicit identity-aware memory, while a VLM asynchronously verifies and refines attributes from rendered frames. It also includes inference acceleration via asynchronous visual verification, adaptive prompt transition, and model quantization.

Result: IAMFlow achieves the best overall performance on NarraStream-Bench, outperforming the strongest baseline by 2.56 points, and achieves a 1.39× speedup over the most efficient baseline in the 60-second multi-prompt setting.

Conclusion: Training-free identity-aware memory with explicit entity tracking effectively resolves long-term inconsistency in narrative video generation, and the proposed benchmark enables standardized evaluation of this task.

Abstract: Autoregressive video generation has improved rapidly in visual fidelity and interactivity, but it still suffers from long-term inconsistency and memory degradation. Most existing solutions either compress historical frames using predefined strategies or retrieve keyframes based on coarse implicit attention signals, both of which fail to handle evolving prompts with shifting entity references, leading to identity drift, character duplication, and attribute loss. To address this, we propose IAMFlow, a training-free identity-aware memory framework that explicitly models and tracks persistent entity identities, enabling consistent generation across prompt transitions. Specifically, an LLM extracts entities with visual attributes from each prompt and assigns unique global IDs for identity-aware memory, while a VLM asynchronously verifies and refines attributes from rendered frames, enabling explicit entity tracking in place of implicit similarity-based matching. To keep the proposed framework computationally practical, we design a systematic inference acceleration pipeline, including asynchronous visual verification, adaptive prompt transition, and model quantization, which achieves faster generation than existing baselines. Furthermore, we introduce NarraStream-Bench, a benchmark for narrative streaming video generation that features 324 multi-prompt scripts spanning six dimensions and a three-dimensional evaluation protocol that integrates both traditional metrics and multimodal large language model-based assessments. Extensive experiments show that IAMFlow, despite being training-free, achieves the best overall performance on NarraStream-Bench, outperforming the strongest baseline by 2.56 points, while achieving a 1.39$\times$ speedup over the most efficient baseline in the 60-second multi-prompt setting.

</details>


### [309] [EgoExoMem: Cross-View Memory Reasoning over Synchronized Egocentric and Exocentric Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18734)
*Ruiping Liu,Junwei Zheng,Yufan Chen,Di Wen,Shaofang Quan,Chengzhi Wu,Jiaming Zhang,Kailun Yang,Kunyu Peng,Rainer Stiefelhagen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces EgoExoMem, a benchmark for cross-view memory reasoning over synchronized egocentric and exocentric videos, and proposes E²-Select, a training-free frame selection method that achieves 58.2% accuracy, outperforming existing MLLMs which only reach 55.3%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Egocentric memory alone is insufficient for comprehensive spatial-temporal reasoning in embodied intelligence; humans benefit from recalling from both field (ego) and observer (exo) perspectives, motivating the need for cross-view memory reasoning.

Method: E²-Select: a training-free frame selection method for synchronized ego-exo videos that combines relevance-based budget allocation with per-view k-DPP sampling to handle view asymmetry and cross-view temporal consistency.

Result: Best existing MLLM reaches only 55.3% on the benchmark; E²-Select achieves state-of-the-art 58.2% over frame-selection and RAG-based memory baselines.

Conclusion: Ego and exo views provide complementary memory cues; systematic view-preference conflicts between question framing and answer grounding reveal cross-view memory reasoning remains a challenging, unsolved problem.

Abstract: Egocentric memory is widely used in embodied intelligence, but it may be insufficient for comprehensive spatial-temporal reasoning. Inspired by human recall from both field and observer perspectives, we introduce EgoExoMem, the first benchmark for cross-view memory reasoning over synchronized egocentric and exocentric videos. EgoExoMem contains $2.6K$ high-quality MCQs across eight temporal, spatial, and cross-view QA types. To support dual-view retrieval, we propose E$^2$-Select, a training-free frame selection method for synchronized ego-exo videos. It combines relevance-based budget allocation with per-view k-DPP sampling to handle view asymmetry and cross-view temporal consistency. Experiments show that ego and exo views provide complementary memory cues, while existing MLLMs remain far from solving the benchmark: the best model reaches only $55.3\%$. E$^2$-Select achieves state-of-the-art performance of $58.2\%$ over frame-selection and RAG-based memory baselines. Further analysis reveals systematic view-preference conflicts between question framing and answer grounding, underscoring the novelty and challenge of cross-view memory reasoning.

</details>


### [310] [PIXLRelight: Controllable Relighting via Intrinsic Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18735)
*Miguel Farinha,Ronald Clark*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PIXLRelight is a feed-forward neural method for single-image relighting that bridges physically based rendering (PBR) with learned image synthesis via shared intrinsic conditioning, enabling arbitrary PBR-style lighting control in under 0.1 second per image.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing relighting methods suffer from limited lighting control (text/environment maps only), error accumulation when chaining inverse and forward rendering, or costly per-image optimization. The authors aim to achieve fast, physically controllable, high-quality relighting from a single image.

Method: At training time, paired multi-illumination photographs are decomposed into albedo, diffuse shading, and non-diffuse residuals. At inference, the same conditioning is obtained from path-traced renders of a coarse 3D reconstruction under user-specified PBR lights. A transformer-based neural renderer applies target illumination via per-pixel affine modulation.

Result: State-of-the-art relighting quality with arbitrary PBR-style lighting control, operating in under 0.1 second per image.

Conclusion: PIXLRelight effectively bridges PBR and learned synthesis for fast, controllable, high-quality single-image relighting.

Abstract: We present PIXLRelight, a feed-forward approach for physically controllable single-image relighting. Existing methods either provide limited lighting control (e.g. through text or environment maps), accumulate errors when chaining inverse and forward rendering, or require costly per-image optimization. Our key idea is to bridge physically based rendering (PBR) and learned image synthesis through a shared intrinsic conditioning that can be obtained from either real photographs or PBR renders. At training time, paired multi-illumination photographs are decomposed into albedo, diffuse shading, and non-diffuse residuals, which condition the model. At inference time, the same conditioning is computed from a path-traced render of a coarse 3D reconstruction of the input under user-specified PBR lights. A transformer-based neural renderer then applies the target illumination to the source photograph, preserving fine image detail through a per-pixel affine modulation. PIXLRelight enables arbitrary PBR-style lighting control, achieves state-of-the-art relighting quality, and runs in under a tenth of a second per image. Code and models are available at https://mlfarinha.github.io/pixl-relight/.

</details>


### [311] [Spectral Progressive Diffusion for Efficient Image and Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18736)
*Howard Xiao,Brian Chao,Lior Yariv,Gordon Wetzstein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Spectral Progressive Diffusion accelerates pretrained diffusion models by progressively growing resolution along the denoising trajectory, leveraging the frequency-domain structure where low frequencies are generated first and high frequencies later, using spectral noise expansion and an optimal resolution schedule derived from the model's power spectrum.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models generate low-frequency components early and high-frequency details late in the denoising process, making high-resolution computation on noise-dominated frequencies redundant and inefficient.

Method: Proposes Spectral Progressive Diffusion with (1) a spectral noise expansion mechanism, (2) an optimal resolution schedule derived from the model's power spectrum, supporting both training-free acceleration and a fine-tuning recipe.

Result: Significant speedups on state-of-the-art pretrained image and video generation models while preserving visual quality.

Conclusion: Exploiting the spectral structure of diffusion models enables efficient, training-free acceleration by progressively growing resolution during denoising, with additional fine-tuning further improving efficiency and quality.

Abstract: Diffusion models have been shown to implicitly generate visual content autoregressively in the frequency domain, where low-frequency components are generated earlier in the denoising process while high-frequency details emerge only in later timesteps. This structure offers a natural opportunity for efficient generation, as high-resolution computation on noise-dominated frequencies is largely redundant. We propose Spectral Progressive Diffusion, a general framework that progressively grows resolution along the denoising trajectory of pretrained diffusion models. To this end, we develop a spectral noise expansion mechanism and derive an optimal resolution schedule from the model's power spectrum. Our framework supports training-free acceleration and a novel fine-tuning recipe that further improves efficiency and quality. We demonstrate significant speedups on state-of-the-art pretrained image and video generation models while preserving visual quality.

</details>


### [312] [LongLive-2.0: An NVFP4 Parallel Infrastructure for Long Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18739)
*Yukang Chen,Luozhou Wang,Wei Huang,Shuai Yang,Bohan Zhang,Yicheng Xiao,Ruihang Chu,Weian Mao,Qixin Hu,Shaoteng Liu,Yuyang Zhao,Huizi Mao,Ying-Cong Chen,Enze Xie,Xiaojuan Qi,Song Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LongLive-2.0 is an NVFP4-based parallel system for training and inference of long video generation, introducing Balanced SP training with teacher-forcing, NVFP4 precision for speed/memory efficiency, and achieving up to 2.15x training speedup and 1.84x inference speedup, with 45.7 FPS on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address speed and memory bottlenecks in long video generation workflows, both for training and inference.

Method: Uses NVFP4 precision throughout; introduces Balanced SP (sequence-parallel autoregressive training with teacher-forcing layout); employs W4A4 NVFP4 inference, quantized KV cache, and asynchronous streaming VAE decoding on Blackwell GPUs; deploys SP inference on non-Blackwell GPUs.

Result: Up to 2.15x speedup in training and 1.84x in inference; LongLive-2.0-5B achieves 45.7 FPS with strong benchmark performance.

Conclusion: This is the first NVFP4 training and inference system for long video generation, demonstrating that a high-quality infrastructure and dataset enable a clean training pipeline without needing ODE initialization or distribution matching distillation.

Abstract: We present LongLive-2.0, an NVFP4-based parallel infrastructure throughout the full training and inference workflow of long video generation, addressing speed and memory bottlenecks. For training, we introduce sequence-parallel autoregressive (AR) training, instantiated as Balanced SP, which co-designs the efficient teacher-forcing layout with SP execution by pairing clean-history and noisy-target temporal chunks on each rank, enabling a natural teacher-forcing mask with SP-aware chunked VAE encoding. Combined with NVFP4 precision, it reduces GPU memory cost and accelerates GEMM computation during training, the proportion of which increases as video length grows. Moreover, we show that a high-quality infrastructure and dataset enable a remarkably clean training pipeline. Unlike existing Self-Forcing series methods that rely on ODE initialization and subsequent distribution matching distillation (DMD), LongLive-2.0 directly tunes a diffusion model into a long, multi-shot, interactive auto-regressive (AR) diffusion model. It can be further converted to real-time generation (4 to 2 denoising steps) with standalone LoRA weights. For inference on Blackwell GPUs, we enable W4A4 NVFP4 inference, quantize KV cache into NVFP4 for memory savings, and boost end-to-end throughput with asynchronous streaming VAE decoding. On non-Blackwell GPU architectures, we deploy SP inference to match the speed on Blackwell GPUs, while the quantized KV cache can lower inter-GPU communication of SP. Experiments show up to 2.15x speedup in training, and 1.84x in inference. LongLive-2.0-5B achieves 45.7 FPS inference while attaining strong performance on benchmarks. To our knowledge, LongLive-2.0 is the first NVFP4 training and inference system for long video generation.

</details>


### [313] [Vision-OPD: Learning to See Fine Details for Multimodal LLMs via On-Policy Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18740)
*Qianhao Yuan,Jie Lou,Xing Yu,Hongyu Lin,Le Sun,Xianpei Han,Yaojie Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision-OPD is a self-distillation framework that transfers a multimodal LLM's own regional perception ability (from image crops) to its full-image policy, improving fine-grained visual understanding without external models or labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs struggle with fine-grained visual understanding because they fail to focus on small but critical evidence in full images, despite having sufficient local recognition ability (as shown by better performance on cropped images).

Method: Vision-OPD uses a regional-to-global self-distillation framework: a crop-conditioned teacher and a full-image-conditioned student from the same MLLM. The student generates on-policy rollouts, and token-level KL divergence between teacher and student next-token distributions is minimized along these rollouts.

Result: Vision-OPD achieves competitive or superior performance on multiple fine-grained visual understanding benchmarks compared to much larger open-source, closed-source, and agentic "Thinking-with-Images" models.

Conclusion: The model can internalize the benefit of visual zooming without needing external teachers, ground-truth labels, reward verifiers, or inference-time tool use.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) still struggle with fine-grained visual understanding, where answers often depend on small but decisive evidence in the full image. We observe a regional-to-global perception gap: the same MLLM answers fine-grained questions more accurately when conditioned on evidence-centered crops than on the corresponding full images, suggesting that many failures stem from difficulty to focus on relevant evidence rather than insufficient local recognition ability. Motivated by this observation, we propose Vision-OPD (Vision On-Policy Distillation), a regional-to-global self-distillation framework that transfers the model's own privileged regional perception to its full-image policy. Vision-OPD instantiates two conditional policies from the same MLLM: a crop-conditioned teacher and a full-image-conditioned student. The student generates on-policy rollouts, and Vision-OPD minimizes token-level divergence between the teacher and student next-token distributions along these rollouts. This enables the model to internalize the benefit of visual zooming without external teacher models, ground-truth labels, reward verifiers, or inference-time tool use. Experiments on multiple fine-grained visual understanding benchmarks show that Vision-OPD models achieve competitive or superior performance against much larger open-source, closed-source, and "Thinking-with-Images" agentic models.

</details>


### [314] [Aurora: Unified Video Editing with a Tool-Using Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18748)
*Yongsheng Yu,Ziyun Zeng,Zhiyuan Xiao,Zhenghong Zhou,Hang Hua,Wei Xiong,Jiebo Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Aurora is an agentic video editing framework that uses a VLM agent to resolve underspecified user requests (missing text, images, spatial grounding) before passing them to a unified video diffusion transformer, improving editing performance on underspecified inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video editing models assume users provide complete, model-ready inputs (text, reference images, spatial grounding), but real-world requests often omit these. The paper aims to bridge the gap between raw user requests and model requirements.

Method: Pairs a tool-augmented VLM agent (trained with supervised data and preference pairs) with a unified video diffusion transformer. The agent maps raw requests to structured edit plans, resolving textual and visual underspecification before generation.

Result: Outperforms instruction-only baselines on AgentEdit-Bench and two existing video editing benchmarks. The VLM agent transfers to compatible frozen video editing models.

Conclusion: Agent-enhanced video editing effectively handles real-world underspecified requests, and the learned VLM agent generalizes across different video editing models.

Abstract: Recent video editing models have converged on a unified conditioning design: a single diffusion transformer jointly consumes text, source video, and reference images, and one set of weights covers replacement, removal, style transfer, and reference-driven insertion. The design is flexible, but it assumes that the user already provides model-ready text, reference images, and spatial grounding for local edits, which real requests often omit. We present Aurora, an agentic video editing framework that pairs a tool-augmented vision-language model (VLM) agent with a unified video diffusion transformer. The VLM agent maps a raw user request to a structured edit plan aligned with the transformer's conditioning channels, thereby resolving textual and visual underspecification before generation. We train the VLM agent with supervised data for complete edit planning and reference-image selection, together with preference pairs for robust tool use and instruction refinement. We introduce AgentEdit-Bench to evaluate agent-enhanced video editing under textual and visual underspecification. Experiments on AgentEdit-Bench and two existing video editing benchmarks show that Aurora improves over instruction-only baselines and that the VLM agent transfers to compatible frozen video editing models. Project page: https://yeates.github.io/Aurora-Page

</details>


### [315] [Can These Views Be One Scene? Evaluating Multiview 3D Consistency when 3D Foundation Models Hallucinate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18754)
*Soumava Paul,Prakhar Kaushik,Alan Yuille*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper studies reliability issues in multiview 3D consistency metrics, showing that neural reconstruction backbones can hallucinate geometry, and proposes COLMAP-based failure-aware metrics that achieve up to 4x better correlation with human judgments than existing methods like MEt3R.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multiview 3D consistency metrics fail when NVS/sparse-view reconstructions contain artifacts, outliers, or noise. Neural metrics like MEt3R rely on learned backbones with poorly characterized failure modes, while reference-based metrics require ground truth. The authors aim to systematically study and improve the reliability of 3D consistency evaluation.

Method: The authors (1) construct a controlled robustness benchmark for multiview 3D consistency, (2) develop a parametric family decomposing neural metrics into backbone, residual, and aggregation components (recovering MEt3R and producing variants up to 3x more robust), (3) systematically analyze failure modes of VGGT, MASt3R, DUSt3R, and Fast3R (hallucination of geometry), and (4) introduce COLMAP-based metrics using matches, registration, dense support, and reconstruction failure as failure-aware signals.

Result: Neural backbones can hallucinate dense geometry and cross-view support for unrelated scenes, repeated images, and random noise. The proposed COLMAP-based metrics achieve up to 4x higher correlation with human judgments than MEt3R on real NVS outputs.

Conclusion: Classical geometric verification (COLMAP-based) is more reliable than learned neural backbones for multiview 3D consistency evaluation, especially in the presence of artifacts and outliers. The parametric decomposition framework helps build more robust neural variants.

Abstract: Multiview 3D evaluation assumes that the images being scored are observations of one static 3D scene. This assumption can fail in NVS and sparse-view reconstruction: inputs or generated outputs may contain artifacts, outlier frames, repeated views, or noise, yet still receive high 3D consistency scores. Existing reference-based metrics require ground truth, while ground-truth-free metrics such as MEt3R depend on learned reconstruction backbones whose failure modes are poorly characterized. We study this reliability problem by comparing neural reconstruction priors with classical geometric verification. We introduce \benchmark, a controlled robustness benchmark for multiview 3D consistency, and a parametric family that decomposes neural metrics into backbone, residual, and aggregation components. This family recovers MEt3R and yields variants up to $3\times$ more robust. Our analysis shows that VGGT, MASt3R, DUSt3R, and Fast3R can hallucinate dense geometry and cross-view support for unrelated scenes, repeated images, and random noise. We introduce COLMAP-based metrics that use matches, registration, dense support, and reconstruction failure as failure-aware consistency signals. On real NVS outputs and a structured human study, these metrics achieve up to $4\times$ higher correlation with human judgments than MEt3R.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [316] [Covert Multi-bit LLM Watermarking: An Information Theory and Coding Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16709)
*Sidong Guo,Tyler Kann,Teodora Baluta,Matthieu R. Bloch*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a block-autoregressive model for multi-bit watermarking in LLMs, derives information-theoretic capacity bounds, and develops a practical polar-code-based algorithm achieving 0.375 bits/token with <10% BER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable multi-bit covert embedding in LLM outputs by leveraging the statistical knowledge of LLM cover text, moving beyond simple single-bit watermarking schemes.

Method: A block-autoregressive model with limited non-causal access to token distributions per block; information-theoretic analysis using Gelfand-Pinsker and channel synthesis coding; CMDP-based optimization across blocks; polar code construction for practical implementation.

Result: Achieves bit-error rate below 10% at 0.375 bits/token over short token lengths with negligible perplexity and distortion degradation.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides exact capacity characterization for multi-bit LLM watermarking, and the polar-code-based algorithm demonstrates practical feasibility with high reliability and low distortion.

Abstract: We study the problem of multi-bit watermarking for large language models (LLMs). We introduce a block-autoregressive model inspired by multi-token prediction, in which the encoder has limited non-causal access to token distributions within each block. This formulation enables an information-theoretic characterization of multi-bit watermarking capacity, by which the knowledge of LLM cover statistics is leveraged to enable a multi-bit covert embedding. We study the information-theoretic limits of the model by combining Gelfand-Pinsker and channel synthesis coding techniques and obtain an exact characterization of the capacity. The embedding strategy is further optimized across blocks using a constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) and we develop an explicit algorithm based on polar codes following the information-theoretic principles. Our algorithm achieves a bit-error rate below 10 percent with a rate of 0.375 bits/token over short token lengths with negligible perplexity and distortion degradation.

</details>


### [317] [Achieving $α$-Fairness in Clustered Cell-Free Networking: A Tight Relaxation Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16960)
*Chaowen Deng,Jie Fan,Boxiang Ren,Ziyuan Lyu,Jingchen Peng,Hao Wu,Junyuan Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified alpha-fairness scheme for clustered cell-free networking that balances spectral efficiency and inter-subnetwork fairness, achieving up to 11% improvement in fairness and 45% gain in minimum capacity with only 5% throughput loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fairness across subnetworks in clustered cell-free networking remains a critical yet unsolved challenge, motivating the need for a scheme that balances overall performance and fairness.

Method: The authors reformulate the combinatorial clustering problem into a continuous optimization using closed-form deterministic equivalents of ergodic sum capacity, classify the problem into four cases based on alpha values, establish equivalence between integer and continuous relaxation, and develop guaranteed-convergence algorithms for each case.

Result: The proposed scheme achieves 11% improvement in Jain's fairness index, 45% gain in minimum subnetwork capacity, with only 5% reduction in aggregate throughput.

Conclusion: The alpha-fairness scheme effectively balances overall spectral efficiency and inter-subnetwork fairness in clustered cell-free networking, offering a viable solution for practical deployment.

Abstract: Clustered cell-free networking has emerged as a promising architecture to balance the high performance of cell-free massive MIMO and the scalability of traditional cellular systems. However, achieving fairness across subnetworks remains a critical yet largely unsolved challenge. This paper investigates the fairness problem in clustered cell-free networking and proposes a unified and tunable alpha-fairness scheme that effectively balances overall spectral efficiency and inter-subnetwork fairness. Using the closed-form deterministic equivalent of the ergodic sum capacity, we reformulate the combinatorial clustering problem as a continuous optimization problem. Leveraging the concavity/convexity properties of the alpha-fair objective, we classify the problem into four distinct cases according to the value of alpha. For each case, we establish the exact equivalence between the original integer program and its continuous relaxation, and develop efficient algorithms with guaranteed convergence. Extensive simulations show that the proposed scheme achieves up to 11% improvement in Jain's fairness index and 45% gain in minimum subnetwork capacity, with only a negligible 5% reduction in aggregate throughput.

</details>


### [318] [Intermediate Constacyclic Codes and Scalar-Residue Reed--Muller Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17022)
*Yaoran Yang,Yutong Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper resolves an open problem by proving that a sharp upper bound on the minimum distance of a class of constacyclic codes (C(q,m,r,ℓ)) is actually exact for all admissible intermediate parameters, and also determines the minimum affine support for non-terminal scalar-residue layers of generalized Reed-Muller codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve an open problem posed by Sun, Ding and Wang (2024) regarding the exact minimum distance of a new class of constacyclic codes in the intermediate range 2<r<q-1, where only upper and lower bounds were previously known.

Method: The proof uses three key components: hidden scalar homogeneity of the evaluation model, an orbit-counting obstruction for minimum Reed-Muller supports, and a homogeneous pencil construction that attains the second weight.

Result: The exact minimum distance formula is established for all parameters, showing that the previous upper bound is tight. Additionally, the paper determines the minimum affine support of every non-terminal scalar-residue layer of generalized Reed-Muller codes, revealing a dichotomy: the first Reed-Muller weight survives only for residue classes 0 and 1.

Conclusion: The open problem is fully settled, and the findings extend the understanding of weight distributions in constacyclic and Reed-Muller codes.

Abstract: A 2024 paper of Sun, Ding and Wang introduced a second class of constacyclic codes over finite fields, denoted $C(q,m,r,\ell)$, with length $(q^m-1)/r$, where $r\mid(q-1)$ and the defining monomials have total $q$-ary degree congruent to $r-1$ modulo $r$. In the non-projective intermediate range $2<r<q-1$ the paper gave a sharp-looking upper bound and a BCH-type lower bound, and left the minimum distance open. We prove that the upper bound is the exact minimum distance for every admissible intermediate parameter. More precisely, if $\ell=(q-1)a+b<(q-1)m-1$, $0\le b\le q-2$, and $b\equiv r-1\pmod r$, then, for every prime power $q$, every divisor $r$ of $q-1$ with $2<r<q-1$, and every $m\ge2$, \[
  d(C(q,m,r,\ell))=
  \begin{cases}
  \displaystyle \frac{q-1}{r}(q-b+1)q^{m-a-2},&0\le a\le m-2,\\[1mm]
  \displaystyle \frac{q-b+r-2}{r},&a=m-1.
  \end{cases} \] The first line settles the open problem of Sun, Ding and Wang; the second line is the terminal case already forced by their BCH bound. We also determine the minimum affine support of every non-terminal scalar-residue layer of a generalized Reed--Muller code. The resulting dichotomy says that the first Reed--Muller weight survives exactly for residue classes $0$ and $1$, while every other residue-matched layer starts at the second Reed--Muller weight. The proof uses the hidden scalar homogeneity of the evaluation model, an orbit-counting obstruction for minimum Reed--Muller supports, and a homogeneous pencil construction that attains the second weight.

</details>


### [319] [On Trajectory-Based Stability Analysis for $1$-bit Sigma-Delta Quantization and its Application to the Second-Order Case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17127)
*Rohan Joy,Felix Krahmer,Alessandro Lupoli*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper presents the first stability guarantees for ΣΔ quantization that outperform the classical ℓ¹-based bound, using trajectory analysis instead of invariant-set methods to achieve significantly improved filter length requirements for second-order schemes with sparse feedback filters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between the conservative ℓ¹-based sufficient stability condition (‖h‖_ℓ¹ + ‖f‖_∞ ≤ 2) and empirically observed stability beyond this bound, by establishing improved theoretical stability guarantees.

Method: Trajectory analysis of state variables (rather than invariant-set characterization), applied to second-order ΣΔ schemes with sparse feedback filters as proposed by Günturk.

Result: Improved filter length from O(1/(1-‖f‖_∞)) under ℓ¹ criterion to O(1/√(1-‖f‖_∞)) for guaranteeing stability.

Conclusion: The trajectory-based analysis approach enables treatment of longer filters beyond what invariant-set methods can handle, yielding the first stability guarantees that outperform the traditional ℓ¹ condition.

Abstract: A state-of-the-art strategy for digitally representing a bandlimited signal $f$ is $ΣΔ$ quantization. $ΣΔ$ quantization schemes choose a bit sequence $(q_n)$ representing the samples $(y_n)$ of $f$ sequentially based on a state sequence $(u_n)$ defined via a recurrence relation of the form \begin{equation*}
  u_n = (h*u)_n + y_n - q_n, \end{equation*} where $h_j = 0$ for $j\le 0.$ The effectiveness of a quantization scheme crucially depends on the fact that it is stable, i.e. , the state variable remains uniformly bounded in a given class of signals. Thus, a common strategy is to choose $$q_n = \operatorname{sign}((h*u)_n + y_n).$$ It is well known that a sufficient condition for this quantization rule to induce stability is that $$ \|h\|_{\ell^1}+\|f\|_{\infty}\le 2.$$ At the same time, one empirically observes that this condition is conservative and stability holds significantly beyond this bound. In this paper, we address this gap by establishing the first stability guarantees beyond first order that outperform the $\ell^1$ based stability condition. In contrast to many previous approaches, our analysis describes the trajectories of the state variables rather than characterizing the invariant set, an approach that had previously been performed only in some specific example cases. This viewpoint has the main advantage that it makes it possible to treat longer filters, which are difficult to handle through invariant-set analysis because of the resulting high dimensionality. We apply our technique to second-order $ΣΔ$ schemes with sparse feedback filters as proposed by Günturk \cite{gunturk2003one}, showing that the filter length required to guarantee stability significantly improves from the length $O\left(\frac{1}{1-\|f\|_{\infty}}\right)$ needed to apply the $\ell^1$ based criterion to $O\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\|f\|_{\infty}}}\right)$.

</details>


### [320] [ISI Modeling and BER Performance for Rotating Light-Trail Image Sensor Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17272)
*Shin Asaoka,Shan Lu,Zhengqiang Tang,and Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper presents an analytical framework for image sensor communication (ISC) using propeller-LED transmitters, including a light trail model, noise modeling, closed-form BER derivation via Q-function, adjacent-only ISI model, and a method for selecting control angles to maximize throughput under BER constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rigorous analytical framework for ISC with propeller-LED transmitters that enables closed-form BER derivation and throughput optimization, moving beyond empirical or simulation-based approaches.

Method: Constructs a single-LED single-blink light trail model mapping optical power to pixels; integrates probabilistic noise for closed-form BER via Q-function; proposes adjacent-only ISI model based on trimodal histograms; uses hardest-pair midpoint threshold for per-segment/marginalized BER; derives sufficiency conditions for adjacent-only ISI validity.

Result: Validates the analytical BER tightness against Monte Carlo simulations and experimental results; uses analytical BER to select control angles maximizing throughput under target BER reliability constraints.

Conclusion: The proposed analytical framework accurately models ISC with propeller-LED transmitters and enables principled throughput optimization under BER constraints.

Abstract: Image sensor communication (ISC) employing a propeller-LED transmitter encodes data along rotating light trails. We present an analytical framework that (i) constructs a single-LED, single-blink light trail model that maps optical power to pixel values, and (ii) integrates a probabilistic noise model to derive a closed-form bit-error rate (BER) using the $Q$-function. Trimodal pixel-value histograms motivate an adjacent-only inter-symbol interference (ISI) model in which the decision at segment $j$ depends on adjacent segments. Applying a hardest-pair midpoint threshold yields per-segment BER and a general BER after marginalization. We further provide practical sufficiency conditions under which adjacent-only ISI is adequate, and validate its tightness against Monte Carlo simulations and experimental results. Using the analytical BER, we select the control angle that maximizes throughput while satisfying a target BER reliability constraint.

</details>


### [321] [SERE: A Stabilized Element-Wise Method for Downlink Rate Estimation in Clustered Cell-Free Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17297)
*Panpan Niu,Han Hao,Hao Wu,Junyuan Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a stabilized element-wise rate estimation method for downlink rate estimation in clustered cell-free networks, achieving <6% relative error with much lower complexity than Monte Carlo simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rate estimation methods (Monte Carlo simulations or RMT-based) are either too computationally expensive or fail to address the unique inter-subnetwork interference in clustered cell-free networks.

Method: Establishes diagonal element-wise convergence of resolvent matrices to derive deterministic equivalents for inter-subnetwork interference and downlink ergodic rate; introduces a stabilized variable transformation to handle numerical instability for small regularization parameters, enabling unified formulation for both RZF and ZF precoding.

Result: Proposed method achieves relative error below 6% while significantly reducing computational complexity compared to Monte Carlo simulation.

Conclusion: The proposed method provides an accurate and efficient unified solution for downlink rate estimation in clustered cell-free networks under both RZF and ZF precoding.

Abstract: Clustered cell-free networks have emerged as a promising architecture for sixth generation ultra-dense wireless communication systems by enabling local cooperation among base stations while controlling system complexity. For resource allocation and performance optimization of such networks, accurate and efficient estimation of the ergodic achievable downlink rate is a fundamental prerequisite. Existing rate estimation approaches mainly rely on computationally prohibitive Monte Carlo simulations or adopt random matrix theory-based methods, which have been well-developed for conventional cellular and cell-free networks.
