However, existing RMT-based methods have not addressed the unique inter-subnetwork interference in clustered cell-free networks, and therefore lack an efficient solution for accurate downlink rate estimation under both regularized zero-forcing and zero-forcing precoding. In this paper, we propose a stabilized element-wise rate estimation method for downlink rate estimation in clustered cell-free networks. We establish the diagonal element-wise convergence of resolvent matrices, which enables the derivation of deterministic equivalents for inter-subnetwork interference and the downlink ergodic rate. We further introduce a stabilized variable transformation to address the numerical instability when the regularization parameter is very small, hereby enabling a unified formulation applicable to both regularized zero-forcing and zero-forcing precoding. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves a relative error below 6% while significantly reducing computational complexity compared with the Monte Carlo simulation.

</details>


### [322] [Algebraic Resolutions of Seven Open Problems on Cyclic and Negacyclic Codes Supporting Designs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17371)
*Yutong Zhang,Yaoran Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: A unified algebraic solution to seven open problems on cyclic/negacyclic/constacyclic codes supporting designs, using Cayley parametrizations, quotient transport, and projective-order congruences to characterize root sets, designs, and existence criteria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve long-standing open problems by Wang, Tang, and Ding concerning the algebraic structure of cyclic, negacyclic, and constacyclic codes that support combinatorial designs.

Method: Uses Cayley parametrization of the unit circle to link trace-zero conditions to semilinear equations on PG(1,q); quotient transport from U_{2(q^2+1)} to U_{q^2+1} for negacyclic codes; projective-order congruence analysis for constacyclic ovoid codes.

Result: Characterized complementary designs S(3,q0+1,q+1), Baer sublines as minimum zero sets, exact existence criterion for λ-constacyclic ovoid codes (λ not a square), universal weight enumerator for lifted ovoid codes, and construction of consecutive-root negacyclic MDS codes yielding complete 5-designs.

Conclusion: All seven open problems are solved; a proper negacyclic [11,5,7]_{23} code is explicitly constructed as an example of a complete simple 5-design.

Abstract: This paper gives a unified algebraic solution to seven open problems of Wang, Tang and Ding on cyclic, negacyclic and constacyclic codes supporting designs. For the cyclic code \[ C\left(\frac{p^s-1}{2},\frac{p^s+1}{2}\right), \] a Cayley parametrization of the unit circle reduces the trace-zero condition to a semilinear equation on \(\PG(1,q)\). Its large root sets are exactly the \(\F_{p^{\gcd(m,s)}}\)-sublines, yielding the complementary design \[ \overline{S(3,q_0+1,q+1)}. \] For the length \(q^2+1\) negacyclic code, a quotient transport from \(\U_{2(q^2+1)}\) to \(\U_{q^2+1}\) and a unit-circle parametrization show that the minimum zero sets are precisely the Baer sublines of \(\PG(1,q^2)\). Equivalently, the corresponding support design is the complement of the non-tangent plane sections of an elliptic quadric \(\Q^-(3,q)\).
  For constacyclic ovoid codes of length \(q^2+1\) over \(\F_q\), the exact existence criterion is \[ λ\in\F_q^*,\qquad \exists\ λ\text{-constacyclic ovoid code} \Longleftrightarrow λ\notin(\F_q^*)^2. \] In particular, negacyclic ovoid codes exist exactly when \(q\equiv3\pmod4\). The proof uses the corrected projective-order congruence \[ a=(q+1)c,\qquad c\equiv b\pmod{q-1},\qquad \operatorname{ord}(θ\F_q^*)=\frac{q^2+1}{\gcd(q^2+1,c)}. \] The paper also derives a universal weight enumerator for lifted ovoid codes over extension fields, independent of the chosen ovoid. Finally, consecutive-root negacyclic MDS codes are constructed to give complete simple \(5\)-designs, including a proper negacyclic \([11,5,7]_{23}\) code whose minimum supports form the complete \(5-(11,7,15)\) design.

</details>


### [323] [Channel Modeling and LED Spot Detection for Dense Image-Sensor Visible Light Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17375)
*Tianhao Shi,Shan Lu,Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a robust decoding framework for high-density LED arrays in VLC systems that handles ISI, radial distortion, and vignetting using pilot-aided geometric recognition, PSF-constrained Hough transform, and distortion correction, achieving superior accuracy and throughput without reducing LED density.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-density LED arrays enable high-speed VLC transmission but suffer from severe inter-symbol interference (ISI) due to blurred/spatially overlapped optical spots from focal shift, resolution limits, and interference. Radial distortion and vignetting further degrade detection. Existing methods reduce LED signaling density to mitigate ISI, sacrificing throughput.

Method: A pilot-aided geometric recognition method using PSF-constrained Hough transform and circle-center alignment refinement, combined with radial distortion correction and vignetting-aware compensation to restore geometric consistency and suppress edge-related detection errors. Prior structural knowledge from pilot frames helps separate overlapping LED signals.

Result: Experimental results on a real-world VLC testbed demonstrate superior decoding accuracy and throughput compared to conventional Hough-based and low-density baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework shows strong potential for high-efficiency VLC applications in interference-prone environments without sacrificing LED signaling density.

Abstract: High-density LED arrays enable high-speed transmission in image-sensor-based visible-light communication (VLC) systems. However, when optical spots become blurred and spatially overlapped due to focal shift, resolution limitations, or interference, severe inter-symbol interference (ISI) occurs, significantly degrading decoding performance. Furthermore, radial distortion introduces geometric deformation of the LED grid, while vignetting leads to incomplete and asymmetric spot shapes at the periphery, both of which further hinder reliable signal detection. Existing methods mitigate ISI by reducing LED transmission signaling density.
  This paper proposes a robust decoding framework that maintains full LED signaling density. We introduce a pilot-aided geometric recognition method that uses a PSF-constrained Hough transform and circle-center alignment refinement. \textbf{In addition, radial distortion correction and vignetting-aware compensation are incorporated to restore geometric consistency and suppress edge-related detection errors.} By leveraging prior structural knowledge from pilot frames, the system effectively separates overlapping LED signals under severe optical distortion.
  Experimental results on a real-world VLC testbed confirm that the proposed method achieves superior decoding accuracy and throughput compared to conventional Hough-based and low-density baseline methods. The results highlight its potential for high-efficiency VLC applications in interference-prone environments.

</details>


### [324] [DL-Driven Optimization for ISAC System Equipped With Pinching and Movable Antennas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17629)
*Nemanja Stefan Perović,Keshav Singh,Chih-Peng Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper optimizes sum-rate for an ISAC system with pinching antennas (PAs) and movable antennas (MAs) using a deep learning network, showing performance gains over fixed-antenna systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ISAC is promising for future wireless systems via shared hardware/spectrum. PAs and MAs can further boost ISAC performance, motivating optimization of sum-rate under sensing constraints.

Method: Derives closed-form optimal sensing receive combiner; proposes an unsupervised deep learning network to jointly optimize PA/MA positions, communication precoding, and sensing beamformer. Loss function includes objective and penalty terms for antenna position constraints.

Result: ISAC with PAs and MAs achieves higher sum-rate than fixed-antenna systems, with advantage growing with transmit power. Communication performance is slightly more sensitive to sensing SINR threshold than vice versa.

Conclusion: PAs and MAs significantly improve ISAC sum-rate; the proposed DL-based optimization effectively handles the complex joint optimization problem.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is considered to be a promising technology for future wireless systems due to its ability to provide communication and sensing services using shared hardware and spectrum resources. Moreover, the introduction of recently developed pinching antennas (PAs) and movable antennas (MAs) has the potential to further improve the performance gains of ISAC. Therefore, our goal is to study the optimization of the sum-rate for an ISAC system equipped with PAs and MAs, capable of satisfying minimal sensing requirements. To achieve it, we derive a closed-form solution for the optimal sensing receive combiner, and show that it is determined by other optimization variables. For these other variables (i.e., the positions of the transmit PAs, the positions of the users' MAs, the communication precoding matrices, and the sensing transmit beamformer), we propose a deep learning (DL) network that finds their optimal values. To train the network in an unsupervised manner, we formulate a loss function consisting of the objective function, as well as the penalty terms related to the constraints for the PAs and MAs positions. Simulation results show that using PAs and MAs in ISAC systems provides a larger sum-rate compared to ISAC systems with only fixed antennas, and that this performance advantage is increased with the maximum transmit power. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the communication performance of the considered system is a bit more affected by the sensing signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) threshold compared to the sensing performance.

</details>


### [325] [The information-theoretic complexity of differentiable functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17801)
*Matthijs Ruijgrok*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper introduces V-complexity, a measure of function complexity based on piecewise constant approximations, compares it to compression-based measures (RLE, LZ77), and demonstrates its application in defining Effective Complexity for complex systems, using cream diffusion in coffee as an example where complexity peaks mid-process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize intuitions about the simplicity/complexity of differentiable functions and provide a rigorous measure that can serve as an ingredient for defining Effective Complexity in complex systems.

Method: V-complexity is defined via approximations of a function by piecewise constant functions, considering both approximation quality and the number of intervals. It is compared to compression-based complexity measures (Run Length Encoding and Lempel Ziv 77). Effective Complexity is then defined using V-complexity and applied to the diffusion equation model.

Result: V-complexity formalizes intuitive notions of function complexity. It is hypothesized to be equivalent to compression-based measures under RLE and LZ77. For the coffee diffusion model, V-complexity starts at zero, rises to a maximum, and falls back to zero at equilibrium, matching results from cellular automaton and Apparent Complexity approaches.

Conclusion: V-complexity is a useful measure for quantifying complexity of differentiable functions and can serve as a component for defining Effective Complexity in complex systems, with empirical validation via the diffusion example.

Abstract: A measure for the complexity of a differentiable function f(x) on an interval is introduced. It is based on approximations of the function by piecewise constant functions. The measure takes into account the quality of the approximation and the number of intervals in the approximating function.
  This measure, called the V-complexity of f(x), is shown to formalize some intuitions about the simplicity or complexity of f(x).
  The V-complexity is then compared to another measure of complexity, namely how compressible an approximation of f(x) is. It is hypothesized that V-complexity is equivalent to the compression measure, in the case of the Run Length Encoding and the Lempel Ziv 77 algorithms.
  V-complexity can be used as an ingredient in the definition of the Effective Complexity (EC) of a Complex System. When the perceived regularities of such a system are described by a differentiable function on an interval, the EC can be defined as the V-complexity of that function. EC is applied to the model of diffusion of cream in a cup of coffee. The perceived regularity of this model is given by the diffusion equation. The V-complexity of the solution of the equation starts at zero, quickly increases to a maximum and then decreases back to zero as the liquid reaches its equilibrium state. It is shown that this is also the result when a cellular automaton approach and the concept of Apparent Complexity is used.

</details>


### [326] [Transformer-Based Hybrid Beamforming with Reconfigurable Pixel Antenna for HAPS Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17858)
*Ruiqi Wang,Ziwei Wan,Keke Ying,Zhen Gao*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Transformer-based hybrid beamforming framework (PR-HBFNet) for RPA-equipped massive MIMO in HAPS communications, which uses a Transformer encoder for pattern selection and model-driven residual learning for computing analog/digital precoders, achieving near-optimal spectral efficiency with lower complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient beamforming for reconfigurable pixel antenna (RPA)-equipped massive MIMO in high-altitude platform station (HAPS) communications, where traditional beamforming methods face high computational complexity.

Method: The PR-HBFNet consists of: 1) a pattern reconfigurable network using a Transformer encoder to select radiation patterns for each antenna element, and 2) a hybrid beamforming network using model-driven residual learning over SVD-based initializations to compute analog and digital precoders.

Result: The proposed PR-HBFNet closely approaches the spectral efficiency of a greedy benchmark while significantly reducing computational complexity.

Conclusion: Transformer-based hybrid beamforming is effective and efficient for RPA-equipped massive MIMO in HAPS communications, offering near-optimal performance with lower complexity than greedy methods.

Abstract: This paper proposes a Transformer-based hybrid beamforming framework for reconfigurable pixel antenna (RPA)-equipped massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) in high-altitude platform station (HAPS) communications. The proposed pattern reconfigurable hybrid beamforming network (PR-HBFNet) comprises two key components: 1) a pattern reconfigurable network that leverages a Transformer encoder to determine the radiation pattern for each antenna element, and 2) a hybrid beamforming network that employs model-driven residual learning to compute analog and digital precoders over SVD-based initializations. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed PR-HBFNet closely approaches the spectral efficiency of a greedy benchmark while significantly reducing computational complexity.

</details>


### [327] [A Hexagonal Counterexample to Log-Convexity of Fisher Information Along the Heat Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18081)
*Jiayang Zou,Luyao Fan,Jiayang Gao,Jia Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper disproves the Cheng-Geng log-convexity conjecture in dimension two (and higher) by constructing a counterexample density on ℝ², using a hexagonal perturbation on the triangular torus transferred via a Gaussian envelope, with numerical support. This also disproves several related multidimensional conjectures (Gaussian completely monotone, McKean's, Toscani's entropy power). The paper also initiates study of sharp constants θ_d^*, proving θ_1^*=1 and establishing dimension monotonicity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disprove the Cheng-Geng log-convexity conjecture in dimension two and higher, and to disprove related multidimensional conjectures including the Gaussian completely monotone conjecture, McKean's conjecture, and Toscani's entropy power conjecture.

Method: The counterexample is constructed as a small hexagonal perturbation on the triangular torus, transferred to ℝ² using a Gaussian envelope, with explicit two-dimensional numerics. The paper also proves theoretical results about sharp constants θ_d^*.

Result: The Cheng-Geng log-convexity conjecture is disproven in dimension two and, by tensorization, in all dimensions d≥2. Consequently, multidimensional forms of Gaussian completely monotone, McKean's, and Toscani's entropy power conjectures also fail. Additionally, θ_1^*=1 is proven and monotonicity in dimension is established.

Conclusion: The counterexample disproves several important conjectures in information theory and convexity. The paper also initiates the study of sharp constants θ_d^*, with foundational results.

Abstract: We construct a smooth, strictly positive, Gaussian-decaying density on $\R^2$ for which Fisher information along the heat flow is not log-convex. This disproves the Cheng--Geng log-convexity conjecture in dimension two and, by tensorization, in every dimension $d\ge2$. Consequently, the multidimensional forms of the Gaussian completely monotone conjecture, McKean's conjecture, and Toscani's entropy power conjecture also fail. This complements the recent one-dimensional counterexample of Gu and Sellke. The counterexample is a small hexagonal perturbation on the triangular torus, transferred to $\R^2$ by a Gaussian envelope, and supported by explicit two-dimensional numerics. Finally, we initiate the study of the sharp constants $θ_d^*$ by proving $θ_1^*=1$, establishing monotonicity in the dimension, and recording an intrinsic simplex resonance family in every fixed dimension. The explicit two-dimensional counterexample was found by GPT-5.5 Pro.

</details>


### [328] [Movable Antenna-Aided Secure LEO Satellite Networks: Joint Antenna Position and Beamforming Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18099)
*Suhong Luo,Pan Tang,Jianhua Zhang,Ji Wang,Yixuan Li,Zihang Ding,Xingwang Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes an MA-assisted secure transmission scheme using movable antennas and alternating optimization to maximize average secrecy rate for 6G LEO satellite communications against eavesdropping satellites.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 6G LEO satellite communications face serious security issues due to broadcast nature; small angular separations between legitimate and eavesdropping satellites in dense constellations further challenge secure transmission design.

Method: Joint optimization of transmit beamforming and MA positions via an AO framework: SDR for beamforming, SCA (high-accuracy) and DE (low-complexity) for MA position optimization.

Result: The proposed MA-assisted scheme consistently achieves superior secrecy rate performance compared to conventional fixed-position antenna schemes.

Conclusion: Movable antenna technology with joint beamforming and position optimization is an effective PLS solution for dense LEO satellite constellations with small angular separations.

Abstract: The broadcast characteristics of sixth-generation (6G) low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite communications raise serious security issues. Movable antenna (MA) technology offers a promising physical layer security (PLS) solution by flexibly reconfiguring antenna positions to exploit additional spatial degrees of freedom. However, in highly dense LEO satellite constellations, the legitimate satellite and potential eavesdropping satellites may exhibit small angular separations, which poses significant challenges for the design of secure transmission schemes. To address this challenge, this paper proposes an MA-assisted secure transmission scheme for time-varying LEO satellite communications, where a ground station equipped with an MA array communicates with a serving satellite, while the other visible satellites are regarded as potential eavesdroppers. We maximize the average secrecy rate by jointly optimizing the transmit beamforming and MA positions. An alternating optimization (AO) framework is developed, where semidefinite relaxation is adopted for the beamforming optimization subproblem, while high-accuracy successive convex approximation (SCA) and low-complexity differential evolution (DE) algorithms are proposed for the MA position optimization subproblem. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed MA-assisted LEO secure transmission scheme consistently achieves superior performance compared to the conventional fixed-position antenna scheme.

</details>


### [329] [Existence and Counting Bounds for High-Memory Spatially-Coupled Codes via the Combinatorial Nullstellensatz](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18323)
*Lei Huang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper uses algebraic-combinatorial methods (Combinatorial Nullstellensatz and Alon-Füredi theorem) to derive nonconstructive existence and counting bounds for edge-spreading assignments that eliminate short cycles in SC-LDPC codes at the protograph level, focusing on sufficient memory conditions and lower bounds on feasible designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Short cycle configurations and harmful structures strongly affect the finite-length performance of SC-LDPC codes. Existing constructive frameworks (e.g., CLLL/Moser-Tardos) are limited, and sharper nonconstructive bounds are needed to characterize the feasible design space.

Method: The paper encodes cycle-activation conditions as polynomial vanishing constraints over finite grids, then applies the Combinatorial Nullstellensatz to derive sufficient memory conditions for eliminating prescribed cycles. The Alon-Füredi theorem is used to obtain lower bounds on the number of feasible edge-spreading assignments.

Result: Explicit memory conditions are derived for destroying all 4-cycles, and both 4- and 6-cycles in fully connected (γ,κ) base graphs. For fixed γ, these bounds are asymptotically tight up to a constant factor compared to known lower bounds. A counting bound for assignments eliminating all 4-cycles (girth ≥ 6) is also provided.

Conclusion: The results offer a refined algebraic-combinatorial characterization of the feasible design space for high-memory SC-LDPC codes, though no explicit construction algorithm is given.

Abstract: The finite-length performance of spatially-coupled low-density parity-check (SC-LDPC) codes is strongly affected by short cycle configurations and the harmful structures induced by them. This paper studies SC-LDPC code design directly at the protograph level, where the design variables are the edge-spreading assignments specified by the partition matrix. In contrast to CLLL/Moser--Tardos based constructive frameworks for QC-SC-LDPC codes, we focus on sharper nonconstructive existence and counting bounds. By encoding cycle-activation conditions as polynomial vanishing constraints over finite grids, we apply the Combinatorial Nullstellensatz to derive sufficient memory conditions for eliminating prescribed cycle-induced harmful structures. For fully connected $(γ,κ)$ base graphs, the resulting bounds explicitly characterize the memory required to destroy all $4$-cycles as well as all $4$- and $6$-cycles, and for fixed $γ$, they are asymptotically tight up to a constant factor compared with known lower bounds. We further apply the Alon--Füredi theorem to obtain lower bounds on the number of feasible edge-spreading assignments, including an explicit counting bound for assignments that eliminate all $4$-cycles and hence yield girth at least six. These results provide a refined algebraic-combinatorial characterization of the feasible design space for high-memory SC-LDPC codes, although no corresponding construction algorithm is provided.

</details>


### [330] [The Sharma-Mittal Entropy is Subadditive and Supermodular on the Majorization Lattice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18600)
*Roberto Bruno,Ugo Vaccaro*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proves that Sharma-Mittal entropy is subadditive and supermodular on the lattice of probability distributions ordered by majorization, unifying previous results for Shannon, Tsallis, and Rényi entropies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify and extend known results about subadditivity and supermodularity of various entropy measures (Shannon, Tsallis, Rényi) by proving these properties hold for the more general Sharma-Mittal entropy.

Method: The authors prove that Sharma-Mittal entropy satisfies subadditivity and supermodularity on the lattice of n-dimensional probability distributions, where the ordering is defined by majorization among vectors.

Result: Sharma-Mittal entropy is both subadditive and supermodular under majorization ordering, thereby unifying and extending earlier results for Shannon, Tsallis, and Rényi entropies.

Conclusion: The Sharma-Mittal entropy generalizes and encompasses prior entropy measures in terms of these lattice-theoretic properties, providing a unified framework.

Abstract: We prove that Sharma-Mittal entropy is a subadditive and supermodular function on the lattice of all $n$-dimensional probability distributions, ordered according to the partial order relation defined by majorization among vectors. Our result unifies and extends analogous results presented in the literature for the Shannon entropy, the Tsallis entropy, and the Rényi entropy.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [331] [Kelvin v1.0: A Neural Pre-Encoder for H.264: A standards-compliant learned preprocessor with -27.62% BD-VMAF on UVG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16376)
*Marco Graziano*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Kelvin is a lightweight learned pre-encoder that adjusts pixels within ±1/255 to improve perceptual quality of H.264 encoding. It achieves ~27% BD-VMAF improvement over libx264, uses a hybrid codec proxy for differentiability, and is designed for constrained H.264 workloads where x265 is not an option.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve perceptual quality of H.264 video encoding without modifying the encoder, decoder, or bitstream compatibility, targeting scenarios where staying on H.264 is a constraint (e.g., legacy systems, CDNs, hardware decoders).

Method: A learned pre-encoder (Kelvin) applies content-adaptive pixel adjustments bounded at ±1/255 per channel. Training uses a hybrid codec proxy: a differentiable rate estimator (Spearman ρ=0.986 vs real bits) and a U-Net distortion proxy trained on real encoder outputs, overcoming the non-differentiability of H.264.

Result: -27.62% BD-VMAF on UVG benchmark (7/7 wins), -5.18% BD-VMAF-NEG (6/7 wins). On MCL-JCV: 28/30 wins, ~27.70% BD-VMAF. x265 medium still outperforms Kelvin on all metrics. Per-sequence data, failure taxonomy, and five-baseline sanity panel are published.

Conclusion: Kelvin demonstrates significant perceptual gains within the H.264 constraint, but is not a universal replacement—it is positioned explicitly for workloads where H.264 is mandatory and x265 is unavailable. Failure modes (rate-floor, distribution shift, metric saturation) are documented honestly.

Abstract: Kelvin is a lightweight learned pre-encoder that sits in front of an unmodified libx264 encoder. It applies content-adaptive pixel adjustments, bounded at +/-1/255 per channel, so that the encoder allocates bits where they matter most perceptually, while emitting a standard H.264 bitstream compatible with every existing decoder, player, and CDN. On the seven-sequence 1080p UVG benchmark, Kelvin v1.0 achieves a mean BD-VMAF of -27.62% (7 of 7 wins) and BD-VMAF-NEG of -5.18% (6 of 7 wins) relative to baseline libx264 at preset medium. On the 30-sequence MCL-JCV public set (28 unseen by training), the same checkpoint wins on 28 of 30 clips by BD-VMAF; with the two diagnosable failures removed the mean is -27.70% BD-VMAF and -5.37% BD-VMAF-NEG, consistent with UVG to within one percentage point. A central engineering challenge is the non-differentiability of H.264: we describe a hybrid codec proxy that combines a calibrated differentiable rate estimator (Spearman rho = 0.986 vs. real libx264 bits-per-pixel) with a U-Net distortion proxy trained on real encoder outputs. We publish full per-sequence rate-distortion data, a named failure-mode taxonomy on MCL-JCV (rate-floor violation, distribution shift, metric saturation), a five-baseline sanity panel (hqdn3d, unsharp, -tune psnr, -tune ssim, x265 medium), and honest positioning: x265 medium beats Kelvin on every metric on the same corpus. Kelvin is therefore designed for workloads where remaining on H.264 is a constraint rather than a choice.

</details>


### [332] [Flow Matching with Optimized Subclass Priors for Medical Image Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16469)
*Felix Nützel,Mischa Dombrowski,Bernhard Kainz*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a generative augmentation strategy for rare diseases in medical imaging that uses two-level informative priors—Gaussian mixture modeling to partition coarse labels into submodes and subclass-conditioned source distributions—to improve tail-class generation fidelity and downstream classification performance on long-tailed chest X-ray and CT benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rare diseases are underrepresented in medical imaging datasets, causing classifiers to fail on tail classes where detection matters most. Existing generative augmentation methods struggle because coarse disease labels aggregate diverse subtypes and acquisition settings, and a shared Gaussian source forces rare subpopulations through long transport paths.

Method: The method introduces informative priors at two levels: (1) partitioning coarse labels into coherent submodes via Gaussian mixture modeling in the latent space, and (2) learning subclass-conditioned source distributions that re-center and re-scale the starting distribution per submode. Geometric control is applied to concentrate displacement directions around prototypes and cap path-length outliers.

Result: On MIMIC-LT, NIH-LT, and CT-RATE benchmarks, the method consistently improves tail-class generation fidelity and diversity (FID, IRS), and reliably improves downstream balanced accuracy and macro-F1 over non-augmented baselines across modalities.

Conclusion: The proposed two-level prior strategy is a promising augmentation approach for addressing rare disease challenges in medical imaging, effectively improving both generation quality and downstream classifier performance on long-tailed datasets.

Abstract: Rare diseases dominate the diagnostic challenge in medical imaging yet are severely underrepresented in clinical datasets, causing classifiers to fail on exactly the conditions where reliable detection matters most. Generative augmentation can supply the missing tail-class coverage, but coarse disease labels aggregate diverse subtypes and acquisition settings into multi-modal conditionals that bias generators toward dominant submodes, while a shared Gaussian source forces rare subpopulations through disproportionately long transport paths. We propose an offline strategy that introduces informative priors at two levels: first, we partition each coarse label into coherent submodes via Gaussian mixture modeling in the generative model's latent space; second, we learn subclass-conditioned source distributions that re-center and re-scale the starting distribution per submode, shortening trajectories and reducing within-subclass dispersion. To prevent degenerate solutions we impose explicit geometric control, moderately concentrating normalized displacement directions around learnable prototypes while capping path-length outliers. On long-tailed chest X-ray (MIMIC-LT, NIH-LT) and CT slice (CT-RATE) benchmarks the proposed method consistently improves tail-class generation fidelity and diversity (FID, IRS) and is a promising augmentation strategy that reliably improves downstream balanced accuracy and macro-F1 over a non-augmented baseline across modalities.

</details>


### [333] [Deep Learning for MRI Slice Interpolation: The Critical Role of Problem Formulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16476)
*Shamit Savant*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Deep learning interpolation of prostate MRI slices found that problem formulation (using adjacent slices) has 290x more impact on performance than architectural complexity, with U-Net achieving best results (PSNR 30.08 dB, SSIM 0.898) and DDPM failing due to stochastic-deterministic mismatch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical MRI suffers from coarse through-plane resolution compared to in-plane resolution, limiting diagnostic utility in prostate imaging.

Method: Evaluated five deep learning architectures (CNN, U-Net, two GAN variants, DDPM) for interpolating intermediate MRI slices to double through-plane resolution, comparing problem formulations using adjacent vs. distant slices.

Result: Using adjacent slices (i-1, i+1) gave 58% SSIM improvement over distant slices (i-2, i+2). U-Net achieved PSNR 30.08 dB and SSIM 0.898, 10.1% improvement over linear interpolation. DDPM showed poor quality due to stochastic-deterministic mismatch.

Conclusion: Problem formulation has 290x more impact than architectural sophistication in medical imaging tasks.

Abstract: Through-plane resolution in clinical MRI is typically much coarser than in-plane resolution, limiting diagnostic utility. This work investigates deep learning approaches to interpolate intermediate MRI slices in prostate imaging, effectively doubling through-plane resolution. I evaluated five architectures (CNN, U-Net, two GAN variants, and DDPM) and discovered that problem formulation has dramatically more impact than architectural complexity. By reformulating the interpolation task to use adjacent slices (i-1, i+1) rather than distant slices (i-2, i+2), I achieved a 58% improvement in SSIM performance across all deterministic architectures. The U-Net model achieved the best results with PSNR of 30.08 dB and SSIM of 0.898, representing a 10.1% improvement over linear interpolation baseline. A DDPM was also evaluated but showed poor reconstruction quality due to fundamental mismatch between stochastic generation and deterministic reconstruction requirements. These findings demonstrate that problem formulation can have 290x more impact than architectural sophistication in medical imaging tasks.

</details>


### [334] [Sustainable Real-Time 8K60 HEVC Encoding for V2X: Repurposing Legacy NVENC Hardware at the Vehicular Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16738)
*Kasidis Arunruangsirilert,Jiro Katto*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Repurposing legacy NVIDIA Pascal GPUs (GP104/GP102) with dual-NVENC 2-Way Split Frame Encoding achieves real-time 8K60 HEVC encoding for V2X/ToD applications, with under 1% RD penalty, better energy efficiency than flagship models, and lower e-waste.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ultra-low-latency 8K60 video telemetry is needed for V2X and Tele-Operated Driving, but deploying modern hardware faces supply chain issues, high power costs, and e-waste concerns.

Method: Leveraging legacy NVIDIA Pascal GPUs (GP104, GP102) with dual-NVENC capability and 2-Way Split Frame Encoding (SFE) for real-time 8K60 HEVC encoding; micro-architectural analysis comparing throughput and energy efficiency across GPU dies.

Result: Real-time 8K60 throughput achieved with &lt;1% RD penalty; smaller GPU dies outperform larger flagship models in throughput and energy efficiency; Pascal's architecture aligns well with ultra-low-latency V2X pipelines.

Conclusion: Repurposed mid-range Pascal GPUs are a cost-effective, sustainable solution for modern Intelligent Transportation Systems, mitigating e-waste while meeting performance requirements.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communications and Tele-Operated Driving (ToD) demands ultra-low-latency, 8K60 video telemetry. However, deploying modern hardware at the vehicular edge is frequently hindered by supply chain constraints, high power budgets, and growing e-waste concerns. This paper investigates a highly sustainable alternative: repurposing legacy NVIDIA Pascal GPUs for real-time 8K HEVC edge encoding. We demonstrate that triggering 2-Way Split Frame Encoding (SFE) on dual-NVENC GP104 and GP102 silicon successfully unlocks real-time 8K60 throughput with a negligible Rate-Distortion penalty of under 1%. Crucially, our micro-architectural analysis reveals that smaller GPU dies significantly outperform larger flagship models in both raw throughput and energy efficiency. Because fixed-function encoding forces general-purpose Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) cores to sustain maximum frequencies while remaining idle, GPUs with fewer CUDA cores waste drastically less power. While benchmarking against the state-of-the-art RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell highlights a generational compression efficiency gap, Pascal's functional HEVC architecture and native lack of B-frames align perfectly with ultra-low-latency V2X pipelines. Ultimately, repurposed mid-range Pascal GPUs present a highly capable, cost-effective, and e-waste mitigating solution for modern Intelligent Transportation Systems.

</details>


### [335] [Adaptive Fused Prior Transfer for Controllable Generative Image Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16817)
*Yifei Pei,Ying Liu,Nam Ling*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: AFP-GIC is a controllable generative image compression codec that transfers an adaptive fused prior from a frozen AdaCode model, improving very-low-bitrate reconstruction by predicting fused priors at the decoder side without transmitting them, achieving better perceptual quality and reduced latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Very-low-bitrate reconstruction in learned image compression is difficult because transmitted representations often fail to preserve fine textures and local structures. Existing controllable codecs rely on single-codebook token-based priors, which provide limited guidance for the decoder to infer missing details under severe bit constraints.

Method: Proposes AFP-GIC, a controllable codec that transfers an adaptive fused prior from a frozen pretrained AdaCode model. Encoder-side fused-prior features guide latent formation; the decoder predicts a compatible fused prior from the compressed representation and control variables, enabling prior-guided reconstruction without transmitting the fused prior itself.

Result: Reduces decoder latency by 18.1% and overall parameter count by 31.10 million (20.5%) relative to DC-VIC. Shows competitive PSNR on Kodak, CLIC2020, and DIV2K, with clear perceptual gains in NIQE scores and very-low-bitrate visual comparisons.

Conclusion: AFP-GIC effectively addresses the decoder-side reconstruction-prior problem in controllable generative compression. The fused-prior family generalizes single-codebook approaches, and the theoretical analysis links decoder-side fused-prior alignment to a reconstruction-error upper bound.

Abstract: Learned image compression has achieved competitive rate-distortion performance, but very-low-bitrate reconstruction remains difficult because the transmitted representation often cannot preserve fine textures and local structures. Perceptual and generative codecs address this problem by using learned reconstruction priors, and controllable codecs allow one model to cover different bitrate and reconstruction preferences. However, controllability alone does not resolve the decoder-side reconstruction-prior problem: under severe bit constraints, the decoder must infer missing details from limited transmitted information, while existing codebook-based controllable designs generally rely on single-codebook token-based priors. This paper proposes Adaptive Fused Prior Transfer for Controllable Generative Image Compression (AFP-GIC), a controllable codec that transfers an adaptive fused prior from a frozen pretrained AdaCode model. Encoder-side fused-prior features guide latent formation, while the decoder predicts a compatible fused prior from the compressed representation and selected control variables, enabling prior-guided reconstruction without transmitting the fused prior itself. A motivating analysis relates decoder-side fused-prior alignment to a reconstruction-error upper bound and shows that the fused-prior family contains single-codebook choices as special cases. Under the unified benchmark, AFP-GIC reduces decoder latency by 18.1% and the overall parameter count by 31.10 million (20.5%) relative to DC-VIC. Experiments on Kodak, CLIC2020, and DIV2K show competitive PSNR, with the clearest perceptual gains in NIQE scores and very-low-bitrate visual comparisons.

</details>


### [336] [RDDM: A Residual-Driven Drifting Model for High-Fidelity Low-Dose CT Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17188)
*Jianxu Wang,Qing Lyu,Ge Wang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Residual-Driven Drifting Model (RDDM) for low-dose CT denoising that achieves state-of-the-art performance with one-step inference (≈15ms per slice), balancing high fidelity and real-time efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LDCT denoising methods either produce over-smoothed results (pixel-level objectives) or require expensive multi-step iterative inference (diffusion models), lacking efficient, high-fidelity solutions suitable for real-time clinical use.

Method: RDDM extends Drifting Models by introducing a residual drifting field combining an attractive force (from LDCT-NDCT residuals) and a repulsive force (from generated residuals), enabling one-step denoising. Three variants (RDDM-Fine, etc.) are developed with different parameter settings and pixel-level supervision for different application needs.

Result: RDDM achieves state-of-the-art denoising; RDDM-Fine yields superior PSNR, SSIM, and best FID of 5.87 with realistic textures. Inference takes only ~15ms for a 512×512 slice.

Conclusion: RDDM is a promising solution for high-fidelity and real-time LDCT denoising, suitable for clinical applications.

Abstract: Low-dose CT (LDCT) denoising remains an important yet challenging problem in medical imaging. Although recent learning-based methods have shown promising performance, those optimized using classical pixel-level objectives often produce over-smoothed reconstructions. Existing mainstream generative models, such as diffusion models, have improved fidelity at the cost of expensive multi-step iterative inference, which limits their practicality for real-time use. To address this gap, we propose a Residual-Driven Drifting Model (RDDM) for effective, efficient, and high-fidelity LDCT denoising. Inspired by the recently proposed Drifting Models, RDDM incorporates the multi-step distribution evolution into the training dynamics through a residual drifting field, thereby enabling one-step denoising. Specifically, the residual drifting field is formed by an attractive force induced by the residuals between LDCT and normal-dose CT (NDCT) and a repulsive force induced by the generated residuals. In addition, by adjusting the parameter settings and incorporating pixel-level supervision, we develop three RDDM variants, covering application needs from detail preservation to stronger noise suppression. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RDDM achieves state-of-the-art denoising performance among supervised baselines. In particular, RDDM-Fine produces reconstructions that are highly consistent with NDCT, achieving superior PSNR and SSIM together with the best FID of 5.87 while preserving realistic anatomical textures. Moreover, RDDM enables on-the-fly inference, requiring only about 15 ms to denoise a single 512 x 512 LDCT slice. These results establish RDDM as a promising solution for high-fidelity and real-time LDCT denoising in clinical applications.

</details>


### [337] [LUMEN: Low-light Unified Multi-stage Enhancement Network using depth-guided flash, clustering, and attention-based Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17893)
*Bibhabasu Debnath,Sahana Ray,Sanjay Ghosh*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: LUMEN is a multi-stage low-light image enhancement framework that uses depth-aware virtual flash simulation and transformer-based feature fusion to achieve state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing low-light enhancement methods apply uniform enhancement without considering depth-dependent light attenuation and sensor noise in real-world scenes, leading to suboptimal results.

Method: LUMEN consists of: (1) depth estimation via encoder-decoder, (2) soft clustering for depth-aware pixel partitioning, (3) virtual flash simulation, (4) efficient attention-based fusion blocks combining flash features, depth representations, and image features, and (5) a composite loss function balancing reconstruction, perceptual, structural, color, edge, and depth consistency.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on LOL-v1 and LOL-v2 benchmarks, producing visually natural results compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: Depth-aware, multi-stage enhancement with virtual flash simulation and transformer fusion effectively addresses low-light image degradation, outperforming uniform enhancement approaches.

Abstract: Low-light image enhancement remains a challenging problem due to severe noise, color distortion, contrast degradation, and loss of structural details under insufficient illumination. Existing methods typically apply uniform enhancement without considering the depth-dependent nature of light attenuation and sensor noise in real-world scenes. To address this limitation, we propose LUMEN, a multi-stage enhancement framework that integrates virtual flash simulation with transformer-based feature fusion. The proposed framework first estimates scene depth from low-light inputs using a dedicated encoder-decoder network, after which a soft clustering module partitions pixels into depth-aware regions, enabling depth-dependent flash simulation. The simulated flash features, together with depth representations, are fused with image features through efficient attention-based fusion blocks to enhance global context while preserving fine details. A composite loss function combining reconstruction, perceptual, structural, color, edge, and depth consistency objectives ensures both visual fidelity and perceptual quality. Extensive experiments on LOL-v1 and LOL-v2 benchmarks demonstrate that LUMEN achieves state-of-the-art performance and produces visually natural results compared with several state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [338] [See Silhouettes in Motion with Neuromorphic Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17984)
*Pei Zhang,Shijie Lin,Zhou Ge,Jinpeng Chen,Wei Pu*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a dual-modal (frames + events) approach for real-time, high-frame-rate binarization on CPU-only devices, leveraging event cameras to overcome motion blur and harsh lighting in mobile platforms like drones and self-driving cars.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frame-based imaging struggles on mobile platforms (drones, self-driving cars, underwater vehicles) due to rapid motion and harsh lighting causing motion blur and loss of detail. Binarization is important for visual communication but existing methods fail under these dynamic conditions.

Method: A dual-modal approach combining frames (conventional imaging) and events (event camera data with microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range) to achieve real-time, high-frame-rate binarization on CPU-only devices. It uses an asynchronous workflow to bypass event scarcity issues that break traditional time-binning reconstruction.

Result: Competitive performance against leading techniques in reducing motion blur, impressive improvements under challenging illumination, and maintains clear target shapes even at extreme kilohertz frame rates.

Conclusion: This work paves the way towards lightweight perception and interaction in embodied intelligence on resource-constrained edge platforms.

Abstract: Quasi-bimodal objects, such as text, road signs, and barcodes, play a basic yet vital role in daily visual communication. By boiling these down to clear silhouettes, binarization uses a minimal language to convey essential vision cues for maximum downstream efficiency. The catch is that frame-based imaging often struggles on mobile platforms like drones, self-driving cars, and underwater vehicles. In these dynamic scenes, rapid motion and harsh lighting can make it blind, causing severe motion blur and erasing crucial details. To overcome the limits, neuromorphic vision via event cameras, featuring microsecond-level temporal resolution and high dynamic range, steps in as a natural solution. Building upon this event-driven sensing paradigm, we introduce a simple yet effective dual-modal approach that harnesses the synergy between frames and events to achieve real-time, high-frame-rate binarization on CPU-only devices. Extensive evaluations present that it earns competitive performance against leading techniques in reducing motion blur, while delivering impressive improvements under challenging illumination. Besides, our asynchronous workflow bypasses event scarcity that breaks traditional time-binning reconstruction, maintaining clear target shapes even at extreme kilohertz frame rates. Its binary results further serve as reliable representations that facilitate a range of downstream tasks. This work paves the way towards lightweight perception and interaction in embodied intelligence on resource-constrained edge platforms.

</details>


### [339] [Inter-LPCM: Learning-based Inter-Frame Predictive Coding for LiDAR Point Cloud Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18006)
*Chang Sun,Hui Yuan,Shiqi Jiang,Chongzhen Tian,Guanghui Zhang,Raouf Hamzaoui*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes Inter-LPCM, a learning-based inter-frame predictive coding method for LiDAR point cloud compression in the spherical coordinate system, which improves upon traditional methods by introducing inter-frame radius prediction, attention-based elevation angle prediction, RD-optimized quantization, and specialized entropy models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing spherical coordinate-based point cloud compression methods have limitations: the predictive geometry coding (PredGeom) in G-PCC uses a simple linear model for inter-frame prediction, failing to capture complex motion patterns; learning-based methods in the spherical domain do not exploit inter-frame correlations to reduce geometry redundancy.

Method: The Inter-LPCM method includes: (1) delta coding for azimuth prediction using predefined angular resolution; (2) an inter-frame radius predictive (Inter-RP) model using neighboring points from current and registered reference frames; (3) a lightweight attention-based prediction (LAEP) model for elevation angles; (4) an RD-optimized quantization step selection; (5) distinct entropy coding models adapted to each spherical coordinate component's statistical priors.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates strong RD performance improvements over traditional spherical coordinate-based compression methods like PredGeom in G-PCC.

Conclusion: The Inter-LPCM effectively addresses limitations of existing methods by learning inter-frame correlations for all spherical coordinates (azimuth, radius, elevation), achieving better compression efficiency with lightweight models.

Abstract: Because LiDAR sensors acquire point clouds with a fixed angular resolution, the resulting data can be systematically parameterized and efficiently compressed in the spherical coordinate system. Traditional spherical coordinate-based point cloud compression methods have demonstrated strong rate-distortion (RD) performance, with the predictive geometry coding (PredGeom) method in the geometry-based point cloud compression (G-PCC) standard being a prominent example. Although PredGeom includes an inter-frame prediction mode, it relies on a simple linear model, which limits its ability to capture complex motion patterns and structural dependencies. Meanwhile, existing learning-based compression methods in the spherical domain do not exploit inter-frame correlations to reduce geometry redundancy. To address these limitations, we propose a learning-based inter-frame predictive coding method, termed Inter-LPCM. For azimuth prediction, we employ a delta coding strategy based on the predefined angular resolution. To improve radius compression, we introduce an inter-frame radius predictive (Inter-RP) model that estimates the current point's radius using neighboring points from both the current frame and the registered reference frame. In addition, we design a lightweight attention-based prediction (LAEP) model to predict elevation angles by capturing long-range geometric correlations across different coordinates. For quantization, we propose an RD-optimized method to select quantization steps in the spherical coordinate system. For entropy coding, we design distinct models for each spherical coordinate component. These models are adapted to the statistical priors of each coordinate, enabling more accurate probability estimation. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/SDUChangSun/Inter-LPCM

</details>


### [340] [CATRF: Codec-Adaptive TriPlane Radiance Fields for Volumetric Content Delivery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18054)
*Tung-I Chen,Lingdong Wang,Subhransu Maji,Ramesh K. Sitaraman*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: CATRF is a compression framework for plane-factorized radiance fields that integrates standard video codecs (JPEG/VP9/HEVC/AV1) directly into the training loop via straight-through estimation, achieving better rate-distortion trade-offs and outperforming compressed 3DGS methods in efficiency and speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Volumetric media has high bandwidth demands that limit practical deployment. Existing implicit/hybrid representations still need careful coding to reach bitrates comparable to 2D video, motivating the need for a codec-aware compression approach.

Method: CATRF quantizes and packs 2D feature planes into codec-friendly canvases, runs a standard codec roundtrip (JPEG/VP9/HEVC/AV1), then unpacks/dequantizes decoded features before volume rendering. A straight-through estimator (STE) enables the non-differentiable codec pipeline to be included in training.

Result: CATRF achieves better rate-distortion trade-offs than both codec-agnostic and learned-codec-in-the-loop baselines on static/dynamic benchmarks, and outperforms recent compressed 3DGS methods in compression efficiency and decoding speed.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates a practical path toward low-bitrate, compression-resilient volumetric representations suitable for free-viewpoint video streaming applications.

Abstract: Volumetric media promises next-generation content delivery applications, but its bandwidth demand remains a key bottleneck. Implicit and hybrid volumetric representations reduce model sizes, yet still require careful coding to reach 2D video-like bitrates. We present CATRF, a standard-codec-in-the-loop compression framework for plane-factorized radiance fields. During training, we quantize and pack 2D feature planes into codec-friendly canvases, run a standard codec roundtrip (JPEG/VP9/HEVC/AV1), then unpack and dequantize the decoded features before volume rendering. We use a straight-through estimator (STE) to insert the non-differentiable, standard codec pipeline into the training loop, allowing radiance-field features to adapt directly to the real, client-side codec distortions without introducing any learned codec parameters. On both static and dynamic benchmarks, CATRF consistently achieves a better rate-distortion trade-off over codec-agnostic and learned-codec-in-the-loop baselines, and also outperforms recent compressed 3DGS methods in both compression efficiency and decoding speed. These results highlight a practical path toward low-bitrate, compression-resilient volumetric representations for free-viewpoint video streaming.

</details>


### [341] [Evaluating the Effect of Compression on Video Temporal Consistency Using Objective Quality Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18378)
*Peter Zsoldos*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper systematically investigates how video compression affects temporal coherence across different codecs and content types, revealing that temporal stability degrades non-linearly with compression and identifying a "predictability anomaly" where unpredictable motion causes more instability than high-magnitude predictable motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize the insufficiently understood relationship between compression intensity and temporal stability, particularly addressing the gap in understanding how temporal coherence errors progress across different bitrate regimes and codecs.

Method: Systematic examination of frame-to-frame coherence errors across multiple bitrate regimes using multiple codecs (AV1, HEVC, VP9, H.264) and diverse content types.

Result: Temporal consistency degrades non-linearly with increasing compression. A "predictability anomaly" is identified where sequences with unpredictable/irregular dynamics suffer disproportionately higher instability than sequences with higher but more predictable motion.

Conclusion: The conventional assumption that motion volume alone dictates encoding difficulty is challenged. The findings highlight the necessity of incorporating temporal-aware metrics in compression pipelines.

Abstract: While video compression algorithms effectively reduce bitrate, aggressive quantization often compromises temporal coherence, introducing artifacts such as flicker, motion inconsistency, and unstable textures. Although spatial quality degradation is well-documented, the relationship between compression intensity and temporal stability remains insufficiently characterized. This paper systematically examines the progression of frame-to-frame coherence errors across different bitrate regimes, utilizing multiple codecs (AV1, HEVC, VP9, H.264) and content types. Our findings reveal that temporal consistency degrades non-linearly with increasing compression. Most critically, we identify a "Predictability anomaly" where sequences with unpredictable or irregular dynamics experience disproportionately higher instability than sequences with higher, but more predictable, motion magnitude. This challenges the conventional assumption that motion volume alone dictates encoding difficulty and highlights the necessity of temporal-aware metrics in compression pipelines.

</details>


### [342] [Mixtac: A Novel Bio-Inspired Hybrid Tactile Sensor with Synergistic Event-Frame Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18550)
*Yihang Li,Yijin Chen,Junkai Xu,Na Ningguta,Peter B. Shull,Shuo Jiang,Bin He*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Mixtac is a hybrid tactile sensor combining event-based and frame-based data streams with a FGER-Net fusion network, achieving high-frequency (500 Hz) force tracking with long-term accuracy (0.04 N MAE) by emulating biological mechanoreceptors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-based tactile sensors have low sampling rates, while event-based sensors drift during long-term static force estimation. This fundamental tradeoff prevents achieving human-level tactile perception for robotic manipulation.

Method: The hybrid event-frame tactile sensor (Mixtac) emulates biological mechanoreceptors. The FGER-Net fuses event data (for high-frequency tracking) and frame data (for long-term accuracy), using frames to correct event drift during training and guide high-frequency predictions during inference.

Result: Achieved Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.04 N for normal force estimation, bridging the sampling rate gap from 0 to 500 Hz.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach overcomes the tradeoff between sampling rate and drift, paving the way for human-level robotic manipulation through improved tactile sensing.

Abstract: Vision based and event based tactile sensors are important in robotic manipulation research. However, they suffer from a fundamental tradeoff: vision based sensors have low sampling rates, while event based sensors are prone to drift during long term static force estimation. To solve this challenge and achieve human level tactile perception, the novel hybrid event frame tactile sensor (Mixtac) is proposed in this paper by emulating the synergistic function of biological mechanoreceptors, which achieves normal force estimation. The prototype leverages events for high frequency force tracking and frames for long term accuracy. The Frame Guided Event Recurrent Network (FGER-Net) was proposed to fuse the two data streams. Frames were used by the net to correct event drift during training and guide high frequency predictions during inference. Experiments demonstrated an MAE of 0.04 N. This paper could bridge the sampling rate gap from 0 to 500 Hz in current vision based tactile sensors and pave the way for human level robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [343] [Dynamic MRI Reconstruction Via Dual Deep Priors and Low-Rank Plus Sparse Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18709)
*Yongliang Sun,Siddhant Gautam,Chaoyan Huang,Nicole Seiberlich,Ismail Alkhouri,Saiprasad Ravishankar*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a structured Deep Image Prior (DIP) framework for dynamic MRI reconstruction that combines low-rank plus sparse (L+S) decomposition with untrained CNNs, optimized via accelerated eADMM, achieving superior performance without requiring large training datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic MRI reconstruction from undersampled measurements is challenging. Learning-based methods need large training datasets and generalize poorly; training-data-free methods like DIP fail to exploit temporal structure and overfit. A method that works without large datasets while capturing spatiotemporal correlations is needed.

Method: The method uses a structured DIP framework with low-rank plus sparse (L+S) decomposition. Two untrained CNNs parameterize the low-rank background and sparse dynamic components separately. Joint optimization is performed using accelerated extrapolated ADMM (eADMM), combining implicit DIP regularization with classical L+S interpretability.

Result: The proposed method consistently outperforms classical reconstruction and existing supervised and unsupervised MRI reconstruction techniques across various acceleration factors.

Conclusion: The structured DIP with L+S decomposition and eADMM optimization provides effective dynamic MRI reconstruction without needing large training datasets, with theoretical convergence guarantees to critical points.

Abstract: Dynamic MRI reconstruction from undersampled measurements is a challenging inverse problem that requires preserving both spatial reconstruction quality and temporal consistency across the frames of the cine series. While recent learning-based approaches achieve strong performance, they heavily rely on large training, mostly fully sampled, datasets, and may otherwise generalize poorly. In contrast, training-data-free methods such as deep image prior (DIP) adapt directly to individual scans but often fail to fully exploit temporal structure and are prone to overfitting. They are particularly attractive for dynamic MRI due to the limited large, public, high-quality datasets. In this work, we propose a structured DIP framework for dynamic MRI reconstruction that explicitly models spatiotemporal correlations through a low-rank plus sparse (L+S) decomposition. Instead of directly reconstructing the cine image series, we parameterize the low-rank background and sparse dynamic components using two DIP untrained convolutional neural networks, jointly optimized using accelerated extrapolated ADMM (eADMM). This formulation combines the implicit regularization of DIP with the interpretability of classical L+S regularization. We provide a convergence analysis for the proposed eADMM algorithm in the presence of DIP-based nonconvex parameterizations. In particular, we establish a sufficient descent property and show that every cluster point of the generated sequence is a critical point of the associated Lyapunov function. Across various acceleration factors, our numerical results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms classical reconstruction and existing supervised and unsupervised MRI reconstruction techniques.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [344] [Haptic Rendering of Fractional-Order Viscoelasticity: Passivity and Rendering Fidelity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16389)
*Gorkem Gemalmaz,Harun Tolasa,Volkan Patoglu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a unified theoretical framework for passivity analysis of fractional-order viscoelastic models (specifically FO-SLS) under finite-memory discretization, deriving closed-form passivity conditions and effective stiffness/damping expressions, with experimental validation and human-subject evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Haptic rendering of viscoelastic materials (exhibiting creep and stress relaxation) is critical for applications like medical training. Fractional-order models capture time-dependent dynamics with few parameters, but their passivity and rendering performance under finite-memory discretization needed systematic analysis.

Method: Derived closed-form passivity conditions for FO-SLS models using Grunwald-Letnikov derivative with short-memory discretization; provided symbolic expressions for effective stiffness and damping; conducted experimental validation and human-subject studies.

Result: The passivity conditions generalize previously reported integer-order (Kelvin-Voigt, Maxwell, SLS) results as special cases. Experimental validations confirmed theoretical passivity bounds, and human-subject evaluations assessed perceived realism.

Conclusion: Establishes a unified theoretical framework for FO viscoelastic rendering under short-memory discretization, validated both technically and perceptually.

Abstract: Haptic rendering of viscoelastic materials that exhibit creep and stress relaxation is crucial for many applications, such as medical training with realistic biological tissue models. Fractional-order viscoelastic models provide an effective means of describing intrinsically time-dependent dynamics with few parameters, as these models can naturally capture memory effects. In this study, we present analyses of passivity and rendering performance for fractional-order viscoelastic models under finite-memory discretization. We derive closed-form expressions to ensure the passivity of haptic rendering with a fractional-order (FO) standard linear solid (SLS) model based on Grunwald-Letnikov derivative under short-memory discretization. We also provide symbolic expressions for the effective stiffness and damping of such FO-SLS models. The resulting passivity conditions constitute a unified framework that generalizes previously reported results for integer-order Kelvin-Voigt, Maxwell, and SLS models, since these results are special cases of the newly derived condition. Furthermore, we provide experimental validations of the theoretical passivity bounds and human-subject evaluations of perceived realism of FO-SLS models. Overall, this study establishes a unified theoretical framework and experimental evaluations for FO viscoelastic rendering under short-memory discretization.

</details>


### [345] [OrbiSim: World Models as Differentiable Physics Engines for Embodied Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16395)
*Jiajian Li,Jingyuan Huang,Junru Gong,Qi Wang,Xiaokang Yang,Yunbo Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OrbiSim is a fully differentiable physics engine that unifies structured scene assets, neural dynamics, and RL into an end-to-end differentiable simulation loop, outperforming SOTA world models in predictive fidelity and control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing world models focus on unconstrained imagination in latent/visual domains and lack a physically-grounded, differentiable pathway that bridges structured assets, neural dynamics, and downstream RL policy training.

Method: OrbiSim builds a unified, fully differentiable simulation loop spanning explicit state transitions to visual observation generation, enabling end-to-end differentiability for contact modeling, gradient-based policy optimization, and physical inference.

Result: OrbiSim significantly outperforms state-of-the-art world models in both predictive fidelity and control performance, and shows consistent responsiveness to asset/parameter configurations.

Conclusion: OrbiSim demonstrates strong potential as a differentiable tool for enhancing robot simulation and policy training, enabling tasks intractable for classical simulators.

Abstract: We present OrbiSim, a novel robotic simulation paradigm that redefines world models as a fully differentiable physics engine for embodied intelligence. Unlike prior world models that focus on unconstrained imagination in latent or visual domains, OrbiSim establishes a unified, physically-grounded pathway that bridges structured scene assets, neural dynamics, and downstream reinforcement learning. By enabling end-to-end differentiability throughout the entire simulation loop -- spanning from explicit state transitions to visual observation generation -- OrbiSim supports tasks traditionally intractable for classical simulators, such as differentiable contact modeling, gradient-based policy optimization under sparse rewards, and intuitive physical inference. Empirical results demonstrate that OrbiSim significantly outperforms state-of-the-art world models in both predictive fidelity and control performance. Furthermore, its consistent responsiveness to asset configurations and physical parameters suggests its potential as a differentiable tool for enhancing robot simulation and policy training.

</details>


### [346] [Support-Safe Variational Hybrid Filtering for Contact-Mode and Sparse-Law Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16398)
*Marios Papamichalis,Regina Ruane*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VHYDRO is a variational hybrid dynamics learner that prevents branch loss in contact-rich robot dynamics by mixing learned proposals with feasible transition laws, jointly inferring continuous latent states and discrete contact modes, and fitting sparse port-Hamiltonian laws to each regime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contact-rich robot dynamics are hybrid (mixing free, impact, stick-slip regimes), and standard amortized filters permanently lose the correct branch when they place zero probability on feasible contact transitions.

Method: VHYDRO mixes the learned proposal with a feasible transition law before sampling and importance weighting, jointly infers continuous latent state and discrete contact mode, and fits a sparse port-Hamiltonian law to each regime.

Result: Three guarantees show support coverage stabilizes filtering, filters concentrate discrete contact posteriors, and mode-pure segments enable sparse recovery. Empirically: works under heavy occlusion, yields coherent contact-regime segments on ManiSkill/Sawyer/BridgeData, and recovers active physical terms on known hybrid systems.

Conclusion: The support-safe filter prevents branch loss and outperforms post-hoc and mode-free baselines across ARI, change-point F1, and segment purity metrics.

Abstract: Contact-rich robot dynamics are hybrid: a single observation can match several latent states and contact regimes (free, impact, stick--slip). A standard amortized filter that places no probability on a feasible contact transition will permanently lose the branch the robot actually follows. We introduce VHYDRO, a variational hybrid dynamics learner that prevents this branch loss. At each step, VHYDRO mixes the learned proposal with a feasible transition law before sampling and importance weighting, ensuring that every transition retained by the model-feasible carrier remains covered. VHYDRO jointly infers a continuous latent state and a discrete contact mode, and fits a sparse port-Hamiltonian law to each recovered regime. On top of this, three guarantees connect: support coverage stabilizes filtering, the stabilized filter concentrates the discrete contact posterior on coherent regimes, and mode-pure segments admit sparse port-Hamiltonian recovery. The recovery error separates cleanly into filtering, derivative, mode-impurity, and physics-residual parts. Three empirical findings track the same mechanism. Under heavy occlusion the support-safe filter stays usable while a non-defensive proposal collapses. On ManiSkill demonstrations and on four Sawyer/BridgeData task families the discrete state forms temporally coherent contact-regime segments that the discrete state yields a stronger joint profile across ARI, change-point F1, and segment purity than post-hoc and mode-free baselines. On hybrid systems with known equations the mode-conditioned sparse fit recovers the active physical terms; purely predictive baselines do not.

</details>


### [347] [SCAR: Self-Supervised Continuous Action Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16412)
*Hongjia Liu,Fan Feng,Minghao Fu,Xinyue Wang,Haofei Lu,Biwei Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCAR is a joint inverse-forward dynamics framework that learns unified, transferable action representations across different embodiments from visual transitions, using regularization and adversarial invariance to suppress embodiment-specific factors, achieving better cross-embodiment adaptation and cross-task transfer than raw actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning transferable action representations from visual transitions is challenging, especially when world models must generalize across different embodiments under limited data. The authors argue that action should be treated as a distinct representational factor that decouples controllable change from embodiment-specific actuation.

Method: SCAR uses a pretrained generative backbone with an inverse dynamics model (IDM) to infer latent actions from latent observation pairs, and a forward dynamics model (FDM) to predict future dynamics. It regularizes the latent action posterior toward a standard Gaussian prior and introduces adversarial invariance to suppress embodiment- and environment-specific nuisance factors.

Result: On Procgen and Robotwin datasets, SCAR's learned unified latent action representation outperforms embodiment-specific raw actions as a conditioning interface, enabling improved cross-embodiment low-data adaptation and cross-task transfer.

Conclusion: Action can be learned as a shared representation of controllable change across embodiments, providing a more transferable and generalizable interface for world models.

Abstract: Despite the central role of action in embodied intelligence, learning transferable action representations from visual transitions remains a fundamental challenge, particularly when world models must generalize across embodiments under limited data. We argue that action is not merely an auxiliary conditioning signal, but a distinct representational factor that decouples the controllable change from embodiment-specific actuation. In this work, we propose SCAR, a joint inverse-forward dynamics framework for learning unified action representations across embodiments from visual transitions. Built on a pretrained generative backbone, SCAR uses an inverse dynamics model (IDM) to infer latent actions from latent observation pairs and a forward dynamics model (FDM) to predict future dynamics conditioned on them. To make the latent space transferable rather than a generic visual bottleneck, we regularize the latent action posterior toward a standard Gaussian prior to limit arbitrary visual encoding, and introduce adversarial invariance to suppress embodiment- and environment-specific nuisance factors. Experiments on the Procgen and Robotwin dataset show that the learned unified latent action representation serves as a stronger conditioning interface for world modeling than embodiment-specific raw actions, yielding improved cross-embodiment low-data adaptation and cross-task transfer. Taken together, these results suggest that action can be learned as a shared representation of controllable change across embodiments, providing an interface for more transferable and generalizable world models.

</details>


### [348] [MR-SLAM: Immersive Spatial Supervision for Multi-Robot Mapping via Mixed Reality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16432)
*Prakash Aryan,Cem Erdogdu,Kavinaya Kumarchokkappan,Timo Kehrer,Sebastiano Panichella*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MR-SLAM is a mixed reality system using a Meta Quest 3 headset to teleoperate and monitor three simulated TurtleBot3 robots performing multi-robot SLAM, achieving real-time occupancy grid merging, 94.7% cross-instance consistency, and 26.7 m² coverage on consumer hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operating multi-robot SLAM fleets for applications like building inspection or warehouse monitoring requires operators to maintain spatial awareness of each robot's position and mapping state, which scales poorly on conventional 2D interfaces.

Method: An operator wears a Meta Quest 3 headset to teleoperate three simulated TurtleBot3 robots via passthrough view with real-world occlusion, while spatially anchored dashboard panels show mapping progress. Each robot runs an independent SLAM Toolbox instance, and occupancy grids are merged in real time on a ROS 2 back end.

Result: Across five 9-minute sessions: scans at 8.83 ± 0.16 Hz, 17.9 ± 0.8 m² merged occupancy, 94.7 ± 0.5% cross-instance occupancy consistency. Additional session: 6.3 ms median transform jitter, 26.7 m² coverage of a 41 m² grid.

Conclusion: MR-SLAM is positioned as a reference implementation for combining passthrough mixed reality supervision with multi-robot SLAM on consumer hardware.

Abstract: Operating a multi-robot fleet for simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) in applications such as building inspection or warehouse-aisle monitoring requires the operator to maintain spatial awareness of each robot's position and mapping state, a task that scales poorly on conventional 2D interfaces. We present MR-SLAM, a mixed reality (MR) system in which an operator wearing a Meta Quest 3 headset teleoperates three simulated TurtleBot3 robots through a passthrough view with real-world occlusion, while spatially anchored dashboard panels report mapping progress in situ.
