Each robot runs an independent SLAM Toolbox instance whose occupancy grid is merged in real time on a Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) back end. Across five 9-minute evaluation sessions, the system delivered scans at 8.83 +/- 0.16 Hz, mapped 17.9 +/- 0.8 m^2 of merged occupancy, and reached 94.7 +/- 0.5% cross-instance occupancy consistency across robot pairs. An additional session recorded 6.3 ms median transform jitter and 26.7 m^2 coverage of a 41 m^2 grid. We position MR-SLAM as a reference implementation for combining passthrough mixed reality supervision with multi-robot SLAM on consumer hardware.

</details>


### [349] [Hierarchical Two-Stage Framework for Environment-Aware Long-Horizon Vessel Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16442)
*Ganeshaaraj Gnanavel,Tharindu Fernando,Sridha Sridharan,Clinton Fookes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical two-stage vessel trajectory forecasting framework combining coarse long-term and grid-aware short-term predictors with environmental modules, achieving 25% ADE and 17% FDE improvements over state-of-the-art on Australian CTS data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon vessel trajectory forecasting under real ocean conditions is critical for collision avoidance, traffic management, and route planning, but is challenged by long-range temporal dependencies and dynamic environmental factors (currents, wind, waves).

Method: A hierarchical two-stage framework with: (1) coarse long-term predictor + grid-aware short-term predictor via hierarchical fusion; (2) Spatio-Temporal Graph Transformer on discretized maritime cells; (3) environmental module using cross-modal attention and feature-wise modulation; (4) learnable Savitzky-Golay smoothing layer.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art by 25% in ADE and 17% in FDE on Australian CTS data (NW region) with 3h input and 10h prediction horizon; ablation studies validate each component's contribution.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical two-stage framework effectively addresses long-horizon vessel trajectory forecasting under dynamic ocean conditions, demonstrating significant improvements over existing methods.

Abstract: Long-horizon vessel trajectory forecasting under real ocean conditions is critical for collision avoidance, traffic management, and route planning. However, achieving accurate predictions is challenging due to long-range temporal dependencies and dynamic environmental factors such as currents, wind, and waves. To address these issues, we propose a hierarchical two-stage framework that combines a coarse long-term predictor with a grid-aware short-term predictor through a hierarchical fusion mechanism. The short-term branch leverages a Spatio-Temporal Graph Transformer on discretized maritime cells to capture localized dynamics, while the long-term branch encodes overarching navigational intent. An integrated environmental module incorporates oceanographic parameters, including surface currents, wind vectors, and significant wave height, using cross-modal attention and feature-wise modulation for adaptive response to varying sea conditions. Additionally, a learnable Savitzky-Golay smoothing layer enhances temporal coherence in fused trajectories. We evaluate our approach on Australian Craft Tracking System (CTS) data from the North West region, aligned with Copernicus Marine Service products, using a 3-hour input and a 10-hour prediction horizon. Experimental results show that our framework outperforms the state-of-the-art by 25% in Average Displacement Error (ADE) and 17% in Final Displacement Error (FDE). Ablation studies further validate the contribution of each component.

</details>


### [350] [No Plan, Yet Human: A Reactive Robotics Model Predicts Human Planning Failures on a Clinical Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16514)
*Michael Migacev,Vito Mengers,Antonia Köngeter,Oliver Brock*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AICON, a reactive gradient-descent framework originally for robotic manipulation, reproduces human difficulty ordering on Tower of London planning problems and captures failure modes of patients with reduced planning capacity (e.g., Parkinson's), outperforming planning baselines for impaired groups while planning baselines better model healthy controls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why some sequential planning problems are harder than others, requiring models that capture specific difficulty patterns and failure modes mirroring humans with reduced planning capacity.

Method: Applied AICON (reactive gradient-descent framework, no lookahead planning) to Tower of London test; compared with structural task parameters and planning baseline; evaluated on human difficulty ordering across 24 problems and held-out generalization via leave-two-out evaluation.

Result: AICON reproduces fine-grained human difficulty ordering better than structural parameters, generalizes to held-out problems, and outperforms a planning baseline for groups with reduced planning capacity, while the planning baseline better captures healthy controls.

Conclusion: As planning capacity is reduced, human behavior shifts toward the reactive mode AICON models; AICON captures biological behavior across perception, eye movements, and sequential planning, suggesting its core abstraction reflects real organizational principles of biological systems.

Abstract: Understanding why some sequential planning problems are harder than others requires models that go beyond average performance. They should capture the specific pattern of which problems are hard, and ideally fail in the same way people do when planning capacity is reduced. We apply AICON, a reactive gradient-descent framework developed for robotic manipulation, to the Tower of London test, a cognitive test used to assess planning in Parkinson's disease, mild cognitive impairment, and stroke. Without any lookahead planning or knowledge of human cognition, AICON reproduces the fine-grained human difficulty ordering across 24 problems better than structural task parameters and generalizes to held-out problems in a leave-two-out evaluation. Crucially, AICON outperforms a planning baseline for groups with reduced planning capacity while the planning baseline better captures healthy controls. This dissociation was predicted by the original AICON paper, which noted that the model's failure modes resemble those of Parkinson's patients who struggle with goal hierarchies but not move counts. This suggests that as planning capacity is reduced, human behavior shifts toward the reactive mode AICON models. The finding extends a broader pattern: AICON, originally built for robotics, now captures aspects of biological behavior across perception, eye movements, and sequential planning, suggesting its core abstraction reflects something real about how biological systems are organized.

</details>


### [351] [A Mechanistic Model for Collective Motion from Sensorimotor Regularities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16522)
*Vito Mengers,Bao Duc Cao,Oliver Brock*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a sensorimotor model of collective animal behavior, grounded in what individuals can perceive and execute, demonstrating that collective behaviors emerge from interacting sensorimotor regularities without prescribed interaction forces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-propelled particle models are descriptive and leave open how collective behavior is actually produced. Empirical evidence (e.g., locusts not aligning with neighbors) shows the gap, necessitating a mechanistic model at the sensorimotor level.

Method: The authors extend a robotics-based modeling framework where each agent perceives neighbors via bearing and apparent-size cues within a limited field of view, maintains uncertain internal state estimates, and selects actions via gradient descent on desired social distance — without prescribed interaction forces.

Result: The model produces polarized motion, milling, ring formations, and subgroup fragmentation. Global sensitivity analysis reveals behavioral transitions are governed by sensorimotor parameters (field of view geometry, sensory noise, turning agility, memory).

Conclusion: Collective behavior emerges from interacting sensorimotor regularities; species differences arise from differences in embodiment and environment.

Abstract: Collective behavior in animals has long been modeled through self-propelled particle models, which reproduce striking group-level phenomena through abstract interaction forces. Yet these models are fundamentally descriptive: they leave open the question of how collective behavior is actually produced. Recent empirical work makes this gap concrete: locusts do not align with neighbors, sensory and cognitive mechanisms mediate interaction instead. A mechanistic model must therefore operate at the sensorimotor level, grounded in what individual organisms can actually perceive, estimate, and physically execute. We present such a model based on a modeling framework from robotics, extended here to collective motion. Each agent perceives neighbors through bearing and apparent-size cues within a limited field of view, maintains uncertain internal state estimates, and selects actions through gradient descent on a desired social distance -- without any prescribed interaction forces. This simple model produces diverse collective behaviors including polarized motion, milling, ring formations, and subgroup fragmentation. A global sensitivity analysis shows that behavioral transitions are governed by sensorimotor parameters corresponding to measurable biological quantities: field of view geometry, sensory noise, turning agility, and memory. Collective behavior can therefore be understood as the emergent outcome of interacting sensorimotor regularities, and differences across species as the emergent outcome of differences in embodiment and environment.

</details>


### [352] [Nori Bot: A Sub-$1,000 Floor-to-Counter Mobile Manipulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16537)
*Antonio Li,Sungjoon Park,Wen Ni Chew*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Nori Bot is a $947 17-DoF dual-arm mobile manipulator that addresses three key limitations of sub-$1,000 platforms: fixed-height workspace, reactive-only control, and servo burn-out from stall conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing low-cost open-source mobile manipulators (e.g., XLeRobot at $660) suffer from three limitations: fixed-height workspace, reactive-only control, and no stall-induced burn-out protection for cheap Feetech servos.

Method: Nori Bot integrates (1) a 600mm Z-axis lift on the existing servo bus, (2) a thin-client Raspberry Pi 4 with the OpenClaw proactive agent runtime for autonomous task triggering, and (3) a software safety stack using motor current-based sensorless grip-force feedback on a soft TPU finger.

Result: Achieves a 17-DoF dual-arm mobile manipulator at $947 (~3% the cost of comparable commercial platforms) with floor-to-counter reach, autonomous proactive control, and stall protection.

Conclusion: Nori Bot demonstrates that key limitations of ultra-low-cost manipulators can be overcome with clever hardware-software co-design. Code, CAD, and skill manifest will be released.

Abstract: Open-source mobile manipulators have reached $660 (XLeRobot) but every sub-$1,000 platform shares three limitations: a fixed-height workspace, reactive-only control, and no protection against the stall-induced burn-out that destroys cheap Feetech servos. We present Nori Bot, a 17-DoF dual-arm mobile manipulator at $947 (~3% the cost of comparable commercial platforms) that addresses all three: (1) a 600mm Z-axis lift on the existing servo bus for floor-to-counter reach; (2) a thin-client Raspberry Pi 4 paired with the OpenClaw proactive agent runtime so cron jobs and hooks trigger physical tasks autonomously; and (3) a software safety stack with sensorless grip-force feedback via motor current on a soft TPU finger. Code, CAD, and the skill manifest will be released.

</details>


### [353] [Policy Library CBF: Finite-Horizon Safety at Runtime via Parallel Rollouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16588)
*Taekyung Kim,Hideki Okamoto,Bardh Hoxha,Georgios Fainekos,Dimitra Panagou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes PL-CBF, a runtime safety filter that selects the safest fallback policy from a library to minimally modify a nominal policy, proving finite-horizon safety coverage and demonstrating millisecond-level runtime across various robotic domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical autonomy in unstructured environments faces challenges for online safety certification under evolving constraints, where single-policy safety filters are insufficient.

Method: PL-CBF evaluates a library of fallback policies via parallel finite-horizon rollouts, selects the least invasive safe mode, and enforces safety via a quadratic program that minimally modifies a nominal policy. Theoretical analysis uses a finite-horizon language metric over closed-loop behaviors.

Result: Simulations on planar double-integrator (4 states), highway driving with friction changes (8 states), and 3D quadrotor navigation (12 states) show improved safety coverage over single-policy filters with millisecond-level runtime.

Conclusion: PL-CBF achieves better safety coverage than single-policy approaches while maintaining real-time feasibility, making it suitable for safety-critical autonomy in dynamic unstructured environments.

Abstract: Safety-critical autonomy in unstructured environments poses significant challenges for online safety certification under evolving constraints. We propose Policy Library Control Barrier Function~(PL-CBF), a runtime safety filter that evaluates a library of fallback policies via parallel finite-horizon rollouts, selects the least invasive safe mode, and enforces safety by solving a quadratic program that minimally modifies a nominal policy. We provide a theoretical analysis based on a finite-horizon language metric over closed-loop behaviors, characterizing policy-library coverage requirements for certifying finite-horizon safety. Simulations on a planar double-integrator (4 states), highway driving with abrupt friction changes using a realistic nonlinear vehicle model (8 states), and 3D quadrotor navigation in crowded dynamic environments (12 states) demonstrate improved safety coverage over single-policy safety filters while retaining millisecond-level runtime.

</details>


### [354] [Bayesian Networks for Path-Based Sensors: Gathering Information and Path Planning in Communication Denied Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16673)
*Alkesh K. Srivastava,George P. Kontoudis,Donald Sofge,Michael Otte*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A new Bayesian Network-based method for updating belief maps from path-based sensor observations enables faster convergence than prior averaging-based approaches in both single- and multi-robot hazard detection scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Path-based sensors (e.g., boolean sensors returning 1 if an event is detected anywhere along a path) produce observations that lack spatial specificity about where events occur. Prior methods approximate the posterior by averaging over alternative event histories, which is suboptimal. The authors aim to develop a more principled Bayesian approach for belief map updates.

Method: The authors introduce a Bayesian Network (BN) formulation that explicitly models probabilistic relationships between latent variables (event locations) and path-based sensor measurements. This enables a principled Bayesian belief update. They also plan paths to maximize information gain using Shannon information theory. The method handles false positives and false negatives. The problem is set in static hazard detection in communication-denied environments, where robot return = sensor reading 0 (no hazard) and robot failure = reading 1 (hazard detected).

Result: The new BN-based method achieves quicker convergence of the belief map compared to prior work in both single-robot and multi-robot scenarios.

Conclusion: The Bayesian Network formulation provides a more principled and effective approach for updating belief maps from path-based sensor observations, leading to faster convergence and improved information gathering.

Abstract: A "path-based sensor" produces a single observation along a continuous path. For example, a boolean path-based sensor returns a single "1" if an event of interest is detected at any point along the path and a "0" otherwise. Notably, a "1" provides no direct information about where along the path the event(s) may have occurred. Previous work has demonstrated that observations from multiple path-based sensors can be fused to create a Bayesian belief map over the spatial locations of the underlying event or phenomenon. Moreover, path planning can employ Shannon information theory to accelerate the rate of convergence of the belief map. In this paper, we present a new method to update the belief map based on a path-based sensor observation, and then plan paths to increase information gain. In contrast to prior work that approximates the posterior by averaging over the alternative event histories, we introduce a Bayesian Network (BN) formulation that models the probabilistic relationships between the latent variables and path-based sensor measurements, enabling a more principled Bayesian belief update. We consider static hazard detection in a communication-denied environment as a representative problem setting. The event of a robot returning from its path corresponds to a path-based hazard sensor reading of "0" (hazard not detected), while a robot failing to return corresponds to a reading of "1" (hazard detected). We consider false positives and false negatives. We find that the new method leads to quicker convergence of the belief map than prior work in both single- and multi-robot cases.

</details>


### [355] [DriveSafer: End-to-End Autonomous Driving with Safety Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16737)
*Shounak Sural,Raj Rajkumar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DriveSafer is a safety framework that reduces catastrophic planning failures in generative end-to-end autonomous driving models by 48%, using training-time safety constraints and inference-time safety guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern generative E2E planners suffer from catastrophic failures in safety-critical scenarios due to violations of physical constraints and safety requirements. The authors aim to improve safety outcomes by targeting the reduction of these catastrophic failures rather than enhancing average planning quality.

Method: DriveSafer is a failure-aware safety framework that steers generative planners towards safe behaviors using both training-time safety constraints and inference-time safety guidance.

Result: DriveSafer reduces catastrophic failures (PDMS=0) by 48% and reduces drivable-area compliance failures by over 65% compared to DiffusionDrive on the NAVSIM benchmark.

Conclusion: Targeted reduction of catastrophic planning failures through safety-focused frameworks can significantly improve safety outcomes in end-to-end autonomous driving without focusing on enhancing average planning quality.

Abstract: End-to-End (E2E) autonomous driving models have shown growing capability in recent years, with performance improving on increasingly challenging benchmarks. However, modern generative E2E planners still suffer from a substantial number of catastrophic failures in safety-critical scenarios. We find that many such failures arise from violations of physical constraints and safety requirements, leading to unsafe behavior. Motivated by this finding, in this paper, we focus on improving safety outcomes in generative end-to-end driving with a targeted reduction of catastrophic planning failures, instead of enhancing average planning quality. Towards this end, we propose DriveSafer, a failure-aware safety framework for end-to-end planners. DriveSafer explicitly steers generative planners towards safe behaviors leveraging both training-time safety constraints and inference-time safety guidance. Compared to the state-of-the-art DiffusionDrive model, on the NAVSIM benchmark, DriveSafer reduces the number of catastrophic failures (PDMS=0) by 48%, with over 65% reduction in drivable-area compliance failures.

</details>


### [356] [LACE: Latent Visual Representation for Cross-Embodiment Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16743)
*Yoo Sung Jang,Kanchana Ranasinghe,Cristina Mata,Yichi Zhang,Jorge Mendez-Mendez,Michael S. Ryoo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LACE aligns human and robot visual representations using sparse supervision from shared body parts, enabling robot policies to leverage human demonstration data when robot data is scarce.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-embodiment learning from human demonstrations is hindered by the visual gap between human and robot embodiments; existing SSL backbones fail to establish correspondence between human and robot hands.

Method: LACE aligns human and robot visual representations in latent space using correspondences between shared body parts (obtained via forward kinematics). A semantic alignment loss matches distributions of corresponding features for patch-level to semantic-level alignment, while a Gram loss preserves pretrained feature quality.

Result: In zero-shot transfer, policies using LACE-DINO outperform those using DINO by 65%, with consistent gains in low-data regimes and out-of-distribution environments.

Conclusion: Single robot demonstration is sufficient to train the model, and this alignment enables robot policies to effectively leverage abundant human data when robot demonstrations are scarce.

Abstract: Cross-embodiment learning from human demonstrations is hindered by the visual gap between human and robot embodiments. While self-supervised learning (SSL) backbones encode rich inter-class semantics of general objects, we show they fail to establish correspondence between human and robot hands. We propose LACE, a framework that aligns human and robot visual representations in the latent space of these backbones by leveraging correspondences between shared body parts across embodiments as sparse supervision. These annotations can be automatically obtained via forward kinematics, and single robot demonstration is sufficient to train the model. Our semantic alignment loss matches distributions incurred by corresponding features, lifting patch-level supervision to semantic-level alignment, while a Gram loss preserves pretrained feature quality. This alignment enables robot policies to leverage abundant human data when robot demonstrations are scarce: in zero-shot transfer, policies using LACE-DINO outperform those using DINO by a large margin (65\%), with consistent gains in low-data regimes and out-of-distribution environments.

</details>


### [357] ["I'm Not Mad, Just Focused'': Understanding Human Emotions in Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16816)
*Seung Chan Hong,Dana Kulić,Leimin Tian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a VLM-based emotion recognition system that uses contextual understanding to improve HRC, showing better alignment with human annotations and user preference for emotion-adaptive robot behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ER models in HRC are limited by reliance on acted datasets and single-modality inputs (e.g., facial expressions), lacking contextual understanding.

Method: Proposes a VLM-based ER system leveraging contextual understanding; evaluated via semantic/sentiment similarity with human annotations on an HRC dataset, and a user study with a service robot in a collaborative delivery task.

Result: The VLM-ER system achieves higher semantic similarity and positive sentiment alignment than a CNN baseline. Users preferred emotion-adaptive robot behavior facilitated by the system.

Conclusion: VLM-based ER with contextual understanding enhances emotion interpretation and user satisfaction in HRC, outperforming traditional single-modality approaches.

Abstract: Human-robot collaboration (HRC) can benefit from robots' abilities to interpret human emotional states. However, current emotion recognition (ER) models in HRC often fall short, particularly due to their reliance on acted datasets and single-modality inputs like facial expressions. We propose a novel vision language model (VLM)-based ER system that leverages contextual understanding to improve emotion interpretation in HRC. We first evaluate the VLM-ER system by assessing its semantic and sentiment similarity with human annotations on an existing HRC dataset. Then, in a user study with a service robot in a collaborative delivery task, we evaluate the effects of modulating the robot's behaviour based on the user's emotional state inferred by the VLM-ER system. The results show that the proposed VLM-ER system achieves higher semantic similarity and positive sentiment alignment with human annotations compared to a baseline convolutional neural network-based system. Further, participants in the user study preferred emotion-adaptive robot behaviour facilitated by the VLM-ER system.

</details>


### [358] [Pedestrian-Aware LLM-Driven Behavioral Planning for Autonomous Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16858)
*Aidana Baimbetova,Haruki Yonekura,Hamada Rizk,Hirozumi Yamaguchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes an LLM-based decision-making framework for autonomous vehicles in dense pedestrian environments. It converts scene observations into natural language prompts for the LLM to infer intent and generate cautious driving decisions. The framework achieves 68% success rate zero-shot (vs 17.7% for RL), 96% with few-shot memory, and outperforms DQN in cross-behavior generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL-based AV control systems struggle to generalize to unpredictable pedestrian behaviors and out-of-distribution scenarios, rely on handcrafted rewards, and produce opaque decisions, making them unsuitable for safety-critical pedestrian-rich environments.

Method: A framework that converts structured scene observations into natural-language reasoning prompts. An LLM infers pedestrian intent, anticipates risk, and generates tactical driving decisions, which are then executed by a motion planner for smooth, kinematically feasible control. Tested in SUMO with zero-shot and few-shot memory variants.

Result: Zero-shot: 68% collision-free success (vs 17.7% for deep RL). Few-shot (single pedestrian): 96.0% (vs DQN 82.0%). Cross-behavior transfer: turn-back memory transfers to hesitation (82%) and bidirectional crossing (90%). The system also initiates earlier responses and maintains wider safety buffers.

Conclusion: LLM-based decision-making significantly outperforms deep RL baselines in pedestrian-rich AV scenarios, generalizes across unseen behaviors via episodic memory, and produces interpretable, human-aligned decisions, highlighting a promising direction for safety-critical autonomous driving.

Abstract: Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) must make reliable decisions in dense urban environments where pedestrian behavior is variable, sometimes abnormal, and often unseen during training. Reinforcement learning (RL)-based AV control systems perform well in structured traffic but struggle to generalize to unpredictable pedestrian interactions and out-of-distribution scenarios. Their reliance on handcrafted rewards and opaque decisions further limits their suitability for safety-critical, pedestrian-rich environments. To address these limitations, we introduce a Large Language Model (LLM)-based decision-making framework for pedestrian-aware behavioral planning. The system converts structured scene observations into natural-language reasoning prompts, enabling the LLM to infer pedestrian intent, anticipate risk, and generate cautious tactical driving decisions. These decisions are executed by a motion planner that ensures smooth, kinematically feasible control. We evaluate the framework in SUMO across multiple pedestrian-interaction scenarios, including unexpected jaywalking, turn-back crossing, hesitation, and bidirectional crossing. In zero-shot evaluation, the LLM-based agent achieves a 68% collision-free success rate, substantially outperforming deep RL baselines (17.7%). With few-shot episodic memory in a single-pedestrian scenario, performance increases to 96.0%, exceeding a custom DQN controller (82.0%). Cross-behavior evaluation further shows that memory derived from turn-back interactions transfers to unseen hesitation and bidirectional crossing scenarios, achieving 82.0% and 90.0% success, respectively. The system consistently initiates earlier responses, maintains wider safety buffers, and produces interpretable, human-aligned decisions.

</details>


### [359] [Plan First, Diffuse Later: Extrinsic Graph Guidance for Long-Horizon Diffusion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16863)
*Yaniv Hassidof,Adir Morgan,Yilun Du,Kiril Solovey*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: XDiffuser improves long-horizon planning in diffusion models by using extrinsic search (outside denoising) with a lightweight graph-based planner to guide trajectory generation, outperforming intrinsic search methods especially on low-quality data and unseen combinatorial tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compositional diffusion models for long-horizon planning suffer from poor global coherence, and existing intrinsic search methods are computationally expensive due to repeated model evaluations.

Method: XDiffuser performs extrinsic search by first computing a plan over a state-space graph (lightweight local connectivity oracle), then uses this plan to guide the denoising process for a single trajectory, offloading exploration outside the diffusion model.

Result: Outperforms diffusion-based baselines on long-horizon tasks, with large gains in low-quality data regimes and on unseen tasks beyond goal-reaching (multi-agent coordination, TSP-style reasoning).

Conclusion: Extrinsic search is a more effective and efficient exploration alternative for diffusion-based planning, enabling classical algorithms to solve unseen combinatorial tasks at test time.

Abstract: Compositional diffusion models offer a promising route to long-horizon planning by denoising multiple overlapping sub-trajectories while ensuring that together they constitute a global solution. However, enforcing local behavior over long chains is often insufficient for a coherent global structure to emerge. Recent works tackle this limitation through intrinsic search, which explores multiple paths during the denoising process. While intrinsic search improves global coherence, it comes at the cost of repeated evaluations of an already compute-heavy model. In this work, we argue that extrinsic search, performed outside the denoising process, offers a more effective mode of exploration for long-horizon planning while naturally enabling the use of classical algorithms to solve unseen combinatorial tasks at test time. Our eXtrinsic search-guided Diffuser (XDiffuser) first computes a plan over a state-space graph -- serving as a lightweight local connectivity oracle for the diffusion model. The plan is then used to guide denoising for a single trajectory, effectively offloading the burden of exploration. XDiffuser outperforms diffusion-based baselines on long-horizon tasks, with particularly large gains in the low-quality data regime and on unseen tasks beyond goal-reaching, including multi-agent coordination and TSP-style reasoning. Project website: https://yanivhass.github.io/XDiffuser-site/

</details>


### [360] [SSTL: Self-Sensing Tendon Loop for Hysteresis Modeling and Compensation in Tendon-Sheath Mechanisms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16870)
*Myeongbo Park,Junhyun Park,Ihsan Ullah,Chunggil An,Minho Hwang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A double-pass tendon loop (SSTL) is proposed to measure input-output tension profiles proximally, enabling learning-based hysteresis compensation for flexible endoscopic robots, reducing RMSE by 88.1% versus uncompensated baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tendon-sheath mechanisms (TSMs) in flexible endoscopic robots suffer from configuration-dependent hysteresis caused by friction and elasticity, creating a systematic discrepancy between proximal actuation and distal output that varies with insertion tube configuration.

Method: The Self-Sensing Tendon Loop (SSTL) is a double-pass tendon loop routed through the insertion tube and around a distal pulley, returning to the proximal end. Both input and output tensions are measured proximally. A learning-based mapping uses the SSTL tension profile to estimate hysteresis parameters of the actuation TSM, which are then used by a feedforward controller for compensation.

Result: Across sinusoidal and random trajectories under three insertion tube configurations, the method reduces average RMSE by 88.1% compared to uncompensated baseline, achieving 97.8% of the performance of direct identification requiring direct measurement of input/output tension profiles.

Conclusion: The SSTL effectively estimates configuration-dependent hysteresis without distal sensors, achieving near-identical performance to direct identification while requiring only proximal measurements.

Abstract: Flexible endoscopic robots enable minimally invasive access through natural orifices, but their control accuracy is limited by configuration-dependent hysteresis in the tendon-sheath mechanisms (TSMs). Tendon-sheath friction and tendon elasticity induce a systematic discrepancy between the proximal actuation input and distal output, and this discrepancy varies with the insertion tube configuration. To address this challenge, this paper proposes the Self-Sensing Tendon Loop (SSTL), a double-pass tendon loop routed through the insertion tube and wrapped around a distal pulley, and returned to the proximal end. The loop structure allows both the input and output tensions of the SSTL to be measured proximally, thereby providing an input-output tension profile without requiring distal force or fiber-optic sensors. Because the SSTL shares the same routing path as the actuation TSM, the two TSMs exhibit strongly correlated hysteresis behaviors. From the SSTL tension profile, a learning-based mapping estimates the configuration-dependent hysteresis parameters of the actuation TSM, which are then used by a feedforward controller to compensate for actuation hysteresis. We validate the proposed method by tracking actuation tendon tension under three different insertion tube configurations. Across sinusoidal and random trajectories, the proposed method reduces average RMSE by 88.1% compared with the uncompensated baseline, achieving 97.8% of the performance of direct identification, which requires direct measurement of the input and output tension profile of the actuation TSM.

</details>


### [361] [SADP: Subgoal-Aware Diffusion Policy for Explainable Robots Learned from Foundation Model Generated Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16871)
*Site Hu,Takato Horii*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SADP leverages foundation models to generate subgoal-annotated demonstrations and trains diffusion policies conditioned on both task-level and subgoal-level descriptions, achieving higher task success rates while enabling interpretable execution monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning methods lack subgoal structure and execution progress modeling, and standard datasets lack subgoal-level supervision, hindering explainable robot behavior during long-horizon manipulation.

Method: SADP uses foundation models to auto-generate subgoal-annotated demonstrations, trains diffusion policies conditioned on both task-level and subgoal-level descriptions, and adds a lightweight auxiliary head to predict subgoal completion states.

Result: SADP achieves higher task success rates than strong task-conditioned diffusion baselines in RLBench simulations and UR5e real-world evaluations, while providing subgoal-level execution signals.

Conclusion: Built-in interpretability (subgoal-aware structure) can coexist with high task performance, unlike post-hoc explanation approaches.

Abstract: Explainable robots require not only successful task execution but also the ability to expose internal decision-making process in a user-friendly manner. However, most imitation learning methods are trained solely on task-level demonstrations, without explicitly modeling subgoal structure or execution progress. This limitation is further exacerbated by the scarcity of subgoal-level supervision in standard robot learning datasets, which restricts the development of robots that can convey the subtasks they are executing during long-horizon manipulation. To address this issue, this paper proposes Subgoal-Aware Diffusion Policy (SADP), a framework that leverages foundation models to autonomously generate subgoal-annotated demonstrations and trains diffusion policies on these datasets. SADP structures policy execution around human-interpretable subgoals by conditioning action generation on both task-level and subgoal-level descriptions. A lightweight auxiliary head further predicts subgoal completion states, allowing the robot to expose its current execution stage and monitor subgoal progression. Experiments in RLBench simulations and real-world evaluations on a UR5e robot demonstrate that SADP achieves higher task success rates than strong task-conditioned diffusion baselines, while providing subgoal-level execution signals for monitoring progress and diagnosing failures. These results highlight that built-in, rather than post-hoc, interpretability can coexist with high task performance.

</details>


### [362] [Beyond Safety Filtering: Control Barrier Function-Informed Reinforcement Learning for Connected and Automated Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16894)
*Jianye Xu,Bassam Alrifaee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Control Barrier Function (CBF)-informed reward design for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning that converts CBF constraint values into reward signals for safer learning, outperforming heuristic baselines in a multi-lane intersection scenario with connected vehicles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward design in RL is typically hand-crafted using heuristics that are difficult to tune, motivating the need for a more principled approach.

Method: Converts Control Barrier Function (CBF) constraint values under joint MARL actions into a reward signal that explicitly guides safe learning.

Result: Achieves highest task performance and is less sensitive to reward hyperparameters compared to two heuristic reward baselines in a four-way multi-lane intersection with connected and automated vehicles.

Conclusion: CBF-informed reward design yields consistently strong performance across the tested hyperparameter range, offering a more robust alternative to heuristic reward tuning in MARL.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) uses rewards to guide learning, yet reward design is typically hand-crafted using heuristics that can be difficult to tune. We propose a Control Barrier Function (CBF)-informed reward design for Multi-Agent RL (MARL) that converts CBF constraint values under joint MARL actions into a reward signal that explicitly guides safe learning. We compare against two heuristic reward baselines in a four-way multi-lane intersection with connected and automated vehicles. Results show that our method achieves the highest task performance and is less sensitive to reward hyperparameters, yielding consistently strong performance across the tested hyperparameter range. Code for reproducing the experimental results and a video demonstration are available at https://github.com/bassamlab/SigmaRL.

</details>


### [363] [MORN: Metacognitive Object-Goal Regulation for Resource-Rational Long-Horizon Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16932)
*Xi Lin,Jiayi Li,Kangyi Wu,Jiaqiao Tang,Qingrong He,Lin Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MORN is a metacognitive navigation architecture inspired by Dual-Process Theory that injects resource-rationality into ObjectNav agents. It uses a System 2 meta-controller to monitor and regulate a System 1 locomotor, dynamically managing time and battery budgets through neuro-cognitive states. On HM3D, it improves Goal Completion Rate from 0.23 to 0.30 and reduces Wasted Step Fraction from 0.90 to 0.70.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current zero-shot ObjectNav agents using VLMs are purely reactive and lack global resource awareness, causing them to waste critical budgets (time, battery) on infeasible subgoals due to partial observability. They fail to balance local exploration with global mission viability.

Method: MORN is a Dual-Process Theory-inspired architecture that augments frozen navigation backbones with a System 2 meta-controller. It formalizes three neuro-cognitive states: Potentiality Index, Persistence Gating, and Evidence Accumulation, allowing dynamic regulation of mission schedules based on online estimates of progress velocity and perceptual uncertainty.

Result: On HM3D: Goal Completion Rate improved from 0.23 to 0.30; Wasted Step Fraction reduced from 0.90 to 0.70.

Conclusion: In resource-constrained autonomy, metacognitive awareness of global resources is as critical as reactive navigation ability. The Sunk Cost Fallacy can be effectively neutralized through dynamic regulation.

Abstract: Robots deployed in unstructured human environments must frequently execute long-horizon missions, such as find the mug, then the chair, then the printer, under strict operational constraints. While contemporary zero-shot Object Navigation (ObjectNav) agents leverage Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to effectively localize semantic targets, they operate as purely reactive systems that inherently lack global resource awareness. Consequently, these agents inadvertently exhaust critical budgets, including time and battery, on infeasible subgoals due to partial observability, failing to balance local exploration with global mission viability. To bridge this gap by injecting resource-rationality into the navigation loop, we present MORN (Metacognitive Object-goal Regulation Navigation), an executive architecture inspired by Dual-Process Theory in cognitive science. MORN augments frozen navigation backbones with a System 2 meta-controller that continuously monitors the System 1 locomotor. By formalizing three neuro-cognitive states, Potentiality Index, Persistence Gating, and Evidence Accumulation, MORN dynamically regulates the mission schedule based on online estimates of progress velocity and perceptual uncertainty. This mechanism effectively neutralizes the Sunk Cost Fallacy, enabling agents to abort zombie goals early and decisively commit to achievable ones. Extensive experiments on the HM3D dataset demonstrate that MORN improves Goal Completion Rate (CR) from 0.23 to 0.30 and reduces Wasted Step Fraction (WSF) from 0.90 to 0.70, establishing that in resource-constrained autonomy, the metacognitive awareness of global resources is as critical as the reactive ability to navigate.

</details>


### [364] [NORM-Nav: Zero-Shot Mobile Robot Navigation with Natural Language Behavioral Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16979)
*Dongjie Huo,Junhui Wang,Chao Gao,Yan Qiao,Dong Zhang,Guyue Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NORM-Nav is a zero-shot framework that uses LLMs to parse natural language behavioral constraints into multi-layer costmaps for socially-aware robot navigation, outperforming baselines in both simulation and real-world experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile robots in human-centered environments need to follow local behavioral conventions (social norms), not just avoid collisions—something conventional costmap-based navigation overlooks.

Method: Uses an LLM to parse natural language instructions into structured constraints, grounds them via real-time vision-LiDAR perception, and encodes them as multi-layer costmaps (geometric, semantic, directional, velocity cues) compatible with standard grid-based planners.

Result: NORM-Nav improves task success rates and produces trajectories closer to human references than representative baselines in both simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Integrating LLM-parsed behavioral constraints into costmap-based planning is a zero-shot, effective approach for socially appropriate robot navigation in human-centered environments.

Abstract: Mobile robots operating in human-centered environments must generate not only collision-free paths but also trajectories that follow local behavioral conventions. Conventional costmap-based navigation emphasizes geometric feasibility and often overlooks such requirements, which can result in socially inappropriate behaviors. This paper presents NORM-Nav, a zero-shot framework that integrates natural language behavioral constraints into costmap-based planning. An LLM parses each instruction into structured constraints and grounds them using real-time vision--LiDAR perception. These constraints are encoded as multi-layer costmaps that represent geometric, semantic, directional, and velocity cues and are directly compatible with standard grid-based planners. Simulation and real-world experiments indicate that NORM-Nav improves task success rates and produces trajectories closer to human references than representative baselines. The project website is available at https://ei-nav.github.io/NORM-Nav.

</details>


### [365] [Generalizable and Actionable Parts Pose Estimation with Symmetry Annotation-Free Learning Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17033)
*Wenxiao Chen,Xueyu Yuan,Liu Liu,Di Wu,Dan Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SAFAG is a symmetry annotation-free framework for generalizable and actionable parts pose estimation that uses stepwise refinement and self-supervised learning to handle symmetry issues, showing superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GAPart pose estimation methods suffer from insufficient handling of symmetry issues or require rich symmetry annotations, which limits their applicability in data-lacking scenarios.

Method: Proposes a two-stage candidate-to-final quaternion regression framework with stepwise refinement, and treats symmetry prediction as a probability distribution problem solved via self-supervised learning.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate superior performance and robustness compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: SAFAG eliminates the need for symmetry annotations while achieving precise pose estimation, with strong potential for embodied AI applications.

Abstract: Urgently needed generalizable robot object interaction and manipulation requires high-quality Cross-Category object perception. As a pioneer of this area, Generalizable and Actionable Parts (GAParts) understanding has attracted increasing attention from relevant researchers. However, most recent works either have insufficient design regarding the symmetry issue or require rich symmetry annotation, which severely impedes precise GAPart pose estimation in data-lacking scenarios. In this paper, we propose SAFAG, a novel Symmetry Annotation-Free framework for Generalizable and Actionable Parts Pose Estimation. Specifically, we suggest a stepwise refinement two-stage framework for candidate-to-final quaternion regression, and tackle the symmetry prediction as a probability distribution problem with self-supervised learning strategy. The experimental results demonstrate the superior performance and robustness of our SAFAG. We believe that our work has the enormous potential to be applied in many areas of embodied AI system.

</details>


### [366] [How to Instruct Your Robot: Dense Language Annotations Power Robot Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17077)
*Bosung Kim,Ruiyi Wang,David Acuna,Jaehun Jung,Alexander Trevithick,Brandon Cui,Yejin Choi,Prithviraj Ammanabrolu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeMiAn is a two-stage approach that re-labels robot demonstration segments with dense, multi-aspect annotations (motion, scene, pose, reasoning) generated by VLMs, and uses a learned instructor to select task-appropriate annotations at deployment, improving policy learning without collecting new demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling robot policy learning is bottlenecked by the high cost of collecting demonstrations, while language annotations for existing data are comparatively cheap. The authors aim to extract more signal from fixed robot/egocentric-video corpora using dense language re-annotation.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) Re-labels demonstration segments with VLM-generated annotations along four aspects (physical motion, scene composition, arm pose, reasoning). (2) A learned instructor maps task description and initial scene snapshot to a task-appropriate annotation at deployment, running asynchronously to hide generation latency behind policy execution.

Result: On 1M robot clips and 50K EgoVerse videos, DeMiAn improves both a vision-language-action policy and a video-based world-action model. RoboCasa: +5 points over task-only baseline, within 3 points of per-task oracle. No fixed annotation aspect dominates; improves composite-task and OOD performance, and shifts compute-performance frontier.

Conclusion: Dense re-annotation is a practical scaling lever for robot policy learning, enabling better performance without collecting new demonstrations and demonstrating that selecting the right dense language matters.

Abstract: Scaling robot policy learning is bottlenecked by the cost of collecting demonstrations, while language annotations for existing demonstrations are comparatively cheap. We study language density as a lever for extracting more signal from a fixed robot or egocentric-video corpus. We introduce DeMiAn (Dense Multi-aspect Annotation), a two-stage approach that first re-labels demonstration segments with VLM-generated annotations along four complementary aspects: physical motion, scene composition, arm pose, and reasoning. A learned instructor then maps a task description and initial scene snapshot to a task-appropriate annotation at deployment, running asynchronously so generation latency is hidden behind policy execution. Across over 1M robot manipulation clips and 50K EgoVerse human-egocentric videos, DeMiAn improves both a vision-language-action policy and a video-based world-action model without collecting new demonstrations. On RoboCasa, the instructor raises success by 5 points over a task-only baseline and comes within 3 points of a per-task oracle. No fixed annotation aspect dominates across tasks, showing that selecting the right dense language matters. DeMiAn also improves composite-task and out-of-distribution performance, and shifts the compute-performance frontier in both mid-training and post-training after accounting for annotation-generation FLOPs. These results position dense re-annotation as a practical scaling lever for robot policy learning.

</details>


### [367] [Contrastive Conceptor Activation Steering (COAST): Unlocking Vision-Language-Action Models through Hidden States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17144)
*Miranda Muqing Miao,Subin Kim,Brandon Yang,Lyle Ungar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: COAST is a training-free method that steers VLA model activations toward success-relevant subspaces using conceptors, improving success rates by over 20% (simulation) and 40% (real-robot) across diverse architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models are brittle in practice despite powerful VLM pre-training, frequently failing at simple robotic tasks. The authors aim to unlock latent task-relevant knowledge already present in VLA representations.

Method: COAST uses conceptors (linear operators that soft-project data into principal components of a target distribution) to identify success-critical subspaces from a few success/failure examples, then steers VLA latents toward these subspaces at inference time.

Result: COAST improves absolute mean task success rate by over 20% in simulation and 40% on real robots across three architecturally distinct policies (flow-matching VLA, autoregressive VLA, Diffusion Policy). Conceptors can transfer to new tasks with shared failure modes without refitting.

Conclusion: Current VLAs retain substantial task-relevant knowledge in latent representations, and the action expert's decoding bottleneck can be mitigated by steering residual streams toward task-relevant subspaces. COAST offers a lightweight, training-free path to unlocking these capabilities.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models leverage powerful perceptual priors from web-scale Vision-Language Model (VLM) pre-training, yet they remain surprisingly brittle in practice, frequently failing at simple robotic tasks. To mitigate this, we propose Contrastive Conceptor Activation Steering (COAST). COAST builds on the notion of a "conceptor", a linear operator that soft-projects data into the principal components of a target distribution. COAST uses conceptors to identify success-critical subspaces for a target robotic task from a few examples of success and failure rollouts. At inference time, it steers VLA latents into these identified success subspaces to improve task outcomes. Across three architecturally distinct neural policies (flow-matching VLA, autoregressive VLA, and Diffusion Policy), COAST improves absolute mean simulation and real-robot task success rate by over 20 and 40% respectively. The activation subspace geometry reveals that failure modes share substantial structure across tasks while success representations remain largely task-specific. When tasks share similar failure modes, this structure enables previously fitted conceptors to improve performance on new tasks without refitting. Ultimately, our results suggest that current VLAs retain substantial task-relevant knowledge in their latent representations, and that the action expert's decoding bottleneck could be mitigated by steering its residual stream toward task-relevant subspaces. COAST provides a lightweight, training-free path to unlocking these latent capabilities by steering the model towards its own "success" distributions.

</details>


### [368] [Event-Grounded Sparse Autoencoders for Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17204)
*Xinchen Jin,Aditya Chatterjee,Pranav Kumar,Rohan Paleja*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes an event-grounded interpretability pipeline for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies, using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to link hidden representations to behavioral events (e.g., end-effector keyframes) rather than text contexts, enabling causal analysis through closed-loop rollouts. Experiments on OpenVLA and π₀.₅ show event-grounded ranking yields strong causal effects, though SAE usability varies with architecture and intervention site.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mechanistic interpretability tools for language/vision models don't transfer cleanly to VLAs because VLA outputs are robot actions (not tokens) and interventions require expensive closed-loop rollouts. The authors aim to bridge this gap by anchoring analysis to behavioral events.

Method: (1) Cluster end-effector keyframes per task using visual, state, and temporal cues. (2) Link SAE features to these behaviorally salient events. (3) Optionally annotate with VLM semantic context. (4) Evaluate causal effects via closed-loop rollouts across simulation and real-robot settings.

Result: Event-grounded ranking yields the strongest causal effects on OpenVLA and transfers to π₀.₅'s continuous action chunks. SAE is a sparse but imperfect intervention basis; usability varies with architecture and intervention site. Aggressive intervention reveals safety and interpretability limits.

Conclusion: Event-grounded SAE analysis is a practical starting point for behavior-anchored VLA interpretability. Future work should explore features beyond action-aligned coordinates, finer-grained closed-loop evaluation, and safe interventions for high-stakes deployments.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies translate language and visual inputs into robot actions, where their hidden representations directly shape closed-loop behavior. However, mechanistic interpretability tools from language and vision-language models do not transfer cleanly to VLAs: outputs are robot actions rather than human-readable tokens, and interventions can only be tested via expensive closed-loop rollouts. We propose an event-grounded interpretability pipeline that anchors SAE feature analysis to behavioral events rather than text contexts. End-effector keyframes are clustered within each task using visual, state, and temporal cues, linking SAE features to behaviorally salient events and, via optional VLM annotations, to semantic context. To our knowledge, our pipeline is among the first to ground SAE-based VLA analysis in closed-loop behavioral events. Across two simulation architectures and a real-robot study, event-grounded ranking yields the strongest causal effects on OpenVLA and transfers to the continuous action chunks of $π_{0.5}$. SAE is a sparse but imperfect intervention basis: usability varies with architecture and intervention site, and aggressive intervention reveals safety and interpretability limits. Overall, event-grounded SAE analysis emerges as a practical starting point for behavior-anchored VLA interpretability, motivating future work on SAE features beyond action-aligned coordinates, finer-grained closed-loop evaluation, and safe interventions for high-stakes VLA deployments. Code is available at \url{https://github.com/xc-j/Event-SAE}.

</details>


### [369] [Generating Realistic Safety-Critical Scenarios for Vehicle-Pedestrian Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17229)
*Qingwen Pu,Kun Xie,Yuan Zhu,Guocong Zhai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A three-stage framework (real-world pre-training → simulation-based online RL → large-scale scenario generation) produces behaviorally realistic safety-critical vehicle-pedestrian interaction data, outperforming baselines with trajectory errors as low as ADE=0.072m and passing a Turing test for indistinguishability from real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world datasets rarely capture high-risk vehicle-pedestrian scenarios, and simulation platforms lack realistic behavior, creating a gap for rigorous ADS validation.

Method: Stage 1: Pre-train MA-SST-DDPG agents on real-world safety-critical data. Stage 2: Deploy agents in CARLA for online RL to generalize across scenarios. Stage 3: Generate 198K+ high-resolution episodes (VPSCI dataset) using the refined model.

Result: Refined MA-SST-DDPG achieved lowest trajectory errors (ADE=0.072m, FDE=0.142m); distributional equivalence with real data in conflict severity and behavioral response; passed Turing test.

Conclusion: The framework effectively produces high-fidelity safety-critical data for ADS development and simulation-based safety evaluation.

Abstract: Automated driving system deployment requires rigorous validation across safety-critical vehicle-pedestrian interactions, yet real-world datasets rarely capture high-risk scenarios while simulation platforms lack realistic behavior. In response, this study proposes a three-stage framework that combines real-world grounding with adaptive simulation to generate behaviorally realistic safety-critical scenarios at scale. Stage 1 pre-trains multi-agent state-space Transformer-enhanced DDPG (MA-SST-DDPG) agents on real-world safety-critical data to learn human-like interactive evasive behaviors through data-driven learning. Stage 2 deploys pre-trained multi-agents in CARLA for online reinforcement learning to generalize across diverse scenarios, integrating real-world knowledge with simulation experience to produce a refined MA-SST-DDPG model. Stage 3 uses CARLA with the refined model to generate over 198,000 high-resolution interaction episodes from eight intersection scenarios, culminating in the Vehicle-Pedestrian Safety-Critical Interaction (VPSCI) dataset. The Refined MA-SST-DDPG model outperformed baseline methods in reproducing realistic evasive behaviors, achieving the lowest trajectory errors (ADE = 0.072 m, FDE = 0.142 m). Statistical comparison confirmed distributional equivalence between the generated and real-world data in both conflict severity and behavioral response. A Turing test confirmed that the three-stage framework generated evasive behaviors were indistinguishable from real-world interactions. These results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness in producing high-fidelity safety-critical data, offering valuable sources for the development of ADS and simulation-based safety evaluations.

</details>


### [370] [SEDualVLN: A Spatially-Enhanced Dual-System for Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17249)
*Jingzhi Huang,Junkai Huang,Wenxuan Song,Haoyang Yang,Hailong Huang,Haoang Li,Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SEDualVLN is a dual-system VLN framework combining a spatially-aware VLM (System 1, fast action generation) with an MLLM + mapping module (System 2, slow waypoint planning) to achieve SOTA on VLN-CE benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLN approaches suffer from either limited long-horizon reasoning (end-to-end VLM) or poor spatial grounding and slow inference (zero-shot MLLM). The authors aim to bridge this gap by combining both paradigms with spatial enhancements.

Method: Proposes a dual-system framework: System 1 (VLM with global/local spatial awareness) for fast action generation; System 2 (MLLM + mapping module) for slow waypoint planning using top-down 3D maps and rendered path images. A fast-slow coordination mechanism integrates both.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on VLN-CE benchmarks. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of each system and module.

Conclusion: Spatially-enhanced dual-system cooperation effectively addresses the limitations of both end-to-end and zero-shot VLN paradigms, providing strong generalization and efficient reasoning.

Abstract: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) approaches have currently followed two primary paradigms: the end-to-end Vision-Language Model (VLM) policy fine-tuned on navigation trajectories to directly predict actions, and the zero-shot modular pipeline integrating pre-trained Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for training-free generalization to unseen environments. However, end-to-end methods struggle with long-horizon navigation and lack dynamic reasoning, whereas zero-shot methods are constrained by limited spatial grounding for reliable planning and also require substantial reasoning time. To bridge this gap, we introduce SEDualVLN, a spatially-enhanced dual-system VLN framework. System 1 is a VLM model enhanced with both global and local spatial awareness, used for action generation. System 2 integrates a general MLLM with a mapping module, wherein the MLLM plans waypoints by leveraging top-down views of the real-time 3D map alongside streams of rendered path images. Both systems leverage different forms of spatial enhancement to cultivate the agent's sense of direction in VLN tasks. Ultimately, they cooperate to complete the navigation task through a fast-slow coordinated approach. SEDualVLN achieves state-of-the-art performance on VLN-CE benchmarks, and further ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of each system and module.

</details>


### [371] [Stretch-ICP: A Continuous-Trajectory Registration and Deskewing Algorithm in Scenarios of Aggressive Motions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17264)
*Simon-Pierre Deschênes,Veronica Vannini,Philippe Giguère,François Pomerleau*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces the TIGS dataset (high-angular-speed lidar/IMU recordings from tumbling down a hill) and two methods—SAAVE (for estimating angular velocities during gyroscope saturation) and Stretch-ICP (a robust scan registration algorithm)—that together significantly improve SLAM robustness under aggressive motions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust autonomy in complex environments is challenged by extreme motions (uneven/slippery terrain) that corrupt sensor data and degrade state estimation, creating a need for more robust algorithms and a suitable benchmark dataset.

Method: (1) TIGS dataset: real-world recordings from a mechanical lidar and IMU tumbling downhill, with angular speeds up to 4× higher than existing datasets. (2) SAAVE: estimates angular velocities when gyroscope measurements are saturated. (3) Stretch-ICP: a novel registration and deskewing algorithm that reconstructs smoother 6-DOF trajectories.

Result: SAAVE reduces angular speed estimation error by 83.4%; Stretch-ICP reduces linear and angular velocity errors by 95.2% and 94.8% respectively at scan boundaries.

Conclusion: The proposed dataset and algorithms collectively improve the robustness and consistency of lidar-inertial state estimation under aggressive motions.

Abstract: Robust robotic autonomy remains challenging in complex environments, where loss of stability on uneven or slippery terrain can induce extreme accelerations and angular velocities. Such motions corrupt sensor measurements and degrade state estimation, motivating the need for improved algorithmic robustness. To investigate this issue, we introduce the Tumbling-Induced Gyroscope Saturation (TIGS) dataset, which consists of recordings from a mechanical lidar and an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) tumbling down a hill. The dataset contains angular speeds up to four times higher than those in similar datasets and is publicly available. We then propose two complementary methods to improve Simultaneous Localization And Mapping (SLAM) robustness and evaluate them on TIGS. First, Saturation-Aware Angular Velocity Estimation (SAAVE) estimates angular velocities when gyroscope measurements become saturated during aggressive motions, reducing angular speed estimation error by 83.4%. Second, Stretch-ICP, a novel registration and deskewing algorithm, enables reconstruction of smoother 6-Degrees Of Freedom (DOF) trajectories under aggressive motions compared to classical Iterative Closest Point (ICP). Stretch-ICP reduces linear and angular velocity errors by 95.2% and 94.8%, respectively, at scan boundaries. Together, these contributions improve the robustness and consistency of lidar-inertial state estimation under aggressive motions.

</details>


### [372] [Task Capability Improvement Algorithm for Collaborative Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17293)
*Keshab Patra,Arpita Sinha,Anirban Guha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper shows that applying forces at grasp points (not center of gravity) creates additional moments that improve manipulator task capability by 5.86%, enhancing collaborative object manipulation, resource allocation, and fault tolerance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve cooperative task capability in multi-manipulator systems by leveraging additional moments that naturally arise from off-center force application, rather than treating them as undesirable.

Method: The method involves applying forces at the object's grasp point (rather than center of gravity), utilizing the resulting undesired moments as additional moments to enhance individual and collaborative manipulator capability.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate a 5.86% improvement in task capability compared to the baseline without using moments for enhancement.

Conclusion: Additional moments from off-center force application can improve individual and collaborative manipulator capability, leading to better object transportation, optimal resource allocation, and maximum fault tolerance.

Abstract: This work introduces a cooperative task capability improvement utilizing additional moments. The manipulators apply forces at the object's grasp point. Applying forces at a point other than the object's center of gravity produces undesired moments. The undesired moment acts as an additional moment. It improves the capability of an individual manipulator and, hence, the entire collaborative group. Any improvements in task capability directly add up to the object and transportation capability. The group's enhanced capability also helps achieve optimal capability, optimal resource allocation, and maximum fault tolerance in object manipulation. Our simulation results show an improvement in the capability of 5.86 \% compared to when no moment is used to enhance the capability of the manipulators.

</details>


### [373] [HCLM: A Hierarchical Framework for Cooperative Loco-Manipulation with Dual Quadrupeds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17300)
*Qixuan Li,Chen Le,Jincheng Yu,Xinlei Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HCLM is a hierarchical framework for dual quadruped robots to perform cooperative loco-manipulation, combining a Joint Diffusion Policy for high-level coordination with a Whole-Body Controller for low-level motion execution, validated in simulations and real-world tasks like carrying, packing, and handovers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coordinating multi-robot collaborative manipulation across floating bases is highly challenging due to conflicting demands of spatial coordination, robust locomotion, and closed-chain physical interactions between the robots.

Method: A hierarchical architecture with (1) high-level Joint Diffusion Policy using SE(3)-invariant task-space representation for coordinate-agnostic coordination, and (2) low-level Whole-Body Controller with kinematic MPC for collision-free velocity distribution and a reactive execution layer with cooperative admittance for force regulation.

Result: Demonstrated reliable task execution, strict configuration agnosticism, and exceptional resilience against severe physical perturbations in cooperative carrying, packing, and handovers tasks.

Conclusion: The framework offers a highly robust pathway for multi-robot embodied coordination, successfully bridging high-level reasoning and low-level motion execution for dual quadrupedal systems.

Abstract: We introduce HCLM, a hierarchical framework for general-purpose cooperative loco-manipulation with dual quadrupedal systems. Coordinating multi-robot collaborative manipulation across floating bases is highly challenging due to the conflicting demands of spatial coordination, robust locomotion, and closed-chain physical interactions. To resolve this, our architecture systematically decouples high-level collaborative reasoning from low-level robust motion execution. At the high level, a centralized Joint Diffusion Policy leverages an SE(3)-invariant task-space representation to learn coordinate-agnostic spatial coordination patterns. To translate these frame-agnostic references into physical motion, a task-centric hybrid Whole-Body Controller synergizes a proactive kinematic Model Predictive Control for collision-free velocity distribution with a reactive execution layer. Crucially, this reactive layer guarantees rapid responsiveness for precise end-effector tracking, while concurrently integrating active force regulation via a cooperative admittance scheme to safely resolve kinematic conflicts and strictly regulate internal stresses during closed-chain interactions. We validate the framework across progressively challenging simulated scenarios, including cooperative carrying, packing and handovers, and successfully deploy the latter in the real world. The results demonstrate reliable task execution, strict configuration agnosticism, and exceptional resilience against severe physical perturbations, offering a highly robust pathway for multi-robot embodied coordination.

</details>


### [374] [Beyond Geometry: Efficient Topologically-Grounded Navigation in Complex 3D Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17302)
*Yifan Du,Chengwei Zhang,Siyu Liao,Zhongfeng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A surface extraction framework reduces robot navigation state space by 80%+ via ground support, overhead clearance, and connectivity constraints, achieving sub-millisecond A* planning and 100% success on 300 queries across Matterport3D and PCT scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground robot navigation in complex 3D environments suffers from geometric ambiguity (non-traversable structures like furniture appearing similar to navigable ground) and high computational cost of searching massive voxel spaces.

Method: A surface extraction framework that constructs a reduced state space by enforcing three constraints: ground support, overhead clearance, and seed-based connectivity.

Result: Over 80% state space reduction, sub-millisecond A* search on Matterport3D scenes, and 100% planning success across all 300 tested queries (5 Matterport3D + 3 PCT benchmark scenes).

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses geometric ambiguity and computational cost issues in complex 3D ground robot navigation.

Abstract: Ground robot navigation in complex 3D environments is often hindered by geometric ambiguity, where non-traversable structures such as furniture share local geometric properties with navigable ground. Furthermore, the computational cost of searching massive voxel spaces remains a significant challenge. To address these issues, we present a surface extraction framework that constructs a reduced state space of physically reachable standing positions by enforcing ground support, overhead clearance, and seed-based connectivity constraints. Evaluation across five Matterport3D indoor scenes and three PCT benchmark scenes demonstrates over 80\% state space reduction and sub-millisecond A* search on the Matterport3D scenes, with 100\% planning success across all 300 tested queries.

</details>


### [375] [Efficient Feature-Free Initialization for Monocular Visual-Inertial Systems Using a Feed-Forward 3D Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17327)
*Yuantai Zhang,Jiaqi Yang,Huajian Zeng,Changhao Chen,Haoang Li,Liang Li,Dezhen Song,Xingxing Zuo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a feature-free visual-inertial initialization method that uses feed-forward 3D models to predict point clouds directly from images, eliminating the need for visual feature tracking. It achieves >90% success rate and reduces required data duration to under 1.2 seconds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VINS initialization methods rely heavily on visual feature correspondences and require 3-4 seconds of data, limiting efficiency and applicability. The authors aim to reduce complexity and improve reliability by removing feature tracking requirements.

Method: A feature-free initialization framework that leverages up-to-scale point clouds predicted by a feed-forward 3D model, bypassing traditional visual feature tracking and estimation steps.

Result: Achieves over 90% initialization success rate, reduces required data duration to under 1.2 seconds (vs. typical 3-4s), and demonstrates robust performance in visually degraded environments on public and self-collected datasets.

Conclusion: The feature-free approach substantially reduces system complexity and improves initialization reliability, especially in visually degraded environments where traditional feature-based methods fail.

Abstract: Fast and reliable initialization is critical for monocular visual-inertial navigation systems (VINS), as it establishes the starting conditions for subsequent state estimation. Despite steady progress, most existing methods heavily rely on visual feature correspondences and require 3-4 seconds of sensory data for successful initialization, which limits their applicability and efficiency. With the advent of feed-forward 3D models that can directly predict point clouds from images, we revisit the visual-inertial initialization problem from a concise perspective. In this work, we propose a feature-free initialization framework that leverages up-to-scale point clouds predicted by a feed-forward 3D model, thereby obviating the need for visual feature tracking and estimation. This design substantially reduces system complexity and improves the reliability of initialization. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate that the proposed feature-free initialization method achieves the highest success rate, exceeding 90%, and significantly reduces the data duration required for successful initialization, typically to under 1.2 s. We further validate our method on a self-collected dataset covering various indoor and outdoor scenarios, demonstrating robust performance, particularly in visually degraded environments where existing methods often fail. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/Yuantai-Z/FF-VIO-Init.

</details>


### [376] [Tactile-based Multimodal Fusion in Embodied Intelligence: A Survey of Vision, Language, and Contact-Driven Paradigms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17336)
*Zhixiang Cao,Di Tian,Runwei Guan,Yanzhou Mu,Xiaolou Sun,Shaofeng Liang,Daizong Liu,Tao Huang,Yutao Yue,Henghui Ding,Bin Fang,Alex Zhou,Qing-Long Han,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper provides a comprehensive survey of multimodal tactile fusion up to early 2026, proposing a hierarchical taxonomy organizing the field into datasets (Tactile-Vision, Tactile-Language, Tactile-Vision-Language, Tactile-Vision-Other) and methods (Multimodal Perception & Recognition, Cross-Modal Generation, Multimodal Interaction), along with hardware, benchmarks, and future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile sensing provides unique contact-level feedback essential for embodied intelligence, but unimodal tactile perception suffers from sparse spatial coverage and lacks semantic context. Despite growing research in multimodal tactile fusion integrating vision and language, the field remains fragmented across disparate datasets, modalities, and tasks without a unified theoretical framework, motivating the need for a comprehensive survey.

Method: The authors conduct a comprehensive survey of multimodal tactile fusion research up to Q1 2026, proposing a hierarchical taxonomy with two primary dimensions: (1) multimodal datasets categorized into Tactile-Vision, Tactile-Language, Tactile-Vision-Language, and Tactile-Vision-Other; (2) multimodal methods structured into three pillars — Multimodal Perception & Recognition, Cross-Modal Generation, and Multimodal Interaction. The survey also reviews tactile sensing hardware, evaluation metrics, and benchmarks.

Result: The paper presents a structured taxonomy covering four dataset categories and three method pillars, alongside a summary of representative tactile sensing hardware, commonly used evaluation metrics, and benchmark settings in the field.

Conclusion: The survey organizes the fragmented landscape of multimodal tactile fusion, identifies current challenges, and outlines promising future directions for integrating tactile sensing with vision and language in embodied intelligence.
