Method: A conditional diffusion model based on U-Net architecture is constructed. High-grade IMU measurements serve as ground-truth priors, while low-cost IMU measurements are used as conditional inputs to generate high-fidelity virtual IMU data.

Result: Generated virtual IMU data significantly outperforms original low-cost IMU measurements in both positioning and attitude estimation. In airborne mapping experiments, the method produces thinner and more consistent point clouds.

Conclusion: The proposed framework breaks the performance limits of low-cost IMUs and demonstrates the potential of diffusion-based generative learning for creating virtual high-grade IMU data, offering a cost-effective solution for high-precision navigation.

Abstract: Inertial measurement units (IMUs) are fundamental sensing components in multi-source integrated navigation systems, and their performance directly determines the accuracy and reliability of solutions. However, the precision of low-cost IMUs is inherently constrained by hardware limitations. Recently, generative artificial intelligence has demonstrated remarkable capability in modeling complex data distributions and reconstructing high-fidelity signals. Motivated by this, we propose a diffusion-based generative learning framework for synthesizing high-fidelity virtual IMU data from low-cost IMU measurements. Specifically, a conditional diffusion model based on a U-Net architecture is constructed, where high-grade IMU measurements are utilized as ground-truth priors and low-cost IMU measurements are employed as conditional inputs. The virtual IMU data generated by the model is used for subsequent navigation and localization tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the generated virtual IMU data significantly outperform the original low-cost IMU measurements in both positioning and attitude estimation. Furthermore, we transfer the model to airborne mapping experiments, where the proposed method produces thinner and more consistent point clouds. Overall, the proposed framework breaks the performance limits of low-cost IMU and demonstrates the potential of diffusion-based generative learning for virtual high-grade IMU data.

</details>


### [563] [One-hot Coding-based URA with RFFI-Enabled Message Authentication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16437)
*Wenbo Fan,Zeping Sui,Yuhei Takahashi,Jun Cheng,Zilong Liu,Pingzhi Fan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a one-hot coding (OHC)-based URA framework that enables message authentication in unsourced random access by mapping messages onto orthogonal channel uses and exploiting radio-frequency fingerprint identification, achieving secure transmission without extra authentication payload.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: URA transmissions lack device identifiers, creating a security vulnerability where forged messages may be decoded as legitimate, compromising authentication.

Method: The method uses OHC-based common codebook to map distinct messages onto orthogonal channel uses with on-off keying modulation, enabling RF fingerprint identification through device-specific hardware impairments for authentication.

Result: Analytical expressions for per-user error probability and successful spoofing probability are derived; numerical results show the scheme enables secure URA with reliable communication in ultra-short-payload IoT scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed OHC-based URA framework effectively addresses the authentication vulnerability in unsourced random access while preserving the unsourced transmission principle and maintaining communication reliability.

Abstract: Unsourced random access (URA) has emerged as a promising paradigm for enabling massive connectivity in Internet-of-Things (IoT) networks. However, since URA transmissions do not contain device identifiers, the receiver may not associate decoded messages with their originating devices, introducing a security vulnerability: forged messages may be decoded as legitimate. To address this problem, this paper proposes a one-hot coding (OHC)-based URA framework that enables message authentication while preserving the unsourced transmission principle. Specifically, distinct messages are mapped onto orthogonal channel uses via an OHC-based common codebook and transmitted using on-off keying modulation. The resulting orthogonal channel structure enables radio-frequency fingerprint identification to authenticate received signals by exploiting device-specific hardware impairments, thereby authenticating decoded messages without introducing an additional authentication payload. Analytical expressions for the per-user probability of error and the probability of successful spoofing are derived. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed scheme enables secure URA transmission while maintaining reliable communication performance in ultra-short-payload IoT scenarios.

</details>


### [564] [Q-Enhanced SH-SAW Ladder Filter in Thin-Film Lithium Tantalate Using Bartlett Apodization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16586)
*Taran Anusorn,Tzu-Hsuan Hsu,Yuchen Ma,Ruochen Lu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates a high-performance SH-SAW filter at 4.35 GHz using apodized thin-film LiTaO3 on SiO2/Si, achieving Q of 1,522 (from 688), 1.59 dB insertion loss, 3.24% FBW, and >14 dB rejection in a 0.4 mm² footprint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the quality factor and overall performance of SH-SAW filters for low-loss, compact GHz-frequency RF front ends, particularly overcoming ohmic losses from thin aluminum metallization.

Method: Applying Bartlett window apodization to interdigitated SH-SAW resonators on a 42°Y-cut thin-film LiTaO3 on SiO2/Si platform to suppress in-band spurious modes and enhance Q.

Result: Q increased from 688 to 1,522; third-order ladder filter at 4.3 GHz achieved 1.59 dB insertion loss, 3.24% 3 dB FBW, >14 dB out-of-band rejection, and 0.4 mm² footprint.

Conclusion: Apodized thin-film LiTaO3 designs are promising for low-loss, miniaturized, cost-effective RF acoustic solutions in next-generation communication and sensing applications.

Abstract: Shear-horizontal surface acoustic wave (SH-SAW) filters have shown strong potential for low-loss, compact, GHz-frequency RF front ends. In this work, we demonstrate a high-performance SH-SAW filter design at 4.35 GHz utilizing 42°Y-cut thin-film lithium tantalate (LiTaO3) on a SiO2/Si platform. Despite the limitations of thin aluminum metallization and its associated ohmic losses, we show that implementing a Bartlett window apodization technique, primarily intended for in-band spurious-mode suppression, yields a significantly improved quality factor (Q) of 1,522 from 688 in conventional interdigitated SH-SAW resonators. This enhancement enables a third-order ladder filter at 4.3 GHz with an insertion loss of 1.59 dB, compared with 1.65 dB for a conventional SH-SAW filter. In addition, our filter with apodized resonator designs achieves a 3 dB fractional bandwidth (FBW) of 3.24% and out-of-band rejection exceeding 14 dB, all within a compact footprint of 0.4 mm2. These results suggest that apodized thin-film LiTaO3 designs are highly promising for low-loss, miniaturized, cost-effective radio frequency acoustic solutions in next-generation communication and sensing applications.

</details>


### [565] [Uplink Channel Estimation for Multi-User MISO Systems Assisted by a Fluid Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16609)
*Amarilton L. Magalhães,André L. F. de Almeida,George C. Alexandropoulos*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a channel estimation framework for fluid reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (FRIS) under position uncertainty, using a two-time-scale configuration protocol and tensor modeling to jointly estimate channels and motion-induced phase coefficients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FRIS systems suffer from element-position mismatches due to calibration errors or motion uncertainties, which degrade channel state information. Channel estimation for FRIS under position uncertainty has not been explored before.

Method: A two-time-scale FRIS configuration protocol is designed to capture both reflection phase-shift and element-motion dynamics. Orthogonal pilot sequences and tensor modeling are used to derive a closed-form solution for joint estimation of individual channels and motion-induced phase coefficients.

Result: The proposed framework achieves notable performance in the presence of unknown position deviations.

Conclusion: The proposed CE framework effectively addresses position uncertainty in FRIS-assisted uplink systems, filling a gap in existing research.

Abstract: Fluid reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (FRISs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for wireless communications, wherein the reflecting elements can dynamically select their effective radiating positions from a dense preset grid, thereby introducing an additional degree of freedom. In contrast to conventional RIS architectures, FRISs can achieve spatial diversity with fewer physical elements. However, beyond the cascaded channel structure, FRIS-assisted systems are also affected by uncertainties arising from element-position mismatches caused by calibration inaccuracies or motion errors, which may degrade channel state information. To the best of our knowledge, channel estimation (CE) for FRIS-assisted systems under position uncertainty remains unexplored. To fill this gap, we propose a CE framework for a multi-user FRIS-assisted uplink system based on a two-time-scale FRIS configuration protocol that captures both reflection phase-shift and element-motion dynamics. By capitalizing on orthogonal pilot sequences and tensor modeling, we derive a closed-form solution that jointly estimates the individual channels and the motion-induced phase coefficients. Numerical results demonstrate notable performance in the presence of unknown position deviations.

</details>


### [566] [Against the Monolithic Wireless World Model: Why NextG Needs Composable and Agentic Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16689)
*Aladin Djuhera,Farhan Ahmed,Vlad C. Andrei,Swanand Ravindra Kadhe,Alecio Binotto,Haris Gacanin,Holger Boche*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper argues that the analogy between LLMs and AI-native 6G wireless foundation models is flawed because wireless data lacks the self-contained, reusable token structure of text/code. Instead of monolithic models, the authors propose composable agentic architectures where general reasoning models orchestrate specialized components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors identify that current AI-native 6G visions drawing direct analogies to LLMs are structurally incomplete, as wireless data (CSI tensors, IQ samples, scheduler logs) is configuration-dependent, simulator-conditioned, task-disaggregated, and weakly grounded—lacking the broad, reusable, self-contained tokenized substrate that drives LLM success.

Method: The paper conducts a structural critique of the LLM-to-wireless analogy by analyzing data properties, then proposes an alternative paradigm of composable and agentic network architectures where general reasoning models orchestrate specialized signal processing models, classical algorithms, digital twins, standards-aware retrieval, and safety checks.

Result: Key finding: monolithic models (including MoE and wireless world models) are not realistic near-term paths. Emerging evidence supports composable agentic architectures as a more viable approach for deployable AI-native networks.

Conclusion: The wireless domain should focus on composable agentic architectures rather than pursuing monolithic foundation models, due to fundamental data structure differences that cannot be resolved through current pre/post-training techniques.

Abstract: AI-native 6G visions increasingly invoke wireless foundation models, large multimodal models, and wireless world models as the natural endpoint of AI-native networking, drawing an analogy to recent developments in large language models (LLMs). We argue that this analogy is structurally incomplete. The success of LLMs is based on a broad, reusable, and largely self-contained tokenized data substrate, whereas the wireless domain lacks an equivalent data foundation. Unlike text, code, or images, wireless data such as CSI tensors, IQ samples, or scheduler logs are not self-contained: their meaning is configuration-dependent, simulator-conditioned, task-disaggregated, and weakly grounded in operational feedback, all structural bottlenecks that undermine current pre- and post-training recipes. We therefore argue that monolithic models, including mixture-of-experts (MoE) and wireless world models, are not the most realistic near-term path toward deployable AI-native networks. Instead, emerging evidence points toward composable and agentic network architectures, where general reasoning models orchestrate specialized signal processing models, classical algorithms, digital twins, standards-aware retrieval, and safety checks through explicit programmable interfaces.

</details>


### [567] [Constellation-Independent Range Estimation in Payload-Based OFDM-ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16831)
*Dongil Yang,Kaitao Meng,Christos Masouros,Kawon Han*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a region-of-interest mismatched filter (ROI-MMF) for OFDM-ISAC systems that suppresses data-dependent sidelobes in multi-target/clutter-rich environments, achieving ranging MSE near the CRB with low computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Payload-based OFDM-ISAC suffers from data-dependent sidelobes induced by non-constant-modulus modulation symbols in multi-target and clutter-rich environments, degrading sensing performance.

Method: The proposed ROI-MMF suppresses sidelobes within a prescribed delay region while preserving the mainlobe response, using the Woodbury identity for efficient closed-form implementation with complexity scaling by ROI size rather than subcarrier count.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows superior ranging MSE compared to MF and RF; simulations across various constellations achieve ranging MSE approaching the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB); experimentally validated with over-the-air OFDM-ISAC testbed.

Conclusion: The ROI-MMF preserves target ranging performance even under non-constant-modulus constellations, offering an efficient and practical solution for OFDM-ISAC sensing in challenging environments.

Abstract: Orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) is a key waveform for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) due to its spectral efficiency and compatibility with modern wireless standards. In multi-target and clutter-rich environments, however, payload-based OFDM-ISAC can suffer from data-dependent sidelobes induced by non-constant-modulus modulation symbols. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a region-of-interest mismatched filter (ROI-MMF) that suppresses sidelobes within a prescribed delay region while preserving the mainlobe response. By leveraging the Woodbury identity, the proposed design admits an efficient closed-form implementation whose complexity scales with the ROI size rather than the number of subcarriers. We theoretically provide the ranging mean-square error (MSE) of the designed ROI-MMF, which shows the superior performance compared to conventional matched filtering (MF) and reciprocal filtering (RF) sensing receivers. Simulations across various constellations show that the proposed sensing receiver achieves a ranging MSE approaching the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB), which notably confirms that our design preserves the target ranging performance even under the non-constant-modulus constellation. Finally, the framework is experimentally validated with our over-the-air OFDM-ISAC testbed.

</details>


### [568] [Performance Analysis of Movable Antenna Arrays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16907)
*Gayani Siriwardana,Peter J. Smith,Himal A. Suraweera,Rajitha Senanayake*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper mathematically analyzes continuous movable antenna (MA) arrays, providing asymptotic approximations for SNR's upper tail CDF and a closed-form LCR expression under correlated elements, validated by simulations showing MA arrays outperform fixed arrays and single fluid antennas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide rigorous mathematical analysis for continuous movable antenna arrays (multiple antennas moving along a line), filling the gap in theoretical understanding of their performance compared to fixed arrays and single fluid antennas.

Method: Linear antenna array with multiple fixed elements moving along a line; full spatially coherent correlation model and continuous positioning; asymptotically exact approximations for upper tail of SNR CDF (correlated/uncorrelated cases); novel closed-form expression for LCR under non-separable 2D correlation.

Result: Validated through simulations: LCR expressions are accurate, CDF bounds are tight in upper tail. MA arrays outperform single fluid antenna and fixed array systems; reduced inter-element spacing yields further gains.

Conclusion: Continuous MA arrays offer significant performance advantages over fixed arrays and single fluid antennas, with mathematical foundations established for SNR statistics and level crossing behavior.

Abstract: This paper provides a thorough mathematical analysis of continuous movable antenna (MA) arrays. Focusing on the multiple antenna case, we consider a linear antenna array with multiple fixed antenna elements that moves along a line. We assume a full, spatially coherent correlation model and continuous positioning of the array. We provide asymptotically exact approximations to the upper tail of the cumulative distribution function (cdf) of the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), considering both correlated and uncorrelated antenna elements in the array. We also obtain a novel closed-form expression for the level crossing rate (LCR) of the SNR under correlated array elements, where a non-separable two-dimensional correlation is present. The analysis is validated through simulations, confirming both the accuracy of the LCR expressions and the tightness of the cdf bounds in the upper tail. Numerical results show that the proposed MA array outperforms single fluid antenna and fixed array systems, with reduced inter-element spacing providing further performance gains.

</details>


### [569] [Analysis of Fluid Antenna Systems with Continuous Positioning and Spatial Correlation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16920)
*Gayani Siriwardana,Peter J. Smith,Himal A. Suraweera,Rajitha Senanayake*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper develops a level-crossing-rate (LCR) framework to analyze multi-user fluid antenna systems with continuous positioning, deriving asymptotically exact approximations for performance metrics like SNR, SIR, and SINR under various fading conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exact performance distributions for multi-user fluid antenna systems with continuous positioning over a track of length L under spatial correlation are analytically intractable, motivating the need for a tractable approximation framework.

Method: The authors develop a level-crossing-rate (LCR) framework to derive asymptotically exact approximations and tight bounds for the cumulative distribution function (cdf) of the optimized metric. They analyze single fluid antenna scenarios and extend to multi-antenna receiver layouts with maximum-ratio combining.

Result: High-threshold tail probabilities scale linearly with L; the required L to neutralize a co-channel interferer is derived; about one wavelength of movement can reduce outage by three orders of magnitude. Monte Carlo results validate accuracy.

Conclusion: The LCR framework provides actionable insights for fluid antenna system design, showing significant performance gains with limited movement and offering tractable approximations for otherwise intractable distributions.

Abstract: We analyze multi-user fluid antenna systems with continuous positioning over a track of length L under a spatial correlation model, where exact performance distributions become analytically intractable. We develop a level-crossing-rate (LCR) framework that yields asymptotically exact approximations and tight bounds for the cumulative distribution function (cdf) of the optimized metric S* = sup_{0 <= l <= L}, where S(l) denotes the performance metric at antenna position l. For a single fluid antenna, we characterize the cdfs of signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), signal-to interference ratio (SIR) and signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) under Rayleigh fading and extend the approach to Ricean desired channels. We further treat two multi-antenna receiver layouts with maximum-ratio combining: (i) a fluid antenna with a fixed antenna and (ii) a two-element moving array, deriving new LCR results for the practically important case where array-element correlation and positional correlation are inherently coupled. The analysis provides actionable insights: high-threshold tail probabilities scale linearly with L, we derive the required L to neutralize a co-channel interferer, and we show that about one wavelength of movement can reduce outage by three orders of magnitude. Monte Carlo results validate the accuracy across the considered scenarios and regimes.

</details>


### [570] [Estimating Target Doppler in Unsynchronized Multistatic ISAC Deployments with Mobile Nodes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16934)
*Zaman Bhalli,Michele Rossi,Joerg Widmer,Marco Canil*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents the first method to estimate target Doppler frequency in multistatic ISAC systems with mobile and asynchronous nodes using at least 4 RXs, without external reflectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Doppler estimation in real-world ISAC scenarios is challenging due to clock asynchrony between TX and RXs combined with node mobility, which introduces additional Doppler components and phase offsets that distort target-induced frequency shifts. Existing works addressed these challenges separately or relied on external reference reflectors.

Method: The method leverages invariance of phase offsets across multipath components and exploits geometrical relationships, requiring at least 4 Receivers (RXs) in a configuration with a mobile TX and multiple static RXs, without external reflectors.

Result: Numerical simulations in various scenarios show the proposed solution is a valid approach for estimating target Doppler shifts in unsynchronized multistatic ISAC deployments with mobile nodes.

Conclusion: The first feasible method for target Doppler estimation in mobile/asynchronous multistatic ISAC systems is presented, solving the problem without external reflectors when at least 4 RXs are available.

Abstract: Integrated Sensing And Communication (ISAC) is recognized as a key enabler for future 6th Generation (6G) networks, combining communication capabilities with pervasive sensing. In such systems, the estimation of the Doppler shift plays a crucial role for target characterization. However, typical real-world ISAC scenarios largely involve bistatic or multistatic configurations and mobile ISAC nodes. Under these conditions, Doppler estimation becomes particularly challenging, as clock asynchrony between the Transmitter (TX) and the Receivers (RXs), combined with their mobility, introduces additional Doppler components and phase offsets that distort or disrupt the target-induced frequency shift. Existing works have considered these challenges separately or relied on external reference reflectors. In this paper, we present the first method to estimate the Doppler frequency of a target with mobile and asynchronous ISAC nodes in a multistatic configuration, considering the case of a mobile TX and multiple static RXs, and without leveraging any external reflector. By leveraging the invariance of the phase offsets across multipath components and exploiting geometrical relationships, we show that the problem is solvable if at least 4 RXs are present. We evaluate the proposed solution through numerical simulations in various scenarios, showing that it is a valid approach for estimating target Doppler shifts in unsynchronized multistatic ISAC deployments with mobile nodes.

</details>


### [571] [Design and Practical Validation of a Novel Modulation Scheme for RIS Detection and Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17009)
*Aymen Khaleel,Adam Umra,Aydin Sezgin*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new modulation scheme for RIS detection and identification that uses passive beamforming and beam sweeping to reduce miss-detection and false-alarm probabilities, validated through simulations and prototype experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RIS-ID is critical for BS to adaptively assign appropriate RIS to UE, but existing methods suffer from high miss-detection and false-alarm probabilities.

Method: Proposes a modulation scheme leveraging RIS's passive beamforming gain for over-the-air modulation of RIS ID, combined with passive beam sweeping to extend detection coverage in angular space.

Result: Validated through computer simulations and prototype experiments, demonstrating effectiveness in reducing miss-detection and false-alarm probabilities.

Conclusion: The proposed modulation scheme effectively enhances the reliability of RIS-ID.

Abstract: The reconfigurable intelligent surfaces detection and identification (RISs-ID) is a critical process that enables a base station (BS) to adaptively assign the appropriate RIS to a given user equipment (UE). This work proposes a novel modulation scheme to enhance the reliability of RIS-ID by reducing the miss detection and false-alarm probabilities. Specifically, we leverage the RIS's passive beamforming gain to enable over-the-air modulation of the RIS ID, combined with passive beam sweeping to extend detection coverage in angular space. The proposed modulation scheme is validated through computer simulations and prototype experiments, demonstrating its effectiveness in reducing miss-detection and false-alarm probabilities.

</details>


### [572] [Connectionless Bluetooth LE Channel Sounding via PAwR for Scalable and Energy-Efficient Ranging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17094)
*Leon Schex,Markus Cremer,Uwe Dettmar*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a connectionless Bluetooth Channel Sounding architecture using LE CS Test commands with Periodic Advertising with Responses (PAwR), eliminating per-pair connection overhead. It uses a deterministic channel sequence and distributed peer-to-peer assignment matrix to enable scalable, collision-free ranging with 69% reduced payload, 40-48% lower steady-state power consumption, ~98% lower initiation overhead, and up to 88% total charge reduction over 24h, supporting up to 16,384 devices per PAwR train.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bluetooth Core Spec v6.0's Channel Sounding (CS) for high-accuracy ranging is tied to LE ACL connections, which introduces initiation overhead, limits concurrent partners, and transfers results over the connection itself. The authors aim to overcome these limitations with a connectionless approach.

Method: The architecture combines LE CS Test commands with PAwR. A Central Orchestrator, Gateway, and Tag/Anchor devices coordinate via a Peer-to-Peer Assignment Matrix distributed through PAwR. Deterministic channel sequences prevent collisions; a compact data plane reduces payload by ~69% by omitting recoverable fields; results are reported through PAwR response slots.

Result: On Nordic nRF54L15: deterministic channel management eliminates collision-induced outliers. At 1s update cycle: 40-48% reduction in steady-state active charge, ~98% reduction in per-switch initiation overhead, up to 88% lower total charge over 24h. Capacity projected at 16,384 active devices per PAwR train.

Conclusion: The connectionless CS architecture significantly improves scalability, power efficiency, and flexibility over standard connection-based CS, enabling massive parallel ranging with minimal overhead.

Abstract: Bluetooth Core Specification v6.0 introduces Channel Sounding (CS) as a standardized high-accuracy ranging primitive for Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE). However, standard CS usage remains tied to per-pair LE asynchronous connection logical transport (LE ACL) connections, which adds initiation overhead, limits concurrent partners, and transfers results over the connection itself. We present a connectionless CS architecture that combines the LE CS Test command with Periodic Advertising with Responses (PAwR). A Central Orchestrator, a Gateway, and synchronized Tag/Anchor devices coordinate measurement configurations and aggregate results at the application layer. Each device derives its role, channel sequence, and response slot assignment from its device index and a Peer-to-Peer Assignment Matrix distributed via PAwR. The deterministic channel sequence prevents same-step collisions across parallel CS procedures, while matrix updates reconfigure arbitrary device-to-device pairings within a PAwR subevent group. A compact data plane omits fields recoverable from the shared measurement configuration and reduces the serialized ranging-data payload by approximately 69%, enabling result reporting through PAwR response slots. A proof-of-concept evaluation on the Nordic nRF54L15 platform shows that deterministic channel management eliminates the collision-induced outliers observed under simulated dense-deployment channel overlaps. At a 1 s update cycle, the architecture reduces steady-state active charge by 40-48% relative to a fair connected baseline and cuts per-switch initiation overhead by approximately 98%. Under per-cycle partner switching, these effects combine to up to 88% lower total charge over a 24 h horizon. An empirical timing model projects a capacity upper bound of 16,384 active devices per PAwR train at four CS procedures per device per cycle, 37 channels, and a single antenna path.

</details>


### [573] [A reversed solar illumination dependence of unintended emission from Starlink Direct-to-Cell satellites at 72-234 MHz with the EDA2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17150)
*Haofan Dong,Houtianfu Wang,Hanlin Cai,Ozgur B. Akan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Starlink Direct-to-Cell (DTC) satellites emit 1.45x brighter unintended electromagnetic radiation at sub-300 MHz than Ku-only v2-Mini satellites, show unique polarization anomalies, and exhibit a reversed bright/dark flux ratio, with a narrow ~24 kHz excess at 230.627 MHz attributable to an active on-board source rather than passive mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize the previously unstudied unintended electromagnetic radiation (UEMR) from second-generation Starlink Direct-to-Cell satellites at sub-300 MHz frequencies, given their new cellular connectivity payload.

Method: Reanalyzed 112,534 detections from 1,806 Starlink satellites (175 DTC vs. 1,623 Ku-only v2-Mini) using EDA2 at 21 frequencies between 72.685-234.375 MHz, with statistical comparisons (Cliff's delta, Benjamini-Hochberg corrections) and fine-channel inspection.

Result: DTC satellites have 1.45x higher flux density (p=2.6e-11), significant polarization anomalies in 11/21 channels, a reversed illuminated/eclipsed flux ratio (0.47 vs 1.18 for Ku-only), and a narrow ~24 kHz excess at 230.627 MHz that is not clock-related, unresolved, and heterogeneously expressed.

Conclusion: The DTC UEMR originates from an active on-board source with higher effective duty cycle at lower equilibrium temperature, not from passive mechanisms scaling with solar photocurrent, and three falsifiable tests rule out clock fundamentals, uniform thermal scaling, or a few permanently bright units.

Abstract: Second-generation Starlink Direct-to-Cell (DTC) satellites carry an additional payload for direct cellular phone connectivity whose unintended electromagnetic radiation (UEMR) at sub-300 MHz frequencies has not been individually characterised. We reanalyse 112,534 detections from 1,806 Starlink satellites observed with the Engineering Development Array version 2 (EDA2) at 21 frequencies between 72.685 and 234.375 MHz (Grigg et al. 2025), separating 175 DTC and 1,623 Ku-only v2-Mini comparison satellites via the McDowell General Catalogue (McDowell 2020). DTC satellites emit a range-corrected flux density 1.45x that of the Ku-only comparison (Cliff's delta = +0.30, p = 2.6e-11). At 230.469 MHz the XX detection fraction reaches 0.811 against a 0.481 baseline (p ~ 1e-274), and 11 of 21 frequency channels show Benjamini-Hochberg-significant polarisation anomalies. The DTC population is brighter in eclipse than in sunlight (illuminated/eclipsed flux density ratio 0.47) while the Ku-only comparison shows the opposite sense (1.18); the reversal persists across altitude, sub-satellite latitude, frequency, and launch-epoch matching. The reversal strongly disfavours UEMR mechanisms that scale monotonically with instantaneous solar photocurrent and favours an active on-board source whose effective duty cycle is larger at lower equilibrium temperature. Within the 230.469 MHz coarse channel, fine-channel inspection isolates the excess to a single ~24 kHz bin near 230.627 MHz, tail-driven and absent at five control channels. Three falsifiable mechanism-discrimination tests show this feature is not coincident with the LOFAR-resolved Bassa et al. (2024) clock fundamentals, is unresolved at the EDA2 24 kHz resolution, and is heterogeneously expressed across the v2-Mini fleet rather than driven by a few permanently bright units or by uniform thermal scaling.

</details>


### [574] [Leveraging Deep Reinforcement Learning for Clustered Cell-Free Networking Over User Mobility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17266)
*Ouyang Zhou,Junyuan Wang,Bo Qian,Antonio Pérez Yuste,Yusheng Ji*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a DRL-based DDPG-C²F framework for clustered cell-free networking that adapts to user mobility with minimal channel estimation (one channel per AP), outperforming existing methods across various scenarios while reducing handover cost and being robust to dynamic user changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing clustered cell-free networking methods rely on clustering algorithms, graph partitioning, or continuous optimization using full channel information between all users and all APs. This incurs huge channel measurement and computational costs, making them impractical for dynamic environments where optimal network partitions change frequently due to user mobility. Moreover, existing methods are often problem-specific with different optimization algorithms.

Method: The authors propose a deep deterministic policy gradient based clustered cell-free networking (DDPG-C²F) framework using deep reinforcement learning. It requires only one channel estimation per AP as neural network input, drastically reducing measurement costs. The framework is adaptable to various application scenarios with different objectives and constraints.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate that DDPG-C²F outperforms existing baselines in all tested scenarios. It reduces handover cost under user mobility and is robust to dynamic scenarios with random user joining or leaving.

Conclusion: The DRL-based approach enables rapid adaptation to user movement with minimal channel information, making clustered cell-free networking practical for real-world dynamic environments.

Abstract: Clustered cell-free networking paves a new way for enabling scalable joint transmission among access points (APs) by partitioning the whole network into non-overlapping subnetworks. Previous works adopted clustering algorithms, graph partitioning methods or conventional continuous optimization theories to partition a network based on the channels between all users and all APs, resulting in huge channel measurement and computational costs. This makes these methods difficult to be implemented in practical systems since the optimal network partition could vary frequently due to user mobility. In addition, existing methods were usually designed for specific clustered cell-free networking problems with different optimization algorithms employed. In this paper, we leverage deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for clustered cell-free networking so as to rapidly adapt to user movements in dynamic environments, and propose a deep deterministic policy gradient based clustered cell-free networking (DDPG-C$^{2}$F) framework that can be adapted in various application scenarios. Moreover, in our framework, only one single channel needs to be estimated at each AP as the input of the neural network, which greatly reduces the channel measurement costs for clustered cell-free networking, and the training and inference costs of our framework. The proposed DDPG-C$^{2}$F framework is then applied to various clustered cell-free networking problems with different objectives and constraints to demonstrate its performance. Simulation results show that our framework outperforms existing baselines in all scenarios. Moreover, we show that the proposed framework can reduce the handover cost over user mobility, and is robust to dynamic scenarios with random user joining or leaving.

</details>


### [575] [UPSim: UxNB Propagation Simulator for 3D Map-Driven FR3 Deployments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17378)
*Evgenii Vinogradov*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: UPSim is a semi-deterministic FR3 air-to-ground propagation simulator for UAV networks that uses shadow projection and deterministic visibility regions to achieve scalable, spatially consistent channel modeling, verified against ray tracing data with the 3D-GloBFP dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a scalable middle ground between expensive full ray tracing and purely stochastic channel generation for mobility-aware UAV network planning in FR3 bands.

Method: Derives deterministic visibility regions from 3D building geometry via shadow projection, augments with LOS-specific and altitude-aware path loss, correlated large-scale fading, and small-scale fading, calibrated against FR3 ray tracing using the 3D-GloBFP dataset.

Result: Accurately reproduces empirical channel distributions and supports route-based analysis of channel evolution, exposing trajectory-level statistics like outage distances over complex urban layouts.

Conclusion: UPSim offers a highly scalable, practical solution for mobility-aware planning and radio-map construction in aerial access scenarios, bridging the gap between full ray tracing and stochastic models.

Abstract: We introduce UPSim (UxNB Propagation Simulator), a ray tracing-calibrated, semi-deterministic solution for spatially consistent FR3 air-to-ground propagation modeling in uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) networks. Instead of launching rays for every receiver position, UPSim derives deterministic visibility regions from 3D building geometry via shadow projection. It then augments these regions with line-of-sight (LOS) state-specific and altitude-aware path loss, correlated large-scale fading, and small-scale fading. Calibration and validation against FR3 ray tracing data using the global 3D-GloBFP building dataset demonstrate that UPSim accurately reproduces empirical channel distributions. Furthermore, the resulting maps support route-based analysis of channel evolution over complex urban layouts, exposing critical trajectory-level statistics such as outage distances. Consequently, UPSim offers a highly scalable, practical middle ground between computationally expensive full ray tracing and purely stochastic channel generation for mobility-aware planning and radio-map construction in aerial access scenarios.

</details>


### [576] [FPGA-Based Experimental Analysis of Fixed-Point Precision Impact on SOP Estimation in Coherent Communications Receivers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17521)
*Geraldo Gomes,Rafael Vieira,Hani Kbashi,Aleksandr Donodin,Shekhar Saxena,Stylianos Sygletos,Ian Phillips,Jaroslaw E. Prilepsky,Mikael Mazur,Sergei K. Turitsyn,Pedro Freire*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper experimentally evaluates the trade-off between sensing and communication performance in a fixed-point precision MIMO equalizer using FPGA, finding that 7-bit precision yields significant improvements in noise floor and angular error, but further increases cause communication performance to saturate while hardware complexity continues to grow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify the trade-off between sensing accuracy and communication performance when varying fixed-point precision in MIMO equalizers implemented on FPGA.

Method: Experimental evaluation of a fixed-point precision MIMO equalizer on FPGA, measuring noise floor, angular error, communication performance, and hardware complexity across different bit precisions.

Result: At 7-bit precision, noise floor drops by 100x and angular error by 63%. However, communication performance saturates beyond this point while hardware complexity continues to increase.

Conclusion: There exists an optimal precision point (around 7-bit) where sensing benefits are significant, but further precision increases offer diminishing returns for communication at the cost of higher hardware complexity.

Abstract: We experimentally evaluated the sensing-communication trade-off from the fixed-point precision MIMO equalizer using FPGA. At 7-bit, noise floor drops 100x and angular error 63%, but the communication performance saturates while the hardware complexity rises.

</details>


### [577] [DFT-s-OFDM with Chirping for Integrated Sensing and Communications in 6G and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17612)
*Yujie Liu,Yong Liang Guan,David González G.,Halim Yanikomeroglu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper reviews communication and radar waveforms for 6G, proposes DFT-s-OFDM with chirping as a promising single-carrier chirping waveform for ISAC, and outlines future research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for advanced waveforms that can support integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) in 6G and beyond, covering both terrestrial and non-terrestrial networks.

Method: The paper provides an overview of standardized communication waveforms and chirp-based radar/IoT waveforms, then introduces and analyzes DFT-s-OFDM with chirping from both communication and sensing perspectives.

Result: DFT-s-OFDM with chirping is identified as a promising single-carrier chirping waveform with advantageous properties for both communication and sensing.

Conclusion: Future research directions are outlined to further explore the potential and opportunities of chirped DFT-s-OFDM for ISAC in 6G and beyond.

Abstract: The sixth generation (6G) of mobile communications and beyond is expected to enable advanced functionalities, such as integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), while involving diverse terminal/user equipment types from terrestrial to non-terrestrial networks. As waveforms are acknowledged as a fundamental technology driving 6G and beyond, this article presents a contribution in this technical domain. First, it provides an overview of several standardized communication waveforms, as well as chirp-based waveforms for radar sensing and Internet of Things (IoT) applications. This article then presents single-carrier chirping waveform: discrete Fourier transform spread orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (DFT-s-OFDM) with chirping. Its fundamental principles, key properties, performances, and advantages are examined from both communication and sensing perspectives. Finally, several future research directions are outlined to further explore its potential and opportunities for ISAC.

</details>


### [578] [A Framework of Near-Field Communication with Different Array Geometries: Analysis, Optimization, and General Channel Estimation Algorithms Based on Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17690)
*Kangda Zhi,Yi Song,Tianyu Yang,Tuo Wu,Tengjiao Wang,Songyan Xue,Fangzhou Wu,Giuseppe Caire*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper establishes a framework for near-field communication in XL-MIMO systems, proposing a denoising autoencoder-aided AMP algorithm for channel estimation and an optimization algorithm for joint array geometry and hybrid precoding design, demonstrating the benefits of modular curved arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of near-field spatial non-stationary channels in XL-MIMO systems and understand how different array geometries (especially curved arrays) impact communication performance.

Method: Formulates near-field spatial non-stationary channel models for uniform and modular curved arrays; proposes a denoising autoencoder (AE)-aided approximated message passing (AMP) algorithm for channel estimation with a theoretical replica bound; and develops an optimization algorithm for joint array geometry and hybrid precoding design.

Result: The AE-AMP algorithm provides robust and generalizable channel estimation outperforming conventional and deep-learning-based benchmarks; modular curved arrays improve data rates over planar arrays.

Conclusion: Array curvature significantly extends the near-field region for cell edges, and the proposed learning-based framework effectively enables high-performance XL-MIMO systems with curved arrays.

Abstract: This work establishes a framework of near-field communication under different array geometries of extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO). We first formulate the near-field spatial non-stationary channel model which is characterized by the distance between the user and each antenna on uniform and modular curved arrays. By fixing the total number of antennas while varying the degree of curvature, we investigate a fair case where the horizontal arc length of the curved array is the same as the planar array. We explicitly unveil the non-trivial impact of array curvature on extending the near-field region for cell edges. Then, for arbitrary array geometries and arbitrary-field channels, we estimate the spatial-domain channel by tackling a compressed sensing problem with a learned regularizer. Without relying on specific codebooks, we propose a denoising autoencoder (AE)-aided approximated message passing (AMP) algorithm and provide the corresponding theoretical replica bound. Finally, based on the estimated channel, we propose an optimization algorithm to maximize the sum user rate for sub-connected XL-MIMO systems by jointly designing the array geometry and hybrid precoding in the downlink. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed AE-AMP algorithm can effectively estimate the spatial non-stationary near-field channels with robustness and generalities compared to several conventional and deep-learning-based benchmarks. The improvement of data rate by using modular curved arrays with the estimated channel is also validated.

</details>


### [579] [Observation Modeling of Reference--Background Residuals in Single-Snapshot FDA-MIMO-GPR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17728)
*Yisu Yan,Jifeng Guo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the impact of reference-background medium mismatches in single-snapshot FDA-MIMO-GPR imaging under the distorted Born approximation, showing that residual errors cause cross-frequency and cross-channel covariance, leading to low-dimensional pseudo-anomaly artifacts after Tikhonov reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model and characterize the effect of reference medium vs. true background medium mismatches in distorted-Born-approximation-based GPR imaging, specifically for single-snapshot FDA-MIMO-GPR systems.

Method: The paper (1) establishes a reference-state observation model under distorted Born approximation, (2) defines the reference-background residual, (3) derives its response using Cole-Cole dispersive mapping, reference propagation kernels, and FDA frequency-transmit organization, (4) constructs observation-domain covariance and analyzes its off-diagonal channel-block structure, and (5) uses a standard Tikhonov estimator to analyze error propagation.

Result: Numerical results show pronounced cross-frequency and cross-channel covariance under mismatched reference states, which after Tikhonov reconstruction appear as low-dimensional, concentrated pseudo-anomaly errors, primarily caused by multiple transmit-receive channels jointly observing the same residual field and organized by FDA space-frequency coding.

Conclusion: The reference-background residual should be explicitly modeled in reference-state selection, background suppression, and channel-covariance analysis for single-snapshot FDA-MIMO-GPR.

Abstract: Reference media are widely used in distorted-Born-approximation-based GPR imaging to represent partially known propagation effects. When the true host background differs from the chosen reference medium, the difference enters the observations and propagates into anomaly estimates. For single-snapshot FDA-MIMO-GPR, this paper establishes a reference-state observation model under the distorted Born approximation and defines that difference as the reference--background medium residual, namely, the effective residual between the reference medium and the physical background medium. Hereafter, this quantity is abbreviated as the reference--background residual. Its response is derived from the Cole--Cole dispersive mapping, the reference propagation kernels, and the FDA frequency--transmit organization. The paper then constructs its observation-domain covariance, analyzes the off-diagonal channel-block structure, and uses a standard Tikhonov estimator to show how the response transfers to reconstruction error and covariance over an anomaly candidate region. Numerical results show pronounced cross-frequency and cross-channel covariance under mismatched reference states. After Tikhonov reconstruction, these structures appear as low-dimensional, concentrated pseudo-anomaly errors. Right-hand-side coherence and inter-channel correlation arise mainly because multiple transmit--receive channels jointly observe the same residual field, while FDA space-frequency coding determines their organization in the observation and reconstruction domains. The reference--background residual should therefore be modeled explicitly in reference-state selection, background suppression, and channel-covariance analysis for single-snapshot FDA-MIMO-GPR.

</details>


### [580] [Curriculum-Guided Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Intelligence for Multi-UAV Cooperative ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17905)
*Kang Yan,Luping Xiang,Kang Zheng,Jienan Chen,Qiang Liu,Kun Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a multi-UAV cooperative ISAC system with heterogeneous-agent collaboration (UAVs + ground BS) for joint sensing, tracking, and communication, using a curriculum-based HAPPO algorithm to optimize trajectory and beamforming, achieving >30% sensing improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ISAC studies focus on single-UAV or homogeneous-agent designs, lacking solutions for heterogeneous multi-UAV collaborative scenarios in 6G low-altitude economy applications.

Method: Formulates a PCRB minimization problem under communication constraints with joint trajectory-beamforming optimization, solved by a curriculum-based heterogeneous-agent proximal policy optimization (C-HAPPO) algorithm using Kronecker/QR decomposition for action dimensionality reduction.

Result: Achieves >30% improvement in sensing performance, faster convergence, and higher tracking accuracy compared to existing baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed C-HAPPO algorithm demonstrates scalability and effectiveness for complex multi-UAV ISAC scenarios in 6G networks.

Abstract: Seamlessly unifying communication and sensing, sixth-generation (6G) networks are poised to transform into intelligent platforms with high spectral-energy efficiency and real-time environmental awareness. In the low-altitude economy, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) enable air-ground integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) for applications such as logistics and inspection, yet most studies focus on single-UAV or homogeneous-agent designs. In contrast, this paper proposes a multi-UAV cooperative ISAC system that enables heterogeneous-agent collaboration between multiple UAVs and a ground base station (BS) for joint target sensing, tracking, and communication. The system is formulated as a posterior Cramer-Rao bound (PCRB) minimization problem under communication performance constraints, utilizing joint trajectory-beamforming optimization. To tackle the NP-hard nature of this problem, we design a curriculum-based heterogeneous-agent proximal policy optimization (C-HAPPO) algorithm, where curriculum learning guides progressive policy refinement and Kronecker/QR decomposition mitigates action dimensionality. Simulation results show that the proposed approach achieves more than a 30% improvement in sensing performance, faster convergence, and higher tracking accuracy than existing baselines, demonstrating its scalability and effectiveness for complex multi-UAV ISAC scenarios.

</details>


### [581] [A Computationally Efficient Reciprocal Effective Roughness Model for Diffuse Scattering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17988)
*Giacomo Melloni,Enrico M. Vitucci,Vittorio Degli Esposti,Samuel Berweger,Jack Chuang,Camillo Gentile,Nada Golmie*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a directive, reciprocal diffuse scattering model for ray-tracing that reduces computational cost by an order of magnitude while maintaining or slightly improving accuracy compared to the Effective Roughness approach, validated across eight materials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ray-tracing for electromagnetic propagation modeling becomes computationally prohibitive as digital twin environments scale to millions of facets with continuously updating material parameters, especially at mmWave/sub-THz frequencies where diffuse scattering contributes significantly (up to 40% of received power), and existing models like Effective Roughness (ER) are physically consistent but computationally expensive when directive lobes or iterative parameter tuning are needed.

Method: The authors develop a directive, reciprocal diffuse scattering model that preserves the structure of the Effective Roughness (ER) approach while enabling an order-of-magnitude reduction in computational cost.

Result: Validation across eight materials shows no loss in accuracy, with a slight improvement observed, and computational cost reduced by an order of magnitude.

Conclusion: The proposed model provides a scalable and physically meaningful solution for ray-tracing in scenarios where diffuse scattering is non-negligible.

Abstract: Ray-tracing (RT) has become central to site-specific electromagnetic propagation modeling in dynamic complex environments. Yet its computational burden grows sharply as high-fidelity digital twins of these environments scale to millions of facets whose material parameters must be continuously updated as the environment changes. The challenge is amplified at mmWave and sub-THz frequencies, where surface roughness becomes comparable to the wavelength and so diffuse scattering can account for up to 40% of the received power, making accurate yet tractable models essential. The popular Effective Roughness (ER) approach offers physical consistency but become increasingly costly when highly directive lobes are required or when parameters must be iteratively tuned. This communication introduces a directive, reciprocal diffuse scattering model that preserves the structure of the ER while enabling an order-of-magnitude reduction in computational cost. Validation across eight materials shows no loss in accuracy - and a slight improvement - demonstrating a scalable and physically meaningful solution for RT in scenarios where diffuse scattering is non-negligible.

</details>


### [582] [PRiSE-EEG: A Prior-Guided Foundation Model with Depth-Stratified Experts for Cross-Paradigm EEG Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18085)
*Wei Xiong,Jiangtong Li,Kun Zhu,Jie Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: PRiSE-EEG is a prior-guided EEG foundation model with CKA-calibrated Depth-Stratified Experts that uses depth-wise expert allocation (shared in shallow layers, specialized in deep layers) based on CKA analysis, achieving strong cross-paradigm performance on 12 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EEG foundation models use uniform adaptation mechanisms across paradigms, ignoring optimization conflicts and depth-wise specialization patterns observed through gradient similarity (substantial conflicts) and CKA analysis (shallow layers preserve similarity, deeper layers specialize).

Method: PRiSE-EEG: (1) Forms continuous multi-channel EEG patches using weak static cortical/network priors and dynamic short-time channel interactions. (2) Allocates shared vs. specialized experts across MoE Transformer blocks via sigmoid mapping from layer-wise CKA sharedness, preserving common regularities in early blocks and specialized capacity in later blocks.

Result: Strong cross-paradigm performance on 12 public EEG benchmarks. Compact ablations show CKA-derived expert allocation outperforms dense Transformers, uniform MoE, and manually fixed expert ratios.

Conclusion: CKA-calibrated depth-stratified expert allocation effectively balances shared and specialized representations in EEG foundation models, outperforming uniform adaptation approaches.

Abstract: EEG foundation models aim to learn reusable representations across heterogeneous paradigms, yet existing approaches often use uniform adaptation mechanisms and are typically reported under separate downstream fine-tuning protocols. In this work, we first analyze dense EEG Transformers from two complementary perspectives. Gradient similarity across six downstream datasets reveals substantial optimization conflicts among EEG paradigms, while CKA analysis on mixed-paradigm batches shows a consistent depth-wise transition: shallow layers preserve stronger cross-paradigm similarity, whereas deeper layers become increasingly specialized. Motivated by these findings, we propose \textbf{PRiSE-EEG}, a prior-guided EEG foundation model with CKA-calibrated Depth-Stratified Experts. PRiSE-EEG forms continuous multi-channel EEG patches using weak static cortical and network priors and dynamic short-time channel interactions, then allocates shared and specialized experts across MoE Transformer blocks according to a sigmoid mapping from layer-wise CKA sharedness. This design preserves common EEG regularities in early blocks while assigning more specialized capacity to later task-specific transformations. Experiments on 12 public EEG benchmarks show strong cross-paradigm performance under matched protocols. Compact ablations further show that CKA-derived expert allocation improves over dense Transformers, uniform MoE, and manually fixed shared-specific expert ratios.

</details>


### [583] [From Coverage to Sensing: ISAC meets FR3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18120)
*Ahmad Bazzi,Florian Gast,Fan Liu,Shi Jin,Gerhard Fettweis,Marwa Chafii*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper outlines a roadmap for integrating native sensing capabilities into future 6G systems using FR3 midband spectrum, covering aspects from legacy radar coexistence, hierarchical beam alignment, squint mitigation, payload-based sensing, channel simulation, and massive MIMO as reconfigurable sensor arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable FR3 spectrum for both high-throughput communications and native sensing services (localization, detection, situational awareness) in future 6G, bridging the gap from today's coverage-oriented deployments.

Method: The paper proposes: (1) time-multiplexed FR2 radar coordination under 6G MAC as radar-as-a-service; (2) hierarchical FR3 beam-alignment (coarse at lower freq, refinement at upper FR3); (3) analysis of wideband squint phenomena; (4) payload-based sensing beyond pilots; (5) calibrated channel simulators and digital twins for ISAC.

Result: Quantified sensing and communication capabilities via range-angle Cramér-Rao bounds in near-field; identified intra/inter-beam squint in wideband FR3 arrays; demonstrated feasibility of sensing from payload resource elements.

Conclusion: FR3 can serve as a native sensing-communication platform in 6G, with massive MIMO and distributed deployments acting as large reconfigurable sensor arrays, supported by digital twins for algorithm training and evaluation.

Abstract: Future 6G systems are expected to exploit upper midband spectrum in frequency range 3 (FR3) not only for high throughput communications, but also for sensing services such as localization, detection, and situational awareness. The following paper develops a concrete path from today's coverage-oriented deployments to FR3 networks that treat sensing as a native function. We first show how existing FR2 radars can be time-multiplexed and coordinated under a $6$G medium access control as radar-as-a-service, forming a bridge between legacy sensing and network-managed integrated sensing and communications (ISAC). We then propose a hierarchical FR3 beam-alignment strategy in which coarse access occurs at lower frequencies and refinement occurs at upper FR3, and quantify the resulting sensing and communication capabilities via range-angle Cram{é}r-Rao bounds in the near field. We identify intra- and inter-beam squint phenomena specific to wideband FR3 arrays, and discuss design approaches to mitigate them. On the signal-processing side, we argue that FR3 sensing cannot rely solely on pilot resources and discuss how much sensing information can be extracted from payload resource elements. We further highlight the role of calibrated FR3 channel simulators and real-time models as the core of wireless digital twins for training and evaluating ISAC algorithms, and discuss how massive MIMO and dense or distributed deployments at FR3 naturally act as large reconfigurable sensor arrays.

</details>


### [584] [R$^{2}$Net: 2D Deep Residual Learning with Height Embedding for 3D Radio Map Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18127)
*Huiting Rao,Junyuan Wang,Huiling Zhu,Cheng-Xiang Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a 2D radio residual network (R²Net) for 3D radio map estimation by embedding height information into 2D images, with specialized variants for indoor (R²Net-In) and outdoor (R²Net-Out) scenarios. It also releases a new 3D indoor radio map dataset (3DiRM3200).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing radio map estimation methods are limited to 2D (assuming same receiver height), but radio maps vary significantly at different heights. Accurate 3D radio map estimation is needed for applications like cellular handover.

Method: 1) Embed height information into 2D images; 2) Propose a 2D radio residual network (R²Net) for 3D estimation; 3) Design R²Net-In for indoor (capturing penetration loss) and R²Net-Out for outdoor (capturing diffraction loss); 4) Create a 3D indoor radio map dataset (3DiRM3200).

Result: R²Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in estimation accuracy, computational/storage costs, and inference speed.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses 3D radio map estimation, and the new dataset fills a gap in publicly available 3D radio map data.

Abstract: Acquiring channel knowledge is required by many applications. For instance, handover in cellular networks is mainly decided based on the knowledge of pathloss. In contrast to traditional statistical distance-determined models that might provide misleading pathloss estimates, researchers started to explore deep learning methods recently to accurately estimate the radio map that characterizes the spatial distribution of pathloss according to the specific physical wireless propagation environment. However, existing works mainly focused on 2D radio map estimation by assuming that all receivers are at the same height. In fact, radio maps could be significantly different at different receiver heights, highlighting the importance of 3D radio map estimation. In this paper, we first propose a method to embed height information into 2D images, and then propose a general 2D radio residual network (R$^{2}$Net) for 3D radio map estimation. Since pathloss exhibits different characteristics in indoor and outdoor scenarios, we specifically propose R$^{2}$Net-In for indoor scenarios and R$^{2}$Net-Out for outdoor scenarios to better capture penetration loss and diffraction loss, respectively. Extensive experimental results show that our R$^{2}$Net significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art benchmarks in terms of estimation accuracy, computational and storage costs, and inference speed. In addition, due to the lack of publicly available 3D radio map datasets, a 3D indoor radio map dataset (3DiRM3200) is created, which took more than $1,000$ labour hours. The dataset and codes will be available at https://github.com/lighttime2023/3DiRM3200.git.

</details>


### [585] [Buffer-Parameterized Machine Learning Surrogate Models for Cross-Technology Signal Integrity Analysis and Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18170)
*Julian Withöft,Werner John,Emre Ecik,Ralf Brüning,Jürgen Götze*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a buffer-parameterized ML surrogate modeling methodology for PCB SI analysis that handles cross-technology variations without retraining, benchmarks various ML architectures, and demonstrates massive computational speedups for eye mask compliance checking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ML surrogate models for PCB signal integrity analysis rely on fixed buffer parameters, requiring costly new data generation and retraining for every technology shift. This paper aims to eliminate that inefficiency.

Method: The authors propose a buffer-parameterized ML surrogate model that treats IC buffer characteristics (clock frequency, supply voltage, rise/fall times, jitter, internal resistors/capacitors) as dynamic inputs alongside PCB parameters. They benchmark RFR/GBM, SVR/KRR, GPR, and neural networks, then validate on a 44-parameter interconnect.

Result: Anisotropic GPR excels in low-data regimes, while neural networks heavily outperform other models on large datasets. The framework achieves massive computational speedups for eye mask compliance checking compared to simulation.

Conclusion: The buffer-parameterized approach successfully enables cross-technology SI prediction without retraining, and the benchmarking provides clear guidance on optimal ML architecture selection based on data availability.

Abstract: Signal integrity (SI) analysis in printed circuit board (PCB) interconnects faces increasing complexity due to diverse integrated circuit (IC) buffer technologies, varying operating conditions, and manufacturing tolerances. Existing machine learning (ML) surrogate models for predicting SI metrics such as the inner eye contour, eye-height (EH), eye-width (EW), and transient waveform features typically rely on fixed buffer parameters, requiring costly new data generation and retraining cycles for every technology shift. This paper introduces a buffer-parameterized ML surrogate modeling methodology capable of handling cross-technology variations without retraining by treating IC buffer characteristics, e.g., clock frequency, supply voltage, rise/fall times, jitter, and internal resistors and capacitors, as dynamic model inputs alongside PCB parameters. To identify the optimal surrogate architecture for this high-dimensional space, a comprehensive benchmarking study compares tree-based methods (RFR/GBM), kernel methods (SVR/KRR), Gaussian process regression (GPR), and neural networks. The framework is subsequently validated on a complex interconnect with 44 design parameters. Results show that while anisotropic GPR excels in low-data regimes, neural networks heavily outperform other models on large datasets. Finally, the practical value of the ML surrogate models is demonstrated through a cross-technology design space exploration and optimization scenario, showcasing massive computational speedups for eye mask compliance checking compared to simulation.

</details>


### [586] [Fast 5G Signal Acquisition by Using Non-Uniform Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18189)
*Alejandro Gonzalez Garrido,Carla Amatetti*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a deterministic non-uniform (multi-coset) sampling framework for fast signal acquisition, treating acquisition as a parametric inference problem in delay-Doppler space rather than waveform reconstruction. It formulates compressed-domain acquisition via a generalized likelihood ratio test, introduces an offline design procedure for coset pattern selection, and demonstrates 2.8x–34.2x reductions in mean acquisition time for 5G NR synchronization with associated estimation penalty trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional sampling theory focuses on waveform reconstruction at Nyquist rate, but acquisition is fundamentally a parametric inference problem in delay-Doppler space. The goal is not to reconstruct the full signal but to preserve statistics needed for detection and estimation, motivating a sampling framework that directly addresses acquisition.

Method: The paper (1) formulates compressed-domain acquisition through a generalized likelihood ratio test, (2) uses multi-coset sampling with reduced correlator structures operating directly on retained samples, and (3) introduces an offline exhaustive design procedure that selects coset patterns by minimizing a cost jointly enforcing peak isolation and uniform energy coverage over the delay search interval.

Result: Evaluated on 5G NR synchronization (PSS/SSS) under worst-case Doppler, the framework achieves 2.8x to 34.2x reductions in mean acquisition time vs. uniform sampling, with measured delay/Doppler RMSE quantifying the estimation penalty from aggressive sample reduction.

Conclusion: Multi-coset sampling offers a clear complexity-performance trade-off for fast synchronization-oriented receivers, confirming its potential for compressed-domain acquisition in 5G NR and similar systems.

Abstract: This paper proposes a framework for fast signal acquisition based on deterministic non-uniform sampling, with emphasis on multi-coset architectures and receivers driven by known synchronization sequences, pilots, or preambles. Unlike conventional sampling theory, which is formulated from a waveform-reconstruction perspective, the proposed approach is derived from the observation that acquisition is fundamentally a parametric inference problem in delay-Doppler space. Accordingly, the objective is not to reconstruct the full Nyquist-rate signal, but to preserve the statistics required for detection and estimation. The paper formulates compressed-domain acquisition through a generalized likelihood ratio test and shows how multi-coset sampling leads to reduced correlator structures operating directly on the retained samples. An offline exhaustive design procedure is introduced to select the coset pattern for a given sampling ratio by minimizing a cost that jointly enforces peak isolation in the acquisition surface and uniform retained-energy coverage over the delay search interval. The framework is evaluated on 5G NR synchronization using the PSS/SSS signals under a worst-case Doppler scenario. Results show that substantial reductions in mean acquisition time can be achieved relative to uniform sampling, with measured gains ranging from 2.8x to 34.2x, depending on the selected compression ratio. The corresponding delay and Doppler root-mean-square errors quantify the estimation penalty introduced by aggressive sample reduction. These results demonstrate a clear complexity-performance trade-off and confirm the potential of multi-coset sampling for fast synchronization-oriented receivers.

</details>


### [587] [Subject-Specific Analysis of Self-Initiated Attention Shifts from EEG with Controlled Internal and External Attention Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18251)
*Yuwen Zeng,Dengzhe Hou,Zhang Zhang,Sai Sun,Yongsong Huang,Chia-huei Tseng,Satoshi Shioiri*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This study uses interpretable machine learning (SHAP) to analyze EEG features distinguishing self-initiated vs. externally instructed attention shifts, finding higher-frequency bands and frontal regions are key discriminators within a controlled paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-initiated attention shifts are crucial for voluntary behavior but difficult to study due to no explicit temporal markers. Previous work identified neural correlates, but how multi-dimensional EEG features contribute to characterization within an interpretable framework remains unclear.

Method: A machine learning approach with two analyses: (1) performance-oriented assessment of frequency-specific topographic patterns, and (2) model-based feature attribution using SHAP. Built on a previous experimental paradigm enabling controlled comparison under identical visual stimulation.

Result: Reliable within-subject classification performance was achieved. Higher-frequency bands and frontal regions contributed strongly to model decisions, though high-frequency results require cautious interpretation due to potential non-neural artifacts.

Conclusion: Interpretable machine learning is valuable for analyzing subject-specific EEG patterns in controlled settings, with potential applications in personalized and asynchronous brain-machine interfaces.

Abstract: Self-initiated attention shifts play a critical role in voluntary behavior but are difficult to study due to the absence of explicit temporal markers. While previous studies have examined their neural correlates, it remains unclear how multi-dimensional electroencephalography (EEG) features contribute to their characterization within an interpretable computational framework. In this study, we build on an experimental paradigm developed in our previous work, which enables controlled comparison between task-constrained self-initiated shifts and externally instructed shifts under identical visual stimulation. Within this setting, we investigate whether preparatory EEG activity can distinguish these two types of attention shifts. We adopt a machine learning-based approach and conduct two complementary analyses: (1) a performance-oriented assessment of frequency-specific topographic patterns, and (2) a model-based feature attribution analysis using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). These analyses provide a structured view of how spectral features across regions of interest contribute to model behavior. Our results demonstrate reliable within-subject classification performance, indicating that preparatory EEG activity contains subject-specific discriminative information within this paradigm. The analysis shows that higher-frequency bands and frontal regions contribute strongly to model decisions, although such contributions should be interpreted cautiously due to the potential influence of non-neural artifacts in high-frequency EEG signals. Overall, this work highlights the value of interpretable machine learning for analyzing subject-specific EEG signal patterns in a controlled experimental setting, with potential applications in personalized and asynchronous brain-machine interface systems.

</details>


### [588] [Mixture-of-Experts Diffusion Models for Adaptive Massive MIMO Channel Estimation via Variational Bayesian Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18325)
*Zhuorui Jiang,Jun Fang,Boyu Ning,Hongbin Li,Ying-Chang Liang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a mixture-of-experts (MoE) diffusion model framework with variational Bayesian inference for massive MIMO channel estimation, using multiple pre-trained DMs (each for a specific channel type) to automatically adapt to varying propagation environments, outperforming single-prior DM methods especially under imbalanced channel conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DM-based channel estimation methods rely on a single data-driven prior, limiting their adaptability to varying channel distributions in real-world scenarios.

Method: Proposes an MoE diffusion model framework with variational Bayesian inference, using multiple pre-trained DMs (each specialized for a specific propagation channel type) and a probabilistic graphical model where the channel is a latent variable drawn from one of these priors, jointly inferring channel and expert indicator.

Result: Evaluated on 3GPP CDL channels; achieves clear performance improvement over single-prior DM methods, especially when channel samples from different propagation environments are imbalanced.

Conclusion: The proposed MoE-DM approach with variational Bayesian inference enables automatic model adaptation for channel estimation and outperforms single-prior methods in diverse/realistic channel conditions.

Abstract: Channel estimation is essential to massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. While recent generative model-based approaches using lightweight diffusion models (DMs) have achieved superior performance, they typically rely on a single data-driven prior, which limits their adaptability to varying channel distributions in real-world scenarios. To address this deficiency, we propose a mixture-of-experts (MoE) diffusion model (DM) framework combined with variational Bayesian inference. Specifically, our approach employs multiple pre-trained DMs, with each trained on a specific type of propagation channels. We then propose a probabilistic graphical model in which the channel is modeled as a latent variable drawn from one of these candidate generative priors with a certain probability. By integrating variational Bayesian inference with DM-based data priors, the underlying channel along with the expert indicator variable are jointly inferred, thus enabling automatic model adaptation for channel estimation. The effectiveness of our approach is evaluated on 3GPP CDL channels. Simulation results demonstrate that our proposed approach achieves a clear performance improvement over the standard DM-based method that employs a single prior trained on aggregated data from all channel types, particularly when the channel samples from different propagation environments are imbalanced.

</details>


### [589] [Multi-dimensional hierarchical dictionary search for large MIMO-OFDM systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18363)
*Nay Klaimi,Philippe Mary,Luc Le Magoarou*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a low-complexity strategy to improve the atom selection step in greedy sparse recovery algorithms for massive MIMO systems, leveraging structural features to reduce computational complexity while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
