Our approach maximizes the data log-likelihood to recover the graph structure, using two normalizing-flow architectures: contractive residual flows and neural spline flows. Through experiments on synthetic and real-world data, we show that SCOUT outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both causal graph recovery and unknown target recovery across various interventional and noise settings.

</details>


### [667] [Does Weight Decay Enhance Training Stability?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16622)
*Marius Saether,Amir Kolic,Tomaso Poggio,Pierfrancesco Beneventano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether weight decay stabilizes training dynamics in deep learning, revealing it slows progressive sharpening and causes architecture-dependent phase transitions at the Edge of Stability, with CNNs dampening oscillations and MLPs showing sharpness thresholds below theoretical bounds, driven by alignment of parameter vector and sharpness gradient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the mechanism by which weight decay stabilizes training dynamics, given it is often credited with stabilizing training beyond its classical role as static regularization, and to reconcile different notions of stability in the literature.

Method: The authors analyze weight decay's effects at the Edge of Stability (EoS), examining parameter-space dynamics and loss sharpness. They develop a mathematical framework to model observed phenomena and identify the global alignment of parameter vector and sharpness gradient as the mechanistic driver.

Result: Weight decay robustly slows progressive sharpening. A striking architecture-dependent phase transition emerges: CNNs experience dampened oscillations at EoS, while MLPs exhibit sharpness stabilizing below the theoretical 2/η boundary. These effects translate to stability in function-space (NTK).

Conclusion: Weight decay does stabilize training dynamics through specific mechanisms, but curvature thresholds from convex/quadratic heuristics may not be reliable stability diagnostics under regularization. The findings reveal architecture-dependent behaviors that challenge existing theoretical understanding.

Abstract: In modern deep learning, weight decay is often credited with "stabilizing" training dynamics, diverging from its classical role as a static regularization penalty. We investigate a fundamental question: *does weight decay stabilize training dynamics, and if so, through which mechanism?* Indeed, training stability is understood through different but related notions in the literature. We consider how weight decay affects the parameter-space dynamics and loss sharpness by analyzing its effects at the \emph{Edge of Stability} (EoS). We show that weight decay robustly slows *progressive sharpening}. Furthermore, we uncover a striking architecture-dependent phase transition. In CNNs, weight decay dampens the oscillations at the EoS, while in MLPs, increasing weight decay causes a phase transition in which the sharpness stabilizes at a threshold significantly below the theoretical $\frac{2}η$ boundary. We develop a mathematical framework that accurately models these phenomena and identify the global alignment of the parameter vector and the sharpness gradient as the mechanistic driver of the phase transition. Importantly, we show that these phenomena translate into stability in terms of search in function-space (NTK). Last, this shows that curvature thresholds obtained from convex/quadratic heuristics may not be reliable stability diagnostics under regularization.

</details>


### [668] [Learning How to Cube](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16632)
*Ferhat Erata,Sam Kouteili,Thanos Typaldos,Timos Antonopoulos,Robert B. Jones,Byron Cook,Ruzica Piskac*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a neuro-symbolic post-training framework that uses MCTS-based data curation, SFT, and DPO to train a 4B transformer model for effective cubing in SAT solving, achieving performance matching the best symbolic heuristics and surpassing large frontier LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cube-and-Conquer (C&C) is effective for solving challenging SAT problems, but no prior work has shown that transformer-based models can learn effective cubing heuristics. The authors aim to bridge this gap.

Method: A neuro-symbolic post-training framework with: (1) MCTS-based data curation pipeline using symbolic heuristics to explore splitting decisions; (2) two-stage post-training: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by direct preference optimization (DPO); (3) preference data grounded in solver statistics with reasoning traces from a teacher model.

Result: A 4B-parameter model achieves pass@5 score of 53 on 100 SAT benchmarks, surpassing Claude-Sonnet-4 (50) and matching the best symbolic heuristic (53). SFT alone improves pass@5 from 46 to 51, DPO adds 2 additional benchmarks.

Conclusion: Transformers can be trained to make effective cubing decisions in a domain traditionally dominated by symbolic methods.

Abstract: Despite the effectiveness of Cube-and-Conquer (C&C) for solving challenging Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) problems, no prior work has shown that transformer-based models can learn effective cubing heuristics. We introduce a neuro-symbolic post-training framework for this task. We design an MCTS-based data curation pipeline that uses symbolic heuristics to explore splitting decisions over SAT competition formulas, producing preference data grounded in solver statistics and augmented with reasoning traces from a teacher model. Our two-stage post-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by direct preference optimization (DPO), enables a 4B-parameter model to achieve a pass@5 score of 53 on 100 SAT competition benchmarks, surpassing frontier LLMs such as Claude-Sonnet-4 (50) and matching the best symbolic heuristic (53). Ablations show that SFT alone improves pass@5 from 46 to 51, with DPO adding 2 additional benchmarks; an entropy/agreement ablation on realized first-cube decisions further shows that SFT, not DPO, accounts for the root-level decision diversity that produces complementary per-run coverage over deterministic symbolic methods. This demonstrates that transformers can be trained to make effective cubing decisions in a domain traditionally dominated by symbolic methods.

</details>


### [669] [MedMIX: Modality-Internal Expert Fusion for Multimodal Medical Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16639)
*Seungik Cho,Anqi Li,Wei Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MedMIX is a multimodal framework that handles incomplete modalities by combining intra-modality expert fusion, learned inter-modality fusion, and large-small model collaboration, achieving robust performance across three medical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal clinical prediction faces three key challenges: multiple foundation models with complementary strengths per modality, pervasive missing modalities at both training and test time, and sample-specific variation in how much each modality contributes.

Method: MedMIX combines three components: (1) intra-modality expert fusion (aggregating complementary embeddings from multiple small expert models per modality), (2) learned inter-modality fusion (fusing across available modalities adaptively), and (3) large-small model collaboration (using large teacher models during training to improve deployed representations without extra inference cost).

Result: Across OpenI, MIMIC-IV-MM, and MMIST-ccRCC benchmarks, MedMIX achieves consistently strong performance, remains robust under controlled missing-modality perturbations, and sustains robustness under cross-cohort shift on MIMIC-III.

Conclusion: MedMIX is a practical framework that unifies within-modality expert collaboration, sample-specific cross-modality fusion, and efficient large-small model collaboration while remaining robust to incomplete modalities.

Abstract: Multimodal clinical prediction faces three challenges: multiple foundation models (FMs) with complementary strengths per modality, pervasive missing modalities at training and test time, and sample-specific variation in modality contributions. We introduce MedMIX, a multimodal framework that combines intra-modality expert fusion, learned inter-modality fusion, and training-only large--small model collaboration for robust medical prediction under incomplete modalities. Within each modality, MedMIX aggregates complementary embeddings from multiple small expert models; across modalities, it performs learned fusion over available modalities; and during training, it leverages large teacher models to improve deployed representations without additional inference cost. Across three heterogeneous benchmarks (OpenI, MIMIC-IV-MM, and MMIST-ccRCC), MedMIX achieves consistently strong performance while remaining robust under controlled missing-modality perturbations, and further demonstrates sustained robustness under cross-cohort shift on MIMIC-III. These results highlight MedMIX as a practical framework that unifies within-modality expert collaboration, sample-specific cross-modality fusion, and efficient large--small model collaboration while remaining robust to incomplete modalities.

</details>


### [670] [Provably Shorter Scratchpads in Hybrid DeltaNet-Attention Decoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16640)
*Tomasz Steifer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid recurrent-attention decoders (like Qwen3-Next) have formal advantages over pure architectures, solving parity-conditioned retrieval with constant steps, while pure Gated DeltaNet cannot solve it and pure Gated Attention needs polynomial scratchpad.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether hybrid recurrent-attention architectures (combining Gated Attention and Gated DeltaNet) provide formal advantages in expressivity or efficiency over pure architectures.

Method: Define a parity-conditioned retrieval task and analyze its solvability under constant-precision assumptions across three architectures: hybrid (Qwen-style), pure Gated DeltaNet, and pure Gated Attention.

Result: The hybrid architecture solves the task with O(1) chain-of-thought steps; pure Gated DeltaNet has no solution; pure Gated Attention requires at least polynomial scratchpad.

Conclusion: Hybrid recurrent-attention architectures offer a formal expressivity and efficiency edge over both pure recurrent (DeltaNet) and pure attention-based models on certain tasks.

Abstract: We investigate the expressive power of hybrid recurrent-attention decoders, a class of architectures used in recent open-source language models such as Qwen3-Next and its successors. These models combine Gated Attention heads with recurrent Gated DeltaNet heads. Is there a formal advantage, in terms of model expressivity or efficiency, to such a hybrid architecture? We show that there is. We define parity-conditioned retrieval task and show that under constant-precision assumption, a Qwen-style hybrid of Gated DeltaNet and Gated Attention solves this task with a constant scratchpad, or equivalently $O(1)$ chain-of-thought steps. In contrast, no similar solution exists for pure Gated DeltaNet models, while pure Gated Attention requires at least a polynomial scratchpad.

</details>


### [671] [In-context learning enables continental-scale subsurface temperature prediction from sparse local observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16665)
*Daniel O'Malley,Christopher W. Johnson,Javier E. Santos,Pablo Lara,Sandro Malusà,Bharat Srikishan,John Kath,Arnab Mazumder,Mohamed Mehana,David Coblentz,Nathan DeBardeleben,Earl Lawrence,Hari Viswanathan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In-Context Earth is a transformer-based model that uses sparse local borehole measurements as context to predict continuous subsurface temperature fields across continents, achieving superior accuracy (MAE 4.7°C in US) and calibrated uncertainty, adapting to new regions with only 20 local observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continental-scale subsurface temperature knowledge is limited by the high cost and sparsity of borehole measurements, yet such information is critical for geothermal resource assessment and understanding heat transport in the shallow crust.

Method: A transformer-based model (In-Context Earth) that uses sparse local borehole observations as geological context to predict continuous temperature-at-depth fields with calibrated uncertainty, employing in-context learning.

Result: Achieves MAE of 4.7°C in the contiguous US, outperforming physics-informed and other AI models; adapts to Alberta (2.2°C), Australia (6.2°C), and UK (5.4°C) with only 20 local observations; uncertainty calibrated (KS statistic 2.5%); learns internal representations of unobserved subsurface properties.

Conclusion: In-context learning can effectively use sparse borehole observations for continental-scale subsurface characterization without requiring dense measurements or region-specific retraining.

Abstract: Continental-scale knowledge of subsurface temperature is limited by the cost and sparsity of borehole measurements, but such information is essential for geothermal resource assessment and for understanding heat transport in the shallow crust. The thermal field reflects the interaction between lithology, crustal structure, radiogenic heat production, and advective fluid flow, sometimes producing sharp anomalies that are smoothed by conventional interpolation or difficult to capture with physical models. Here we introduce In-Context Earth, a transformer-based model that uses sparse local borehole observations as geological context to predict continuous temperature-at-depth fields with calibrated uncertainty. In the contiguous United States, the model achieves a mean absolute error of 4.7 °C, outperforming the physics-informed Stanford Thermal Model, a model based on AlphaEarth embeddings, the multimodal Transparent Earth model, and universal kriging, while resolving sharper thermal gradients in geothermal provinces. Its uncertainty estimates are well calibrated, with a Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic of 2.5%. Without finetuning, the model adapts to Alberta, Australia, and the United Kingdom (UK) using only 20 local observations at inference time, maintaining high accuracy in geologically distinct test regions with a mean absolute error of 2.2 °C in Alberta, 6.2 °C in Australia, and 5.4 °C in the UK. Interpretability analyses show that the model learns internal representations of subsurface properties it never observes during training, including seismic velocities, geochemistry, and crustal structure, and uses these representations in physically consistent ways. More broadly, this work shows that in-context learning can use sparse borehole observations for continental-scale subsurface characterization, without requiring dense measurements or region-specific retraining.

</details>


### [672] [GraViti: Graph-Level Variational Autoencoders with Relaxed Permutation Invariance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16668)
*Roman Bresson,Konstantinos Divriotis,Johannes F. Lutzeyer,Iakovos Evdaimon,Michalis Vazirgiannis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraViti is a transformer-based graph-level VAE that learns compact latent representations of entire graphs, achieving SOTA reconstruction and practical generative performance with single-step decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph VAEs typically operate on node-level embeddings, lacking a true graph-level latent space for interpolation, property-guided search, and smooth downstream tasks.

Method: A transformer-based variational autoencoder that encodes entire graphs into compact latent vectors and decodes them back to graphs, with a design that leverages canonical node ordering when available.

Result: SOTA reconstruction accuracy on large datasets; solid generative performance; learns chemical domain rules; single-step decoding is lightweight yet maintains sample quality.

Conclusion: Graph-level latent spaces are effective for molecular generation, and enforcing permutation invariance can be detrimental when reliable canonical orderings exist.

Abstract: We introduce GraViti, a transformer-based graph-level variational autoencoder that maps entire graphs to compact latent vectors. This design produces a true graph-level latent space that supports smooth interpolation, property-guided search, and other downstream tasks beyond the constraints of node-level embeddings. On molecular benchmarks, GraViti learns to decode valid samples that follow the chemical constraints present in the training data, showing that the model recovers domain rules directly from graph-level representations. We also show that, in domains where a reliable canonical node ordering exists such as molecules or bayesian networks, enforcing permutation invariance can prove detrimental for consistent reconstruction. GraViti achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction accuracy on large datasets, and provides solid generative performance. Its single-step decoding offers a lightweight alternative to more complex generation pipelines while maintaining practical sample quality.

</details>


### [673] [Identify Then Project: Contrastive Learning of Latent Dynamics from Partial Observations with Port-Hamiltonian Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16682)
*Peilun Li,Kaiyuan Tan,Daniel Moyer,Thomas Beckers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-stage teacher-student framework that first learns latent dynamics via contrastive learning, then projects them onto a port-Hamiltonian submanifold to enforce physical consistency, outperforming a single-stage joint learning alternative especially in dissipative and high-dimensional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent state representations and dynamics are needed when direct observation-space modeling fails (partial/high-dimensional observations), and representation learning and physics-aware modeling are inherently coupled in such settings.

Method: A two-stage identify-then-project framework: (1) a contrastive teacher learns continuous-time latent dynamics from partial observations; (2) a student projects the identified representation onto a port-Hamiltonian submanifold via a learned affine chart. A single-stage joint variant is also considered as a counterfactual.

Result: The two-stage approach preserves teacher's dynamics while enforcing physical structure, and is more reliable than the single-stage alternative, particularly in dissipative regimes and high-dimensional visual settings.

Conclusion: Affine projection is the natural bridge between contrastive latent identification and port-Hamiltonian systems; the two-stage teacher-student framework is the preferred approach for structured latent dynamics learning.

Abstract: Identifying latent state representations and dynamics is essential when direct modeling in observation space is infeasible, particularly under partial and high-dimensional observations. In such settings, representation learning and physics-aware modeling are inherently coupled. We study this problem for latent port-Hamiltonian systems, a structured class encompassing both conservative and dissipative dynamics. We propose a two-stage identify-then-project framework. First, a contrastive teacher learns continuous-time latent dynamics from partial observations. Then, a student projects the identified teacher representation and dynamics onto a port-Hamiltonian submanifold via a learned affine chart, yielding a physically consistent realization. As a conceptual counterfactual, we also consider a single-stage variant that jointly learns latent identification and port-Hamiltonian structure, but find it to be less reliable, motivating the proposed two-stage teacher-student framework. We show theoretically that affine projection is the natural bridge between the affine gauge of contrastive latent identification and the port-Hamiltonian systems. Empirically, we demonstrate that the proposed two-stage approach preserves the teacher's dynamics while enforcing physical structure, and performs more reliably than the single-stage alternative, particularly in dissipative regimes and high-dimensional visual settings.

</details>


### [674] [Scalable Knowledge Editing for Mixture-of-Experts LLMs via Tensor-Structured Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16686)
*Roman Maksimov,Vladimir Aletov,Dmitry Bylinkin,Daniil Medyakov,Vladimir Solodkin,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends MEMIT-style knowledge editing to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs by exploiting per-expert tensor structure and the Woodbury matrix identity, achieving matching editing quality with up to 6x speedup over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge editing methods target dense feed-forward layers, but modern LLMs increasingly use Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. This leaves MoE-based production models without principled editing tools, motivating the need for KE methods that work on sparse MoE architectures.

Method: The method is a MEMIT-like framework that: (1) formulates the editing objective at the per-expert level using the tensor structure of MoE layers; (2) applies the Woodbury matrix identity to avoid materializing/inverting the full stacked matrix of expert weights; (3) reduces updates to inversions of fixed low-rank matrices without requiring additional backward passes.

Result: The approach matches the editing quality of strong baselines on main KE metrics while accelerating the editing procedure by up to 6x, due to batched MEMIT-style formulation and low-dimensional inversions enabled by the Woodbury identity.

Conclusion: Closed-form, parameter-modifying KE can be extended efficiently beyond dense layers to MoE architectures, opening a path toward scalable knowledge editing in modern sparse LLM architectures.

Abstract: Knowledge editing (KE) provides a lightweight alternative to repeated fine-tuning of LLMs. However, most existing KE methods target dense feed-forward layers, while modern LLMs increasingly adopt Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures for their superior memory footprint and inference efficiency. This mismatch leaves a growing class of production models without principled editing tools. We propose a MEMIT-like framework for knowledge editing in MoE-based LLMs. Our method exploits the tensor structure of MoE layers to formulate the editing objective faithfully at the per expert level, and applies the Woodbury matrix identity to avoid materializing or inverting the full stacked matrix of expert weights. The resulting update reduces to inversions of fixed low-rank matrices and requires no additional backward passes. Empirically, our approach matches the editing quality of strong baselines on the main KE metrics while accelerating the editing procedure by up to 6x, owing to the batched MEMIT-style formulation and the low-dimensional inversions enabled by the Woodbury identity. These results show that closed-form, parameter-modifying KE can be extended efficiently beyond dense layers, opening a path toward scalable knowledge editing in modern sparse LLM architectures.

</details>


### [675] [EfficientTDMPC: Improved MPC Objectives for Sample-Efficient Continuous Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16692)
*Thomas Evers,Cristian Meo,Wendelin Bohmer,Justin Dauwels,Yaniv Oren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EfficientTDMPC improves sample efficiency in model-based RL for continuous control by ensembling dynamics models, applying uncertainty penalties in planning, and enabling higher UTD ratios, achieving SOTA on HumanoidBench-Hard and DMC hard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TD-MPC methods suffer from accumulated errors in learned model and value networks during planning, which reduces sample efficiency in low-data regimes.

Method: (1) Ensemble of dynamics models with averaged return estimates across models and rollout depths; (2) Uncertainty penalty in planner objective to avoid high-uncertainty actions; (3) Practical improvements for buffer freshness and compute reduction; (4) Higher UTD ratio utilization.

Result: Achieves SOTA sample efficiency on HumanoidBench-Hard and DMC hard benchmarks, and matches SOTA on DMC easy benchmarks in low data regimes.

Conclusion: The proposed enhancements to TD-MPC successfully reduce planning errors and improve sample efficiency, setting new SOTA results on challenging continuous control tasks.

Abstract: We introduce EfficientTDMPC, a sample-efficient model-based reinforcement learning method for continuous control built on the TD-MPC family of algorithms. Central to this family is a planner that aims to find an action sequence that maximizes the estimated return. The return is estimated using a learned model and value networks, each of which can introduce error. EfficientTDMPC proposes to reduce this error in two ways. First, it introduces an ensemble of dynamics models and averages the return estimates across those models and across different rollout depths. Second, it adds the option to apply an uncertainty penalty to the planner objective, yielding a planner that avoids actions with uncertain return estimates. It then adds practical improvements which increase buffer data freshness and reduce compute. Lastly, we find that our contributions enable EfficientTDMPC to benefit more from a higher update-to-data (UTD) ratio, further improving sample efficiency. To the best of our knowledge, in the low data regime of each benchmark, EfficientTDMPC achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) in terms of sample efficiency on HumanoidBench-Hard and DMC hard, while matching SOTA on DMC easy.

</details>


### [676] [UB-SMoE: Universally Balanced Sparse Mixture-of-Experts for Resource-adaptive Federated Fine-tuning of Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16690)
*Van-Tuan Tran,Hong-Hanh Nguyen-Le,Marco Ruffini,Merim Dzaferagic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UB-SMoE introduces Dynamic Modulated Routing and Universal Pseudo-Gradient to fix expert imbalance and non-differentiability issues in heterogeneous federated fine-tuning with sparse Mixture-of-Experts, achieving up to 45% computational reduction and 8.7× performance improvement on low-resource clients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing heterogeneous LoRA-rank methods offer minimal computational savings due to dense feed-forward dominance. While SMoE could help via conditional computation, its naive application creates expert utilization imbalance and non-differentiable Top-K routing, hurting convergence—especially for resource-constrained clients.

Method: Proposes UB-SMoE with two key components: (1) Dynamic Modulated Routing (DMR) to rebalance expert utilization, and (2) Universal Pseudo-Gradient (PG) to reconstruct learning signals for non-activated experts, forming a self-reinforcing cycle that sustains expert viability across heterogeneous clients.

Result: Achieves up to 45.0% computational reduction on low-resource clients and improves their performance by 8.7× compared to existing heterogeneous LoRA-rank methods.

Conclusion: UB-SMoE effectively addresses both expert imbalance and non-differentiability discordances in heterogeneous federated SMoE settings, significantly improving efficiency and performance for resource-constrained clients.

Abstract: Heterogeneous LoRA-rank methods address system heterogeneity in federated fine-tuning of foundation models by assigning client-specific ranks based on computational capabilities. However, these methods achieve only marginal computational savings, as dense feed-forward computations dominate. Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (SMoE) provides a promising alternative through conditional computation, yet we identify that its naive application to heterogeneous federated settings introduces two critical discordances: (i) expert utilization imbalance and (ii) non-differentiability of Top-K routing. Our convergence analysis demonstrates that these discordances lead to degraded convergence, particularly for resource-constrained clients. To address these challenges, we propose Universally Balanced Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (UB-SMoE), which introduces Dynamic Modulated Routing (DMR) to rebalance expert utilization, and Universal Pseudo-Gradient (PG) to reconstruct learning signals for non-activated experts. These mechanisms form a self-reinforcing cycle that maintains expert viability across heterogeneous clients. Experiments on benchmarks show that UB-SMoE achieves up to $45.0\%$ computational reduction on low-resource clients while improving their performance by $8.7 \times$ compared to existing heterogeneous LoRA-rank methods.

</details>


### [677] [Your SaaS Is an Insurance Product: A Modeling Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16699)
*Caio Gomes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Capped-usage SaaS products (LLM subscriptions, cloud platforms) share a structural identity with insurance products, and the paper proposes an actuarial pricing framework using frequency-severity decomposition, premium principles, and Monte Carlo reserve adequacy for these products.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors identify that capped-usage SaaS subscriptions share a structural signature with insurance (fixed premium, stochastic heavy-tailed demand, non-fungible caps, portfolio tail risk), and argue this is not an analogy but the same operational problem, yet current ML/AI practice lacks actuarial tools to price such products properly.

Method: The paper proposes a modeling framework built from frequency-severity decomposition, insurance premium calculation principles, and Monte Carlo simulation for reserve adequacy. It maps this framework to publicly observed subscription tiers in LLM and cloud domains, grounds it in canonical health-insurance economics, and demonstrates divergence from traditional unit economics via a worked example.

Result: The framework demonstrates divergence from traditional unit economics when applied to capped-usage SaaS products, showing that standard per-unit pricing fails to capture the insurance-like risk structure of these products.

Conclusion: The contribution is operational rather than theoretical — providing vocabulary and tools from actuarial science that are currently absent from CS and ML practice, not a new theorem.

Abstract: Capped-usage SaaS products -- LLM subscriptions such as Claude Code and ChatGPT, cloud platforms such as Vercel and Cloudflare Workers, corporate benefit platforms, identity-verification services with liability transfer -- share a structural signature with insurance products: a fixed premium decoupled from realized consumption, stochastic per-user demand with heavy-tailed severity, a non-fungible cap that resets on a fixed schedule, and a portfolio-level exposure that requires reserve adequacy under tail risk. We argue that this is not an analogy. It is the same operational problem actuarial science has been tooled for decades to address, restated with new dependent variables (tokens, bandwidth bytes, function-invocations, gym check-ins) in place of medical claims. This paper proposes a modeling framework for capped-usage SaaS pricing built from frequency-severity decomposition, premium calculation principles, and Monte Carlo reserve adequacy. We map the framework to publicly observable subscription tiers in two domains (LLM services and cloud platforms), ground it in canonical health-insurance economics (Arrow 1963; Pauly 1968; Manning et al. 1987; Brot-Goldberg et al. 2017), and demonstrate divergence from traditional unit economics through a worked example. The contribution is operational rather than theoretical: not a new theorem, but vocabulary and tools currently absent from cs.LG/stat.ML practice.

</details>


### [678] [Convex Dataset Valuation for Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16704)
*Siqi Zeng,Christopher Jung,Rui Li,Zhe Kang,Ming Li,Nima Noorshams,Zhigang Wang,Fuchun Peng,Han Zhao,Xue Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a convex dataset valuation method based on kernel mean matching in gradient space for selecting and weighting auxiliary datasets during LLM post-training under budget constraints, outperforming existing baselines with low computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Developers face compute, labeling, and licensing cost constraints that preclude using all available auxiliary data during LLM post-training, especially in dataset marketplace settings. Existing gradient alignment scores provide incomplete valuation signals as they ignore redundancy among datasets.

Method: A scalable convex dataset-level valuation method using kernel mean matching (KMM) in gradient space, which jointly accounts for alignment with the target task and redundancy across auxiliary datasets.

Result: The proposed approach consistently outperforms existing valuation baselines across diverse post-training settings and tasks, achieving stronger performance with low computational overhead.

Conclusion: Dataset valuation is positioned as a practical decision tool for post-training data selection in market-constrained LLM settings; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Improving LLM performance on downstream tasks sometimes requires leveraging auxiliary datasets during post-training. In practice, however, developers face constraints on compute, labeling, and licensing costs that preclude using all available data, necessitating principled dataset-level selection. These constraints are increasingly shaped by dataset marketplaces, where data acquisition is governed by budgets and negotiation. We study dataset valuation as a subset selection problem during LLM post-training. Our goal is to identify and weight auxiliary datasets so as to maximize target task performance given constrained budgets. We first show that commonly used gradient alignment scores provide a reasonable yet incomplete valuation signal, as they ignore redundancy among datasets. To address this, we propose a scalable convex dataset-level valuation method based on kernel mean matching (KMM) in gradient space, which jointly accounts for alignment with the target task and redundancy across auxiliary datasets. Through extensive experiments across diverse post-training settings and tasks, we show that our approach consistently outperforms existing valuation baselines, achieving stronger performance with low computational overhead. Our results position dataset valuation as a practical decision tool for post-training data selection in market-constrained large language model settings. The code is available at https://github.com/uiuctml/convex_data_valuation.

</details>


### [679] [Isolating Nonlinear Independent Sources in fMRI with $β$-TCVAE Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16708)
*Qiang Li,Shujian Yu,Jesus Malo,Jingyu Liu,Tülay Adali,Vince D. Calhoun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper adapts the β-TCVAE framework to fMRI data for nonlinear source disentanglement, recovering biologically meaningful brain networks like the default mode network without requiring additional hyperparameters during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ICA assumes linear mixing, limiting its ability to capture nonlinear brain dynamics, while deep representation learning methods have been insufficiently validated on real neuroimaging data like fMRI.

Method: The authors adapt and modify the β-TCVAE (Total Correlation Variational Autoencoder) framework to fMRI data for nonlinear source disentanglement of spatial and temporal brain signals.

Result: The β-TCVAE recovers meaningful nonlinear spatial components with biological relevance, including well-established intrinsic connectivity networks such as the default mode network. The latent structure also captures coherent brain organization patterns via functional network connectivity evaluation.

Conclusion: This pilot study demonstrates that β-TCVAE bridges nonlinear representation learning and fMRI analysis, offering a promising alternative to linear ICA for modeling complex brain dynamics.

Abstract: Learning meaningful latent representations from nonlinear fMRI data remains a fundamental challenge in neuroimaging analysis. Traditional independent component analysis, widely used due to its ability to estimate interpretable functional brain networks, relies on a linear mixing assumption for latent sources, limiting its ability to capture the inherently nonlinear and complex organization of brain dynamics. More recently, deep representation learning methods have emerged as promising alternatives for modeling nonlinear latent structure. However, many of these approaches have been evaluated primarily on simulated datasets or natural image benchmarks, with comparatively limited validation on real-world neuroimaging data such as fMRI. In this work, we are motivated by the $β$-TCVAE (Total Correlation Variational Autoencoder), a refinement of the $β$-VAE framework for learning latent representations without introducing additional hyperparameters during training. We adapt and modify this model to fMRI data for nonlinear source disentanglement, aiming to separate mixed spatial and temporal brain signals into interpretable components. We show that the $β$-TCVAE framework can recover meaningful nonlinear spatial components with biological relevance, including well-established intrinsic connectivity networks such as the default mode network. Furthermore, we evaluate the learned representations using functional network connectivity, showing that the latent structure captures coherent and interpretable brain organization patterns. This study provides a pilot investigation that bridges nonlinear representation learning and fMRI analysis.

</details>


### [680] [EmoMind: Decoding Affective Captions from Human Brain fMRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16739)
*Bilal A. Mohammed,Lin Gu,Ruogo Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EmoMind is the first end-to-end pipeline that decodes affective captions from fMRI signals by combining a semantically grounded scene description with a continuous 34-dimensional emotion vector, outperforming GPT-4 with discrete labels on subject-specific affective structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current brain-to-text systems recover semantic content but discard affect, and existing language models collapse rich inter-subject emotional variability into coarse discrete bins. The authors aim to enable individualized affective caption generation directly from brain activity.

Method: EmoMind first retrieves a semantically grounded neutral scene description from brain-decoded visual features, then rewrites it using a continuous 34-dimensional emotion vector decoded from the same fMRI recording. A classifier-free guidance mechanism with an identity-preserving null branch controls the balance between content preservation and affective expression.

Result: EmoMind significantly outperforms GPT-4 prompted with brain-decoded top-5 emotion labels across two independent fMRI datasets on all three evaluation axes (subject-specificity, structural geometry, causal control), with the largest gains on metrics requiring person-specific affective structure.

Conclusion: Continuous brain-decoded affect is a viable control signal for individualized affective caption generation, opening new directions for studying individual affective brain organization.

Abstract: Decoding visual experience from brain activity has advanced substantially, but cur- rent brain-to-text systems largely recover semantic content while discarding affect. Additionally, language models can generate emotional text when prompted with categorical labels, but such labels collapse rich inter-subject variability into coarse discrete bins. We present EmoMind, the first end-to-end pipeline for decoding affective captions directly from fMRI signals. EmoMind first retrieves a semanti- cally grounded neutral scene description from brain-decoded visual features, then rewrites it using a continuous 34-dimensional emotion vector decoded from the same fMRI recording. To control the balance between content preservation and affective expression, we train the rewriter with classifier-free guidance against an identity-preserving null branch, enabling smooth interpolation between semantic fidelity and affective expressivity. We evaluate affective caption generation with a three-axis validation framework spanning subject-specificity, structural geometry, and causal control. We further augment this framework with a synthetic-brain substitution test that probes robustness to the measurement apparatus, and we benchmark each axis against GPT-4 prompted with brain-decoded top-5 emotion labels as a strong discrete baseline. Across two independent emotion fMRI datasets, EmoMind significantly outperforms label-prompted GPT-4 on all three axes, with the largest gains on metrics that require person-specific affective structure rather than population-level emotion aggregation. These results establish continuous brain-decoded affect as a viable control signal for individualized affective cap- tion generation and open new directions for studying individual affective brain organisation.

</details>


### [681] [Propagation of Chaos in Contextual Flow Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16747)
*Shi Chen,Zhengjiang Lin,Kaizhao Liu,Philippe Rigollet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a statistical theory for transformers in large-context settings using contextual flow maps (CFMs), establishing optimal convergence rates between finite and infinite-context models via propagation of chaos and McKean-Vlasov dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rigorous quantitative theory connecting finite-context transformers to their idealized infinite-context counterparts, treating context length as a statistical resource rather than just a computational parameter.

Method: The authors model transformers as contextual flow maps (CFMs) — dynamical systems evolving a token with contextual measure across attention blocks. They exploit McKean-Vlasov structure and propagation of chaos to derive forward/backward bounds, using a novel Eulerian adjoint formulation for loss gradients.

Result: Optimal Wasserstein rate n^{-1/d} for general CFMs and parametric rate n^{-1/2} for restricted CFMs (including transformers), uniformly along depth and across gradient descent iterations.

Conclusion: The finite-context model consistently approximates the infinite-context system with optimal statistical rates, and the proposed forward-adjoint stability framework may be broadly applicable beyond this setting.

Abstract: We develop a quantitative statistical theory of transformers in the large-context regime by adopting the abstraction of contextual flow maps (CFMs): dynamical systems that evolve a distinguished token in the presence of a contextual measure across a stack of attention blocks. Within this framework, the finite-context model approximates an idealized infinite-context system in which the contextual measure is replaced by its underlying population, so that the context length $n$ becomes a statistical resource. Exploiting the McKean--Vlasov structure of the dynamics and the classical machinery of propagation of chaos, we establish a forward bound controlling the deviation between the finite- and infinite-context CFMs uniformly along depth, and a backward bound controlling the deviation between the corresponding training trajectories uniformly across iterations of online gradient descent. Both bounds achieve the optimal Wasserstein rate $n^{-1/d}$ for general CFMs and parametric rate $n^{-1/2}$ for a restricted class of CFMs that includes transformers as a special case. The analysis rests on a new Eulerian adjoint formulation of the loss gradient and stability estimates for the resulting forward--adjoint system, both of which may be of independent interest.

</details>


### [682] [PH-Dreamer: A Physics-Driven World Model via Port-Hamiltonian Generative Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18303)
*Xueyu Luan,Chenwei Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified Port-Hamiltonian framework for world models that embeds physical priors (energy routing, dissipation) into recurrent state space architectures, enabling physically structured latent dynamics. The method includes a kinematics-aware energy estimator and an energy-guided Actor-Critic with Lagrangian multipliers, achieving better returns, higher simulator fidelity, and reductions in phase space volume (4.18-8.41%), energy consumption (up to 7.80%), and jerk (up to 9.38%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recurrent state space world models enable efficient latent imagination but produce physically unstructured dynamics that violate conservation and dissipative principles.

Method: Three synergistic mechanisms: (1) Port-Hamiltonian latent transitions with implicit physical priors (energy flow/dissipation), (2) Kinematics-aware energy world model estimating Hamiltonian and power balance, (3) Energy-guided Actor-Critic using Lagrangian multipliers for policy regularization toward lower energy and smoother control.

Result: Superior asymptotic returns, tighter/ lower-variance imagined-reward alignment, 4.18-8.41% smaller latent phase space volume, up to 7.80% energy reduction, up to 9.38% jerk reduction.

Conclusion: The Port-Hamiltonian framework successfully imposes physical structure on world models, improving both performance and physical realism without sacrificing efficiency.

Abstract: World models built on recurrent state space architectures enable efficient latent imagination, yet remain physically unstructured, producing dynamics that violate conservation and dissipative principles. We introduce a unified Port-Hamiltonian framework that remedies this through three synergistic mechanisms. First, we embed implicit physical priors into recurrent transitions by modeling projected latent evolution as action controlled energy routing governed by flow and dissipation, biasing the projected PH phase space toward a more compact and physically structured representation. Second, we develop a kinematics aware energy world model that estimates the Hamiltonian and power balance from proprioceptive observations, providing an explicit physical signal for thermodynamic reasoning. Third, leveraging these energy gradients, we establish an energy guided Actor-Critic that uses Lagrangian multipliers to regularize policy optimization toward lower energy and smoother control. Across visual control benchmarks, this paradigm not only attains superior asymptotic returns but also elevates internal simulator fidelity by establishing a tighter, lower variance alignment between imagined and real rewards, all while reducing latent phase space volume by 4.18-8.41%, energy consumption by up to 7.80%, and mean squared jerk by up to 9.38%.

</details>


### [683] [Learning Unbiased Permutations via Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16755)
*Yimeng Min,Carla P. Gomes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PermFlow is a conditional flow matching framework for learning permutation distributions that handles ambiguity by operating on the affine subspace of doubly stochastic matrices, using closed-form tangent-space projections and nearest-target couplings to preserve constraints and capture multiple valid permutations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing differentiable methods like entropy-regularized Sinkhorn collapse under ambiguity, producing only a single softened solution rather than capturing the full multimodal distribution of valid permutations.

Method: (1) Operates on the affine subspace of matrices with unit row/column sums. (2) Uses closed-form tangent-space projector to preserve constraints exactly. (3) Applies nearest-target coupling to route distinct noisy initializations toward distinct valid permutations.

Result: High accuracy on unambiguous inputs; recovers both valid permutations under ambiguity where Sinkhorn-based baselines structurally fail, demonstrated on visual sorting and symmetric linear assignment tasks.

Conclusion: PermFlow successfully captures multimodal permutation distributions instead of collapsing to a single mode, solving a fundamental limitation of prior differentiable permutation methods.

Abstract: Learning permutations is fundamental to sorting, ranking, and matching, but existing differentiable methods based on entropy-regularized Sinkhorn produce a single softened solution and collapse under ambiguity. We present PermFlow, a conditional flow matching framework that operates directly on the affine subspace of matrices with unit row and column sums. A closed-form tangent-space projector preserves these constraints exactly along every trajectory, by construction rather than through iterative correction, and a nearest-target coupling routes distinct noisy initializations toward distinct valid permutations. The result is a model that captures multimodal permutation distributions rather than collapsing them to a single mode. On a visual sorting task with blended-digit ambiguity and a symmetric linear assignment problem, PermFlow achieves high accuracy on unambiguous inputs and recovers both valid permutations under ambiguity, where Sinkhorn-based baselines structurally fail.

</details>


### [684] [Distinguishable Deletion: Unifying Knowledge Erasure and Refusal for Large Language Model Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16776)
*Puning Yang,Junchi Yu,Qizhou Wang,Philip Torr,Bo Han,Xiuying Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Distinguishable Deletion (D²), a new paradigm for LLM unlearning that restricts response distribution in latent representation space (via an energy index) rather than specific tokens, enabling both effective knowledge removal and safe refusal behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM unlearning approaches (Knowledge Deletion and Distinguishable Refusal) suffer from biased deletion or risk re-emergence of harmful knowledge because they operate on token-level suppression or leave underlying knowledge intact.

Method: Proposes D² paradigm with an energy index to quantify knowledge presence and separation between unlearned/retained content; implements Energy-based Unlearning Alignment (EUA) that enforces energy-boundary unlearning during training and energy-based refusal at inference.

Result: Extensive experiments show EUA significantly outperforms previous unlearning methods.

Conclusion: The D² paradigm with energy-based representation-level deletion is superior to existing token-level or inference-only approaches for LLM safety unlearning.

Abstract: Mitigating sensitive and harmful outputs is fundamental to ensuring safe deployment of LLMs. Existing approaches typically follow two paradigms: Knowledge Deletion (KD), which erases undesirable information during training, and Distinguishable Refusal (DR), which steers models away from using sensitive knowledge during inference. Despite rapid progress, KD-based unlearning struggles with biased deletion due to suppressing specific token sequences as a substitute for complete knowledge removal, whereas DR-based unlearning risks the re-emergence of harmful knowledge because the underlying knowledge remains intact. To address these issues, we propose Distinguishable Deletion ($\mathrm{D^2}$), a paradigm that restricts the response distribution in the latent representation rather than specific tokens to erase undesirable knowledge, while distinguishing it from retained knowledge, enabling a refusal mechanism to handle unlearned inputs safely and coherently. To implement $\mathrm{D^2}$, we introduce an energy index that quantifies the presence of knowledge and the separation between unlearned and retained content. Mathematical and empirical analyses show that energy is both accurate and efficient, enabling Energy-based Unlearning Alignment (EUA) to enforce energy-boundary unlearning during training and apply an energy-based refusal mechanism at inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EUA significantly outperforms previous methods, indicating the superiority of $\mathrm{D^2}$. Our code is available at https://github.com/Puning97/EUA-for-LLM-Unlearning.

</details>


### [685] [Lever: Speculative LLM Inference on Smartphones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16786)
*Tuowei Wang,Fengzu Li,Yanfan Sun,Wei Gao,Ju Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lever is an end-to-end system that optimizes speculative decoding for flash-backed LLM inference on smartphones, achieving 2.93x latency reduction over baseline flash-offloaded inference and 1.50x over conventional speculative decoding by jointly optimizing drafting, verification, and execution stages under mobile constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models exceed limited DRAM on smartphones, and flash-backed inference suffers from slow autoregressive decoding due to costly I/O operations. Existing speculative decoding methods assume server-class accelerators and fail to address mobile-specific challenges like prolonged I/O latency, limited computation parallelism, and irregular speculation execution.

Method: Lever jointly optimizes three stages of speculative decoding under mobile constraints: (1) Drafting: builds token trees using an I/O- and compute-aware gain-cost objective; (2) Verification: prunes low-value branches through early-exit prediction to reduce target-model computation; (3) Execution: maps speculation efficiently across mobile CPU-NPU hardware to improve utilization.

Result: Lever reduces inference latency by an average of 2.93x over baseline flash-offloaded inference and 1.50x over conventional speculative decoding, narrowing the latency gap between flash-backed and memory-resident LLM inference.

Conclusion: By tailoring speculative decoding to mobile constraints, Lever enables practical deployment of large language models on smartphones with flash storage, significantly closing the performance gap with memory-resident inference.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly needed for interactive mobile applications, but high-quality models exceed the limited DRAM available on smartphones. Flash storage can hold larger models, yet flash-backed inference is slow because autoregressive decoding repeatedly invokes the target model and incurs costly I/O. We observe that speculative decoding is a natural fit for this setting: a small draft model can remain in DRAM, while a larger flash-resident target model verifies multiple candidate tokens per invocation. However, existing methods assume server-class accelerators and fail to account for prolonged I/O latency, limited computation parallelism, and irregular speculation execution.
  We present Lever, an end-to-end system for efficient flash-backed LLM inference on smartphones. Lever jointly optimizes the three stages of speculative decoding under mobile constraints. For drafting, it builds token trees using an I/O- and compute-aware gain-cost objective. For verification, it prunes low-value branches through early-exit prediction to reduce target-model computation. For execution, it maps speculation efficiently across mobile CPU-NPU hardware to improve utilization. Comprehensive evaluations show that Lever reduces inference latency by an average of 2.93x over baseline flash-offloaded inference and 1.50x over conventional speculative decoding, narrowing the latency gap between flash-backed and memory-resident LLM inference.

</details>


### [686] [The Unlearnability Phenomenon in RLVR for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16787)
*Yulin Chen,He He,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies and characterizes "unlearnable" examples in RLVR training for LLMs—hard examples that remain unlearned despite correct rollouts due to fundamental representation issues (low gradient similarity with other examples and ungeneralizable reasoning patterns) that cannot be fixed by optimization, sampling, or data augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the learning dynamics of RLVR in LLM reasoning, specifically why certain hard examples remain unlearned despite having correct rollouts during training.

Method: The authors systematically investigate the unlearnability phenomenon by testing optimization and sampling techniques, then perform cross-example gradient analysis to examine gradient similarity and reasoning pattern generalizability.

Result: Unlearnable examples exhibit low gradient similarity with other examples and ungeneralizable reasoning patterns; data augmentation fails to improve gradient similarity, indicating fundamental representation flaws.

Conclusion: Current RLVR approaches have fundamental limitations in learning certain hard examples due to representation issues, suggesting the need for new methods beyond existing RL techniques.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) has proven effective in improving Large Language Model's (LLM) reasoning ability. However, the learning dynamics of RLVR remain underexplored. In this paper, we reveal a counterintuitive phenomenon: among hard examples that the model initially struggles with, a substantial subset remains unlearnable even when correct rollouts are present. To understand the phenomenon, we first demonstrate that existing optimization and sampling techniques fail to resolve unlearnability. With cross-example gradient analysis, we show that unlearnable examples have fundamental representation issue, characterized by low gradient similarity with the rest of the examples and ungeneralizable reasoning patterns. We further show that representation flaws are difficult to mitigate in RL, as data augmentation does not improve gradient similarity. Our study provides the first systematic characterization of unlearnable data in RLVR training and reveals fundamental limitations in current RL approaches for reasoning tasks. Code and data are available at \url{https://github.com/yulinchen99/unlearnability-rlvr}.

</details>


### [687] [TIER: Trajectory-Invariant Execution Rewards for Multi-Step Tool Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16790)
*Anay Kulkarni,ChiaEn Lu,Dheeraj Mekala,Jayanth Srinivasa,Gaowen Liu,Jingbo Shang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TIER is a reward framework that uses function schemas and runtime execution (not reference trajectories) to provide dense reward signals, achieving >90% accuracy on compositional tool-use tasks up to 6 steps while trajectory-supervised methods fail beyond step 4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL approaches for tool-use LLMs fail in multi-step settings: outcome-based rewards are too sparse, and trajectory-supervised rewards require annotated reference solutions that penalize valid alternative paths, limiting scalability.

Method: TIER decomposes reward into four components: format validity, schema adherence, execution success, and answer correctness — all derived from function schemas and runtime execution rather than reference trajectories, enabling dense sequence-level feedback.

Result: On DepthBench (1-6 step composition), TIER achieves >90% accuracy across all depths while trajectory-supervised rewards collapse beyond step-4. Consistent improvements are also shown on BFCL v3 and NestFUL benchmarks.

Conclusion: Trajectory-invariant reward design enables scalable RL for compositional tool use, and all four reward components are necessary for optimal performance, with ablation studies confirming the importance of multi-level supervision.

Abstract: Tool use enables large language models to solve complex tasks through sequences of API calls, yet existing reinforcement learning approaches fail to scale to multi-step composition settings. Outcome-based rewards provide only sparse feedback, while trajectory-supervised rewards depend on annotated reference solutions, penalizing valid alternatives and limiting scalability. We propose TIER: Trajectory-Invariant Execution Rewards, a reward framework that derives supervision directly from function schemas and runtime execution, rather than from reference trajectories. The reward decomposes into format validity, schema adherence, execution success, and answer correctness, providing dense, interpretable sequence-level feedback derived from fine-grained verification of individual steps of tool use. This design allows any valid execution path to receive credit, naturally supporting multiple solution strategies and adapting to evolving tool interfaces. On DepthBench, a compositional benchmark stratified by depth (1 to 6 steps), TIER achieves >90% accuracy across steps, where trajectory-supervised rewards collapse beyond step-4. We further demonstrate consistent gains on benchmarks like BFCL v3 and NestFUL. Ablation studies confirm that all reward components are necessary, highlighting the importance of multi-level supervision for compositional reasoning.

</details>


### [688] [PULSE: Generative Phase Evolution for Non-Stationary Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16793)
*Yangyou Liu,Zezhi Shao,Xinyu Chen,Hu Chen,Fei Wang,Yuankai Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PULSE is a physics-informed, plug-and-play framework for non-stationary time series forecasting that uses phase-anchored disentanglement, Phase Router, and Statistic-Aware Mixup to overcome Phase Amnesia, enabling a simple MLP to achieve SOTA results across 12 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time series forecasting under non-stationarity suffers from Phase Amnesia, where models lose sight of evolving global context due to reliance on static historical assumptions.

Method: PULSE adopts a Disentangle--Evolve--Simulate philosophy: (1) phase-anchored disentanglement resolves optimization interference from dominant trends; (2) Phase Router actively generates future trajectories; (3) Statistic-Aware Mixup (SAM) ensures robustness against out-of-distribution volatility. It is grounded in three physical hypotheses: Wold decomposition, dynamical phase evolution, and heteroscedastic manifold generation.

Result: A simple MLP backbone equipped with PULSE achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across 12 real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: Correct physics-informed inductive bias is more critical than raw architectural complexity for non-stationary forecasting.

Abstract: Time series forecasting under non-stationarity faces a fundamental tension between capturing stable representations and adapting to distribution shifts. Existing methods implicitly rely on static historical assumptions, leading to a critical failure mode we term Phase Amnesia, where models become blind to the evolving global context. To resolve this, we formalize non-stationary dynamics through three physical hypotheses: wold decomposition, dynamical phase evolution, and heteroscedastic manifold generation. These principles inspire PULSE, a physics-informed, plug-and-play framework adopting a Disentangle--Evolve--Simulate design philosophy. Specifically, PULSE utilizes phase-anchored disentanglement to resolve optimization interference caused by dominant trends, employs a Phase Router to actively generate future trajectories, and introduces Statistic-Aware Mixup (SAM) to ensure robustness against out-of-distribution volatility. Empirically, PULSE enables a simple MLP backbone to achieve state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across 12 real-world benchmarks. This validates that a correct physics-informed inductive bias is far more critical than raw architectural complexity for non-stationary forecasting. The code is available at: https://github.com/Gemost/PULSE.

</details>


### [689] [Cross-Domain Molecular Relational Learning: Leveraging Chemical Structure-Activity Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16799)
*Peiliang Zhang,Jingling Yuan,Shiqing Wu,Mengqing Hu,Chao Che,Yongjun Zhu,Lin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DisTrans is a domain adversarial training network that uses structural-semantic transfer discrepancy to enable cross-domain molecular relational learning, outperforming 16 baselines in molecular structure and visual image adaptation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MRL methods are limited by intra-domain modeling and domain-closed effects, failing to address cross-domain interaction mechanisms in molecular science. The authors aim to overcome this limitation by enabling effective cross-domain molecular relational learning.

Method: 1) Gradient reversal strategy based on substructure topological discrepancies to learn domain dependence and generate domain-separable structural representations. 2) Cross-domain representation guidance mechanism to align functional-group semantic information between source and target domains, learning cross-domain consistency.

Result: DisTrans outperforms 16 baseline methods across two typical cross-domain strategies, maintaining satisfactory performance even under pronounced inter-domain discrepancy.

Conclusion: The proposed domain adversarial training with structural-semantic transfer discrepancy effectively enables cross-domain molecular relational learning, demonstrating robustness against large domain gaps.

Abstract: Recent advances in molecular representation integrates molecular topological and visual modalities, opening new avenues for precise Molecular Relational Learning (MRL). Existing MRL methods focus on intra-domain modeling, and their inherent domain-closed effect limits applicability to molecular science, particularly in elucidating cross-domain interaction mechanisms. Consequently, the imperative for Cross-Domain Molecular Relational Learning has become increasingly pressing. Benefiting from structure-activity analysis, we propose the Domain Adversarial Training Network with Structural-Semantic Transfer Discrepancy (DisTrans) to optimize cross-domain adaptive representation for molecular structures and visual images. 1) We employ the gradient reversal strategy based on substructure topological discrepancies between domains to learn the domain dependence of molecular structures. This strategy guides the model to adapt to the structural adjacency patterns in the target domain, generating domain-separable structural representations. 2) We apply the cross-domain representation guidance mechanism to align the functional-group semantic information between the source and target domains, learning cross-domain consistency information. The experimental results in two typical cross-domain strategies demonstrate that DisTrans outperforms 16 baseline methods, maintaining satisfactory performance even under pronounced inter-domain discrepancy.

</details>


### [690] [FIM-LoRA: Task-Informative Rank Allocation for LoRA via Calibration-Time Gradient-Variance Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16800)
*Ramakrishnan Sathyavageeswaran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FIM-LoRA improves LoRA by redistributing rank budgets across layers based on gradient variance from eight calibration backward passes, using an efficient eFIM diagonal approximation that cuts memory costs 256x, achieving near-identical performance to standard LoRA on GLUE and commonsense reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA assigns uniform rank to all adapted weight matrices, ignoring that different layers contribute unequally to task adaptation.

Method: Run eight calibration backward passes before fine-tuning, compute gradient variance of each LoRA-B matrix as a proxy for layer informativeness via an efficient eFIM diagonal approximation restricted to LoRA matrices (256x memory reduction), and redistribute the rank budget proportionally per layer.

Result: FIM-LoRA matches LoRA on GLUE (88.6 vs. 88.7) and commonsense reasoning (68.5 vs. 68.7) at the same parameter budget, with interpretable rank maps showing value projections and early-to-middle layers receive higher rank.

Conclusion: Per-layer rank allocation based on gradient variance is a lightweight, interpretable way to improve LoRA's efficiency without adding parameters or training overhead, and the resulting rank patterns align with known transformer layer roles.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) assigns a uniform rank to every adapted weight matrix - a practical convenience that ignores a fundamental reality: different layers contribute unequally to task adaptation. We address this with a lightweight engineering solution: before fine-tuning begins, run eight calibration backward passes, compute the gradient variance of each LoRA-B matrix as a proxy for layer informativeness, and redistribute the rank budget proportionally. The resulting adapter is a standard LoRA with a per-layer rank pattern - no new parameters, no training overhead, no changes to serving infrastructure. We implement this via an efficient approximation of the empirical Fisher Information Matrix (eFIM) diagonal, restricted to LoRA adapter matrices only, which reduces memory cost by approximately 256x compared to full-model Fisher estimation. On GLUE with DeBERTa-v3-base, FIM-LoRA matches LoRA (88.6 vs. 88.7) at the same parameter budget, and on commonsense reasoning with LLaMA-3-8B reaches 68.5 vs. 68.7 for LoRA. The per-layer rank maps are interpretable: value projections and early-to-middle layers consistently receive higher rank, consistent with established findings on transformer layer roles.

</details>


### [691] [Cross-modal Affinity-aligned Multimodal Learning Analytics for Predicting Student Collaboration Satisfaction in Game-Based Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16806)
*Wen-Hsin Tsai,Chia-Ming Lee,Yuk-Ying Tung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes the AAMLA framework with CAMA module to improve collaboration satisfaction prediction in game-based learning by handling modality degradation through affinity matrices and contrastive learning, showing robust results on 50 middle school students.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal learning analytics suffer from modality degradation (e.g., eye gaze becoming unreliable across cohorts), and implicit attention-based fusion methods produce brittle representations that cannot adaptively handle uninformative modalities.

Method: The AAMLA framework uses the CAMA module to model inter-modal relationships via affinity matrices, enforce cross-modal consistency through contrastive learning, and apply modality-specific projection layers to map heterogeneous features into a unified semantic space.

Result: Experiments on 50 middle school students in EcoJourneys show consistent improvements over unimodal baselines and prior cross-attention approaches under standard and modality degradation conditions. SHAP and t-SNE analyses confirm robust, interpretable representations.

Conclusion: The AAMLA framework effectively addresses modality degradation in collaborative learning analytics, producing robust cross-modal representations that improve collaboration satisfaction prediction.

Abstract: Collaborative game-based learning environments offer rich opportunities for small-group knowledge construction, yet automatically predicting student collaboration satisfaction remains challenging. A critical barrier is modality degradation: in educational deployments, individual modalities such as eye gaze exhibit inconsistent informativeness across student cohorts, causing implicit attention-based fusion to produce brittle multimodal representations. We propose the Affinity-Aligned Multimodal Learning Analytics (AAMLA) framework, whose core contribution is the Cross-modal Affinity-guided Modality Alignment (CAMA) module, which explicitly models inter-modal relationships via affinity matrices and enforces cross-modal consistency through contrastive learning, enabling adaptive suppression of uninformative modalities without discarding them. AAMLA further applies modality-specific projection layers to map heterogeneous features, including facial action units, head pose, eye gaze, and interaction trace logs, into a unified semantic space prior to alignment. Experiments on 50 middle school students in the EcoJourneys collaborative learning environment demonstrate consistent improvements over unimodal baselines and prior cross-attention approaches under standard and modality degradation conditions, with SHAP and t-SNE analyses confirming that CAMA produces robust, interpretable cross-modal representations for student collaboration modeling.

</details>


### [692] [Informative Graph Structure Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16809)
*Shen Han,Zhiyao Zhou,Jiawei Chen,Sheng Zhou,Canghong Jin,Hai Lin,Da Zhong Li,Bingde Hu,Can Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that Graph Structure Learning (GSL) suffers from edge redundancy due to similarity-based edge construction, and proposes InGSL—a plug-in module that balances similarity and diversity via mutual information, achieving better performance with fewer edges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GSL methods improve graph quality but dramatically increase edge count, causing storage and computational overhead. This redundancy stems from similarity-based edge construction that over-connects similar neighbors.

Method: InGSL introduces a mutual-information-guided learning strategy that jointly considers both similarity and diversity when constructing edges. It is designed as a plug-in module compatible with existing GSL frameworks.

Result: Experiments on six representative GSL methods demonstrate that InGSL achieves significant performance improvements while using a reduced number of edges.

Conclusion: The similarity-diversity trade-off is key to efficient graph structure learning, and InGSL effectively reduces structure redundancy without sacrificing—and even improving—performance.

Abstract: The quality of graph-structured data is fundamental to the success of modern graph analysis techniques such as Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). However, real-world graph data is often suboptimal, suffering from issues such as noise and incomplete connections. Graph Structure Learning (GSL) has emerged as a promising technique that adaptively optimizes node connections. However, we observe that the effectiveness of GSL often comes at the cost of a dramatic expansion in edge count, resulting in significant storage and computational overhead.
  In this work, we reveal that this limitation stems from the prevalent use of similarity-based edge construction, which predominantly connects highly similar neighbors based on their embeddings, introducing substantial structure redundancy. To address this, we propose a novel Informative Graph Structure Learning method (InGSL), which jointly considers both similarity and diversity in edge construction by incorporating a mutual-information-guided learning strategy. Notably, InGSL serves as a plug-in module that can be seamlessly integrated into existing GSL frameworks. Through extensive experiments on six representative GSL methods, we demonstrate that InGSL achieves significant performance improvements at a reduced number of edges.

</details>


### [693] [Jacobian-Guided Anisotropic Noise Reshaping for Enhancing Representation Utility under Local Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16812)
*Youngmok Ha,Viktor Schlegel,Yidan Sun,Anil Anthony Bharath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method to improve Local Differential Privacy (LDP) by identifying task-relevant subspaces via the Jacobian matrix of a public model, selectively reducing noise along critical dimensions while preserving the privacy budget, leading to ~20% utility improvement over PrivUnit2/PrivUnitG at ε=7.5 on CIFAR-10-C.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional LDP mechanisms are task-agnostic and inject noise uniformly across all dimensions, causing severe data utility degradation regardless of dimensional importance to the downstream task.

Method: The method identifies task-critical subspaces using the Jacobian matrix of a public downstream model, attenuates noise along those dimensions, and reshapes isotropic LDP noise into an anisotropic distribution while preserving the per-dimension privacy budget.

Result: On CIFAR-10-C (Brightness severity 5), the approach improves PrivUnit2 and PrivUnitG utility by approximately 20% at ε=7.5.

Conclusion: The proposed approach generalizes to both linear and non-linear models, integrates seamlessly with existing LDP mechanisms, and significantly enhances data utility without compromising privacy guarantees.

Abstract: While Local Differential Privacy (LDP) serves as a foundational primitive for distributed data collection, its stringent noise injection requirement often leads to severe degradation in data utility. This degradation stems from the task-agnostic nature of conventional LDP mechanisms, which inject noise uniformly across all dimensions regardless of their relative importance to the downstream objective. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach that mitigates noise in task-relevant subspaces of the data representation. Our method identifies task-critical subspaces via the Jacobian matrix of the public downstream model, selectively attenuates noise along those dimensions, and reshapes the isotropic noise of standard LDP into an anisotropic distribution. This method preserves the uniform per-dimension privacy budget while heterogeneously modulating noise impact across dimensions, thereby substantially enhancing data utility. Furthermore, our approach generalizes to both linear and non-linear models and integrates seamlessly with existing mechanisms. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10-C (Brightness corruption at the highest severity level 5) demonstrate that integrating our approach improves the utility of PrivUnit2 and PrivUnitG by approximately 20\% at $ε=7.5$. The source code is available at \url{https://github.com/ymha/jacobian-anr-ldp}.

</details>


### [694] [Atoms as Language: VQ-Atom: Semantic Discretization for Molecular Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16823)
*Takayuki Kimura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VQ-Atom converts continuous atom-level graph representations into discrete, chemically meaningful tokens via vector quantization, improving molecular representation learning and protein-ligand interaction prediction without 3D structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing molecular tokenizations (e.g., SMILES) are syntactic and lack alignment with chemically meaningful substructures, limiting molecular representation learning.

Method: VQ-Atom uses graph neural network embeddings and vector quantization to assign atoms to codebook entries representing chemically meaningful atomic contexts, creating a discrete molecular language for Transformer pretraining.

Result: VQ-Atom consistently improves predictive performance over conventional tokenization in protein-ligand interaction prediction under a protein-cold split setting without 3D structural information.

Conclusion: Semantically grounded discretization can substantially enhance molecular representation learning, and token design is critical for effective language modeling in chemistry.
