Abstract: Molecular representation learning has become a central approach in AI-driven drug discovery, yet existing molecular tokenizations such as SMILES remain largely syntactic and do not naturally align with chemically meaningful substructures. In this work, we introduce VQ-Atom, a semantic discretization framework that converts continuous atom-level graph representations into discrete tokens corresponding to local chemical environments. Using graph neural network embeddings and vector quantization, atoms are assigned to codebook entries representing chemically meaningful atomic contexts. These discrete tokens define a molecular language suitable for Transformer-based pretraining.
  We evaluate VQ-Atom in protein-ligand interaction prediction under a protein-cold split setting without relying on 3D structural information. Experimental results show that VQ-Atom consistently improves predictive performance compared to conventional tokenization approaches, suggesting that semantically grounded discretization can substantially enhance molecular representation learning. Our findings indicate that token design itself plays a critical role in enabling effective language modeling for chemistry.

</details>


### [695] [Confidence Geometry Reveals Trace-Level Correctness in Large Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16824)
*Shuo Liu,Ding Liu,Shi-Ju Ran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs' token-level confidence trajectories encode geometric signals of reasoning correctness, enabling lightweight correctness evaluation and improved answer aggregation without accessing input, text, or hidden states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether the confidence trajectories (how uncertainty evolves during inference) generated by LLMs are relevant to reasoning correctness, and whether these signals can be exploited for improving inference without external verifiers.

Method: The authors extract token-level confidence values during LLM generation, reduce them to low-dimensional representations, measure clustering quality (Davies-Bouldin index) to separate correct/incorrect traces, and propose NeuralConf—a lightweight estimator that learns from confidence trajectories for correctness evaluation.

Result: Confidence trajectories encode content-agnostic geometry separating correct from incorrect traces across GSM8K, MATH, and MMLU. Stronger clustering correlates with higher correctness-discrimination AUC. Correctness signals are enriched in the tail of reasoning. NeuralConf-derived scores improve confidence-weighted answer aggregation over majority voting and static baselines.

Conclusion: LLMs expose trace-intrinsic statistical signals of correctness through their own confidence dynamics, offering a route to improve inference using information already present within generation without external verifiers or hidden states.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) generate not only reasoning text, but also token-level confidence trajectories that record how uncertainty evolves during inference. Whether these trajectories are relevant to reasoning correctness remains unclear. Here we show that confidence trajectories encode a content-agnostic confidence geometry associated with trace-level final-answer correctness. Using only token-level confidence values, without access to the input question, reasoning text, hidden states, or external verifiers, we find that low-dimensional representations of confidence trajectories separate correct from incorrect reasoning traces. Across GSM8K, MATH, and MMLU, this geometric separation is quantitatively linked to downstream predictability: stronger clustering of correct and incorrect traces, measured by the Davies--Bouldin index, consistently corresponds to higher correctness-discrimination AUC. We further show that correctness-related information is enriched in the tail of reasoning, suggesting that late-stage confidence dynamics carry key correctness signals. We propose NeuralConf, a lightweight estimator that learns from confidence trajectories for correctness evaluation. Under a fixed trace budget, NeuralConf-derived scores improve confidence-weighted answer aggregation over majority voting, tail confidence, and other static baselines. These results reveal that LLMs expose trace-intrinsic statistical signals of correctness through their own confidence dynamics, offering a route to improve inference using information already present within generation.

</details>


### [696] [Decoupling KL and Trajectories: A Unified Perspective for SFT, DAgger, Offline RL, and OPD in LLM Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16826)
*Anhao Zhao,Haoran Xin,Yingqi Fan,Junlong Tong,Wenjie Li,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically deconstructs knowledge distillation in LLM post-training by decoupling prefix source and token-level KL direction into four valid objectives. Through controlled experiments on math reasoning, it reveals three key tradeoffs (accuracy-entropy, quality-compute, accuracy-stability) and proposes KL mixing and an entropy-gated length curriculum that improves reasoning metrics while cutting response length by ~3x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The design space of knowledge distillation in LLM post-training is poorly understood, especially when combined with reinforcement learning. Prevailing paradigms (off-policy and on-policy distillation) implicitly couple two orthogonal choices (prefix source and token-level KL direction), leading to unclear tradeoffs and suboptimal practices.

Method: The authors decompose sequence-level KL divergence over autoregressive response distributions, showing that forward KL pairs teacher prefixes with token-level forward KL, and reverse KL pairs student prefixes with token-level reverse KL. They decouple these axes to yield four valid objectives, establish gradient-level identities connecting them to SFT, DAgger-style, offline-RL, and OPD, then conduct extensive controlled experiments on math reasoning.

Result: Three tradeoffs identified: (1) KL direction → accuracy-entropy tradeoff, (2) prefix source → quality-compute tradeoff, (3) training length → accuracy-stability tradeoff. Proposed KL mixing with substantial forward-KL weight prevents entropy collapse and length inflation. Entropy-gated length curriculum improves Avg@k by 3.6 and Pass@k by up to 5.8 points, with ~3x reduction in average response length vs. fixed long-horizon training.

Conclusion: The paper provides a principled framework for designing reasoning distillation objectives that balance accuracy, diversity, compute, and downstream RL behavior, with practical methods (KL mixing and entropy-gated curriculum) that deliver strong empirical gains.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation is central to LLM post-training, yet its design space remains poorly understood, especially alongside reinforcement learning (RL). We show that the prevailing paradigms, off-policy distillation and on-policy distillation (OPD), implicitly couple two orthogonal choices: prefix source and token-level KL direction. This follows from decomposing sequence-level KL over autoregressive response distributions: forward KL pairs teacher prefixes with token-level forward KL, and reverse KL pairs student prefixes with token-level reverse KL. We argue this coupling is not intrinsic: decoupling the two axes yields four valid objectives. We establish gradient-level identities showing forward KL gives SFT-style cross-entropy matching with teacher soft targets, whereas reverse KL gives an RL-style policy-gradient objective with a dense teacher-student log-ratio reward, connecting them to off-policy SFT, DAgger-style on-policy SFT, offline-RL-style distillation, and OPD. We conduct an extensive controlled study on math reasoning, evaluating the four objectives both as standalone methods and as initializations for subsequent RL. The results reveal three tradeoffs: KL direction induces an accuracy-entropy tradeoff, prefix source a quality-compute tradeoff, and training length an accuracy-stability tradeoff. Motivated by these findings, we propose KL mixing and an entropy-gated length curriculum. KL mixing shows long-sequence distillation requires substantial forward-KL weight to prevent entropy collapse and length inflation without sacrificing accuracy. The entropy-gated length curriculum improves Avg@k and Pass@k by 3.6 and up to 5.8 points, and cuts average response length by roughly 3x versus fixed long-horizon training. Our results provide a framework and practical methods for designing reasoning distillation objectives that balance accuracy, diversity, compute, and RL behavior.

</details>


### [697] [PhysioSeq2Seq: A Hybrid Physiological Digital Twin and Sequence-to-Sequence LSTM for Long-Horizon Glucose Forecasting in Type 1 Diabetes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16860)
*Phat Tran,Neville Mehta,Clara Mosquera-Lopez,Robert H. Dodier,Lizhong Chen,Peter G. Jacobs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhysioSeq2Seq is a hybrid architecture combining patient-specific physiological modeling (digital twins) with Seq2Seq LSTM for long-horizon glucose forecasting in type 1 diabetes, achieving significantly reduced bias and error at 240-minute horizons compared to standard LSTM and ODE-only approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard recursive LSTM networks suffer from systematic negative bias at longer horizons due to error compounding, while purely mechanistic ODE models fail to generalize across individuals when parameterized at the population level.

Method: Hybrid architecture combining patient-specific physiological modeling (digital twins) with Seq2Seq LSTM. Twin matching identifies the best-fitting physiological match from 300 parameterized digital twins using CGM history. 10 internal ODE state variables from the matched twin are injected as exogenous covariates into both encoder and decoder of Seq2Seq LSTM for 48-step simultaneous prediction.

Result: At 240-minute horizon: MAE of 39.28 mg/dL, mean error of -10.62 mg/dL. Reduces bias by 13.89 mg/dL over recursive LSTM, reduces MAE by 28.62 mg/dL over ODE-based digital twin.

Conclusion: Eliminating architectural feedback (recursive error compounding) and injecting patient-matched physiological states is an effective and clinically meaningful strategy for long-horizon glucose forecasting in T1D.

Abstract: Accurate long-horizon glucose forecasting is critical for automated insulin delivery systems, which help people with type 1 diabetes (T1D) manage their glucose and avoid dangerous hypoglycemia. However, standard recursive long short-term memory (LSTM) networks suffer from systematic negative bias at longer horizons due to error compounding, while purely mechanistic ordinary differential equation (ODE) models fail to generalize across individuals when parameterized at the population level. We propose PhysioSeq2Seq, a hybrid architecture that combines patient-specific physiological modeling with a sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) LSTM. For each glucose segment, twin matching searches a population of 300 parameterized digital twins to identify the best-fitting physiological match from a 3-hour continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) history. The 10 internal ODE state variables of the matched twin are injected as exogenous covariates into both the encoder and decoder of the Seq2Seq LSTM. This simultaneous 48-step prediction strategy eliminates recursive error compounding, while the ODE features provide a physics-grounded constraint that bounds long-horizon drift within physiologically plausible ranges. PhysioSeq2Seq was trained on CGM and insulin data from 348 participants in the Type 1 Diabetes Exercise Initiative (T1DEXI) dataset and evaluated on 74 held-out participants. At the 240-minute horizon, PhysioSeq2Seq achieves a mean absolute error of 39.28 mg/dL and a mean error of -10.62 mg/dL, reducing bias by 13.89 mg/dL over the recursive LSTM and reducing mean absolute error by 28.62 mg/dL over the ODE-based digital twin. These results show that eliminating architectural feedback and injecting patient-matched physiological states is an effective and clinically meaningful strategy for long-horizon glucose forecasting in T1D.

</details>


### [698] [SE-GA: Memory-Augmented Self-Evolution for GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16883)
*Shilong Jin,Lanjun Wang,Zhuosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SE-GA proposes a self-evolving GUI agent with hierarchical memory and iterative self-improvement, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks (89.0% on ScreenSpot, 75.8% on AndroidControl-High).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI agents fail in multi-step tasks due to limited context windows and static policies that cannot adapt to dynamic environments.

Method: Two key components: (1) Test-Time Memory Extension (TTME) for dynamic retrieval of episodic, semantic, and experiential memories during inference, and (2) Memory-Augmented Self-Evolution (MASE), a training pipeline that uses TTME-collected data to stabilize and enhance the agent's policy.

Result: SOTA performance: 89.0% on ScreenSpot, 75.8% on AndroidControl-High, with strong generalization shown on AndroidWorld benchmark.

Conclusion: The hierarchical memory and self-evolution mechanism effectively address context window constraints and policy adaptability issues in GUI agents.

Abstract: Autonomous Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents often struggle with multi-step tasks due to constrained context windows and static policies that fail to adapt to dynamic environments. To address these limitations, this work proposes the Self-Evolving GUI Agent (SE-GA), a novel framework that integrates hierarchical memory structures with an iterative self-improvement mechanism. At the core of our approach is Test-Time Memory Extension (TTME), which facilitates long-term planning by dynamically retrieving episodic, semantic, and experiential memories to provide salient contexts during inference. To ensure continuous learning, we introduce Memory-Augmented Self-Evolution (MASE), which is a training pipeline that adopts the data collected by TTME to stabilize and enhance the agent's foundational policy. Extensive evaluations across both offline and online benchmarks demonstrate SE-GA achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching success rates of 89.0\% on ScreenSpot and 75.8\% on the challenging AndroidControl-High dataset. Furthermore, significant improvements on the AndroidWorld benchmark highlight the superior generalization to dynamic environments. Open source code: https://github.com/jinshilong-dev/SE-GA

</details>


### [699] [Tensor Channel Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Molecular Polarizability Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16891)
*Jean Philip Filling,Daniel Franzen,Michael Wand*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a tensor-channel equivariant graph neural network (PaiNN-T) that propagates explicit symmetric rank-2 tensor features throughout message passing to directly predict molecular polarizability tensors. On QM7-X, it outperforms both a PaiNN-style readout baseline and MACE in accuracy while being faster, with ablations confirming the gains come from explicit tensor propagation and traceless target parameterization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Molecular polarizability is a tensor-valued property with isotropic and anisotropic components. Existing equivariant GNNs either construct tensor outputs only at readout (losing structural alignment) or use general higher-order equivariant models that are computationally expensive. The authors aim to design a target-aligned architecture that propagates explicit tensor structure throughout message passing for better accuracy and efficiency.

Method: The model builds on PaiNN, augmenting hidden representations with explicit symmetric rank-2 tensor channels aligned with the isotropic/anisotropic decomposition of polarizability. Tensor structure is propagated via geometrically motivated tensor bases during message passing, using a traceless target parameterization matched to the anisotropic component.

Result: On QM7-X, the proposed model achieves lower full-tensor and anisotropic error than PaiNN-style readout and dielectric MACE baselines at nearly identical parameter counts. It also outperforms MACE while being substantially faster at inference. Ablations show gains come from explicit tensor propagation + traceless target parameterization, not increased capacity. Interactions between learned directional features yield the strongest results.

Conclusion: For structured tensor-valued targets, propagating target-aligned tensor features through the network (instead of constructing tensors only at readout) can outperform both readout-only approaches and more general higher-order equivariant models under matched training conditions.

Abstract: We introduce a tensor-channel equivariant graph neural network for direct prediction of molecular polarizability tensors. Building on the efficient PaiNN architecture, we augment the hidden representation with explicit symmetric rank-2 tensor channels aligned with the decomposition of polarizability into isotropic and anisotropic components. In contrast to approaches that construct tensor outputs only at readout, our model propagates tensor structure throughout message passing using geometrically motivated tensor bases. This yields a target-aligned architecture for tensor-valued molecular prediction.
  On optimized QM7-X geometries, the proposed model achieves lower full-tensor and anisotropic error than both a PaiNN-style readout baseline and a dielectric MACE baseline under matched training conditions and at nearly identical parameter count. In this controlled setting, it also outperforms MACE while remaining substantially faster at inference. Ablation studies show that the gain does not arise from increased capacity alone, but from the combination of explicit tensor propagation and a traceless target parameterization matched to the anisotropic part of the polarizability tensor. Among the tensor bases considered, the strongest results are obtained from interactions between learned directional features, indicating that these are particularly effective for modeling molecular polarizability. Rotational equivariance tests further confirm that all compared models are numerically equivariant, so the observed improvements are attributable to better learning of the target tensor itself. Overall, our results show that for structured tensor-valued targets, propagating target-aligned tensor features can outperform both readout-only tensor construction and a more general higher-order equivariant model in the present training setting.

</details>


### [700] [ArtifactLinker: Linking Scientific Artifacts for Automatic State-of-the-Art Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16902)
*Haofei Yu,Jiaxuan You,Peter Clark,Bodhisattwa Prasad Majumder,Kyle Richardson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes ArtifactLinker, a two-stage framework using GNNs/LLMs and coding agents to automatically discover SOTA models from HuggingFace's artifact graph, introducing ArtifactBench with 14K+ artifacts for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the abundance of ML artifacts on platforms like HuggingFace, there is no automated way to discover the state-of-the-art (SOTA) model for a given dataset by leveraging existing artifact relationships.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) ranking missing model-dataset links using GNNs or graph-augmented LLMs, (2) verifying top-ranked links via LLM-based coding agents. Also introduces ArtifactBench with 14,053 artifacts and 51,337 relations.

Result: Graph structures between artifacts are effective for missing link prediction; the end-to-end ArtifactLinker framework successfully discovers potential SOTA results and research insights.

Conclusion: ArtifactLinker effectively automates SOTA discovery by combining graph-based link prediction with LLM-empowered verification, demonstrating practical value for navigating the growing landscape of ML artifacts.

Abstract: Scientific artifacts such as models and datasets are foundations for research. With the rapid growth of platforms like HuggingFace, researchers now have access to a large number of artifacts. Yet, a key challenge remains: how can we automatically discover the state-of-the-art (SOTA) model for a given dataset by fully leveraging existing artifacts? We formalize this task as automatic SOTA discovery by modeling HuggingFace as an artifact graph, where nodes are models/datasets and edges represent evaluations. We propose ArtifactLinker, a two-stage framework: (1) ranking promising unobserved model--dataset links using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) or graph-augmented Large Language Models (LLMs), and (2) verifying top-ranked links via coding experiments with LLM-based agents. We further introduce a benchmark named ArtifactBench with 14,053 artifacts and 51,337 relations to evaluate the performance of both stages. Results show that (1) graph structures between existing artifacts are effective for missing link prediction; (2) end-to-end ranking and verification with ArtifactLinker help discover potential SOTA results and research insights.

</details>


### [701] [AIM: Adversarial Information Masking for Faithfulness Evaluation of Saliency Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16905)
*Chia-Ying Hsieh,Hsin-Yuan Fang,Chun-Shu Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Adversarial Information Masking (AIM), an adversarial feature replacement framework that evaluates both saliency-map faithfulness and masking-operator reliability more robustly than traditional zero or interpolation-based masking methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for saliency faithfulness are confounded by masking operators—zero masking creates out-of-distribution artifacts, while interpolation-based masking preserves residual predictive information, making reliable evaluation difficult.

Method: AIM replaces selected features with values from an adversarial counterpart of the input, compares degradation under complementary masking orders, and assesses reliability via random-attribution bias and stability of faithfulness rankings.

Result: Experiments on image, audio, and EEG tasks show AIM reduces masking-induced bias compared to zero and interpolation-based masking, and reveals modality-dependent differences between signed and unsigned attributions.

Conclusion: AIM provides a more faithful and reliable framework for evaluating saliency methods across diverse modalities, while also exposing the modality-dependent nature of attribution sign information.

Abstract: Post-hoc saliency methods are widely used to interpret deep neural networks, but their faithfulness is difficult to evaluate reliably. Existing evaluations mask features according to saliency-induced feature ordering and measure performance degradation, but this degradation can be confounded by the masking operator: zero masking may create out-of-distribution artifacts, while interpolation-based masking may preserve residual predictive information. We propose Adversarial Information Masking (AIM), a saliency-guided adversarial feature replacement framework for evaluating both saliency-map faithfulness and masking-operator reliability. AIM replaces selected features with values from an adversarial counterpart of the input and compares degradation under complementary masking orders. We assess reliability using random-attribution bias and stability of explanation-method faithfulness rankings. Experiments on image, audio, and EEG tasks suggest that AIM reduces masking-induced bias compared with zero and interpolation-based masking, while revealing modality-dependent differences between signed and unsigned attributions.

</details>


### [702] [Emulating the Forced Response of Climate Models with Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16929)
*Graham Clyne,Julia Kaltenborn,Peer Nowack,Claire Monteleoni,Anasatase Charantonis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper trains a Deep Learning emulator on climate model outputs conditioned on multiple Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) and successfully generates unseen scenarios, demonstrating the ability to capture climate dynamics with multiple forcings (CO2, methane, etc.), while ablation studies show that diverse forcings are essential for representing long-term atmospheric trends.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Climate models are computationally expensive, limiting the ability to run large ensembles needed for robust estimates under scenario uncertainty and internal variability.

Method: A Deep Learning model is trained on multiple SSP scenarios conditioned on various climate forcings (CO2, methane, nitrous oxide, sulphate aerosols, ozone). The emulator is validated against MESMER-M, a statistical emulator of land surface temperature.

Result: The DL emulator successfully generates climate states for unseen scenarios. Ablation studies confirm that including a range of different forcings is necessary to represent long-term atmospheric trends.

Conclusion: Deep Learning can efficiently emulate climate model dynamics across multiple SSP scenarios, offering a computationally cheaper alternative for generating ensemble simulations, provided diverse forcings are included.

Abstract: Global climate models are essential tools to simulate past and potential future pathways of climate change, as well as associated climate impacts. Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) describe a range of future scenarios of global economic and demographic development. These SSPs are intrinsically linked to changes in climate forcings, the external drivers, such as greenhouse gas and aerosol emissions, which in turn lead to the human impact on the energy balance of the Earth over time. These forcings are fundamental boundary conditions in climate models in order to gain insight into the potential climatic impacts of these changes described by each SSP. Running a climate model, however, is extremely computationally expensive, conflicting with the need for large ensembles of simulations for each model to give, e.g., more robust estimates in the presence of internal variability (the inherent, chaotic fluctuations within the climate system) and scenario uncertainty. Recent research has demonstrated the ability to capture climate model dynamics using machine learning when conditioned on forcings from different climatic scenarios. We here train a Deep Learning (DL) model on multiple SSPs and successfully generate scenarios unseen during training. Our emulator is validated against MESMER-M, a statistical emulator of land surface temperature. Our research demonstrates the capacity to generate such changing climate states in response to a variety of simultaneous climate forcings (e.g., carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, sulphate aerosols, and ozone). In particular, our ablation studies underline a need to include a range of different forcings to represent long-term atmospheric trends with a DL emulator.

</details>


### [703] [Extending Pretrained 10-Second ECG Foundation Models to Longer Horizons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16975)
*Wei Tang,Jinpei Han,Kangning Cui,Mattia Carletti,Fredrik K. Gustafsson,Shreyank N Gowda,Patitapaban Palo,Anshul Thakur,Lei Clifton,Jean-michel Morel,Raymond H. Chan,David A. Clifton,Xiao Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a parameter-efficient framework to extend pretrained ECG foundation models from 10-second segments to longer, variable-length recordings without retraining, using a lightweight plug-in module that handles both structural and semantic challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world ECG recordings are often longer and vary in duration, but existing pretrained ECG foundation models are designed for 10-second segments and cannot effectively aggregate information across longer time horizons.

Method: A lightweight plug-in module guided by a frozen pretrained 10-second model, with two components: (i) structurally compatible long-sequence processing, and (ii) semantically informed temporal modeling for meaningful aggregation over time.

Result: The method consistently outperforms sliding-window and pooling-based baselines across multiple long-horizon ECG tasks, datasets, and backbone models, with strong parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: Pretrained short-duration ECG models can be effectively extended to long, variable-length recordings via a lightweight, parameter-efficient plug-in module without retraining the backbone.

Abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) foundation models pretrained on typical diagnostic 10-second ECG segments, have demonstrated strong transferability across a range of clinical applications. However, many real-world applications produce recordings that are typically longer, and are varied in duration during inference time. These 10-second models have no built-in way to combine information across time. Extending them to longer horizons introduces two challenges: structural incompatibilities arising from input-length disparities, and semantic challenges that limit meaningful temporal aggregation. We propose a parameter-efficient framework that extends pretrained ECG foundation models to longer and variable-length ECGs without retraining the backbone. Guided by a frozen pretrained 10-second model, we introduce a lightweight plug-in module that extends the model in two complementary ways: (i) structurally compatible long-sequence processing and (ii) semantically informed temporal modeling. Experiments on multiple long-horizon ECG tasks, datasets, and foundation model backbones demonstrate that our method enables robust long-horizon extension from pretrained snapshot models, consistently outperforming sliding-window and pooling-based baselines with strong parameter efficiency.

</details>


### [704] [Decision-Aware Proximal Bridge Learning for Optimal Treatment Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16989)
*Tomàs Garriga,Alejandro Almodóvar,Axel Brando,Gerard Sanz,Eduard Serrahima de Cambra,Juan Parras*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a decision-aware weighting approach for optimal treatment selection in proximal causal inference with continuous actions, using a policy-targeted weighted bridge loss that improves regret bounds and outperforms global estimation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard causal response estimation allocates modeling effort according to the observed treatment distribution, which is suboptimal for treatment selection since decision-relevant regions may differ from regions with dense data. Decision-aware approaches exist in unconfounded settings but remain underexplored in proximal causal inference with hidden confounding.

Method: The authors propose a policy-targeted weighted bridge loss that emphasizes decision-relevant treatment regions while maintaining global stabilization. They prove a regret bound controlled by a weighted ill-posedness constant. The framework is instantiated as decision-aware variants of proximal bridge solvers, alternating between weighted bridge estimation, response-surface projection, policy update, and weight refinement.

Result: Theoretical regret bound showing weighted bridge loss controls treatment-selection regret. Empirically, decision-aware weighting reduces regret across multiple bridge solvers compared to standard approaches.

Conclusion: Decision-aware weighting improves treatment selection in proximal causal inference settings, bridging the gap between proximal methods and optimal decision-making.

Abstract: Individualized treatment selection with continuous actions requires accurate causal response estimation in decision-relevant regions, rather than uniformly over the entire action space. Estimating a global causal response surface and then choosing the treatment that maximizes it can therefore be suboptimal, since standard estimation objectives allocate modeling effort according to the observed treatment distribution rather than the regions that determine the optimal decision. While decision-aware approaches have been studied in unconfounded settings, this problem remains underexplored in proximal causal inference, where proxy variables and bridge functions enable identification under suitable assumptions even in the presence of hidden confounding. Despite recent progress, proximal methods have primarily focused on treatment-effect and potential-outcome estimation rather than treatment selection and optimal decision-making. To bridge this gap, we introduce a policy-targeted weighted bridge loss that emphasizes decision-relevant treatment regions while retaining global stabilization. We prove a regret bound showing that the proposed weighted bridge loss controls treatment-selection regret through a weighted ill-posedness constant. We instantiate the framework in decision-aware variants of several proximal bridge solvers, yielding practical algorithms that alternate between weighted bridge estimation, response-surface projection, policy update, and weight refinement. Empirically, we find that decision-aware weighting reduces regret across several bridge solvers, suggesting improved treatment selection in proximal settings.

</details>


### [705] [Ranking-Aware Calibration for Reliable Multimodal Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16999)
*Peng Cui,Boyao Yang,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RAC is a training-time framework that improves both calibration and accuracy of vision-language models by using ranking-aware and clean-corrupted pairwise loss signals derived from group-based RL, without requiring external confidence annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning post-training improves reasoning accuracy in vision-language models but produces poorly calibrated policies. Terminal correctness rewards fail to penalize confident errors or tie confidence to visual evidence quality, especially under corrupted/ambiguous inputs where models report high confidence on wrong answers.

Method: RAC introduces two supervision signals: (1) ranking-aware group loss ensuring better rollouts receive higher confidence within the same prompt, and (2) clean-corrupted pairwise loss enforcing confidence attenuation as visual evidence degrades. Both integrate naturally with group-based RL post-training and require no external confidence annotations.

Result: Evaluated on Qwen2.5-VL and InternVL-3.5 across six multimodal reasoning benchmarks (clean and corrupted inputs). Ranking-aware loss improves task accuracy; pairwise corruption loss reduces calibration error under degraded inputs; their combination achieves best calibration while improving accuracy in most settings.

Conclusion: RAC effectively addresses the calibration gap in RL-post-trained vision-language models by leveraging comparison signals from group-based RL, simultaneously improving both accuracy and calibration without additional labeling cost.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning post-training has substantially improved the reasoning accuracy of vision-language models, yet the resulting policies remain poorly calibrated. Terminal correctness rewards provide no gradient that penalizes confident errors more than uncertain ones and no signal that ties confidence to the quality of visual evidence, a gap that becomes especially severe under corrupted or ambiguous inputs where models continue to report high confidence on incorrect answers. We introduce Ranking-Aware Calibration (RAC), a training-time framework that supervises confidence using two comparison signals that group-based RL already produces at no additional labeling cost. The ranking-aware group loss enforces that a better rollout receives higher confidence than a worse one within the same prompt. The clean--corrupted pairwise loss enforces that confidence attenuates as visual evidence degrades. Because the ranking signal forces the policy to distinguish between correct and incorrect reasoning paths, it also reinforces task accuracy beyond what correctness rewards alone produce. Both losses require no external confidence annotations and integrate naturally with group-based RL post-training. We instantiate RAC on Qwen2.5-VL and InternVL-3.5 backbones and evaluate on six multimodal reasoning benchmarks under clean and corrupted inputs. Empirical results show that the ranking-aware loss substantially improves task accuracy by teaching the policy to discriminate between better and worse reasoning, while the pairwise corruption loss reduces calibration error under degraded inputs. Their combination achieves the best calibration across all tested backbones while improving accuracy in the majority of settings.

</details>


### [706] [BoLT: A Benchmark to Democratize Black-box Optimization Research for Expensive LLM Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17000)
*Ruth Wan Theng Chew,Zhiliang Chen,Apivich Hemachandra,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BoLT is a new benchmark for Bayesian optimization and black-box optimization methods, specifically designed for LLM training and inference configuration problems, featuring surrogate models fitted to real experimental data across multi-fidelity, multi-objective, heteroscedastic noise, and high-dimensional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM configuration optimization is often done heuristically, and BBO methods are promising but lack realistic LLM-centric benchmarks to evaluate their effectiveness, as existing benchmarks rely on synthetic functions or small-scale tasks that don't capture real LLM challenges.

Method: BoLT provides lightweight surrogate models fitted to thousands of real LLM experiments, covering diverse optimization problems (multi-fidelity, multi-objective, heteroscedastic noise, high-dimensional). It benchmarks a wide range of BO and BBO methods against these problems.

Result: Selected BO methods consistently outperform others across tasks, and gaps in existing BBO methods are highlighted when applied to LLM-centric problems.

Conclusion: There is a need to modernize benchmarks for the BBO community, and BoLT serves as a foundation for developing and evaluating BBO methods on realistic LLM optimization tasks.

Abstract: Optimization of LLM training and inference configurations, such as hyperparameters, data mixtures, and prompts, is critical to performance, but it is often approached heuristically in practice, leading to potentially suboptimal outcomes. By framing them as noisy, expensive, and derivative-free optimization problems, Bayesian optimization (BO) and other black-box optimization (BBO) methods offer a promising yet underexplored direction for principled, sample-efficient methods. However, LLM training and inference costs are prohibitively high for most of the BBO research community, and new methods are often only evaluated on synthetic test functions and small-scale datasets that fail to capture the challenges of modern LLM optimization problems. This impedes the development of BBO methods and makes it difficult to assess their effectiveness on modern LLM tasks. We introduce BoLT, the first LLM-centric benchmark that democratizes LLM research for the BBO community. BoLT is released at https://github.com/chewwt/bolt. BoLT covers broad and well-motivated LLM optimization problems, involving multi-fidelity, multi-objective, heteroscedastic noise, and high-dimensional search spaces. Each problem in BoLT is grounded in real experimental data and made fully reproducible and accessible through lightweight surrogate models fitted to the results of thousands of real LLM experiments. We benchmark BoLT against an extensive range of BO and BBO methods, showing that selected BO methods consistently outperform others across tasks and highlighting gaps in existing BBO methods on LLM tasks, underscoring the need to modernize benchmarks for the BBO community.

</details>


### [707] [Learning-Zone Energy: Online Data Selection for Efficient RL Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17003)
*Peng Cui,Boyao Yang,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LZE is a data selection framework for RL post-training of LLMs that focuses compute on samples in the model's "learning zone" — those neither too easy nor too hard — using a three-signal scoring mechanism, achieving full-data performance while retaining only 40% of training data and reducing FLOPs by 36%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL post-training methods (GRPO, DAPO) waste compute by treating all prompts uniformly, including those already mastered or too difficult for the model's current capability. The authors aim to address this fundamental inefficiency.

Method: LZE defines a Learning-Zone Energy Score combining three signals: (1) initial-difficulty anchor, (2) normalized outcome-uncertainty, and (3) pass-rate momentum. This score aligns with expected policy gradient update magnitude. A forward pruner with replay skips rollout generation for persistently solved prompts while periodically checking for forgetting.

Result: Evaluated on Qwen-family models (1.5B-8B) across GSM8K, MATH, and DAPO-MATH. Retains only 40% of training data per step yet matches/surpasses full-data baselines. Notable out-of-distribution gains: AIME25 (+45.9%), AMC23 (+18.2%). Estimated 36% reduction in training FLOPs.

Conclusion: LZE is a theoretically grounded, effective data selection framework that significantly improves training efficiency for RL post-training of LLMs without sacrificing (and in some cases improving) performance, especially on out-of-distribution benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training has emerged as the dominant paradigm for eliciting mathematical reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs), yet prevailing techniques such as GRPO and DAPO distribute rollout and gradient budgets nearly uniformly across prompts, squandering compute on samples that are already mastered or remain far beyond the model's current capability. To address this fundamental inefficiency, we propose Learning-Zone Energy (LZE), a theoretically grounded, fully online data selection framework that concentrates computation on the model's active learning frontier. At its core, we define a closed-form Learning-Zone Energy Score that fuses three complementary signals, an initial-difficulty anchor, a normalized outcome-uncertainty term, and a pass-rate momentum, into a single scalar that is provably aligned with the expected magnitude of group-relative policy gradient updates. A forward pruner with replay further reduces wall-clock time cost by skipping rollout generation for persistently solved prompts while periodically checking for forgetting. Evaluated on Qwen-family models (1.5B-8B) across GSM8K, MATH and DAPO-MATH, our method retains only 40% of the training data per step yet matches or surpasses full-data baselines, with especially pronounced out-of-distribution gains on AIME25 (+45.9%) and AMC23 (+18.2%), alongside an estimated 36% reduction in training FLOPs. Our code is available at https://github.com/Stellaris167/LZE.

</details>


### [708] [When Dynamics Shift, Robust Task Inference Wins: Offline Imitation Learning with Behavior Foundation Models Revisited](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17017)
*Rishabh Agrawal,Rahul Jain,Ashutosh Nayyar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a robust minimax optimization approach for Behavior Foundation Models (BFMs) that enables adaptation to worst-case dynamics shifts (e.g., friction, actuation, sensor noise) at task-inference time using only offline data from a single nominal environment, outperforming standard BFM and robust offline IL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing BFMs assume fixed environment dynamics, which limits their robustness under real-world shifts such as changes in friction, actuation, or sensor noise.

Method: The authors formulate BFM task-inference as a robust minimax optimization problem, enabling adaptation to worst-case dynamics perturbations without modifying pretraining and relying solely on offline data from a single nominal environment.

Result: The proposed approach significantly outperforms standard BFM and robust offline IL baselines under dynamics shifts.

Conclusion: Robust policy can be achieved entirely at task-inference time, improving the practicality of BFMs in dynamic settings.

Abstract: Behavior Foundation Models (BFMs) enable scalable imitation learning (IL) by pretraining task-agnostic representations that can be rapidly adapted to new tasks. However, existing BFMs assume fixed environment dynamics, limiting their robustness under real-world shifts such as changes in friction, actuation, or sensor noise. We address this by formulating BFM task-inference as a robust minimax optimization problem, enabling adaptation to worst-case dynamics perturbations without modifying pretraining. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first BFM-based framework that achieves robustness to dynamics shifts while relying solely on offline data from a single nominal environment. Our approach significantly outperforms standard BFM and robust offline IL baselines under dynamics shifts. These results demonstrate that robust policy can be achieved entirely at task-inference time, improving the practicality of BFMs in dynamic settings.

</details>


### [709] [Why Do Reasoning Models Lose Coverage? The Role of Data and Forks in the Road](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17026)
*Ngoc-Hieu Nguyen,Parshin Shojaee,Phuc Minh Nguyen,Nan Zhang,Chandan K Reddy,Khoa D Doan,Rui Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates why reasoning models exhibit "coverage shrinkage" (pass@k degradation) under SFT-based post-training, attributes this to decision-point scenarios in fine-tuning data, and shows that targeted data design and diversity-encouraging decoding can partially mitigate the issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior works observed that reasoning models, despite improving pass@1 accuracy through specialized fine-tuning, suffer from coverage shrinkage (degraded pass@k). The authors aim to understand the underlying cause of this shrinkage phenomenon, particularly under SFT-based post-training.

Method: The authors design controlled case studies simulating decision-point settings (indecipherable nodes in graph branching and reasoning modes), track post-training dynamics, and propose mitigation via targeted data synthesis of decision-points and a diversity-encouraging decoding mechanism.

Result: The shrinkage phenomenon is tightly correlated with the prevalence of decision-point scenarios in training data. Targeted data synthesis and diversity-aware decoding can partially mitigate the shrinkage.

Conclusion: Data-centric factors (decision-point scenarios) are a key driver of shrinkage in reasoning models, and diversity-aware designs offer an effective lever for controlling it.

Abstract: Recent progress in large language models has led to the emergence of reasoning models, which have shown strong performance on complex tasks through specialized fine-tuning procedures. While these methods reliably improve pass@1 accuracy, prior works have observed that they show a coverage shrinkage behavior, where pass@k degrades relative to the base model. In this paper, we investigate the reasoning shrinkage arise under SFT-based post-training. We hypothesize that this behavior is driven by properties of the fine-tuning data, specifically related to decision points or "forks in the road" scenarios where model faces indecipherable patterns with multiple valid reasoning paths. To test this hypothesis, we design controlled case studies that simulate such decision-point settings, spanning indecipherable nodes in graph branching, and reasoning modes. By tracking post-training dynamics in these settings, we find that the shrinkage phenomenon is tightly correlated with the prevalence of decision-point scenarios in the training data. We also demonstrate that this shrinkage behavior can be partially mitigated through targeted data synthesis design of decision-points, and a more systematic diversity-encouraging decoding mechanism. Our findings identify data-centric factors as a key driver of shrinkage in reasoning models and highlight diversity-aware designs as an effective lever for controlling it.

</details>


### [710] [Privacy Policy Enforcement Guardrails for Data-Sensitive Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17034)
*Osama Zafar,Alexander Nemecek,Yiqian Zhang,Wenbiao Li,Debargha Ganguly,Vikash Singh,Vipin Chaudhary,Erman Ayday*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Privacy Policy Enforcement (PPE) framework using dual one-class density estimators with fused text embeddings to detect contextual data leakage in RAG systems, achieving 0.93+ borderline AUROC with 44-55% fewer false positives than traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PII filters fail to detect contextual data leakage where non-regulated attribute clusters collectively identify individuals, motivating a more robust detection framework.

Method: Uses dual one-class density estimators with fused text embeddings, a calibrated abstain region for OOD inputs, and an axis-stratified multi-LLM synthetic data pipeline. The T3+OCSVM is trained on safe and borderline-safe data.

Result: T3+OCSVM achieves borderline AUROC of 0.93+, reduces false positives by 44-55 percentage points, maintains millisecond latency, and outperforms Gaussian Mixture baselines, supervised MLP classifiers, and 14B-parameter LLM judges.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers superior operational suitability for privacy enforcement in RAG systems and provides a robust stress-testing standard for synthetic-data-trained classifiers.

Abstract: Standard PII filters often miss contextual data leakage in RAG systems, such as non-regulated attribute clusters that collectively identify individuals. We introduce a Privacy Policy Enforcement (PPE) framework using dual one-class density estimators with fused text embeddings and a calibrated abstain region for out-of-distribution inputs. Using an axis-stratified, multi-LLM synthetic data pipeline across medicine, finance, and law, we found that traditional Gaussian Mixture baselines fail on borderline-safe stress tests by focusing on linguistic register rather than content.
  Our proposed T3+OCSVM detector, trained on safe and borderline-safe data, achieves a borderline AUROC of 0.93+ while reducing false positives by 44-55 percentage points and maintaining millisecond latency. Compared to supervised MLP classifiers or 14B-parameter LLM judges, our framework offers superior operational suitability, as the former suffers from high abstention rates and the latter from latency and calibration issues. This methodology provides a robust stress-testing standard for any synthetic-data-trained classifier.

</details>


### [711] [D$^2$Evo: Dual Difficulty-Aware Self-Evolution for Data-Efficient Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17037)
*Ru Zhang,Renda Li,Ziyu Ma,Weijie Qiu,Chongyang Tao,Yong Wang,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: D²Evo is a dual difficulty-aware self-evolution RL framework that addresses data scarcity and difficulty shift challenges in LLM reasoning by mining medium-difficulty anchors, training a Questioner to generate appropriately difficult questions, and jointly optimizing components for progressive reasoning improvement, outperforming existing methods with fewer than 2K real samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning for LLM reasoning struggles with two challenges: effective data scarcity (medium-difficulty training samples are hard to find) and dynamic difficulty shifts (samples become too easy as models improve). Existing generation-based methods suffer from anchor-free generation, ignoring co-evolution, and difficulty mismatch.

Method: D²Evo is an iterative framework that: (1) mines medium-difficulty anchors based on the current Solver's capability, (2) trains a Questioner to generate diverse questions at appropriate difficulty levels, and (3) jointly optimizes both Questioner and Solver for progressive reasoning gains.

Result: D²Evo outperforms existing methods on mathematical reasoning benchmarks using fewer than 2K real mathematical samples, and demonstrates strong generalization on general reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: The dual difficulty-aware co-evolution approach effectively addresses data scarcity and difficulty shift problems, achieving superior reasoning performance with minimal real data.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has demonstrated potential for enhancing reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, effective RL training, which requires medium-difficulty training samples, faces two fundamental challenges: Effective Data Scarcity and Dynamic Difficulty Shifts, where medium-difficulty samples are scarce and become trivial as models improve. Existing methods mitigate this scarcity to some extent by generating training samples. However, these approaches suffer from anchor-free generation, ignoring co-evolution, and difficulty mismatch. To address these issues, we propose D$^2$Evo, a Dual Difficulty-aware self-Evolution RL framework. In each iteration, our method mines medium-difficulty anchors based on the current Solver's capability, trains the Questioner to generate diverse questions at appropriate difficulty levels, and jointly optimizes both components to enable progressive reasoning gains. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D$^2$Evo outperforms existing methods on mathematical reasoning benchmarks with fewer than 2K real mathematical samples, and exhibits strong generalization on general reasoning benchmarks.

</details>


### [712] [Privacy-Preserving Generation Fraud Detection for Distributed Photovoltaic Systems: A Solar Irradiance-Fused Federated Learning Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17039)
*Xiaolu Chen,Chenghao Huang,Yanru Zhang,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving federated learning framework for detecting PV generation fraud that uses co-attention for data fusion and prototype alignment to handle class imbalance, showing superior performance on real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Residential PV systems introduce new challenges for generation fraud detection due to intermittency, uncertainty, and distributed nature of PV, while centralized approaches face scalability and privacy issues.

Method: A federated learning framework where each community has a local detector fusing PV generation and weather data via co-attention mechanism; FL aggregates model parameters and prototypes with prototype alignment for class imbalance.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art FL methods on real-world PV dataset; demonstrates scalability across varying community sizes and strong robustness to class imbalance.

Conclusion: The proposed privacy-preserving distributed framework effectively addresses PV generation fraud detection with strong performance, scalability, and robustness.

Abstract: The wide adoption of residential photovoltaic (PV) systems introduces new challenges for generation fraud detection (FD). Unlike traditional electricity theft detection, which focuses on electricity consumption-side behavior, PV generation fraud detection (PVG-FD) is complicated by the inherent intermittency and uncertainty of PV generation. The distributed nature of PV systems poses further challenges for centralized PVG-FD approaches due to scalability and privacy concerns. This paper develops a privacy-preserving distributed PVG-FD framework based on federated learning (FL). In this framework, a utility company manages multiple household communities, where each of which is equipped with a local detector. The framework integrates a novel detection model architecture with privacy-preserving global collaboration. Each community's local model fuses PV generation and weather data via a co-attention mechanism to detect discrepancies critical for PVG-FD. The FL framework enables cross-community collaboration by aggregating model parameters and prototypes, leveraging global knowledge sharing with local refinement while preserving privacy. It also uses prototype alignment to address class imbalance by enhancing fraud sample representation. Extensive experiments on a real-world residential PV dataset validate the effectiveness of the developed method and demonstrate that it outperforms state-of-the-art FL methods across various scenarios. The results also show its scalability across varying community sizes and strong robustness to class imbalance.

</details>


### [713] [1GC-7RC: One Graphic Card -- Seven Research Challenges! How Good Are AI Agents at Doing Your Job?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17046)
*Robin-Nico Kampa,Fabian Deuser,Anna Bößendörfer,Konrad Habel,Norbert Oswald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces 1GC-7RC, a benchmark with seven ML tasks to evaluate AI coding agents' ability to design, implement, and train models from scratch on a single GPU under time constraints, finding substantial performance differences across seven tested agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the growing adoption of autonomous AI coding agents in ML practice, no standardized benchmark exists to evaluate their ability to design, implement, and train models from scratch across diverse domains.

Method: The benchmark comprises seven ML tasks (language modeling, image classification, semantic segmentation, graph learning, tabular prediction, time-series forecasting, text classification) with locked data-prep and evaluation scripts, baseline training scripts, no internet access, no pretrained weights (one exception), task-specific wall-clock budgets (40-120 min), on a single GPU. Seven coding agents were evaluated across 5 runs per task.

Result: Substantial performance differences were observed across agents, revealing varying levels of implicit ML knowledge, planning ability, and time-budget management. Five proprietary agents (Claude Code with Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.6, Opus 4.7; Codex CLI with GPT 5.5; OpenCode with Qwen 3.6+) and two open-source agents (OpenCode with Kimi K2.5, Kimi K2.6) were tested.

Conclusion: The 1GC-7RC benchmark is a modular, extensible platform for reproducible evaluation of autonomous research agents, adaptable to new tasks, GPU budgets, and multi-agent settings. All artifacts are publicly released on GitHub.

Abstract: Autonomous AI coding agents are becoming a core tool for ML practitioners in industry and research alike. Despite this growing adoption, no standardized benchmark exists to evaluate their ability to design, implement, and train models from scratch across diverse domains. We introduce **1GC-7RC** (*Single Graphic Card: Seven Research Challenges*), a benchmark comprising seven ML tasks spanning language modeling, image classification, semantic segmentation, graph learning, tabular prediction, time-series forecasting, and text classification. Each task provides a locked data-preparation and evaluation script together with a baseline training script; the agent may only modify the training code, has no access to pretrained weights (with one controlled exception for semantic segmentation), no internet access, and must complete each task within a task-specific wall-clock budget (40-120 minutes) on a single GPU. We evaluate seven coding agents: five proprietary (Claude Code with Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.6, and Opus 4.7; Codex CLI with GPT 5.5; and OpenCode with Qwen 3.6+) and two open-source (OpenCode with Kimi K2.5, Kimi K2.6). Across 5 runs per agent-task pair, we report substantial performance differences that reveal varying levels of implicit ML knowledge, planning ability, and time-budget management. The benchmark, harness, and all evaluation artifacts are publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/Strolchii/1GC-7RC-Benchmark to facilitate reproducible comparison of future agents. Because our benchmark design is modular, the benchmark can be extended to new tasks and domains, adapted to different GPU budgets, and used to study multi-agent settings, making it a flexible platform for future research on autonomous research agents.

</details>


### [714] [Learning Multi-Timescale Abstractions for Hierarchical Combinatorial Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17058)
*Vivienne Huiling Wang,Tinghuai Wang,Joni Pajarinen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a model-based hierarchical RL framework for Sequential Stochastic Combinatorial Optimization that combines latent-space tree-search planning with an SMDP-aware world model and multi-timescale objectives to handle variable-duration decisions and limited resources, outperforming baselines on challenging benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SSCO tasks are challenging due to the combination of exponentially large action spaces, stochastic dynamics, and long-horizon decision-making under limited resources. While HRL offers a natural decomposition, it places the high-level policy in an SMDP where actions have variable durations, making world model learning for planning difficult.

Method: A model-based hierarchical framework combining: (1) latent-space tree-search planner, (2) SMDP-aware world model for variable-duration decisions, (3) multi-timescale objective structuring latent dynamics to reflect temporal scales of abstract actions, and (4) subgoal-conditioned budget policy learned jointly with the world model for context-aware resource allocation.

Result: The proposed method outperforms strong baselines across challenging SSCO benchmarks.

Conclusion: The SMDP-aware world model with multi-timescale objectives and joint budget policy learning effectively addresses the variable-duration decision challenge in hierarchical SSCO, enabling efficient planning and resource allocation under temporal abstraction.

Abstract: The combination of exponentially large action spaces, stochastic dynamics, and long-horizon decision-making under limited resources makes Sequential Stochastic Combinatorial Optimization (SSCO) particularly challenging for reinforcement learning. Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) offers a natural decomposition, but it places the high-level policy in a Semi-Markov Decision Process (SMDP) where actions have variable durations, making it difficult to learn a world model that is suitable for planning. We introduce a model-based hierarchical framework for sequential stochastic combinatorial decision-making that directly addresses this issue. Our method combines a latent-space tree-search planner with an SMDP-aware world model for variable-duration decisions. A multi-timescale objective structures the latent dynamics so that transition magnitudes reflect the effective temporal scales of abstract actions, enabling efficient lookahead under adaptive temporal abstraction. We further learn a subgoal-conditioned budget policy jointly with the world model to support context-aware resource allocation. Across challenging SSCO benchmarks, our method outperforms strong baselines.

</details>


### [715] [S-Bus: Automatic Read-Set Reconstruction for Multi-Agent LLM State Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17076)
*Sajjad Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S-Bus is an HTTP middleware using a server-side DeliveryLog to prevent Structural Race Conditions in concurrent LLM agents sharing mutable state, providing Observable-Read Isolation (ORI) consistency. It achieves zero Type-I corruptions across 427K+ conflicts but can be harmful in single-shard collaborative writing scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concurrent LLM agents sharing mutable natural-language state suffer from Structural Race Conditions (SRCs) - write-write and cross-shard stale-read conflicts that silently corrupt agent output. Existing frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen) provide no write-ownership semantics over shared state.

Method: S-Bus is an HTTP middleware with a server-side DeliveryLog (per-agent log of HTTP GET operations) that automatically reconstructs each agent's read set at commit time without agent SDK changes under HTTP/1.1. It provides Observable-Read Isolation (ORI) - a partial causal consistency over HTTP-observable read set projection.

Result: Three contributions: (C1) DeliveryLog mechanism with TLAPS, TLC (20,763,484 states, zero violations), and Dafny verification; (C2) Zero Type-I corruptions across 427,308 HTTP-409 conflicts vs PostgreSQL 17 SERIALIZABLE and Redis 7 WATCH/MULTI; (C3) ORI is topology-conditional - harmless in dedicated-shard workloads but harmful in single-shard collaborative writing.

Conclusion: S-Bus effectively prevents Structural Race Conditions in concurrent LLM agents through its DeliveryLog mechanism providing ORI consistency, with strong formal verification. However, its operating envelope is topology-conditional and can be detrimental in single-shard collaborative writing scenarios.

Abstract: Concurrent LLM agents sharing mutable natural-language state produce Structural Race Conditions (SRCs): write-write and cross-shard stale-read conflicts that silently corrupt agent output. Existing multi-agent frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen) provide no write-ownership semantics over shared state.
  We present S-Bus, an HTTP middleware whose central mechanism is a server-side DeliveryLog: a per-agent log of HTTP GET operations that automatically reconstructs each agent's read set at commit time without agent SDK changes under HTTP/1.1. The consistency property the DeliveryLog provides -- Observable-Read Isolation (ORI), a partial causal consistency over the HTTP-observable projection of the read set -- prevents structural race conditions when agents collaborate via shared shards.
  Three contributions: (C1) The DeliveryLog mechanism for automatic HTTP-traffic-based read-set reconstruction, with three-tier mechanised evidence: ReadSetSoundness and ORICommitSafety machine-checked in TLAPS (modulo one retained typing axiom); exhaustive TLC at N=3 (20,763,484 distinct states, zero violations); Dafny discharges 9 inductive soundness lemmas. (C2) Empirical structural-conflict prevention parity against PostgreSQL 17 SERIALIZABLE and Redis 7 WATCH/MULTI on shared-shard contention sweeps with 427,308 active HTTP-409 conflicts: zero Type-I corruptions across all three backends. (C3) ORI's operating envelope is topology-conditional: semantically neutral in dedicated-shard workloads; harmful in single-shard collaborative writing because preservation propagates concurrent contradictions.
  Source code: https://github.com/sajjadanwar0/sbus

</details>


### [716] [Scale Determines Whether Language Models Organize Representation Geometry for Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17084)
*Weilun Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Subspace PGA, a metric that measures how well a language model's representation geometry aligns with the unembedding readout. Across models of varying scales, intermediate layers' geometry is organized for prediction, but small models progressively lose this alignment in late layers during training (while still improving loss), whereas large models preserve it. This is due to a capacity trade-off where dominant directions mask predictive structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tools characterize the shape of representation geometry but do not ask what that shape is organized for. The authors want to test whether layer geometry aligns with the readout subspace of the unembedding matrix, revealing whether representations are structured for prediction.

Method: Subspace PGA (Principal Gradient Analysis) is introduced to measure alignment between a layer's distance structure and the unembedding matrix W_U's readout subspace, comparing against random subspaces. Experiments span seven Pythia models (70M-6.9B) and three cross-family models.

Result: Intermediate geometry is significantly organized for prediction (peak z=9-24). Small models (d≤1024) progressively lose alignment at late layers during training despite improving loss, while large models (d≥2048) preserve it. The loss of alignment is due to dominant directions migrating away from W_U's readout, masking rather than destroying predictive structure.

Conclusion: Scale determines not only how well a model predicts, but how its representation geometry is organized to do so. Spectral metrics and loss curves fail to capture this distinction. The capacity trade-off means that in small models, dominant directions mask predictive structure rather than removing it.

Abstract: In language models, what a representation encodes is determined by the geometry of its representation space: distances, not activations, carry meaning. Existing tools characterize the shape of this geometry but do not ask what that shape is organized for. We introduce Subspace PGA, a metric that tests whether a layer's distance structure aligns with the readout subspace of the unembedding matrix $W_U$ more than with random subspaces of equal size. Across seven Pythia models (70M--6.9B) and three cross-family models, intermediate geometry is significantly organized for prediction (peak $z = 9$--$24$), but the degree is scale-dependent: small models ($d \leq 1024$) progressively lose it at late layers during training -- even as loss keeps improving -- while large models ($d \geq 2048$) preserve it throughout. We trace this to a capacity trade-off: a few dominant directions migrate away from $W_U$'s readout, masking rather than destroying the predictive structure beneath, and removing them restores alignment. Neither spectral metrics nor loss curves capture this distinction. Scale thus determines not only how well a model predicts, but how its representation geometry is organized to do so.

</details>


### [717] [Mechanism Learning: Prototype-Anchored Mechanism Inference for Scientific Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17091)
*Qian Jiang,Liping Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes "mechanism learning," a forecasting framework that predicts future states by estimating local evolution rules (mechanisms) instead of directly predicting states. It uses prototype anchors to cover the mechanism space and demonstrates superior performance on Burgers dynamics, WeatherBench2, and Lorenz96, especially under data scarcity and non-stationarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct state prediction fails under data scarcity, long horizons, non-stationary dynamics, or high-dimensional complexity. Local evolution rules, however, remain reusable and robust in these fragile regimes, motivating a shift from state prediction to mechanism estimation.

Method: The framework compresses local spatiotemporal fragments into mechanism descriptors to form a structured mechanism space. It uses prototype anchors (a sparse set of representative mechanisms) to ground estimates in observed data, enabling forecasting by identifying the currently active local mechanism.

Result: On Burgers dynamics, WeatherBench2, and Lorenz96, the method resists collapse, maintains local consistency, and achieves predictive gains. It improves switching stability in Burgers, achieves SOTA on scarce-data WeatherBench2, and excels on Lorenz96. Ablations confirm improvements come from finite prototype anchoring, not latent capacity.

Conclusion: Mechanism learning offers a principled and robust alternative to direct state prediction for forecasting complex systems, particularly in fragile regimes where traditional methods struggle.

Abstract: Scientific forecasting typically relies on direct state prediction, an approach that grows brittle under data scarcity, extended horizons, non-stationary dynamics, or high-dimensional complexity. While raw state trajectories are highly sensitive in these regimes, underlying local evolution rules often exhibit robust reusability. We introduce mechanism learning, a framework that forecasts future states by estimating the currently active local mechanism. Our method compresses local spatiotemporal fragments into mechanism descriptors, forming a data-driven, structured mechanism space where proximity reflects similar local evolution rules. To ground these estimates in observed data, we utilize prototype anchors, a set of representative mechanisms that sparsely cover the space of local rules. We evaluate this approach on Burgers dynamics, WeatherBench2, and Lorenz96. Empirically, the learned mechanism spaces resist collapse and maintain strong local consistency. Compared to direct prediction and other models including FNO, NODE, LSTM, and reservoir-family methods, our framework demonstrates predictive gains in fragile regimes: it significantly improves switching stability in Burgers dynamics and achieves state-of-the-art performance both under the scarce-data fixed-horizon WeatherBench2 protocol and in intermediate-complexity Lorenz96. Ablation studies and drift diagnostics confirm that these improvements are driven by finite prototype anchoring rather than sheer latent capacity. Together, these results establish mechanism learning as a principled, robust alternative to direct state prediction in forecasting complex systems.

</details>


### [718] [Parallel Recursive LSTM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17108)
*Tristan Gaudreault,Yongyi Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PR-LSTM replaces sequential LSTM recurrence with a balanced-tree hierarchical composition, achieving logarithmic parallel depth while preserving nonlinear gated dynamics, and outperforms RNN, LSTM, and Transformer baselines on formal-language benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers suffer from quadratic cost in long contexts, while recurrent models like LSTMs are limited by sequential computation — the paper aims to reconcile parallelism (like Transformers) with nonlinear state-tracking (like RNNs).

Method: PR-LSTM maps tokens to latent states independently, then recursively merges them in a balanced binary tree using a learned gated composition block, using the reduction pattern of parallel scans as a fixed schedule.

Result: PR-LSTM generalizes well to long sequences on formal-language tasks, solving more tasks than RNN, LSTM, and Transformer baselines, and avoids quadratic scaling.

Conclusion: Recurrent computation can be reorganized into a hierarchical structure to expose parallelism without requiring linear or associative transition dynamics.

Abstract: Transformers have become the dominant architecture for sequence modeling by using self-attention to enable expressive and highly parallel processing. However, the resulting quadratic time and memory costs limit efficiency in long-context settings. Recurrent models such as LSTMs provide explicit nonlinear state updates and strong state-tracking capabilities, yet their strictly sequential computation limits parallelism. We introduce the Parallel Recursive LSTM (PR-LSTM), a hierarchical recurrent architecture that replaces left-to-right recurrence with recursive nonlinear state composition over a balanced computation tree. Tokens are first mapped independently to latent states, which are then recursively merged by a learned gated composition block. This structure uses the reduction pattern underlying parallel scans as a fixed execution schedule, rather than assuming an associative recurrence. As a result, PR-LSTM retains nonlinear gated state representations while reducing recurrent parallel depth from linear to logarithmic. Empirically, PR-LSTM achieves strong sequence-length generalization on formal-language benchmarks, solving more tasks than standard RNN, LSTM, and Transformer baselines, while avoiding the quadratic scaling of attention. These results suggest that recurrent computation can be reorganized hierarchically to expose parallelism without restricting the transition dynamics to linear or associative forms.

</details>


### [719] [DynMuon: A Dynamic Spectral Shaping View of Muon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17109)
*Fangzhou Wu,Rikhav Shah,Sandeep Silwal,Qiuyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes DynMuon, a dynamic spectral-shaping method that schedules the exponent p in Muon-like updates from positive to mildly negative over training, outperforming standard Muon with 10.6-26.5% fewer steps to reach the same target loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve upon Muon (which uses polar factor UV^T) by identifying that different training stages benefit from different spectral-shaping exponents p, and to develop a principled theory for selecting p based on curvature, noise, and training stage.

Method: Replace the standard Muon update matrix M=UΣV^T with UΣ^p V^T (spectral shaping). Develop theory for optimal p based on local curvature, stochastic gradient noise, and training stage. Propose DynMuon that dynamically schedules p from positive to mildly negative over training.

Result: DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than standard Muon across model sizes, architectures, and training settings, requiring 10.6-26.5% fewer steps to reach the same target loss.

Conclusion: Positive p helps early training by emphasizing high-curvature directions and accelerating signal contraction; mildly negative p helps later by reallocating update strength toward low-curvature directions with useful training signals. Dynamic scheduling of p is an effective and efficient improvement.

Abstract: In recent years, Muon has emerged as the dominant method for training large language models, and transformers more broadly. The essential difference, when compared to standard gradient descent methods, is to replace the usual update matrix $M=UΣV^\top$ with its polar factor $UV^\top$. In this work, we consider a class of Muon-like updates, where we replace the update $M$ with $UΣ^p V^\top$ for some parameter $p$. We call this a "spectral-shaping" operation, and develop a theory of how to pick $p$ which depends on (a) local curvature of the loss function, (b) noise stemming from stochastic gradients and label noise, and (c) training stage. Our theory and experimentation reveal a previously overlooked behavior: positive $p$ helps early by emphasizing high-curvature directions and accelerating signal contraction, while mildly negative $p$ helps later by reallocating update strength toward low-curvature directions that still contain useful training signals. Building on the insight, we propose DynMuon, an efficient dynamic spectral shaping method that schedules $p$ from positive to mildly negative over training. Extensive experiments across model sizes, architectures, and training settings show that DynMuon consistently achieves lower validation loss than Muon, while requiring 10.6-26.5% fewer steps to reach the same target loss.

</details>


### [720] [Differentiable Optimization Layers for Guaranteed Fairness in Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17118)
*David Troxell,Noah Roemer,Guido Montúfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a differentiable "fairness layer" for neural networks that guarantees output parity, along with an online primal-dual inference algorithm for provable aggregate fairness in streaming predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
