Motivation: To address the challenge of enforcing fairness constraints in predict-then-optimize frameworks, where traditional per-batch constraints are too restrictive for streaming predictions with small batch sizes.

Method: (1) A differentiable optimization layer (fairness layer) appended to neural network output layers to enforce output parity. (2) An online primal-dual inference algorithm providing provable aggregate fairness guarantees for streaming predictions with arbitrarily small batch sizes.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate effectiveness of the fairness layer and algorithm; theoretical analysis characterizes differentiability and stability properties during training and backpropagation.

Conclusion: The fairness layer and online primal-dual algorithm effectively enforce fairness guarantees in neural networks, with theoretical backing for differentiability and stability, suitable for both batch and streaming settings.

Abstract: Differentiable optimization layers are traditionally integrated in predict-then-optimize frameworks where a neural model estimates parameters that subsequently serve as fixed inputs to downstream decision-making optimization problems. In this work, we introduce the concept of a "fairness layer": a differentiable optimization layer appended to a model's output layer that guarantees a chosen notion of output parity is satisfied when integrated into a neural network. Additionally, we introduce an online primal-dual inference algorithm that provides provable aggregate fairness guarantees for streaming predictions with arbitrarily small batch sizes, where traditional per-batch constraints become overly restrictive. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the fairness layer and associated algorithm, and theoretical analysis characterizes the layer's differentiability and stability properties during model training and backpropagation. Our code for these experiments is publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/dtroxell19/FairDL-ICML-2026.git) and our public Python package documentation can be found online: https://dtroxell19.github.io/fairness_training/.

</details>


### [721] [An Analytical Multiple Criteria Framework for Temporal and Dynamic Business-to-Business Customer Segmentation in Manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17151)
*Muhammad Raees,Konstantinos Papangelis,Vassilis Javed Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a dynamic multi-criteria decision-making method for B2B customer segmentation that extends traditional RFM modeling with stability and growth dimensions, integrates adaptive AHP, multivariate time-series clustering, and graph-based consensus modeling. Tested on a real-world dataset of 3,000+ B2B customers, the method shows strong robustness to temporal shifts and provides effective decision support for domain experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional customer segmentation methods like RFM modeling rely on limited criteria and fail to capture complex business interactions and temporal dynamics in B2B contexts.

Method: The method extends RFM with stability and growth dimensions, integrates adaptive analytical hierarchical process (AHP), evaluates multivariate time-series clustering models, measures between-segment transitions and volatility over time, and applies a graph-based consensus model.

Result: Tested on a real-world manufacturing dataset with 3,000+ B2B customers, the method demonstrates strong robustness to temporal shifts and enables effective decision support.

Conclusion: The proposed dynamic MCDM method provides domain experts with preferential analytics to devise strategies, offering effective decision support for B2B customer segmentation.

Abstract: In sales and marketing, customer segmentation is an important tool for formulating strategies for customer treatment and supply chain management. Most segmentation implementations rely on limited criteria, such as recency, frequency, and monetary (RFM) modeling, which often fail to capture complex business interactions. In this work, we design and evaluate a dynamic multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) method in a business-to-business (B2B) manufacturing context by 1) extending RFM to dimensions of stability and growth, 2) integrating an adaptive and analytical hierarchical process to match business objectives, and 3) evaluating multivariate time-series clustering models. We then measure customer stability, tracking between-segment transitions, and volatility over time, and apply a graph-based consensus model to further strengthen the analysis. We test the efficacy of the proposed method using a real-world manufacturing company dataset to segment more than 3,000 B2B customers, showing strong robustness to temporal shifts. The implementation enables domain experts with preferential analytics to devise their strategies, providing effective decision support for B2B customer segmentation.

</details>


### [722] [Stress-Testing Neural Network Verifiers with Provably Robust Instances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17153)
*David Troxell,Yulia Alexandr,Sofia Hunt,Stephanie Lei,Guido Montúfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces VeriStressGT, a framework for generating neural network verification instances with known ground-truth robustness labels, enabling exact scoring of verifiers. Using this framework, the authors discover numerical issues and bugs in popular verifiers and propose a Verification Difficulty Profile to systematically study verifier failure modes across five state-of-the-art systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing verification benchmarks lack ground-truth labels, forcing evaluators to rely on indirect heuristics that prevent exact scoring and systematic study of verifier failure modes.

Method: The authors propose (1) a reusable framework for generating verification instances with analytically known ground-truth robustness labels, and (2) the Verification Difficulty Profile, a collection of estimable quantities capturing distinct sources of instance hardness.

Result: Discovery of multiple numeric tolerance concerns and an implementation bug in popular verifiers. Evaluation of five state-of-the-art verifiers shows different instances stress distinct aspects of the verification pipeline.

Conclusion: Ground-truth labels are essential for verifier evaluation, and the proposed framework provides actionable targets for improving verifier numerical reliability, relaxation quality, and search behavior.

Abstract: Neural network verifiers aim to provide formal guarantees on model behavior, but existing verification benchmarks are fundamentally limited by their lack of ground-truth labels. As a result, verifier evaluation relies on indirect heuristics, which prevents exact scoring and systematic study of verifier failure modes. We address this gap by introducing a reusable framework for generating verification instances whose ground-truth robustness labels are known a priori through analytic construction. Our framework led to the discovery of multiple numeric tolerance concerns and an implementation bug in popular verifiers, highlighting the need for ground-truth labels. Additionally, to systematically study verifier failure modes, we introduce the verification Difficulty Profile, a collection of estimable quantities capturing distinct sources of instance hardness. Using our framework and these profiles, we evaluate five state-of-the-art verifiers and show that different instances stress distinct aspects of the verification pipeline. We show that these results can aid the future development of verifiers as they provide actionable targets for improving numerical reliability, relaxation quality, and search behavior. Our code is publicly available: https://github.com/dtroxell19/VeriStressGT.git.

</details>


### [723] [When Bits Break Recourse: Counterfactual-Faithful Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17160)
*Chaymae Yahyati,Ismail Lamaakal,Khalid El Makkaoui,Ibrahim Ouahbi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that low-bit quantization can silently break algorithmic recourse (actionable changes to flip model decisions), and proposes Counterfactual-Faithful Quantization (CFQ) to preserve counterfactual behavior through mixed-precision bit allocation and margin-based training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization preserves accuracy under low-bit deployment but can silently break algorithmic recourse—actionable changes that flip decisions may fail or become more costly post-quantization. This reliability gap is invisible to standard accuracy metrics, motivating the need for recourse-aware quantization.

Method: The paper formalizes counterfactual sensitivity via validity, cost, and direction stability, introducing Validity Drop (VD) and Counterfactual Recourse Gap (CRG). It proposes CFQ, which trains quantizer parameters and mixed-precision bit allocations to preserve counterfactual behavior by enforcing target outcomes at teacher recourse points under a global bit budget, supported by margin-based analysis for recourse transfer under bounded perturbations.

Result: Experiments on Adult, German Credit, and COMPAS show that accuracy-matched baselines significantly degrade recourse stability, while CFQ maintains accuracy and substantially improves VD and CRG across bit budgets.

Conclusion: Standard quantization can silently undermine algorithmic recourse even when accuracy is preserved; the proposed CFQ framework effectively bridges this gap by explicitly enforcing counterfactual faithfulness during quantization.

Abstract: Quantization can preserve predictive accuracy under low-bit deployment while silently breaking algorithmic recourse: an actionable change that flips a decision before quantization may fail after quantization, or become substantially more costly. We formalize counterfactual sensitivity under quantization through validity, cost, and direction stability, and introduce two metrics: Validity Drop (VD) and Counterfactual Recourse Gap (CRG) that reveal recourse failures invisible to accuracy. We propose Counterfactual-Faithful Quantization (CFQ), which trains quantizer parameters and mixed-precision bit allocation to preserve counterfactual behavior by enforcing the target outcome at teacher recourse points under a global bit budget. A margin-based analysis gives a sufficient condition for recourse transfer under bounded quantization perturbations. Experiments on Adult, German Credit, and COMPAS show that accuracy-matched baselines can significantly degrade recourse stability, while CFQ maintains accuracy and substantially improves VD and CRG across bit budgets.

</details>


### [724] [TriAxialKV: Toward Extreme Low-Precision KV-Cache Quantization for Agentic Inference Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17170)
*Hanzhang Shen,Haoran Wu,Yiren Zhao,Robert Mullins*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TriAxialKV is a mixed-precision KV-cache quantization scheme that assigns triaxial tags (temporal recency, modality, semantic role) to tokens for fine-grained sensitivity calibration, achieving 4.5x KV cache compression and 30% higher throughput vs. BF16 baselines in agentic workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic workloads (long-context, multimodal, multi-turn tool-using) have heterogeneous token importance along three axes (temporal, modality, semantic role) that existing single-dimension KV-cache compression methods fail to exploit.

Method: Assigns each token a triaxial tag (temporal recency, modality, semantic role), calibrates per-tag sensitivity, and allocates INT2/INT4 bitwidths under a fixed memory budget. Implemented as an end-to-end serving system with fused Triton decode kernels.

Result: Using Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking as a computer-use agent on OSWorld, TriAxialKV matches BF16 KV-cache accuracy while achieving 4.5x KV cache size reduction and 30% higher end-to-end throughput on real GPUs.

Conclusion: Triaxial sensitivity calibration (recency × modality × semantic role) significantly outperforms homogeneous or single-dimension KV-cache quantization for structured agentic workloads.

Abstract: Agentic workloads have emerged as a major workload for LLM inference. They differ significantly from chat-only workloads, requiring long-context processing, the ability to handle multimodal inputs, and structured multi-turn interactions with tool calling capabilities. As a result, their context exhibits structure that can carry different importance along three key axes: temporal recency to the current turn, modality such as text or image tokens, and semantic role such as user queries, tool calls, observations, or reasoning. These axes capture distinct token behaviors and lead to different sensitivities to KV-cache compression. However, existing KV-cache quantization methods are typically homogeneous or exploit only heterogeneity on a single dimension, such as temporal proximity or modality, overlooking the interactions among them. To this end, we introduce TriAxialKV, a novel mixed-precision KV-cache quantization scheme that assigns each token a triaxial tag, calibrates per-tag sensitivity, and allocates INT2/INT4 bitwidths under a fixed memory budget. We implement TriAxialKV as an end-to-end serving system, comprising calibration, mixed-precision quantization and memory management, and custom fused Triton decode kernels. When using Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking as a computer-use agent operating the OSWorld, TriAxialKV matches the accuracy of SGLang with BF16 KV cache while supporting 4.5$\times$ KV cache size and achieving 30% higher end-to-end throughput, when running on real GPU systems.

</details>


### [725] [OpenJarvis: Personal AI, On Personal Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17172)
*Jon Saad-Falcon,Avanika Narayan,Robby Manihani,Tanvir Bhathal,Herumb Shandilya,Hakki Orhun Akengin,Gabriel Bo,Andrew Park,Matthew Hart,Caia Costello,Chuan Li,Christopher Ré,Azalia Mirhoseini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenJarvis decomposes personal AI stacks into five independently optimizable primitives (Intelligence, Engine, Agents, Tools & Memory, Learning) and uses LLM-guided spec search to let cloud models propose edits that improve local models, enabling on-device specs to match or nearly match cloud accuracy while reducing cost ~800x and latency 4x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Replacing cloud frontier models with local models in existing personal AI stacks causes significant accuracy drops (25-39 pp), and existing prompt optimizers only recover ~5 pp of the gap. A new architecture is needed to close the local-cloud gap without sacrificing the benefits of on-device deployment.

Method: OpenJarvis decomposes the personal AI stack into five typed primitives (Intelligence, Engine, Agents, Tools & Memory, Learning) and introduces LLM-guided spec search, where frontier cloud models propose edits across the spec at search time, only non-regressing edits are accepted, and the final spec runs entirely on-device at inference time.

Result: On-device specs match or exceed cloud accuracy on 4 of 8 benchmarks, land within 3.2 pp of the best cloud baseline on average, reduce marginal API cost by ~800x, and reduce end-to-end latency by 4x.

Conclusion: Decomposing personal AI stacks into optimizable primitives combined with cloud-guided search can effectively close the local-cloud accuracy gap while preserving the cost and latency benefits of on-device inference.

Abstract: Personal AI stacks, like OpenClaw and Hermes Agent, are becoming central to daily work, yet they route nearly every query (often over sensitive local data) to cloud-hosted frontier models. Replacing frontier models with local models inside existing stacks does not work: swapping Claude Opus 4.6 for Qwen3.5-9B drops accuracy by 25-39 pp across personal AI tasks like PinchBench and GAIA. Existing stacks bundle agentic prompts, tool descriptions, memory configuration, and runtime settings around a specific cloud model. Only the prompts can be tuned, and state-of-the-art prompt optimizers close just 5 pp of the local-cloud gap on their own. This motivates a decomposed personal AI stack: one that exposes individual primitives which can be optimized individually or jointly to close the local-cloud gap. We present OpenJarvis, an architecture that represents a personal AI system as a typed spec over five primitives: Intelligence, Engine, Agents, Tools & Memory, and Learning. Each primitive is an independently editable field, making the stack end-to-end optimizable and measurable against accuracy, cost, and latency. Towards closing the local-cloud gap without surrendering local-model properties, OpenJarvis introduces LLM-guided spec search, a local-cloud collaboration in which frontier cloud models propose edits across the spec at search time, only non-regressing edits are accepted, and the resulting spec runs entirely on-device at inference time. With LLM-guided spec search, on-device specs match or exceed cloud accuracy on 4 of 8 benchmarks and land within 3.2 pp of the best cloud baseline on average. They also reduce marginal API cost by ~800x and end-to-end latency by 4x.

</details>


### [726] [The Geometry of Projection Heads: Conditioning, Invariance, and Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17180)
*Faris Chaudhry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a geometric theory of projection heads in self-supervised learning, modeling the head as a trainable Riemannian metric on the backbone representation manifold. It shows linear heads perform implicit subspace whitening, nonlinear heads adapt local metrics, smooth nonlinear heads prevent dimensional collapse via negative curvature, and the head acts as a geometric buffer that must be discarded at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why projection heads are necessary in self-supervised learning and why they must be discarded after pretraining, by providing a unified geometric framework.

Method: Models projection head as a trainable Riemannian metric on the backbone manifold; analyzes linear vs. nonlinear heads through geometric lens; studies Hessian eigenvalues at collapsed equilibria; tracks optimization geometry during training; characterizes metric degeneracy for information-invariance trade-off.

Result: Linear heads perform implicit subspace whitening; nonlinear heads adapt local metrics per loss topology; smooth nonlinear heads (e.g., Swish) induce negative Hessian eigenvalues making collapsed equilibria unstable; ReLU/linear heads cannot escape collapse under continuous gradient flow; the head acts as a geometric buffer decoupling backbone from destructive loss constraints.

Conclusion: The projection head serves as a universal geometric buffer that must be discarded because metric degeneracy governs the information-invariance trade-off, and the head absorbs the rigid constraints of the pretraining objective, leaving the backbone with semantically meaningful representations.

Abstract: We develop a geometric theory of projection heads in self-supervised learning by modeling the head as a trainable Riemannian metric on the backbone representation manifold. We show that linear heads perform implicit subspace whitening, while nonlinear heads adapt local metrics to satisfy the specific topological constraints of the loss, with head depth empirically dictating this capacity. Analyzing dimensional collapse, we prove that smooth nonlinear heads natively induce negative eigenvalues in the Hessian at collapsed equilibria, making them unstable. We empirically validate this by continuously tracking the optimization geometry during training, which reveals that smooth activations like Swish can generate explicit negative curvature to escape collapse, whereas linear and ReLU heads under continuous-time gradient flow cannot, relying instead on discrete-time optimization dynamics and BatchNorm. Finally, we geometrically characterize how metric degeneracy governs the information-invariance trade-off, explaining why the head must be discarded. Evaluated across contrastive and decorrelation-based objectives on foundation models, our results demonstrate that the projection head acts as a universal geometric buffer, decoupling the semantic backbone from the rigid, destructive constraints of the pretraining objective.

</details>


### [727] [OPTNet: Ordering Point Transformer Network for Post-disaster 3D Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17197)
*Nhut Le,Ehsan Karimi,Maryam Rahnemoonfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPTNet introduces a learnable Point Sorter module with self-supervised ordering loss to dynamically optimize point permutation for window-based attention, outperforming state-of-the-art on the 3DAeroRelief disaster dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-disaster damage assessment needs fast and accurate 3D point cloud segmentation. Existing methods rely on either expensive neighbor searching (PTv1/PTv2) or suboptimal static serialization (PTv3), which fails to capture complex disaster scene geometries.

Method: OPTNet proposes a learnable Point Sorter module that uses a self-supervised ordering loss to dynamically predict an optimal permutation of points, maximizing locality for window-based attention without fixed serialization.

Result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the 3DAeroRelief dataset.

Conclusion: Learnable ordering is more effective than static serialization for capturing complex disaster scene geometry, offering both efficiency and accuracy improvements for post-disaster assessment.

Abstract: Post-disaster damage assessment requires rapid and accurate semantic segmentation of 3D point clouds to identify critical infrastructure such as damaged buildings and roads. Early Point Transformers (e.g., PTv1, PTv2) relied on computationally expensive neighbor searching (k-NN) and Farthest Point Sampling (FPS). To improve efficiency, recent architectures like Point Transformer V3 (PTv3) adopted static serialization methods, such as Hilbert curves or Z-order, to organize unstructured points for window-based attention. However, these fixed orderings are not optimal for capturing the complex geometry of disaster scenes. In this paper, we propose OPTNet (Ordering Point Transformer Network), which introduces a learnable Point Sorter module. OPTNet utilizes a self-supervised ordering loss to dynamically predict an optimal permutation that maximizes the locality of the attention mechanism. We evaluate our method on the 3DAeroRelief dataset, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [728] [Anytime and Difficulty-Adaptive PAC-Bayes for Constrained Density-Ratio Network with Continual Learning Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17212)
*Paulo Akira F. Enabe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a unified framework for learning under covariate shift using a constrained density-ratio network that approximates the Radon-Nikodym derivative, supports importance-weighted empirical risk, and provides both fixed-time and anytime PAC-Bayes generalization certificates. The method imposes structural constraints (normalization, moment matching, second-moment penalty) via augmented Lagrangian, and is validated through a pre-registered two-campaign protocol on synthetic and real data, showing improved target loss and tight certificates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the covariate shift problem in machine learning, where the source (training) and target (test) distributions differ, by providing a unified framework that combines density ratio estimation, importance weighting, and rigorous generalization guarantees.

Method: A constrained density-ratio network learns the Radon-Nikodym derivative dP/dQ; three hard integral constraints (normalization, moment matching, effective sample size control) are enforced via augmented Lagrangian; PAC-Bayes certificates are applied in both fixed-time (Bernoulli-KL bounds) and anytime (geometric peeling across epochs) regimes.

Result: The method produces calibrated covariate ratios, reduces target 0/1 loss compared to unweighted ERM and classical ratio-estimation baselines, achieves the anytime certificate as promised, and reveals that the single fixed-time coverage failure aligns with label shift magnitude, confirming the covariate-only assumption is tight.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively handles covariate shift with principled generalization guarantees, and the empirical validation confirms both the utility and the operational tightness of the underlying assumptions.

Abstract: A unified framework for learning under covariate shift is presented, in which a constrained density-ratio network approximates the Radon-Nikodym derivative $r^\star = dP/dQ$ from source $Q$ to target $P$, supports an importance-weighted empirical risk, and feeds an anytime PAC-Bayes generalization certificate. A change-of-measure identity decomposes the gap between target risk and importance-weighted source risk into a ratio-bias term, controlled by the $L^2(Q)$ closeness of the learned ratio to $r^\star$, and a generalization-gap term, controlled by the variability of the weighted loss. Three structural identities of a Radon-Nikodym derivative, normalization, moment matching, and a second-moment penalty controlling the effective sample size, are imposed as hard integral constraints through an augmented-Lagrangian scheme. PAC-Bayes is then instantiated on the weighted risk in a fixed-time regime that yields Bernoulli-KL bounds, a KL-regularized objective whose minimizer is the network-weighted Gibbs posterior, and a stability statement on $L^2(Q)$ perturbations of the learned ratio, and in an anytime regime that builds a time-uniform certificate by geometric peeling across epochs. A pre-registered two-campaign protocol combining a patch test against analytic ground truth with a real-data deployment under intrinsic distribution shift validates the framework. The network produces a calibrated covariate ratio on real data, reduces the target $0/1$ loss relative to unweighted empirical risk minimization and to classical direct ratio-estimation baselines, and attains the anytime certificate as the construction promises. A single pre-registered failure of the fixed-time coverage claim is recorded, with per-split coverage aligning one-to-one with the magnitude of the label shift, confirming that the covariate-only assumption is operationally tight rather than a defect of the certificate.

</details>


### [729] [FishBack: Pullback Fisher Geometry for Optimal Activation Steering in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17231)
*Sihan Wang,Jiayi Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FishBack uses the Fisher information metric (pulled back through the model's layers) to derive closed-form optimal steering directions, outperforming Euclidean-based methods (CAA, ActAdd, ITI) on GPT-2 by 1.3×–2.5× in off-target KL reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing activation steering methods assume the space is Euclidean, but this assumption is false — local geometry deviates by >97% in relative spectral norm, with effective dimensionality only 2–17% of ambient space.

Method: Pull back the Fisher information metric of the softmax layer through the Jacobian of subsequent layers, derive a closed-form steering equation for minimum-distortion direction, and apply iteratively without manifold fitting. A layer-wise recursive decomposition is provided.

Result: On GPT-2, iterative pullback steering outperforms Euclidean baselines (CAA, ActAdd, ITI) across three verb-morphology concepts and four layers, with 1.3×–2.5× off-target KL reduction vs. Euclidean gradient ascent and 1.5× vs. CAA at matched concept probability.

Conclusion: The Fisher information metric is the correct geometry for activation steering; existing methods implicitly use approximate metrics whose performance gaps are predicted by a single spectral diagnostic — the ratio of their implicit metric's cost to the Fisher-optimal cost.

Abstract: Activation steering methods modify intermediate representations of language models to control output behavior, but universally assume the activation space is Euclidean. We show this assumption fails drastically: the local geometry induced by the model's own output behavior -- the Fisher information metric of the softmax layer, pulled back through the Jacobian of subsequent layers -- deviates from the Euclidean metric by over 97% in relative spectral norm on GPT-2, with an effective dimensionality of only 2--17% of the ambient space. From this pullback Fisher metric, we derive a closed-form steering equation that identifies the minimum-distortion direction for any target concept, yielding a closed-form optimal direction at each point that can be applied iteratively without manifold fitting or data-driven geometry estimation. We call the resulting framework FishBack. The metric admits a layer-wise recursive decomposition, which reveals that existing methods -- CAA, ActAdd, ITI, and others -- each implicitly adopt a particular approximate metric, and that their performance gaps are quantitatively predicted by a single spectral diagnostic: the ratio of their implicit metric's cost to the Fisher-optimal cost. On GPT-2, iterative pullback steering consistently outperforms all Euclidean baselines across three verb-morphology concepts and four layers, with off-target KL reductions of $1.3\times$--$2.5\times$ relative to Euclidean gradient ascent and $1.5\times$ relative to CAA at matched concept probability.

</details>


### [730] [Dimension-Free Convergence of Discrete Diffusion Models: Adjoint Equations Induce the Right Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17232)
*Kelvin Kan,Xingjian Li,Benjamin J. Zhang,Tuhin Sahai,Stanley Osher,Markos A. Katsoulakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a unified adjoint-equation-based framework for discrete diffusion models that establishes dimension-free (state-space-size-free) convergence guarantees in any integral probability metric (IPM), addressing fundamental limitations of prior KL-based and TV-based analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing convergence theories for discrete diffusion models have fundamental limitations: KL-based analyses diverge under singular priors (e.g., masked distribution), and TV-based bounds depend on state space size S, becoming vacuous for large-vocabulary language tasks (hundreds of thousands of tokens). A dimension-free framework applicable to both masked and uniform priors is needed.

Method: The authors develop four novel techniques: (1) working in the space of observables via adjoint equations instead of directly with probability measures, (2) a regularity analysis that yields bounds on any IPM, (3) a coupling argument to remove S-dependence under uniform transitions, and (4) a score-marginal cancellation technique to remove S-dependence under masked transitions. The framework relies only on a single standard rate-matrix regularity assumption and supports time-inhomogeneous schedules.

Result: The framework achieves convergence bounds that are entirely free of state space size S, applicable to both masked and uniform priors, and represent the first such dimension-free guarantees in any IPM for discrete diffusion models.

Conclusion: The adjoint-equation-based framework sharply departs from prior analyses (pathspace-KL and existing TV-based approaches), avoids their shortcomings, and provides a versatile toolkit for further theoretical study of discrete diffusion models.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion has become a leading framework for generative modeling in various applications including language, vision, and biology. Existing convergence theory, however, exhibits fundamental limitations. KL-based analyses diverge under singular priors such as the masked distribution, while bounds in total variation (TV) depend on the state space size $S$ and become vacuous for modern language tasks, where vocabularies contain hundreds of thousands of tokens. We develop a unified adjoint-equation-based framework that establishes dimension-free convergence guarantees in any integral probability metric (IPM). To the best of our knowledge, our bounds are the first to be entirely free of $S$ and applicable to both masked and uniform priors. Importantly, our theory relies only on a single standard rate-matrix regularity assumption and is compatible with time-inhomogeneous schedules.
  Four novel techniques drive our improvements: working in the space of observables via adjoint equations rather than directly with probability measures, a regularity analysis that yields bounds on any IPM, a coupling argument that removes $S$-dependence under uniform transitions, and a score-marginal cancellation technique that removes $S$-dependence under masked transitions. Our framework thus sharply departs from prior analyses and avoids the shortcomings of pathspace-KL and existing TV-based approaches. Beyond convergence bounds, our framework provides a versatile toolkit for further theoretical study of discrete diffusion models.

</details>


### [731] [Active Budget Allocation for Efficient Scaling Law Estimation via Surrogate-Guided Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17234)
*Viktoria Schram,Markus Hiller,Daniel Beck,Trevor Cohn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores using Successive Halving (SH) and surrogate models for strategic resource allocation to efficiently predict scaling laws, achieving up to 98.7% computational savings and 5.47% performance improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirical scaling law research requires substantial computational resources, yet strategic resource allocation for this task remains underexplored. The authors aim to address this gap.

Method: The authors apply Successive Halving (SH) and SH combined with parametric/non-parametric surrogate models to systematically allocate compute budgets for learning curve collection.

Result: SH with surrogate models achieves lower loss-compute values than uniform allocation or SH alone, with mean relative improvements of 2.84% (real-world) and 5.47% (synthetic) datasets, while saving up to 98.7% computational cost over exhaustive approaches.

Conclusion: Strategic resource allocation using SH and surrogate models enables accurate scaling law estimation at dramatically reduced computational cost.

Abstract: Predicting model performance at larger scales enables the design of training strategies and architectures tailored to specific performance targets. Empirical scaling law research identifies functional forms to aid this prediction task. These describe the relationship between loss and compute using a loss-compute frontier defined by learning curves. Due to the empirical nature of this approach, the computational burden is substantial, making strategic resource allocation essential - yet it remains surprisingly underexplored. In this work, we address this shortcoming by exploring the suitability of Successive Halving (SH) and SH combined with parametric and non-parametric surrogate models. In addition to enabling a more systematic allocation of a given compute budget, our findings show that SH paired with surrogate models yields a set of learning curves that includes one with a lower loss-compute value than what naive uniform allocation or an SH-only approach can obtain. Our experiments demonstrate mean relative improvements of up to 2.84% and 5.47% on real-world and synthetic learning curve datasets. This strategic resource allocation enables us to obtain accurate scaling laws at significantly reduced computational costs, saving up to 98.7% over the traditional exhaustive approach.

</details>


### [732] [Learning in Position-Aware Multinomial Logit Bandits: From Multiplicative to General Position Effects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17238)
*Xi Chen,Shibo Dai,Jiameng Lyu,Yuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies dynamic joint assortment selection and positioning under MNL choice models, proposing round-based learning algorithms (P2MLE-UCB and GP2-UCB) with regret-optimal guarantees that achieve $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{NT})$ regret, closing previous gaps, with efficient optimization subroutines and strong empirical results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in existing literature where assortment and positioning decisions are treated separately or with epoch-based algorithms that leave regret gaps (e.g., $\sqrt{K}$ gap), while modern platforms require prompt, round-based operations.

Method: Proposes two algorithms: (1) P2MLE-UCB for multiplicative position effects using cross-position pairwise maximum likelihood estimation with clipping; (2) GP2-UCB for general position effects. Both are round-based (updating after every feedback). Optimization via Dinkelbach's method and maximum-weight bipartite matching.

Result: P2MLE-UCB achieves $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{NT})$ regret (matching lower bound, closing $\sqrt{K}$ gap). GP2-UCB achieves matching upper bound to minimax lower bound. Numerical experiments on synthetic data and Expedia dataset show consistent outperformance over benchmarks.

Conclusion: The work provides the first regret-optimal characterization for joint assortment and positioning under both multiplicative and general position effects models, with practical round-based algorithms suitable for modern platforms.

Abstract: We study the dynamic joint assortment selection and positioning problem, where the attraction of each product depends on both its intrinsic appeal and its display position under a Multinomial Logit (MNL) choice framework. Our study ranges from the multiplicative position effects model, in which each product's attraction is scaled by a position-specific factor, to a general position effects model assigning independent attraction parameters to every product--position pair to capture heterogeneous synergies. For both models, we design round-based learning algorithms that update decisions after every single feedback, and establish the first regret-optimal characterization. Besides, our round-based algorithms provide the prompt operations needed by modern platforms. For the multiplicative model, we develop a cross-position pairwise maximum likelihood estimator with a clipping mechanism, and prove that our algorithm P2MLE-UCB attains a regret of $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{NT})$, matching the lower bound and closing the $\sqrt{K}$ gap left by prior epoch-based analyses. For the general model, we establish a minimax lower bound and propose GP2-UCB with a matching upper bound. Moreover, we design an efficient subroutine for the per-round joint assortment and positioning optimization based on Dinkelbach's method and maximum-weight bipartite matching. Numerical experiments on synthetic data and the Expedia dataset show that our algorithms consistently outperform state-of-the-art benchmarks.

</details>


### [733] [Drift Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17244)
*Chenrui Ma,Xi Xiao,Lin Zhao,Tianyang Wang,Ferdinando Fioretto,Yanning Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DFM connects Drift Models and Flow Matching, enabling both efficient one-step generation and iterative refinement for flexible quality-efficiency trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Drift Models offer efficient one-step generation but lack test-time scaling flexibility (iterative refinement), unlike Flow Matching/Diffusion models which can trade compute for quality.

Method: Proposes Drift Flow Matching (DFM), a framework that bridges direct transport maps (Drift Models) with flow-based iterative generation, allowing adaptive sampling computation.

Result: Extensive experiments across various tasks and datasets show effectiveness and generality of DFM.

Conclusion: DFM provides a novel generative paradigm that bridges one-step and multi-step generative models, offering flexible quality-efficiency trade-offs.

Abstract: Iterative generative models such as Flow Matching and Diffusion models have demonstrated strong test-time scaling behavior, where additional inference computation can improve generation quality. In contrast, Drift Models offer efficient one-step generation, but their direct generation paradigm limits such flexibility. In this work, we propose Drift Flow Matching (DFM), a framework that connects drifting generative modeling with flow-based iterative generation. DFM preserves the efficiency of direct transport maps while enabling generation to be refined through multiple inference steps when desired. This bridges the gap between one-step Drift Models and multi-step Flow Matching methods, and provides a novel generative paradigm that can adapt sampling computation to different quality--efficiency requirements. Extensive experiments across different tasks and datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of the proposed framework.

</details>


### [734] [Fidelity Probes for Specification--Code Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17246)
*Ferhat Erata,Hao Zhou,Luke Huan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces fidelity probes—natural-language questions with code-derived answers—to measure and iteratively improve specification accuracy. Applied to a COBOL benchmark, fidelity rises from 0.63 to 0.94 over eight iterations, with convergence predictable from a two-state Markov model. Combining LLM-generated and static-analysis probes boosts fidelity, and the method is cross-model verified across five LLM families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem of ensuring that natural-language specifications accurately reflect the behavior of the source code they describe, particularly for legacy systems like COBOL where documentation may be outdated or inconsistent.

Method: Fidelity probes are generated from code (via LLM reading code or static-analysis over control-flow, data-flow, and system-dependence graphs). These are answered from a candidate specification, and agreement fraction (fidelity) is decomposed into contradiction and coverage-gap rates to drive targeted edits. A two-state Markov fixed-point model predicts convergence plateau from just four iterations of rate data. A probe-resampling protocol with held-out set provides Hoeffding-bounded overfitting discriminant.

Result: On a 15-program, ~12k-line COBOL benchmark, fidelity improved from 0.63 to 0.94 over eight iterations. Three graph-grounded mixtures lifted fidelity by +16 to +30 points. Five LLM families (Anthropic, DeepSeek, Google, Alibaba, OpenAI) were tested; three of five non-Claude generators matched Markov predictions, and the frozen-test protocol falsified two generators with drifting probe distributions.

Conclusion: The fidelity probe method effectively drives specification improvement to convergence. LLM and symbolic probe channels are empirically complementary. The convergence behavior is robust across multiple LLM families. The method generalizes to any pair of artifacts expected to describe the same behavior.

Abstract: We introduce fidelity probes: natural-language questions generated from a reference artifact with code-derived ground-truth answers, answered from a candidate specification. The fraction of agreeing probes, which we call the fidelity, decomposes into contradiction and coverage-gap rates that drive targeted spec edits to convergence. On a 15-program, roughly 12k-line COBOL benchmark (AWS CardDemo), we raise frozen-test specification fidelity from 0.63 to 0.94 over eight iterations, with the plateau location predicted by a two-state Markov fixed point $F^\dagger$ from just four iterations of rate data. Probes come from an LLM reading the code or from a static-analysis pipeline over its control-flow, data-flow, and system-dependence graphs, with a tunable mixture. A probe-resampling protocol with a frozen held-out set gives a Hoeffding-bounded overfitting discriminant; our measured train/test gap stays more than an order of magnitude below this envelope. Three graph-grounded mixtures lift fidelity by +16 to +30 points; cross-distribution evaluation shows the LLM and symbolic channels are empirically complementary. A cross-family generator sweep on five independent LLM lineages (Anthropic, DeepSeek, Google, Alibaba, OpenAI) confirms the convergence behaviour is not tied to any single model family: three of five non-Claude generators produce trajectories consistent with the Markov fixed-point prediction, and the frozen-test protocol actively falsifies the two generators whose probe distributions drift across iterations. The method applies to any pair of artifacts that are supposed to describe the same behaviour.

</details>


### [735] [Towards Principled Test-Time Adaptation for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17250)
*Haochun Wang,Ruichen Xu,Georgios Kementzidis,Karen Cho,Sebastian Ramirez Villarreal,Yuefan Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a principled test-time adaptation protocol for time series forecasting using only matured ground truth, diagnoses frequency-domain limitations of existing adapters, and introduces FAC—a lightweight frequency-domain calibration method that achieves competitive performance with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TSF-TTA methods lack a unified formulation due to heterogeneous adaptation protocols (different ways of using revealed targets). The paper aims to establish a cleaner, principled setting.

Method: 1) Proposes a protocol using solely matured ground truth. 2) Diagnoses existing adapters via frequency-domain analysis. 3) Introduces Frequency-Aware Calibration (FAC) that directly parameterizes prediction corrections in the frequency domain.

Result: FAC achieves competitive and consistent performance across diverse datasets, forecasting horizons, and source forecasters, while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters than compared TSF-TTA adapters.

Conclusion: A principled protocol and lightweight frequency-domain calibration method can effectively address distribution shift in TSF-TTA with improved efficiency.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) has recently emerged as a promising approach for improving time series forecasting (TSF) under distribution shift. Existing TSF-TTA methods differ in how they utilize revealed targets, yet the resulting adaptation protocols remain heterogeneous and lack a clearly unified formulation. To address this issue, we revisit TSF-TTA from the perspective of protocol cleanliness and propose an adaptation protocol based solely on matured ground truth, yielding a more principled setting for adaptation. Under this protocol, we further diagnose existing adapters in the frequency domain and find that their prediction corrections often exhibit limited and weakly structured spectral modifications. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Frequency-Aware Calibration (FAC), a lightweight calibration method that directly parameterizes prediction corrections in the frequency domain. Across diverse datasets, forecasting horizons, and source forecasters, FAC achieves competitive and consistent performance while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters than the compared TSF-TTA adapters.

</details>


### [736] [When Molecular Similarity Works: Property Cliffs Reveal Hidden Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17265)
*Di Hu,Kun Li,Haojie Rao,Longtao Hu,Jiameng Chen,Wenbin Hu,Yizhen Zheng,Jiajun Yu,Duanhua Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CliffSplit and CliffLoss are introduced to detect and mitigate property cliff failures in molecular property prediction, revealing 15%+ higher error in cliff-heavy regions and reducing the cliff-to-smooth error gap by up to 30%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard aggregate metrics cannot detect localized failure modes in molecular property prediction, particularly property cliffs where structurally similar molecules have sharply different target properties, creating high-risk blind spots in evaluation.

Method: CliffSplit (a cliff-aware evaluation protocol generating locally supported, cliff-exposed test cases) and CliffLoss (a model-agnostic train-only mitigation mechanism for cliff-sensitive errors).

Result: CliffSplit reveals at least 15% higher error in cliff-heavy QM9 regions; CliffLoss reduces the cliff-to-smooth error gap by up to 30% on Lipophilicity and improves overall MAE by 9.7%.

Conclusion: Molecular similarity failure can be transformed from a descriptive anomaly into a benchmarked evaluation problem, advancing reliable molecular machine learning.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of molecular properties underpins drug discovery and material design, yet even state-of-the-art models remain vulnerable to localized failure modes that aggregate metrics cannot detect. The places where molecular similarity should be most helpful are also places where standard evaluation can be most misleading. Property cliffs expose this gap: structurally similar molecules can still differ sharply in target property, so models with competitive overall performance may fail in high-risk local neighborhoods. To expose and mitigate this failure mode, CliffSplit, a cliff-aware evaluation protocol that constructs locally supported, cliff-exposed test cases, and CliffLoss, a model-agnostic train-only mitigation mechanism for cliff-sensitive errors, are introduced. Experiments on three QM9 targets and three MoleculeNet tasks across five backbones show that CliffSplit reveals at least 15% higher error in cliff-heavy QM9 regions, while CliffLoss reduces the cliff-to-smooth error gap by up to 30% on Lipophilicity and improves overall MAE by 9.7%. Together, these results turn molecular similarity failure from a descriptive anomaly into a benchmarked evaluation problem for molecular machine learning. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Cliff_Loss.

</details>


### [737] [Calibeating for general proper losses: A Bregman divergence approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17269)
*Maximilian Fichtl,Cristóbal Guzmán,Nishant A. Mehta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper generalizes calibeating to a larger family of proper losses (including α-Tsallis and Lipschitz losses) using regret minimization, achieving U-calibration with logarithmic regret and weaker dimension dependence, plus a new regret equality for Be The Regularized Leader based on generalized variance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend Foster and Hart's calibeating framework beyond Brier score and log loss to a broader family of proper losses (α-Tsallis and Lipschitz losses), while improving dimension dependence and unifying the analysis via Bregman divergences.

Method: Uses a Bregman divergence perspective of proper losses; applies regret minimization framework; derives a new regret equality for Be The Regularized Leader; leverages online updating formulas for generalized variance based on Bregman divergences.

Result: Achieves U-calibration with logarithmic regret simultaneously for all losses in the α-Tsallis family (α∈[1,2]) and Lipschitz losses, with weaker dimension dependence than prior work; also establishes a novel regret equality for Be The Regularized Leader.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully generalizes calibeating to a wider class of proper losses with improved theoretical guarantees, and the new regret equality may be of independent interest for future research.

Abstract: This work introduces a general framework for calibeating based on regret minimization. As compared to Foster and Hart's seminal calibeating work which had specialized treatments of Brier score (squared loss) and log loss, we consider a large family of proper losses that includes $α$-Tsallis losses (for $α\in [1, 2]$) and Lipschitz losses. Our results for Tsallis losses also hold for an unscaled version of Tsallis loss that recovers log loss. Our analysis is oriented around the Bregman divergence view of a proper loss. Technically, our results for the family of Tsallis losses that we consider are U-calibration results, simultaneously obtaining logarithmic regret for all losses in this family while having a weaker dependence on the dimension compared to previous results. Of potential independent interest, we also show a new regret equality for the regret of Be The Regularized Leader. This regret equality holds for general proper losses and itself is based on two results related to online updating formulas for the generalized variance, the latter being a previously introduced generalization of variance based on Bregman divergences.

</details>


### [738] [State-of-the-Art Claims Require State-of-the-Art Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17273)
*YongKyung Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a widespread claim-evidence gap in AI/ML benchmarking, showing that SOTA claims often lack proper support. Analysis of ten cross-domain benchmarks reveals that in over half of top-model comparisons, common assumptions of superiority (meaningful effect size, consistency, robustness) fail, as aggregate gains are often driven by outlier datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SOTA claims are pervasive in AI/ML research but often rest on minimal benchmark evidence (e.g., marginal mean score improvements). The authors aim to expose this claim-evidence gap and advocate for more honest, evidence-consistent reporting.

Method: The authors analyze ten cross-domain benchmarks from public leaderboards, testing top-model comparisons against commonly assumed properties of superiority: meaningful effect size, consistency across tasks, and robustness to dataset removal.

Result: In more than half of top-model comparisons, at least one assumed property of superiority does not hold. Aggregate gains are frequently driven by outlier datasets, and this fragility persists even in benchmarks with many tasks.

Conclusion: Claim language should reflect the strength of underlying evidence. No additional experiments are needed—only honest reporting of what results actually show, enabling more precise and interpretable comparisons across models.

Abstract: State-of-the-Art (SOTA) claims pervade Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) research. These claims rest on benchmark evaluations, where models are ranked by aggregate scores across tasks. Public benchmarks or leaderboards are the most visible instance, but the same structure appears in paper tables throughout the literature. However, such minimal evidence often cannot support these strong claims. We identify a widespread claim-evidence gap in AI benchmarking. Claiming SOTA carries implicit assumptions beyond mean score superiority, suggesting that a model meaningfully outperforms alternatives across most tasks. However, a marginal improvement in the mean score merely indicates a top average rank rather than true superiority. Analyzing ten cross-domain benchmarks from public leaderboards, we found that in more than half of top-model comparisons, at least one commonly assumed property of superiority does not hold. These properties include meaningful effect size, consistency across tasks, or robustness to dataset removal. Instead, aggregate gains are frequently driven by outlier datasets. This fragility persists even in benchmarks with many tasks. We argue that claim language should reflect the strength of the underlying evidence. This requires no additional experiments, only honest reporting of what results actually show, enabling more precise and interpretable comparisons across models.

</details>


### [739] [How Do Electrocardiogram Models Scale?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17276)
*Jiawei Li,Fabio Bonassi,Ming Jin,Stefan Gustafsson,Johan Sundström,Thomas B. Schön,Antônio H. Ribeiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically investigates scaling laws for ECG models by pre-training over 120 models (20K to 200M parameters) on 2.3M ECG records, finding that SSL scales robustly while SL is data-bottlenecked, ResNets are more parameter-efficient than Transformers for OOD generalization, and that strategic alignment of architecture and paradigm matters more than brute-force scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling laws are well-established for NLP foundation models but their applicability to ECG models remains unclear, with inconsistent downstream gains reported when increasing model or data size. The exact roles of architectural inductive biases, pre-training paradigms, and expected improvements with scale are largely unanswered.

Method: Systematically investigated neural and loss-to-loss scaling laws by pre-training over 120 ECG models (20K to 200M parameters) on the CODE dataset (2.3M records). Decoupled effects of architecture (ResNet vs. Transformer) and pre-training paradigm (supervised learning vs. self-supervised learning).

Result: (i) SL models are data-bottlenecked in-distribution, SSL scales robustly; (ii) ResNets are 1.3-2.5x more parameter-efficient than Transformers for OOD generalization; SSL is up to 16x more data-efficient and achieves up to 7.6x higher transfer efficiency than SL; (iii) ResNet-based models achieve lowest OOD loss overall, with SSL dominating on unseen clinical tasks and self-supervised Transformers overtaking at very large model sizes.

Conclusion: The path to effective ECG foundation models lies in the strategic alignment of architecture and paradigm rather than brute-force scaling.

Abstract: While scaling laws have established a fundamental framework for foundation models in natural language processing, their applicability to electrocardiogram (ECG) models remains poorly characterized. Indeed, recent studies do not always yield consistent downstream gains as one increases the model size or pre-training dataset size of ECG models, leaving the exact roles of architectural inductive biases, pre-training paradigms, and expected improvements with size largely unanswered. In this work, we systematically investigate neural and loss-to-loss scaling laws within the ECG domain. By pre-training over $120$ models (ranging from $20$K to $200$M parameters) on the large-scale CODE dataset ($2.3$M records), we decouple the effects of model architecture (ResNet vs. Transformer) and pre-training paradigm, namely supervised learning (SL) versus self-supervised learning (SSL). We found that (i) SL models are data-bottlenecked in-distribution, whereas SSL models scale robustly across both model and data sizes; (ii) for out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization, ResNets are $1.3$ to $2.5$ times more parameter-efficient than Transformers, while SSL is up to $16$ times more data-efficient and achieves up to $7.6$ times higher transfer efficiency than SL on unseen clinical tasks; (iii) across the observed scales, ResNet-based models generally achieve the lowest OOD loss, with SSL dominating on unseen clinical tasks and self-supervised Transformers overtaking at very large model sizes. Our results suggest that the path to effective ECG foundation models lies in the strategic alignment of architecture and paradigm rather than brute-force scaling.

</details>


### [740] [UNR-Explainer: Counterfactual Explanations for Unsupervised Node Representation Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17285)
*Hyunju Kang,Geonhee Han,Hogun Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UNR-Explainer generates counterfactual explanations for unsupervised node representation learning using Monte Carlo Tree Search to identify subgraphs that change k-nearest neighbors in embedding space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unsupervised node representation models lack reliable explanation methods, despite the growing demand for interpretability in GNNs.

Method: Uses counterfactual reasoning based on k-nearest neighbor changes in embedding space, powered by Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to find influential subgraphs.

Result: Outperforms baselines on diverse datasets for unsupervised GraphSAGE and DGI models.

Conclusion: The proposed CF explanation approach effectively interprets unsupervised node embeddings, aiding downstream tasks like link prediction and clustering.

Abstract: Node representation learning, such as Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), has emerged as a pivotal method in machine learning. The demand for reliable explanation generation surges, yet unsupervised models remain underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce a method for generating counterfactual (CF) explanations in unsupervised node representation learning. We identify the most important subgraphs that cause a significant change in the k-nearest neighbors of a node of interest in the learned embedding space upon perturbation. The k-nearest neighbor-based CF explanation method provides simple, yet pivotal, information for understanding unsupervised downstream tasks, such as top-k link prediction and clustering. Consequently, we introduce UNR-Explainer for generating expressive CF explanations for Unsupervised Node Representation learning methods based on a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). The proposed method demonstrates superior performance on diverse datasets for unsupervised GraphSAGE and DGI.

</details>


### [741] [LEAP: Learnable End-to-End Adaptive Pruning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17289)
*Mohammad Mozaffari,Younes Hourri,Mohammad Rastegari,Mahyar Najibi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEAP introduces a per-weight Bernoulli-via-Gumbel-sigmoid relaxation for end-to-end unstructured LLM pruning, improving six-task zero-shot accuracy by +2.59 points over the best layer-wise baseline at 50-60% sparsity across 0.5B-8B models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unstructured LLM pruning methods (layer-wise surrogates from OBS principle) sacrifice end-to-end accuracy under aggressive sparsity, while end-to-end methods like MaskLLM/PATCH use categorical-over-patterns parameterization that doesn't scale to unstructured settings.

Method: Replaces intractable categorical parameterization with a per-weight Bernoulli-via-Gumbel-sigmoid relaxation, making end-to-end unstructured mask learning tractable.

Result: Across five LLM families (0.5B-8B parameters) at 50% and 60% sparsity, LEAP improves six-task average zero-shot accuracy by +2.59 points over ADMM (best layer-wise baseline).

Conclusion: End-to-end unstructured mask learning via Gumbel-sigmoid relaxation is a tractable and effective approach for LLM pruning, outperforming layer-wise methods.

Abstract: Unstructured sparsity is now natively accelerated by recent GPU kernels and dataflow hardware, shifting the bottleneck from inference execution to the pruning algorithm. State-of-the-art methods for unstructured LLM pruning are layer-wise surrogates derived from the Optimal Brain Surgeon principle, and they sacrifice end-to-end accuracy, especially under aggressive sparsity. End-to-end alternatives such as MaskLLM and PATCH show that learnable masks can close this gap, but their categorical-over-patterns parameterization scales with the number of valid masks per row and does not port to the unstructured setting. We introduce LEAP, which replaces this intractable parameterization with a per-weight Bernoulli-via-Gumbel- sigmoid relaxation that makes end-to-end unstructured mask learning tractable. Across five LLM families from 0.5B to 8B parameters at 50% and 60% sparsity, LEAP improves six-task average zero-shot accuracy by +2.59 points on average over ADMM, the best layer-wise baseline in our sweep.

</details>


### [742] [Step-wise Rubric Rewards for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17291)
*Weichu Xie,Haozhe Zhao,Wenpu Liu,Yongfu Zhu,Liang Chen,Minghao Ye,Zirong Chen,Yuqi Xu,Shuai Dong,Ziyue Wang,Xinbo Xu,Kean Shi,Ruoyu Wu,Xiaoying Zhang,Wenqi Shao,Baobao Chang,Nan Duan,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SRaR improves RLVR by attributing rubric scores to individual reasoning steps, normalizing signals across rollouts, and decoupling per-step rewards from outcome rewards, reducing misaligned step-level rewards and boosting math reasoning accuracy by 2.75-3.57 points over RaR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods (e.g., RaR) assign a single scalar rubric score to the entire response, causing three issues: (1) loss of multi-criterion structure, (2) rewarding correct and incorrect steps uniformly, and (3) reward hacking via unbounded self-correction. The paper quantifies this: 18.2% of steps in correct answers are wrong yet positively rewarded, and 49.9% of steps in incorrect answers are correct yet penalized.

Method: SRaR introduces three components: (i) an LLM judge attributes each rubric item to a specific reasoning step; (ii) per-step rubric scores are normalized across rollouts so only variable-quality steps produce a learning signal; (iii) a decoupled advantage estimator combines per-step reward with outcome reward while keeping the outcome baseline stable. A 16K-problem rubric dataset is built via contrastive distillation from correct/flawed reasoning paths of a strong model.

Result: Across six math reasoning benchmarks, SRaR outperforms RaR by 3.57 points on Qwen3-8B and 2.75 points on Qwen3-32B. Faithful Reasoning Rate on AIME 2025 improves from 34.5% to 46.7%, and self-correction looping drops from 48.1% to 26.5%.

Conclusion: Step-wise rubric rewards substantially improve RLVR by providing finer-grained, correctly attributed supervision, reducing misaligned step-level credit assignment and reward hacking while boosting both accuracy and reasoning faithfulness.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is widely used to improve reasoning in large language models, but rewards only final-answer correctness with no supervision over intermediate steps. Rubric-based methods such as Rubrics as Rewards (RaR) introduce finer-grained supervision by scoring rollouts against structured criteria, yet the rubric scores are still aggregated into a single scalar applied to the entire response, causing three weaknesses: loss of multi-criterion structure, uniform supervision of correct and incorrect steps, and reward hacking through unbounded self-correction. On 1,000 problems, we find 18.2% of steps in correct-answer responses are wrong yet positively rewarded, while 49.9% of steps in incorrect-answer responses are correct yet penalized. We introduce Step-wise Rubrics as Rewards (SRaR), an RLVR framework that (i) uses an LLM judge to attribute each rubric item to a specific reasoning step, (ii) normalizes per-step rubric scores across rollouts so only steps whose quality varies produce a learning signal, and (iii) combines the per-step reward with the outcome reward through a decoupled advantage estimator that keeps the outcome baseline stable. We further build a 16K-problem rubric dataset by contrastively distilling rubric items from correct and flawed reasoning paths sampled from a strong model. Across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks, SRaR improves average accuracy over RaR by 3.57 points on Qwen3-8B and 2.75 points on Qwen3-32B, raises the Faithful Reasoning Rate on AIME 2025 from 34.5% to 46.7%, and reduces self-correction looping from 48.1% to 26.5%.

</details>


### [743] [DISA: Offline Importance Sampling for Distribution-Matching LLM-RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17295)
*Shaobo Wang,Yujie Chen,Yafeng Sun,Wenjie Qiu,Zhihui Xie,Sihang Li,Yucheng Li,Huiqiang Jiang,Xingzhang Ren,Xuming Hu,Dayiheng Liu,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DISA decouples partition function estimation from policy optimization for distribution-matching RL, achieving better calibration, diversity, and performance across math and code benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard reward-maximizing RL collapses onto a single high-reward mode, while existing distribution-matching methods suffer from calibration errors in the online-learned partition function that distort policy updates and can't be diagnosed independently.

Method: DISA (Decoupled Importance-Sampled Anchoring) draws proposal trajectories offline, estimates the partition function via importance sampling, freezes it before policy optimization begins, thus keeping the distribution-matching objective while separating partition-function estimation from policy learning in data, gradients, loss, and diagnostics.

Result: On two open-weight backbones across six math and three code benchmarks, DISA matches/exceeds FlowRL, outperforms GRPO and GSPO on math averages, exceeds LoRASFT by up to 13.8 Mean@8 points, retains more strategy-level diversity than reward-maximization baselines, and sensitivity studies match the predicted bias-variance pattern.

Conclusion: Decoupling the partition function estimation from the RL loop is an effective strategy for distribution-matching, improving calibration and diversity while maintaining or improving task performance.

Abstract: Modern reasoning agents are increasingly evaluated on their ability to generate multiple valid solution paths, plans, or tool-use traces for a given input. Standard reward-maximizing RL tends to collapse onto the most easily reinforced high-reward mode, whereas distribution-matching RL aims to allocate probability mass across the entire reward-shaped solution set. Achieving this objective requires computing a prompt-dependent partition function over the trajectory space. Because existing distribution-matching methods learn this partition function online alongside the policy, calibration errors in the partition function directly distort policy updates and remain impossible to diagnose independently. We introduce DISA, short for Decoupled Importance-Sampled Anchoring, which moves this calibration problem outside the RL loop. DISA draws proposal trajectories offline, estimates the partition function via importance sampling, and freezes the resulting partition-function estimate before policy optimization begins. This decoupling preserves the distribution-matching objective while strictly separating partition-function estimation from policy learning in data, gradients, loss, and diagnostics. Empirically, on two open-weight backbones across six math and three code benchmarks, DISA matches or exceeds the online-coupled distribution-matching baseline FlowRL, outperforms rewardmaximization baselines GRPO and GSPO on math averages, and exceeds LoRASFT distillation by up to 13.8 Mean@8 points on the same offline trajectories. An LLM-as-judge evaluation further shows that DISA retains substantially more strategy-level diversity than reward-maximization baselines, and sensitivity studies on the proposal strength and inverse temperature follow the bias-variance pattern predicted by the analysis.

</details>


### [744] [Compress the Context, Keep the Commitments: A Formal Framework for Verifiable LLM Context Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17304)
*Natalia Trukhina,Vadim Vashkelis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Context Codec, a framework for compressing LLM conversation context that preserves semantic commitments (goals, constraints, decisions) rather than just tokens, with formal representations, metrics, and a compact notation called CCL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing context management methods (truncation, summarization, retrieval, etc.) reduce length but rarely specify which semantic commitments must survive compression or how to measure their preservation.

Method: Context Codec represents dialogue state as typed, source-grounded semantic atoms with canonical identity, equivalence, conflict, confidence, risk, and evidence spans. It separates five concerns: extraction, normalization, representation, rendering, and verification. It introduces metrics (Critical Atom Recall, Weighted Atom Recall, etc.), a taxonomy of compression errors, a normalization procedure, fallback rules, and Context Compression Language (CCL).

Result: In a small diagnostic study, CCL-Core occupies a useful middle ground between structured prose and JSON: more explicit/auditable than prose, usually more compact than JSON, and less risky than heavily minified notation.

Conclusion: The paper provides a framework for making context compression verifiable rather than claiming shorthand solves compression: compress the conversation, keep the commitments.

Abstract: LLM context is not just tokens; it is a set of commitments. Long-running conversations accumulate goals, constraints, decisions, preferences, tool results, retrieved evidence, artifacts, and safety boundaries that future responses must preserve. Existing context-management methods reduce length through truncation, retrieval, summarization, memory systems, or token-level prompt compression, but they rarely specify which semantic commitments must survive compression or how their preservation should be measured. We propose Context Codec, a commitment-level framework for compressing prompts and chat histories.
  Context Codec represents dialogue state as typed, source-grounded semantic atoms with canonical identity, equivalence, conflict, confidence, risk, and evidence spans. It separates five concerns - extraction, normalization, representation, rendering, and verification - and introduces metrics for Critical Atom Recall, Weighted Atom Recall, Commitment Density, and round-trip recoverability. It also defines a taxonomy of semantic compression errors, a concrete normalization procedure, conservative fallback rules for low-confidence and safety-critical atoms, and Context Compression Language (CCL), an ASCII-first compact rendering of canonical JSON atoms. In a small diagnostic study, CCL-Core occupies a useful middle ground between structured prose and JSON: more explicit and auditable than prose, usually more compact than JSON, and less risky than heavily minified notation. The result is not a claim that shorthand solves compression, but a framework for making context compression verifiable: compress the conversation, keep the commitments.

</details>


### [745] [Learning Higher-Order Structure from Incomplete Spatiotemporal Data: Multi-Scale Hypergraph Laplacians with Neural Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17316)
*Keshu Wu,Sixu Li,Zihao Li,Zhiwen Fan,Xiaopeng Li,Yang Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MSHL is a two-stage framework (Discovery + Refinement) that builds multi-scale hypergraphs from incomplete spatiotemporal data to capture higher-order group structures missed by standard pairwise methods, proving theoretical advantages and demonstrating improved imputation on real traffic networks under structured missingness patterns like block outages and whole-sensor blackouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensor network data loss is rarely random but structured (e.g., calibration outages, clustered failures), yet standard imputation methods assume uniform-random missingness and rely on pairwise proximity. Existing low-rank and graph-based methods miss collective higher-order relations among groups of sensors and fail under coherent missingness.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) Discovery stage builds multi-scale hypergraphs from complementary topology and residual-correlation evidence using an observation-only selector; (2) Refinement stage adds a hypergraph-conditioned residual network that learns nonlinear corrections where informative residual features exist and safely defers to the linear estimate otherwise.

Result: On two real traffic networks under scattered cell missingness, contiguous block outages, and whole-sensor blackouts at five rates, MSHL improves over pairwise-graph baselines when higher-order structure is identifiable and matches it within sampling noise otherwise. Theoretical proofs show MSHL captures group-conservation patterns inaccessible to pairwise priors and adapts to the best fixed scale up to a logarithmic factor.

Conclusion: Missing data should be treated not as isolated entries to fill, but as evidence of structure to discover—a broader principle for reliable infrastructure learning.

Abstract: Sensor networks increasingly govern modern infrastructure, yet the data they lose are rarely missing in the uniform-random patterns assumed by standard imputation benchmarks. Loop detectors go offline during calibration, roadside cabinets silence clusters of nearby sensors, and newly installed instruments provide no history. Such failures create structured absences whose values are constrained by higher-order relations among groups of sensors, not merely by pairwise proximity. Existing low-rank and graph-based methods often miss this collective structure and can fail when missingness becomes coherent. We introduce Multi-Scale Hypergraph Laplacians (MSHL), a two-stage framework for learning higher-order structure from incomplete spatiotemporal observations. The Discovery stage builds a multi-scale hypergraph from complementary topology and residual-correlation evidence, with an observation-only selector that adapts to the supported interaction scale. The Refinement stage adds a small hypergraph-conditioned residual network that is safe by construction: it learns nonlinear corrections where informative residual features exist and defers to the linear estimate where they do not. We prove that MSHL represents group-conservation patterns inaccessible to pairwise graph priors, adapts to the best fixed scale up to a logarithmic factor, transfers this advantage to held-out imputation error, and admits a one-sided refinement guarantee. On two real traffic networks evaluated across scattered cell missingness, contiguous block outages, and whole-sensor blackouts at five rates, MSHL improves over a pairwise-graph baseline whenever higher-order structure is identifiable and otherwise matches it within sampling noise. The results point to a broader principle for reliable infrastructure learning: missing data should be treated not as isolated entries to fill, but as evidence of structure to discover.

</details>


### [746] [Leveraging Error Diversity in Group Rollouts for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17333)
*Wenpu Liu,Yuqi Xu,Weichu Xie,Yongfu Zhu,Shuai Dong,Ziyue Wang,Wenqi Shao,Xiaoying Zhang,Tong Yang,Nan Duan,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Error Diversity Advantage Shaping (EDAS) improves RLVR by adjusting penalties based on error diversity in group rollouts, consistently boosting performance across models and math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods discard the collective structure of group outputs, specifically error diversity, which is a strong predictor of training success.

Method: EDAS modulates advantage signals for incorrect rollouts based on intra-group error diversity: amplifies penalties for repeated errors and attenuates penalties for rare, exploratory ones.

Result: EDAS yields a 6.29-point average improvement over DAPO on Qwen3-8B across seven math benchmarks, with consistent gains across multiple RLVR algorithms and models.

Conclusion: Exploiting latent information in group rollouts (error diversity) is a broadly effective strategy to strengthen RLVR, and EDAS is a simple, plug-and-play solution.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) typically samples multiple responses per prompt and assigns binary rewards based on individual correctness, yet the collective structure of the group output, specifically the distribution of errors, is largely discarded.
