### [827] [Federated Learning by Utility-Constrained Stochastic Aggregation for Improving Rational Participation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18020)
*M Yashwanth,Arunabh Singh,Ashok Nayak,Sai Kiran Bulusu,Anirban Chakraborty*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedUCA is a Federated Learning framework that improves global model performance by accounting for rational client participation, using utility-constrained stochastic aggregation to retain clients who might otherwise leave due to statistical heterogeneity and local utility concerns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL algorithms assume clients passively comply with server orchestration, ignoring that real-world cross-silo clients are rational agents who prioritize local utilities. This can lead to client attrition and training collapse under statistical heterogeneity.

Method: FedUCA formalizes the server as an optimizer that maximizes global performance by sustaining client participation through utility-constrained stochastic aggregation.

Result: Experiments on standard datasets show FedUCA achieves significantly higher client retention and superior global model performance compared to baseline FL methods.

Conclusion: Prioritizing participation feasibility through utility constraints is an effective strategy for maintaining rational client engagement and improving global model outcomes in cross-silo FL settings.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) algorithms implicitly assume that clients passively comply with server-side orchestration by sharing local model updates upon server request. However, this overlooks an important aspect in real-world cross-silo environments: clients are often rational agents who may prioritize their utilities such as local model performance over that of the global model. In settings with significant statistical heterogeneity, rational clients may opt out of the federation if the perceived benefits of collaboration fail to meet their local utility thresholds. Such attrition degrades the global model performance and can lead to the collapse of the federated training process. In this work, we introduce FedUCA, (Federated Learning by Utility-Constrained Stochastic Aggregation for Improving Rational Participation), a framework that formalizes the server's role as an optimizer seeking to maximize global model performance by sustaining client participation. We substantiate our framework through extensive experiments on standard datasets demonstrating that by prioritizing participation feasibility, FedUCA achieves significantly higher client retention and, consequently, a superior global model performance.

</details>


### [828] [Unveiling Memorization-Generalization Coexistence: A Case Study on Arithmetic Tasks with Label Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18022)
*Linyu Liu,Pinyan Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how over-parameterized neural networks can both memorize noisy labels and generalize well, finding that larger models generalize better under noise, with memorization occurring faster than generalization. The authors discover a hidden generalization structure that is suppressed by noise fitting, which can be extracted via frequency-based methods to achieve near-perfect accuracy even with 80% label noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the underlying mechanisms of how highly over-parameterized models can simultaneously memorize noisy labels and generalize well, a phenomenon that remains poorly understood.

Method: Using modular arithmetic tasks with heavy label noise, extensive experiments on two-layer neural networks, frequency-based extraction methods to recover internal generalization structure, and a task-agnostic method to partition networks into generalization and memorization components.

Result: Larger models generalize better under appropriate optimization; noisy labels are memorized faster than clean data; internal generalization structure exists but is suppressed by noise fitting; frequency-based methods achieve near-perfect accuracy even with 80% noise; the generalization structure is distributed across neurons rather than localized in a small subnetwork.

Conclusion: The generalization structure in over-parameterized networks is distributed across neurons, motivating the need for new tools to retrieve generalizable knowledge from such networks, as simple subnetwork partitioning is limited compared to frequency-based extraction.

Abstract: Highly over-parameterized models can simultaneously memorize noisy labels and generalize well, yet how these behaviors coexist remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the underlying mechanisms of this coexistence using modular arithmetic tasks under heavy label noise. Through extensive experiments on two-layer neural networks, we find that larger models tend to generalize better under appropriate optimization and model configurations, while noisy labels are memorized faster than clean data. Over-parameterized models internally form a generalization structure, but its expression in the output is suppressed by the need to fit noisy labels. Remarkably, even with 80\% label noise, near-perfect test accuracy can be achieved by extracting this internal structure using frequency-based methods. We further propose a task-agnostic method to partition networks into generalization and memorization components. Although this subnetwork improves generalization, it is limited compared with frequency-based extraction, indicating that the generalization structure is distributed across neurons and motivating the development of new tools to retrieve generalizable knowledge from over-parameterized networks.

</details>


### [829] [Interaction-Breaking Adversarial Learning Framework for Robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18024)
*Sunwoo Lee,Mingu Kang,Yonghyeon Jo,Seungyul Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes IBAL, an adversarial learning framework for MARL that attacks and defends against interaction-breaking perturbations from an information-theoretic perspective, outperforming existing robust MARL methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robust MARL methods focus on value-oriented attacks but overlook robustness when interaction structures themselves are corrupted by external perturbations.

Method: IBAL uses an information-theoretic approach to construct attacks that break inter-agent interactions (by perturbing observations and actions) and trains agents to perform reliably under such disruptions.

Result: IBAL improves robustness over existing robust MARL baselines across diverse attack settings and yields stronger performance even under agent-missing scenarios.

Conclusion: Interaction-breaking adversarial learning is an effective way to achieve robust coordination in MARL against structural perturbations.

Abstract: Cooperation is central to multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), yet learned coordination can be fragile when external perturbations disrupt inter-agent interactions. Prior robust MARL methods have primarily considered value-oriented attacks, leaving a gap in robustness when interaction structures themselves are corrupted. In this paper, we propose an interaction-breaking adversarial learning (IBAL) framework that takes an information-theoretic view to construct attacks that impede coordination by perturbing agents' observations and actions, and trains agents to perform reliably under such disruptions. Empirically, our approach improves robustness over existing robust MARL baselines across diverse attack settings and yields stronger performance even under agent-missing scenarios.

</details>


### [830] [FedSDR: Federated Self-Distillation with Rectification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18028)
*Ziheng Ren,Zhanming Shen,Hao Wang,Ning Liu,You Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes FedSD (Federated Self-Distillation) as a universal booster for federated LLM fine-tuning, then identifies the "Rewrite Paradox" (increased hallucinations from unconstrained self-distillation), and finally introduces FedSDR (Federated Self-Distillation with Rectification) with a dual-stream LoRA mechanism to resolve this paradox, achieving superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing model-level defenses for federated LLM fine-tuning overlook the root cause of statistical heterogeneity: intrinsic data distribution mismatches.

Method: First proposes FedSD (Federated Self-Distillation) which projects client representations into a smoothed "model-understanding space". Then proposes FedSDR, which augments FedSD with a dual-stream mechanism: (1) local LoRA-S (Smoothing) branch absorbs heterogeneity via distilled data, and (2) parallel global LoRA-R (Rectification) branch anchored to raw data enforces factual correctness. Only LoRA-R is selectively aggregated globally.

Result: FedSD alone outperforms conventional federated algorithms. FedSDR resolves the "Rewrite Paradox" (hallucinations and redundancy from unconstrained self-distillation) and yields a globally aligned and faithful model with superior experimental results.

Conclusion: FedSDR is an effective reinforced framework for federated fine-tuning of LLMs that handles statistical heterogeneity while maintaining factual correctness.

Abstract: Federated fine-tuning of Large Language Models faces severe statistical heterogeneity. However, existing model-level defenses often overlook the root cause: intrinsic data distribution mismatches. In this work, we first establish Federated Self-Distillation (FedSD) as a fundamental and potent strategy. By projecting client representations into a smoothed ``model-understanding space,'' FedSD alone serves as a universal booster, demonstrating superior performance over conventional algorithms. Despite its success, we identify a subtle trade-off termed the Rewrite Paradox -- unconstrained self-distillation can inadvertently increase hallucinations and redundancy. To refine this paradigm, we further propose FedSDR (Federated Self-Distillation with Rectification), the ultimate reinforced framework. It augments FedSD with a dual-stream mechanism: a local LoRA-S (Smoothing) branch to implicitly absorb heterogeneity via distilled data, and a parallel global LoRA-R (Rectification) branch anchored to raw data to enforce factual correctness. By selectively aggregating only LoRA-R, FedSDR yields a globally aligned and faithful model. Extensive experiments verify its superior performance.

</details>


### [831] [Protection Is (Nearly) All You Need: Structural Protection Dominates Scoring in Globally Capped KV Eviction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18053)
*Gabriel Garcia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A study showing that KV cache eviction policies collapse without structural protection at prompt boundaries, and that reserving cache at these boundaries recovers most of the quality, with scoring differences being secondary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KV cache eviction policies (LRU, H2O, SnapKV, etc.) share a prompt-boundary vulnerability that causes them to collapse to near-zero quality on pure-transformer models, and this vulnerability needs to be systematically studied and addressed.

Method: Evaluate seven KV cache eviction policies with and without structural protection (reserving 10% cache at prompt boundaries) across six transformer models and LongBench tasks. Use attention-mass analysis and NIAH-32K regime-transfer experiments to understand the vulnerability.

Result: Without protection, all policies collapse (F1 ≤ 0.064). With 10% boundary reservation, 69-90% of reference-ceiling quality is recovered at 13% retention. Position-0 sink holds ~75% of prefix mass; other boundary tokens are at ~0.41× uniform expectation. Simplified scoring variants are nearly equivalent to LRU at K=32, with small advantages at K=8.

Conclusion: Structural protection at prompt boundaries is the dominant factor for KV cache eviction quality; scoring differences are secondary once boundaries are guarded. Per-head allocation gives modest additional gains.

Abstract: We study KV cache eviction under a shared globally capped decode-time harness. Seven policies (LRU, H2O, SnapKV, StreamingLLM, Ada-KV, QUEST, Random) share a prompt-boundary vulnerability: without structural protection, they collapse to near-zero quality on six pure-transformer models (F1$\leq$0.064). Reserving 10\% of cache at each boundary recovers 69--90\% of the $C{=}2{,}048$ reference-ceiling quality on seven LongBench models at $C{=}256$ (13\% retention); a ten-model panel spans 68--98\%. An attention-mass pilot (Qwen2.5-3B, $N{=}30$) suggests why: the position-0 sink holds ${\sim}75\%$ of prefix mass, while other boundary tokens sit near ${\sim}0.41{\times}$ uniform expectation, so attention scorers retain the sink but still drop structurally critical tokens. With protection, simplified score-isolation variants are TOST-equivalent to LRU at $K{=}32$ ($Δ{=}0.02$); at $K{=}8$, attention policies pairwise converge yet beat LRU by 0.011--0.021 F1 across $C{=}256$ and $C{=}512$. Faithful Ada-KV/QUEST add ${\sim}0.03$--$0.04$ F1 on Mistral-7B and Phi-3.5 beyond simplified variants. A NIAH-32K regime-transfer pilot on Qwen3-4B (decode vs.\ prefill, $C{\in}\{512,2048\}$) shows near-identical protection lifts (ratio 0.99--1.00). At 64K, protection helps but recovery is modest; faithful per-head scoring matches full-cache ceiling on Gemma-3-4B at 6.3\% retention only when the model already supports strong 64K retrieval without eviction. Overall: protection dominates; scoring differences are secondary once boundaries are guarded; per-head allocation gives a further modest gain.

</details>


### [832] [FLAG: Foundation model representation with Latent diffusion Alignment via Graph for spatial gene expression prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18055)
*Qi Si,Penglei Wang,Yushuai Wu,Yifeng Jiao,Xuyang Liu,Xin Guo,Yuan Qi,Yuan Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLAG is a diffusion-based framework that predicts spatial gene expression from H&E slides by modeling structured distributions, overcoming the Gene Dimension Curse via spatial graph encoding and GFM alignment, and introducing new structural metrics (GSC, SSC) to evaluate gene-gene and gene-spatial relationships.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current spatial gene expression prediction methods treat the task as isolated pointwise tasks, ignoring critical biological structures such as gene coordination and spatial distribution patterns.

Method: FLAG uses a diffusion-based framework with a spatial graph encoder for topological consistency and Gene Foundation Model (GFM) alignment to preserve gene-gene fidelity, addressing the "Gene Dimension Curse" in high-dimensional joint modeling.

Result: FLAG achieves competitive traditional accuracy (PCC/MSE) while significantly improving structural fidelity in capturing gene-gene and gene-spatial relationships, validated by novel metrics (GSC, SSC).

Conclusion: Redefining spatial gene expression prediction as structured distribution modeling better preserves essential biological relationships, and FLAG's diffusion-based approach effectively overcomes high-dimensional modeling challenges.

Abstract: Predicting spatial gene expression from routine H\&E enables large-scale molecular profiling, yet current models treat this as isolated pointwise tasks, thereby overlooking essential biological structures like gene coordination and spatial distribution. To preserve these relationships, we introduce \textbf{FLAG}, a diffusion-based framework that redefines this task as structured distribution modeling. At the same time, we identify the critical \textbf{Gene Dimension Curse}, where joint modeling gene expression and their spatial interactions fail in high-dimensional spaces, and FLAG solves this challenge by integrating a spatial graph encoder for topological consistency and utilizing Gene Foundation Model (GFM) alignment for gene-gene fidelity in the generation process. To rigorously assess model performance, we propose a set of novel structural evaluation metrics, including Gene Structural Correlation (\textbf{GSC}) and Spatial Structural Correlation (\textbf{SSC}). Our experiments demonstrate that FLAG is highly competitive in traditional accuracy (PCC/MSE) while achieving significantly enhanced structural fidelity in capturing both gene-gene and gene-spatial relationships. The code is available at https://github.com/darkflash03/FLAG.

</details>


### [833] [Improving Spatio-Temporal Residual Error Propagation by Mitigating Over-Squashing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18068)
*Seyed Mohamad Moghadas,Esther Rodrigo Bonet,Bruno Cornelis,Adrian Munteanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Teger is a backbone-agnostic uncertainty module that uses Forman curvature-guided graph rewiring and a low-rank-plus-diagonal covariance head to capture spatial-temporal error correlations, improving probabilistic forecasting across LSTM, Transformer, and xLSTM backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Residual error propagation in recurrent models degrades long-horizon forecasting, and existing deep time-series models neglect both temporal error dependence and spatial correlation across observed networks, limiting uncertainty quantification quality.

Method: Teger introduces (1) a spatial curvature-aware graph rewiring mechanism using discrete Forman curvature to strengthen information-bottleneck edges, and (2) a low-rank-plus-diagonal covariance head with Woodbury identity for tractable inference, integrated into any autoregressive encoder.

Result: Evaluated on LSTM, Transformer, and xLSTM backbones across four real-world spatio-temporal datasets, Teger achieves consistent CRPS improvement. Theoretical analysis connects curvature-aware rewiring to oversquashing alleviation, improved spectral connectivity, reduced effective resistance, and better covariance calibration bounds.

Conclusion: Teger effectively addresses both spatial and temporal limitations of error-correlated autoregressive forecasting, providing theoretically grounded and empirically validated improvements for probabilistic multivariate time series forecasting.

Abstract: Residual error propagation remains a fundamental problem in recurrent models, where small prediction inaccuracies compound over time and degrade long-horizon performance. Accurately modeling the correlation structure of such residuals is critical for reliable uncertainty quantification in probabilistic multivariate timeseries forecasting. While recent time-series deep models efficiently parametrize time-varying contemporaneous correlations, they often assume temporal independence of errors and neglect spatial correlation across the observed network. In this paper, we introduce Teger, a structured uncertainty module that overcomes the spa- tial and temporal limitations of error-correlated autoregressive forecasting. Teger proposes a spatial curvature-aware graph rewiring mechanism explicitly strengthening information-bottleneck edges identified by discrete Forman curvature. The component is integrated into a low-rank-plus-diagonal covariance head, preserving tractable inference via the Woodbury identity. Teger is backbone-agnostic, requiring only the latent state produced by any autoregressive encoder. We provide theoretical evidence of Teger, and experimentally evaluate it on LSTM, Transformer, and xLSTM backbones across four real-world spatio-temporal datasets, showing consistent improvement in Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). We further provide a formal theoretical analysis connecting curvature-aware rewiring to (i) oversquashing alleviation, (ii) improved spectral connectivity, (iii) reduced effective resistance, and (iv) improved covariance calibration bounds

</details>


### [834] [Equilibrium Selection in Multi-Agent Policy Gradients via Opponent-Aware Basin Entry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18078)
*Yevhen Shcherbinin,Arina Redina,Maxim Kalpin,Vlad Kochetov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that peer-learning corrections in Meta-MAPG increase the probability of entering target stable-Nash basins (e.g., cooperative equilibria), with annealing to recover local convergence guarantees afterward.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent policy-gradient methods converge locally near stable Nash equilibria, but it remains unclear which equilibrium is selected. The authors aim to understand and control equilibrium selection via basin-entry probability.

Method: The authors decompose Meta-MAPG updates into ordinary policy gradient, own-learning, and peer-learning corrections. They analyze basin-entry probability under a local alignment condition and propose annealing the correction after entering the target basin.

Result: Peer-learning correction is identified as the key mechanism for equilibrium selection. Experiments in Stag Hunt, iterated Prisoner's Dilemma, and neural-policy coordination show increased entry into cooperative basins under peer-aware updates.

Conclusion: Annealing the correction after basin entry recovers ordinary policy-gradient dynamics and inherits local stable-Nash convergence guarantees, providing a principled way to steer multi-agent learning toward desired equilibria.

Abstract: Multi-agent policy-gradient methods have been shown to converge locally near stable Nash equilibria. Local convergence, however, does not determine which equilibrium is reached. We study this question through basin-entry probability with respect to a target set of equilibria selected by an external criterion, such as payoff dominance. For finite-unroll Meta-MAPG, we show that the update decomposes into ordinary policy gradient plus own-learning and peer-learning corrections, with controlled sampling noise and finite-unroll bias. We identify the peer-learning correction as the main equilibrium-selection mechanism: under a local alignment condition, the probability of entering the certified attraction region of the target stable-Nash set increases, relative to ordinary policy gradient. Because persistent correction may shift zero-update points of the original game, annealing the correction after entering the basin recovers ordinary policy-gradient dynamics and inherits local stable-Nash convergence guarantees. Experiments in Stag Hunt, iterated Prisoner's Dilemma, and preliminary neural-policy coordination environments support this basin-entry view, showing increased entry into cooperative basins under peer-aware updates.

</details>


### [835] [The Expressive Power of Low Precision Softmax Transformers with (Summarized) Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18079)
*Moritz Brösamle,Stephan Eckstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that standard softmax transformers with rounding can simulate Turing machines via Chain-of-Thought, with model size scaling logarithmically with context length, and demonstrates better empirical alignment with learnability than prior high-precision results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing expressivity results for transformers rely on unrealistic assumptions (hardmax attention, high precision, architectural modifications) that disconnect them from models used in practice. This paper aims to bridge that gap.

Method: The authors construct hardmax transformers with ternary activations and well-separated attention scores that simulate Turing machines using CoT, then convert them to equivalent softmax transformers without unrealistic parameter magnitudes or activation precision.

Result: Softmax transformers with rounding can simulate Turing machines with model size scaling logarithmically in context length. Summarized CoT simulates Turing machines more efficiently, scaling logarithmically in space bound rather than time bound. Empirical results on Sudoku show better alignment with learnability than prior high-precision results.

Conclusion: Standard transformer decoders with softmax attention and rounding are more realistic expressivity models than prior high-precision approaches, and their theoretical predictions better align with empirical learnability.

Abstract: Existing expressivity results for transformers typically rely on hardmax attention, high precision, and other architectural modifications that disconnect them from the models used in practice. We bridge this gap by analyzing standard transformer decoders with softmax attention and rounding of activations and attention weights, while allowing depth and width to grow logarithmically with the context length. As an intermediate step, we construct hardmax transformers with ternary activations and well-separated attention scores that simulate Turing machines using Chain-of-Thought (CoT). This lets us convert the constructions to equivalent softmax transformers without the unrealistic parameter magnitudes or activation precision that prior approaches would require. Using the same technique, we analyze a recently proposed summarized CoT paradigm and show that it simulates Turing machines more efficiently, with model size scaling logarithmically in a space bound rather than a time bound. We empirically test predictions made by our results on a Sudoku reasoning task and find better alignment with learnability than for prior high-precision results. Our code is available at https://github.com/moritzbroe/transformer-expressivity.

</details>


### [836] [pyforce-1.0.0: Python Framework for data-driven model Order Reduction of multi-physiCs problEms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18082)
*Stefano Riva,Yantao Luo,Carolina Introini,Antonio Cammi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: pyforce is a Python package (v1.0.0) for Data-Driven Reduced Order Modelling in multi-physics Nuclear Engineering, rewritten with pyvista backend and numpy arrays for broader compatibility with VTK-exporting solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce complexity of multi-physics models (nuclear reactor applications), optimize sensor placement, and integrate real measurements for improved physical system knowledge.

Method: Completely re-written from dolfinx-based implementation to use pyvista for mesh, integrals, and visualization; functions stored as numpy arrays; supports VTK format for solver interoperability.

Result: Version 1.0.0 offers improved ease of use, broader compatibility with any solver exporting VTK format, and enhanced backend flexibility.

Conclusion: pyforce 1.0.0 provides a more accessible and versatile reduced order modelling toolkit for nuclear engineering multi-physics problems.

Abstract: pyforce is a Python package implementing Data-Driven Reduced Order Modelling techniques for applications to multi-physics problems, mainly set in the Nuclear Engineering world. The package is part of the ROSE (Reduced Order modelling with data-driven techniques for multi-phySics problEms): mathematical algorithms aimed at reducing the complexity of multi-physics models (for nuclear reactors applications), at searching for optimal sensor positions and at integrating real measures to improve the knowledge on the physical systems. With respect to the previous original implementation based on dolfinx package (v0.6.0), version 1.0.0 of pyforce has been completely re-written using pyvista as backend for mesh importing, computing integrals, and visualisation of results; in addition, functions are stored as numpy arrays, improving the ease of use of the package. This choice allows to use pyforce with any software solver able to export results in VTK format.

</details>


### [837] [Foundation Models for Credit Risk Prediction: A Game Changer?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18147)
*Bart Baesens,Andreas Goethals,Stefan Lessmann,Simon De Vos,Cristián Bravo,David Martens,Victor Medina-Olivares,Christophe Mues,Maria Oskarsdóttir,Seppe vanden Broucke,Tim Verdonck,Wouter Verbeke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks tabular foundation models against traditional ML techniques for PD and LGD modeling in credit risk, finding they perform best overall—especially in small-data settings—without needing hyperparameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While gradient-boosting models with SHAP explainers are quasi-standards in credit risk, continuous improvement is needed, especially for small-data challenges like SME lending, low default portfolios, and class imbalance. Foundation models pretrained on diverse out-of-domain data may help.

Method: The authors benchmark recently proposed tabular foundation models against a broad set of competitors (established and advanced ML techniques) across PD (Probability of Default) and LGD (Loss Given Default) modeling tasks, using multiple datasets, performance indicators, and experimental conditions.

Result: Tabular foundation models generally perform best across datasets and tasks, with significant improvements as dataset size shrinks. These results are achieved out-of-the-box without hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: Tabular foundation models offer a compelling alternative for credit risk modeling, particularly benefiting small-data scenarios while maintaining ease of use and lower computational costs due to no tuning requirements.

Abstract: Predictive models play a pivotal role in credit risk management, guiding critical decisions through accurate estimation of default probabilities and losses. Extensive research has introduced new modeling techniques, complemented by large-scale benchmarking studies consolidating the state-of-the-art. Today, quasi-standards such as gradient-boosting models paired with SHAP explainers have emerged, yet continuous improvement of risk models remains a top priority. Concurrently, rapid advancements in AI, most notably large language models, have disrupted predictive modeling paradigms. Foundation models, pretrained on extensive datasets from diverse domains, have demonstrated remarkable performance by leveraging prior knowledge. While prevalent in natural language processing and computer vision, foundation models for tabular data have only recently emerged. We conjecture that pretraining on out-of-domain data is particularly beneficial in small-data settings, such as SME lending or specialized corporate portfolios, and may help address longstanding challenges including low default portfolios and class imbalance. This paper benchmarks recently proposed tabular foundation models against a broad set of competitors, including established and advanced machine learning techniques, across two core tasks: PD and LGD modeling. Our evaluation encompasses various datasets, performance indicators, and experimental conditions. We find that tabular foundation models generally perform best across datasets and tasks. Moreover, they offer significant improvement in predictive performance as dataset size shrinks. These results are remarkable given that the models are tested out-of-the-box, without hyperparameter tuning, ensuring ease of use and mitigating computational costs.

</details>


### [838] [Elastic-dLLM: Position Preserving Context Compression and Augmentation of Diffusion LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18165)
*Junyi Wu,Tianchen Zhao,Shaoqiu Zhang,Linfeng Zhang,Guohao Dai,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies and mitigates computational redundancy in diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) by compressing redundant [MASK] tokens while preserving positional information, and introduces terminal-aware augmentation to improve efficiency and generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: dLLMs denoise chunks of [MASK] tokens jointly, which incurs substantial computational cost due to large chunk sizes. Much of this cost is wasted on repeatedly processing redundant feature representations of preceding context and many [MASK] tokens.

Method: The authors systematically analyze redundancy in [MASK] tokens, then propose: (1) position-preserving [MASK] token compression to reduce redundant computation, and (2) terminal-aware augmentation that adds a protected terminal [MASK] token for block dLLMs to improve generation quality.

Result: The method accelerates decoding for dLLMs, enables context-folding-like long-context scaling under limited input-length constraints for full-sequence dLLMs (LLaDA-8B-Instruct, LLaDA-1.5), and enhances generation quality with negligible overhead for block dLLMs (LLaDA2.0-mini).

Conclusion: [MASK] tokens are redundant computationally but critical for structural information; compressing them while preserving position yields efficient decoding without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Unlike autoregressive models, which generate one token at a time, dLLMs denoise a chunk of [MASK] tokens jointly and sample one or more tokens per step; despite enabling parallel decoding, this process incurs substantial computational cost due to the large chunk size of masked tokens. We observe that much of this cost is spent on repeatedly processing the preceding context and many [MASK] tokens with the same feature representations, indicating considerable computational redundancy. In this work, we revisit dLLM's redundancy from the perspective of [MASK] tokens. Through systematic analysis, we verify the redundancy of [MASK] tokens while revealing their critical role in providing structural information. Guided by these findings, we propose position-preserving [MASK] token compression and terminal-aware augmentation. By compressing redundant [MASK] computation, this approach accelerates decoding and further provides a natural extension toward context-folding-like long-context scaling under limited input-length constraints for full-sequence dLLMs such as LLaDA-8B-Instruct and LLaDA-1.5. Moreover, for block dLLMs such as LLaDA2.0-mini, it augments the context with a protected terminal [MASK] token to enhance generation quality with negligible overhead.

</details>


### [839] [Ringmaster LMO: Asynchronous Linear Minimization Oracle Momentum Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18174)
*Abdurakhmon Sadiev,Artavazd Maranjyan,Ivan Ilin,Peter Richtárik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Ringmaster LMO, an asynchronous optimization method that extends the delay-thresholding idea of Ringmaster ASGD to LMO-based (Muon-style) updates, achieving optimal time complexity in heterogeneous distributed systems with convergence guarantees under generalized smoothness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Muon and LMO-based methods are strong alternatives to AdamW for training neural networks, but they are typically used synchronously, which is problematic in heterogeneous distributed systems where workers complete gradient computations at different speeds and synchronous training must repeatedly wait for slower workers.

Method: Ringmaster LMO extends the delay-thresholding mechanism of Ringmaster ASGD to general LMO-based updates for unconstrained stochastic nonconvex optimization. It includes a parameter-agnostic variant with decreasing stepsizes and adaptive delay thresholds, and provides convergence guarantees under generalized $(L_0, L_1)$-smoothness.

Result: The method achieves optimal time complexity under heterogeneous worker computation times in the classical Euclidean smooth setting, recovering the optimal time complexity of Ringmaster ASGD. Experiments on stochastic quadratic problems and NanoChat language-model pretraining show advantages grow with system heterogeneity, outperforming synchronous and asynchronous baselines.

Conclusion: Ringmaster LMO effectively addresses the synchronization bottleneck of LMO-based methods in heterogeneous distributed systems by leveraging asynchronous delay-thresholding, achieving both theoretical optimality and practical superiority over existing baselines.

Abstract: Muon has recently emerged as a strong alternative to AdamW for training neural networks, with encouraging large-scale pretraining results and growing evidence that matrix-structured updates can be faster in practice. Yet Muon, and more generally Linear Minimization Oracle (LMO) based methods, are typically used synchronously. This is problematic in heterogeneous distributed systems, where workers complete gradient computations at different speeds and synchronous training must repeatedly wait for slower workers. In this work, we introduce Ringmaster LMO, an asynchronous LMO-based momentum method for unconstrained stochastic nonconvex optimization. Our method builds on the delay-thresholding idea of Ringmaster ASGD. For SGD-type methods, Ringmaster ASGD achieves optimal time complexity by discarding overly stale gradients. Ringmaster LMO extends this mechanism to general LMO-based updates. We establish convergence guarantees under generalized $(L_0, L_1)$-smoothness and further develop a parameter-agnostic variant with decreasing stepsizes and adaptive delay thresholds. Finally, we translate our iteration guarantees into time complexity bounds under heterogeneous worker computation times. In the classical Euclidean smooth setting, these bounds recover the optimal time complexity of Ringmaster ASGD. Experiments on stochastic quadratic problems and NanoChat language-model pretraining show that the advantages of Ringmaster LMO grow with system heterogeneity and that the method outperforms strong synchronous and asynchronous baselines.

</details>


### [840] [UTOPYA: A Multimodal Deep Learning Framework for Physics-Informed Anomaly Detection and Time-Series Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18188)
*Robson W. S. Pessoa,Julien Amblard,Alessandra Russo,Idelfonso B. R. Nogueira*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UTOPYA is a 15.2M-parameter multimodal framework for anomaly detection in batch distillation, using eight data modalities fused via FiLM-conditioned cross-modal attention. It achieves 0.874 AUROC on a 119-experiment dataset, with physics-informed regularization and curriculum learning. Key negative findings show common techniques like Mixup and instance normalization hurt performance in data-scarce settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Batch process anomaly detection faces three key challenges: transient dynamics, scarcity of fault labels, and reliance on single-modality sensor data, motivating a multimodal approach.

Method: UTOPYA fuses eight data modalities via FiLM-conditioned cross-modal attention and gated fusion, uses physics-informed regularization (temporal smoothness, thermodynamic monotonicity), and curriculum learning ordered by physical difficulty.

Result: UTOPYA achieves 0.832 window-level AUROC and 0.874 experiment-level AUROC, outperforming baselines by +0.147. FiLM conditioning is the key enabler (+0.145 AUROC). Many common techniques (Mixup, instance normalization, ensembling) degraded performance.

Conclusion: Multimodal fusion with physics-aware design significantly improves anomaly detection in batch processes, but common regularization techniques can be detrimental in data-scarce, transient-dynamics settings.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in batch processes is hindered by transient dynamics, scarce fault labels, and reliance on single-modality sensor data. This work introduces UTOPYA (Unified Temporal Observation for Physics-Informed Anomaly Detection and Time-Series Prediction), a 15.2M-parameter multimodal framework that jointly addresses anomaly detection, time-series prediction, and phase classification in batch distillation by fusing eight data modalities through Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) conditioned cross-modal attention and gated fusion. A physics-informed regularisation scheme introduced in this work enforces temporal smoothness and thermodynamic monotonicity, while curriculum learning introduces training samples in order of physical difficulty. On the 119-experiment multimodal batch distillation dataset of Arweiler et al. (2026), UTOPYA achieves a window-level test AUROC of 0.832 and 0.874 under multi-signal experiment-level scoring, substantially outperforming four external baselines (PCA, autoencoder, Isolation Forest, and LSTM autoencoder) evaluated under identical conditions (+0.147 window-level AUROC over the best baseline). A multimodal ablation over 15~architectural configurations shows that static context via FiLM conditioning is the key enabler, lifting experiment-level multi-signal AUROC by +0.145 over the unimodal baseline (0.729 to 0.874). Separately, a training ablation across 14 design choices reveals that several widely-adopted techniques, including instance normalisation, Mixup, ensembling, test-time augmentation, and stochastic weight averaging, fail to improve or actively degrade generalisation in this data-scarce setting. These negative results expose a fundamental tension between smoothing-based regularisation and anomaly detection, providing practical guidance for multimodal process monitoring deployment.

</details>


### [841] [Dual-Rate Diffusion: Accelerating diffusion models with an interleaved heavy-light network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18190)
*Grigory Bartosh,David Ruhe,Emiel Hoogeboom,Jonathan Heek,Thomas Mensink,Tim Salimans*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dual-Rate Diffusion accelerates diffusion model sampling by interleaving a sparse heavy context encoder with a light denoising model that reuses extracted features, achieving 2-4x speedup on ImageNet without quality loss and compatible with distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models achieve high-quality generation but suffer from high computational costs due to repeated evaluations of a heavy neural network during inference.

Method: Interleaves a heavy high-capacity context encoder (evaluated sparsely) with a light efficient denoising model (evaluated every step). The context encoder extracts high-dimensional features that are reused by the light denoising model at each step.

Result: Matches standard baseline performance while reducing computational cost by 2-4x on ImageNet benchmarks; compatible with distillation techniques like Moment Matching Distillation for further gains.

Conclusion: Dual-Rate Diffusion significantly accelerates inference without compromising sample quality, and can be combined with distillation methods for even greater efficiency in few-step generation.

Abstract: Diffusion models achieve state-of-the-art generative performance but suffer from high computational costs during inference due to the repeated evaluation of a heavy neural network. In this work, we propose Dual-Rate Diffusion, a method to accelerate sampling by interleaving the execution of a heavy high-capacity context encoder and a light efficient denoising model. The context encoder is evaluated sparsely to extract high-dimensional features, which are effectively reused by the light denoising model at every step to refine the sample efficiently. This approach significantly accelerates inference without compromising sample quality. On ImageNet benchmarks, Dual-Rate Diffusion matches the performance of standard baselines while reducing computational cost by a factor of $2$-$4$. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our method is compatible with distillation techniques, such as Moment Matching Distillation, enabling further efficiency gains in few-step generation.

</details>


### [842] [Concise and Logically Consistent Conformal Sets for Neuro-Symbolic Concept-Based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18202)
*Samuele Bortolotti,Emanuele Marconato,Andrea Pugnana,Andrea Passerini,Stefano Teso*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COCOCO is a post-hoc conformal prediction framework for Neuro-Symbolic Concept-based Models that jointly conformalizes concepts and labels via deduction-abduction revision, satisfying consistency, coverage, and conciseness desiderata.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neuro-Symbolic Concept-based Models (NeSy-CBMs) suffer from overconfident label and concept predictions, making it difficult for stakeholders to gauge trustworthiness in high-stakes applications.

Method: The authors formalize three desiderata (consistency, coverage, conciseness) and propose COCOCO, a post-hoc framework that conformalizes both concepts and labels jointly, then reconciles them via a single deduction-abduction revision step.

Result: COCOCO satisfies all three desiderata, retains distribution-free coverage, is robust to imperfect knowledge, supports user-specified size budgets, and outperforms competitors on 8 datasets.

Conclusion: COCOCO effectively addresses overconfidence in NeSy-CBMs by providing reliable, distribution-free uncertainty quantification with formal guarantees.

Abstract: Neuro-Symbolic Concept-based Models (NeSy-CBMs) are a family of architectures that integrate neural networks with symbolic reasoning for enhanced reliability in high-stakes applications. They work by first extracting high-level concepts from the input and then inferring a task label from these compatibly with given logical constraints. Yet, their label and concept predictions can be overconfident, making it difficult for stakeholders to gauge when the model's decisions can be trusted. We address this issue by integrating ideas from Conformal Prediction (CP), a framework providing rigorous, distribution-free coverage guarantees. We formalize three desiderata -- consistency, coverage, and conciseness -- that any conformal method for NeSy-CBMs should satisfy, and show that existing approaches fall short of at least one. We then introduce COCOCO, a post-hoc framework that conformalizes concepts and labels jointly and reconciles them via a single deduction-abduction revision step. COCOCO satisfies all three desiderata, retains distribution-free coverage, is robust to imperfect knowledge and supports user-specified size budgets. Our experiments on 8 data sets highlight how COCOCO compares favorably against competitors and natural baselines in terms of performance and set size.

</details>


### [843] [A Simplex Witness Certificate for Constant Collapse in Variational Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18224)
*Zegu Zhang,Jianhua Peng,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to detect, monitor, and certify exact constant collapse (where encoder mean becomes input-independent) in variational autoencoders by using a simplex witness head and teacher-student alignment loss, converting this failure mode from a training pathology into a design-and-certificate problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make exact constant collapse in VAEs pre-designable, monitorable during training, and certifiable after training, rather than just an after-the-fact pathology.

Method: Attaches a fixed simplex witness head to the latent mean, uses a teacher-student alignment loss with an exact constant-predictor baseline equal to the teacher information, introduces a computable view gap, and provides a closed-form inverse via centered log-odds embedding.

Result: If alignment loss is below the teacher information baseline, the latent mean cannot be constant collapsed. The simplex witness has a closed-form inverse, and any full-support teacher posterior can be represented with explicit latent energy cost.

Conclusion: Exact constant collapse is converted from a training pathology into a design-and-certificate problem.

Abstract: This note studies exact constant collapse in variational autoencoders, where the encoder mean becomes independent of the input. The goal is to make this specific failure mode pre-designable, monitorable during training, and certifiable after training. The prior is kept as the standard Gaussian. Given a fixed teacher posterior, we attach to the latent mean a fixed simplex witness head. The resulting teacher-student alignment loss has an exact constant-predictor baseline equal to the teacher information. If the alignment loss is below this baseline, the latent mean cannot be input-independent constant collapsed.
  The simplex witness also has a closed-form inverse. Any full-support teacher posterior can be represented by embedding its centered log-odds into the latent space. This gives an explicit latent energy cost and explains when the alignment loss can be made small. A computable view gap handles the case where teacher targets are computed from a different view. Thus exact constant collapse is converted from an after-the-fact training pathology into a design-and-certificate problem.

</details>


### [844] [Are Sparse Autoencoder Benchmarks Reliable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18229)
*David Chanin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper audits SAE quality metrics in SAEBench and finds that two metrics (TPP and SCR) fail reliability tests, while the remaining metrics have higher noise and lower discriminability than assumed, concluding that better SAE benchmarks are needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Progress on SAE architectures depends on reliable benchmarks to distinguish better SAEs from worse ones, motivating an audit of the de-facto standard SAE evaluation suite (SAEBench).

Method: The authors audit SAE quality metrics through three lenses: (1) reseed noise on fixed SAEs, (2) ground-truth correlation on synthetic SAEs, and (3) discriminability across training trajectories.

Result: TPP and SCR fail multiple lenses at canonical settings; all metrics show higher reseed noise and lower discriminability than assumed; sae-probes is the most reliable metric tested but still struggles to separate variants of the same SAE architecture.

Conclusion: The field needs better SAE benchmarks, as current metrics are not as reliable as commonly believed.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a core interpretability tool for large language models, and progress on SAE architectures depends on benchmarks that reliably distinguish better SAEs from worse ones. We audit the SAE quality metrics in SAEBench, the de-facto standard SAE evaluation suite, through three complementary lenses: reseed noise on a fixed SAE, ground-truth correlation on synthetic SAEs, and discriminability across training trajectories. We find that two of these metrics, Targeted Probe Perturbation (TPP) and Spurious Correlation Removal (SCR), fail multiple lenses at their canonical settings and should not be used to evaluate SAEs. The other metrics show higher reseed noise and lower discriminability than the field assumes. The sae-probes variant of $k$-sparse probing is the most reliable metric we tested, but even sae-probes struggles to separate variants of the same SAE architecture. Our results show the field needs better SAE benchmarks.

</details>


### [845] [Attacking the First-Principle: A Black-Box, Query-Free Targeted Mimicry Attack on Binary Function Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18231)
*Gabriel Sauger,Jean-Yves Marion,Sazzadur Rahaman,Victor Matrat,Vincent Tourneur,Muaz Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kelpie is a novel black-box, zero-query framework that executes mimicry attacks on binary function classifiers using functionality-preserving code transformations, successfully evading six state-of-the-art classifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Binary function classifiers are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and existing evasion methods require querying the target classifier. The authors aim to demonstrate stronger, targeted mimicry attacks without any direct interaction.

Method: Kelpie uses code transformations that preserve malicious payload functionality but cause misclassification, operating in a black-box, zero-query setting without needing to interact with the target classifier.

Result: Kelpie successfully evades six state-of-the-art binary function classifiers across different architectures and is validated with a practical keylogger and wiper demonstration.

Conclusion: This is the first demonstration of mimicry attacks in a black-box, zero-query context, raising concerns about the reliability of ML-based binary function classifiers.

Abstract: Binary function classifiers play a crucial role in maintaining the security and integrity of software systems by detecting malicious code and unauthorized modifications. However, machine learning-based classifiers are vulnerable to adversarial attacks that can evade detection. In this study, we present Kelpie, a novel framework for executing mimicry attacks, a stronger type of targeted evasion attacks, on binary function classifiers in a black-box, zero-query setting. Unlike previous approaches that rely on querying the target classifier to refine untargeted evasion attacks, Kelpie leverages code transformations that preserve the functionality of malicious payloads while causing them to be misclassified as we want. Through extensive experimentation, we demonstrate that Kelpie can successfully execute mimicry attacks against six state-of-the-art binary function classifiers representing different model architectures without requiring direct interaction with them. We further validate our approach with a practical demonstration, involving a keylogger and a wiper concealed within benign-looking functions embedded in an application. This work, to our best knowledge, is the first to demonstrate such a mimicry attack in a black-box, zero-query context, raising important questions about the reliability and security of existing machine learning-based binary function classifiers.

</details>


### [846] [Privacy Preserving Reinforcement Learning with One-Sided Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18246)
*Lin William Cong,Guangyan Gan,Hanzhang Qin,Zhenzhen Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: POOL is a novel privacy-preserving RL algorithm for multi-dimensional continuous spaces with one-sided feedback, achieving sample complexity matching non-private lower bounds while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the dual challenges of learning efficiency and privacy preservation in multi-dimensional continuous state/action spaces with one-sided feedback (partial observations and limited reward information).

Method: Proposes POOL, a privacy-preserving RL algorithm designed for one-sided feedback settings with strong theoretical guarantees.

Result: Derives a sample complexity bound matching known lower bounds for non-private RL, showing privacy can be enforced without sacrificing learning efficiency.

Conclusion: Strong privacy guarantees are achievable alongside high learning efficiency, advancing practical privacy-aware RL in multi-dimensional environments with one-sided feedback.

Abstract: We study reinforcement learning (RL) in multi-dimensional continuous state and action spaces with one-sided feedback, where the agent receives partial observations of the state and obtains reward information for only a subset of the state-action space at each time step. This setting introduces substantial challenges in both learning efficiency and privacy preservation. To address these challenges, we propose POOL, a novel privacy-preserving RL algorithm. We conduct a comprehensive theoretical analysis of POOL, deriving a sample complexity bound that matches the known lower bounds for non-private RL. Here, E_rho denotes the privacy parameter, H is the time horizon, and alpha is the optimality-gap parameter. Our findings show that it is possible to enforce strong privacy guarantees while maintaining high learning efficiency, marking a significant step toward practical, privacy-aware RL in multi-dimensional environments with one-sided feedback.

</details>


### [847] [Temporal Task Diversity: Inductive Biases Under Non-Stationarity in Synthetic Sequence Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18281)
*Afiq Abdillah Effiezal Aswadi,Oliver Britton,Ross Baker,Matthew Farrugia-Roberts*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how non-stationary (temporally diverse) training data distributions affect the inductive biases of transformers in in-context linear regression, finding that temporal diversity increases the bias towards generalization over memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern deep learning often assumes fixed data distributions, but many real-world problems involve changing distributions. The authors aim to understand how such non-stationarity impacts inductive biases, generalization, and safety properties of neural networks.

Method: The authors use in-context linear regression sequence modeling as a testbed, where small transformers are trained on task distributions that vary across training time (temporal diversity), and they compare generalization patterns against models trained on fixed distributions.

Result: Temporal diversity in the training task distribution leads to an increased bias towards generalization over memorization in transformers.

Conclusion: Non-stationary data distributions can positively influence the inductive biases of deep learning models, promoting better generalization behavior.

Abstract: Modern deep learning science often assumes that neural networks learn from a fixed data distribution. However, many practically important learning problems involve data distributions that change throughout training. How does such non-stationarity impact the inductive biases of deep learning towards models with different structural, generalisation, and safety properties? A fruitful testbed for studying inductive bias is in-context linear regression sequence modelling, where small transformers display strikingly different generalisation patterns depending on the diversity of the (fixed) training task distribution. In this paper, we explore the effect of diversifying the task distribution across training time, finding that such temporal diversity leads to an increased bias towards generalisation over memorisation.

</details>


### [848] [Alignment Dynamics in LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18309)
*Yuhan Huang,Huanran Chen,Yinpeng Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified framework for understanding why LLM alignment is fragile under fine-tuning, decomposing alignment dynamics into a Rebound Force (governed by current alignment and distribution narrowness) and a Driving Force (shaped by training distribution alignment). It predicts and validates a Rehearsal Priming Effect where prior alignment accelerates re-alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing explanations for alignment fragility under fine-tuning are fragmented—some attribute it to gradient geometry, others to distributional shifts—but no unified account bridges parameter-space learning dynamics with function-space alignment behavior.

Method: The authors introduce a tractable alignment score with a closed-form update, decomposing alignment dynamics into Rebound Force and Driving Force. They derive theoretical predictions and validate them empirically across safety alignment, emergent misalignment, and sentiment settings.

Result: 1) Alignment reversal is explained by the competition between Rebound and Driving Forces. 2) Narrower posterior structure strengthens reversal. 3) Rehearsal Priming Effect: prior alignment leaves latent posterior imprints that accelerate re-alignment upon re-exposure. All predictions confirmed empirically.

Conclusion: The unified dynamical framework bridges parameter-space and function-space views of alignment, explaining both how alignment is disrupted and reactivated during LLM fine-tuning, with practical implications for robust alignment maintenance.

Abstract: Although Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong alignment through supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback, the alignment is often fragile under subsequent fine-tuning. Existing explanations either attribute alignment fragility to gradient geometry or characterize it as a distributional shift in model outputs, yet few provide a unified account that bridges parameter-space learning dynamics with function-space alignment behavior during fine-tuning. In this work, we introduce a tractable alignment score and derive its closed-form update during fine-tuning, yielding a unified framework for alignment dynamics. Our analysis decomposes alignment updates into two competing components: a \textbf{\color{red!60!black} Rebound Force}, governed jointly by the current alignment state and the narrowness of model distribution, and a \textbf{\color{green!60!black} Driving Force}, determined by how the training distribution aligns with outcome-conditioned posteriors over aligned and non-aligned completions. This decomposition explains why prior alignment can be reversed by later fine-tuning and why narrower posterior structure strengthens such reversal. Moreover, our framework predicts a \textbf{Rehearsal Priming Effect}: prior alignment leaves a latent posterior imprint that amplifies the effective Driving Force upon re-exposure, leading to faster re-alignment. We validate these predictions across safety alignment, emergent misalignment, and sentiment settings, demonstrating consistent alignment reversal and accelerated re-alignment under re-exposure. In addition, controlled experiments in safety alignment confirm the predicted dependence of rebound strength on posterior narrowness. Together, these results provide a unified dynamical perspective on how alignment is disrupted and reactivated during LLM fine-tuning.

</details>


### [849] [Dynamic Elliptical Graph Factor Models via Riemannian Optimization with Geodesic Temporal Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18316)
*Chuansen Peng,Xiaojing Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes DEGFM, a Riemannian optimization algorithm for estimating time-varying graph structures from high-dimensional data using a low-rank-plus-diagonal factor model and Grassmann manifold geodesic penalties, with theoretical convergence guarantees and superior empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two key challenges in dynamic graph inference: maintaining temporal coherence of latent graphs across observation windows, and respecting the intrinsic Riemannian geometry of symmetric positive definite (SPD) manifolds where precision matrices reside, which Euclidean methods fail to capture.

Method: Models time-varying precision matrices as low-rank-plus-diagonal with a latent elliptical graph factor model; enforces temporal smoothness via a Riemannian geodesic penalty on the Grassmann manifold; solves non-convex optimization using efficient Riemannian gradient descent with convergence guarantees.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across all evaluation metrics on both synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: DEGFM effectively combines dimensionality reduction (low-rank structure) with proper geometric treatment (Grassmann manifold) to deliver superior dynamic graph estimation, especially in small-sample regimes.

Abstract: Inferring time-varying graph structures from high-dimensional nodal observations is a fundamental problem arising in neuroscience, finance, climatology, and beyond. Two intrinsic challenges govern this problem: maintaining the \emph{temporal coherence} of the latent graph across successive observation windows, and respecting the \emph{intrinsic Riemannian geometry} of the symmetric positive definite manifold on which precision matrices naturally reside, a curved space whose geodesic structure departs fundamentally from that of the ambient Euclidean space. In this paper we propose dynamic estimation on the Grassmann manifold with a factor model (\textsc{Degfm}), a novel algorithm that jointly addresses both challenges. We model the time-varying precision matrix sequence as a low-rank-plus-diagonal structure governed by a latent elliptical graph factor model, which drastically reduces the effective parameter count and enables reliable estimation in the challenging small-sample regime. Temporal coherence is enforced through a Riemannian geodesic penalty defined on the Grassmann manifold, ensuring that the estimated graph trajectory is smooth with respect to the intrinsic geometry rather than the ambient Euclidean space. To solve the resulting non-convex optimization problem over Grassmann-manifold-valued sequences subject to the LRaD constraint, we derive an efficient Riemannian gradient descent algorithm that respects the manifold structure at every iterate and rigorously establish its convergence to a stationary point. Extensive experiments on both synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate that \textsc{Degfm} consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across all evaluation metrics, confirming the practical effectiveness of the proposed framework.

</details>


### [850] [The Symmetries of Three-Layer ReLU Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18319)
*Johanna Marie Gegenfurtner,Moritz Grillo,Guido Montúfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper characterizes parameter symmetries in deep ReLU networks, providing explicit descriptions of fibers for three-layer bottleneck architectures and a polynomial-time algorithm for checking functional equivalence, with analysis of conservation laws along gradient flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the parameter symmetries in deep ReLU networks, which is crucial for analyzing loss landscapes, optimization dynamics, and model identifiability.

Method: Develops a theoretical framework with semi-algebraic geometry to characterize generic parameter fibers, and derives an algorithm for testing functional equivalence.

Result: Complete characterization of generic parameter fibers for three-layer bottleneck ReLU networks; polynomial-time algorithm for functional equivalence; identification of which symmetries induce local conservation laws in gradient flow.

Conclusion: Parameter symmetries in ReLU networks include both discrete and continuous transformations, and their effects on gradient flow dynamics vary; some symmetries lead to conservation laws while others do not.

Abstract: We develop a framework for analyzing parameter symmetries in deep ReLU networks and obtain a complete characterization of the generic parameter fibers for three-layer bottleneck architectures. Our approach provides explicit semi-algebraic descriptions of these fibers and yields a polynomial time algorithm for deciding functional equivalence of two parameters. The symmetries include discrete and continuous transformations arising from layer composition, and depend on whether deeper layers hide or preserve geometric structure from preceding layers. Finally, we show that some of these symmetries induce local conservation laws along gradient flow, while others do not.

</details>


### [851] [ISEP: Implicit Support Expansion for Offline Reinforcement Learning via Stochastic Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18320)
*Yifei Chen,Shaoqin Zhu,Xiaoqiang Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ISEP is an offline RL method that uses interpolated value functions to expand action support beyond the behavior policy, with stochastic optimization to handle multimodality, instantiated via Conditional Flow Matching with classifier-free guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional offline RL enforces strict constraints for safety but prevents discovering optimal behaviors outside the behavior policy's support. ISEP aims to expand feasible action support while maintaining safety guarantees.

Method: ISEP uses a value function interpolated between in-distribution data and policy samples to implicitly expand support, then optimizes policy via stochastic alternation between conservative cloning and optimistic expansion signals. The ISEP-FM instantiation uses Conditional Flow Matching with classifier-free guidance.

Result: The method "densifies" high-reward regions for navigable policy improvement with bounded value error guarantees, and the stochastic action selection strategy avoids mode collapse and invalid actions.

Conclusion: ISEP provides a principled approach to expand offline RL policy support beyond behavioral constraints while preserving safety, using stochastic optimization to handle multimodal landscapes that arise from the expanded support.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning methods typically enforce strict constraints to ensure safety; yet this rigidity often prevents the discovery of optimal behaviors outside the immediate support of the behavior policy. To address this, we propose Implicit Support Expansion via stochastic Policy optimization (ISEP), which leverages a value function interpolated between in-distribution data and policy samples to implicitly expand the feasible action support. This mechanism "densifies" high-reward regions, creating a navigable path for policy improvement while theoretically guaranteeing bounded value error. However, optimizing against this expanded support creates a multimodal landscape where standard deterministic averaging leads to mode collapse and invalid actions. ISEP mitigates this via a stochastic action selection strategy, optimizing the policy by stochastically alternating between conservative cloning and optimistic expansion signals. We instantiate this framework as ISEP-FM using Conditional Flow Matching utilizing classifier-free guidance to effectively capture the interpolated value signal.

</details>


### [852] [Prune, Update and Trim: Robust Structured Pruning for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18331)
*Diego Coello de Portugal Mecke,Tom Hanika,Lars Schmidth-Thieme*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Putri is a post-training pruning method for LLMs that updates un-pruned FFN weights to compensate for pruning error, prunes FFN layers sequentially, and removes individual attention heads instead of full layers, achieving SOTA performance even at extreme sparsity ratios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM inference is costly, especially for long-context or resource-constrained devices, motivating the need for effective post-training pruning methods to reduce model size while maintaining performance.

Method: Putri introduces three key changes: (1) updating un-pruned FFN weights to compensate for pruning error, (2) sequential pruning of FFN layers with consideration of previous updates, and (3) removing individual attention heads instead of full attention layers, with extension to Grouped-Query Attention.

Result: Putri demonstrates SOTA performance across multiple models, various sparsity ranges, and different datasets. Notably, it can prune LLMs at extreme sparsity ratios where previous methods fail.

Conclusion: Putri is a simple yet effective structured pruning method that achieves state-of-the-art results and generalizes well across different models and sparsity levels.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have experienced significant growth and development in recent years. However, performing inference on LLMs remains costly, especially for long-context inference or in resource-constrained devices. This motivates the development of new post-training pruning (PTP) methods. These methods reduce LLMs' requirements by removing a substantial part of the model's parameters. The discarded weights are selected depending on their impact on the models performance. Current PTP methods prune the models by removing the less informative hidden nodes from the FFN layers, and the least important attention layers. We propose Putri, a PTP method that introduces three changes to the State- of-the-art. First, we update the un-pruned weights of the FFN to compensate for the introduced pruning error. Second, the FFN layers are pruned sequentially, taking into account the updates done to the previous layers. Third, instead of removing full attention layers, we remove individual attention-heads. We extend this method such that it can also address Grouped-Query Attention. In summary, Putri is a structure pruning method which remains simple while showing SOTA performance. Pruning experiments on multiple models with a wide variety of sparsity ranges and on different datasets, validate the generality of Putri. Notably, we demonstrate that, unlike previous methods, Putri can prune LLMs on extreme sparsity ratios. The code is available at: https://github.com/Coello-dev/Putri.

</details>


### [853] [Decoupled Conformal Optimisation: Efficient Prediction Sets via Independent Tuning and Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18354)
*Fanyi Wu,Lihua Niu,Samuel Kaski,Michele Caprio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCO decouples tuning and calibration splits in conformal prediction to achieve standard marginal coverage without multiple-testing corrections, often producing smaller prediction sets than PAC-style methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian conformal optimization methods couple held-out data for both structure search and coverage certification, which is unnecessary for standard marginal conformal coverage and may inflate prediction set sizes.

Method: DCO uses a three-stage train-tune-calibrate pipeline: (1) training, (2) independent tuning split for structural selection (e.g., model or conformity score choice), and (3) a fresh calibration split for computing the final conformal quantile, preserving exchangeability for marginal coverage.

Result: On ImageNet-A, average set size dropped from 26.52 to 25.26 and 95th-percentile from 58.95 to 53.73; on Diabetes, average interval width decreased from 2.098 to 1.914. DCO consistently tracks nominal coverage across benchmarks.

Conclusion: DCO provides standard finite-sample marginal coverage with smaller prediction sets than PAC-style calibration, and under consistency assumptions both approaches converge to the same population threshold.

Abstract: Bayesian conformal optimisation methods often use the same held-out data both to search for efficient prediction sets and to certify coverage or risk. This coupling is natural for high-probability risk-control guarantees, but it is not necessary when the target is standard finite-sample marginal conformal coverage. We propose Decoupled Conformal Optimisation (DCO), a train-tune-calibrate design principle that uses an independent tuning split for efficiency-oriented structural selection and a fresh calibration split for the final conformal quantile. Conditional on the tuned structure, standard split-conformal exchangeability yields finite-sample marginal coverage for any candidate class, without a confidence parameter or multiple-testing correction. DCO therefore targets a different finite-sample guarantee from PAC-style methods: marginal conformal coverage rather than high-probability risk control. Under consistency assumptions on the coupled risk bound, the two approaches nevertheless converge to the same population threshold. Across classification and regression benchmarks, including ImageNet-A, CIFAR-100, Diabetes, California Housing, and Concrete, DCO tracks the nominal coverage level closely while often reducing average prediction-set size or interval width relative to PAC-style calibration. On ImageNet-A, for example, the average set size decreases from $26.52$ to $25.26$ and the 95th-percentile set size from $58.95$ to $53.73$; on Diabetes, the average interval width decreases from $2.098$ to $1.914$.

</details>


### [854] [Proximal basin hopping: global optimization with guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18364)
*Guillaume Lauga,Cesare Molinari,Samuel Vaiter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proximal Basin Hopping (PBH) is a new theoretical framework combining proximal optimization and local minimization that yields an algorithm guaranteed to converge to global minimizers with high probability using finite samples, outperforming existing theoretically-backed methods especially in high dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global optimization lacks theoretical guarantees despite empirical success of many algorithms, motivating the need for a framework with both rigorous theory and practical performance.

Method: The paper proposes Proximal Basin Hopping (PBH), a framework that combines proximal optimization with local minimization, and constructs a practical algorithm with finite-sample convergence guarantees to the global minimizer with high probability.

Result: PBH outperforms well-known theoretically-backed algorithms on standard synthetic hard functions and real-world problems like fitting scaling laws for deep learning; performance gap improves with increasing dimensionality.

Conclusion: PBH bridges theory and practice in global optimization, offering strong convergence guarantees while achieving superior empirical performance, particularly in high-dimensional settings.

Abstract: Global optimization is a challenging problem, with plenty of algorithms displaying empirical success, but scarce theoretical backing. In this work, we propose a new theoretical framework called Proximal Basin Hopping (PBH), carefully tailored to combine proximal optimization and local minimization. We use it to construct a practical algorithm that converges to the global minimizer with high probability, when using a finite amount of samples. Proximal Basin Hopping outperforms well known algorithms with theoretical backing on standard synthetic hard functions, and real problems such as fitting scaling laws for deep learning. Furthermore, the higher the dimension, the better the performance gap.

</details>


### [855] [Beyond Inference-Time Search: Reinforcement Learning Synthesizes Reusable Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18374)
*Soheyl Massoudi,Gabriel Apaza,Milad Habibi,Mark Fuge*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates using reinforcement learning (GRPO) to fine-tune a code LLM (Qwen2.5-Coder-14B-Instruct) to generate reusable solvers for combinatorial optimization problems (SDS variant). The resulting policy produces constraint-aware Simulated Annealing code achieving 5.0% gap to VBS, 91x cheaper than Best-of-64, though sensitive to reward design choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs typically solve each combinatorial optimization instance independently via sampling/search/prompting at inference time, incurring high reasoning cost per instance. The authors ask whether reinforcement learning can shift this reasoning cost into model weights so the LLM synthesizes reusable solvers for an entire problem family.

Method: Fine-tuned Qwen2.5-Coder-14B-Instruct with GRPO using a feasibility-gated reward and light structural scaffolding on Synergistic Dependency Selection (SDS), a variant of constrained Quadratic Knapsack designed to expose failures of greedy heuristics.

Result: The policy converges to constraint-aware Simulated Annealing in 99.8% of feasible SDS outputs, achieving 5.0% gap to VBS, and is 91x cheaper than cumulative Best-of-64. Negative ablations show standard stabilizers degrade performance, soft feasibility gate fails, sensitivity to reward normalization.

Conclusion: RL can shift reasoning cost into LLM weights to generate reusable solvers, but results remain sensitive to reward normalization and domain-specific design choices. Transfer to Job Shop Scheduling shows narrower positive evidence.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) typically approach combinatorial optimization as an inference-time procedure, solving each instance separately through sampling, search, or repeated prompting. We ask whether reinforcement learning can instead shift part of this reasoning cost into the weights of a code LLM, so that the model synthesizes a reusable solver for an entire problem family.
